0. Infrastructure & SDK Overview

Infrastructure

Kiptra AI runs on a dual-VPS architecture (DigitalOcean CPU-Optimized droplets behind a load balancer, ~$262–$317/mo total infrastructure) with a modular monolith deployed via Docker Compose. The backend is Hono + Node.js (TypeScript), using NATS JetStream (3-node Raft cluster for HA from Day 1) as the event bus for inter-agent communication, Valkey + BullMQ for job queuing/cron/caching (DigitalOcean Managed Valkey HA with automatic failover replica), and Supabase Pro (PostgreSQL + Row-Level Security) as the multi-tenant database. The frontend is Next.js 16 on Vercel.

AI inference routes through Vercel AI Gateway (zero markup, managed service — zero ops overhead vs. self-hosted LiteLLM or OpenRouter’s 5.5% credit purchase fee), with per-request model routing across GPT-4.1 nano, Claude Haiku 4.5, and Claude Sonnet 4.6 based on task complexity. Supporting infra includes Cloudflare (WAF/CDN), Prometheus + Grafana Cloud + Sentry for monitoring, Pino + Grafana Loki + OpenTelemetry for structured logging/tracing, and GitHub Actions for CI/CD.

SDKs & Their Purpose

thorchh/agent-starter serves as the primary boilerplate, providing native Vercel AI SDK (AI SDK v6) multi-step tool calling (tool() + Zod), streaming, and chain-of-thought in an exact stack match (Next.js 16, shadcn/ui, Tailwind v4). The Vercel AI SDK provides type-safe generateText() / streamText() / generateText({ output: Output.object({ schema }) }) with Zod-validated structured output. Mastra patterns (graph workflows, MCP tool servers via CedarCopilot/cedar-mastra-starter and deep-search template) are adopted selectively as orchestration complexity grows — lightweight-first, upgrade when justified.

better-auth (with its Organization plugin) handles self-hosted multi-tenant RBAC, team invitations, and role scoping — chosen over NextAuth (no built-in multi-tenancy) and Clerk (per-MAU cost). React Flow v12 powers the interactive architecture mind map and live agent status visualization.

Socket.io provides real-time WebSocket push (attached to the raw Node.js http.Server alongside Hono) for full control and to avoid Supabase Realtime’s per-connection billing at scale (500 default on Pro, expandable to 10K). shadcn/ui + Tailwind + TanStack Table form the dashboard UI layer, and Recharts handles CEO financial dashboards.

Integration adapters sit behind an anti-corruption layer so swapping JobTread for Procore or Jobber is a single adapter file.

Context

Kiptra AI is a general-purpose multi-tenant AI agent platform (SaaS) that enables businesses to deploy customizable AI agent teams. The platform is industry-agnostic but launches with construction/remodeling as the first vertical. Slate (slatedesignremodel.com) is the first proof-of-concept customer — not the sole target. The goal is to scale to thousands of customers (individuals + teams with employees) across similar service businesses.

Beyond the core agent platform, Kiptra includes multiple feature modules that differentiate it from generic AI tools: a Business Operating System (Section 15) providing a complete business management framework with KPI dashboards, quarterly goal tracking, and org accountability; a Kitchen & Bath AI Design Tool (Section 16) that replaces legacy Windows-only CAD software with an interactive browser-based 2D/Elevation/3D design canvas powered by three AI engines; a CRM with 13-stage pipeline, contact management, and deal tracking; and Team Chat with multi-platform sync across Kiptra, Slack, and Microsoft Teams.

Each customer gets a configurable set of AI agents (7–9 per tenant depending on industry persona (28 total agent types across Contractor, Dealer, Sales Rep, and Manufacturer personas)) communicating via a hub-and-spoke event bus. AI inference routes through Vercel AI Gateway. The agents are customizable per industry/customer — the Contractor persona’s 7-agent configuration (Leads Agent, Design Agent, Project Management Agent, Sales Agent, Bookkeeping Agent, CEO Agent, Support Agent) is the base template. Dealers add Catalog Pricing and Order Routing agents (9 total); Sales Reps swap PM for Territory Intelligence and Commission Optimizer agents (7 total); Manufacturers add Catalog Publisher, Production Scheduler, and Quality Analytics agents (9 total).

Deliverable: Standalone Architecture Mind Map — A beautiful, interactive visual (React Flow) built as a shareable standalone page. Purpose: explain the platform to non-technical stakeholders (Adam, investors, partners). NOT embedded in the app dashboard — it's a communication tool.

Decisions locked:

  • Language: TypeScript (full-stack)
  • Infra: Dual VPS (DigitalOcean or Hetzner — cost comparison below) + Load Balancer
  • Database: Supabase Pro ($25/mo)
  • Mind map: Standalone interactive React Flow page (shareable)
  • Frontend: Next.js on Vercel (already deployed)

1. Architecture Overview

Architecture
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  VERCEL (Frontend + Auth + Billing APIs)         │
│  Next.js 16 App Router                           │
│  ├── Dashboard (React Flow mind map)             │
│  ├── Agent status + escalation queue             │
│  ├── CEO portal (CEO Agent UI)         │
│  └── better-auth (RBAC + Organizations)           │
└──────────┬───────────────────────────────────────┘
           │ HTTPS (REST + WebSocket)
           ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  DUAL VPS (Agent Backend Service)                 │
│  Primary + Secondary (Hybrid Active-Active/       │
│  Active-Passive) behind DO LB                      │
│  HTTP: both nodes active via LB. NATS event bus +  │
│  scheduled agents: primary only. On primary fail,  │
│  HTTP continues on secondary; event-driven agents  │
│  suspended. Shared state: Managed Valkey + NATS    │
│  3-node Raft cluster. See Section 12 failure modes.│
│  Node.js + TypeScript                            │
│  ├── Agent Executor (7-9 agents per tenant,        │
│  │    28 types across 4 personas)                  │
│  ├── Vercel AI Gateway (managed, zero ops)  │
│  ├── Event Bus Consumer/Producer                 │
│  └── Cron Scheduler (daily briefings, AR checks) │
│                                                  │
│  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐                  │
│  │ NATS       │  │ Valkey     │                  │
│  │ JetStream  │  │ (BullMQ +  │                  │
│  │ (event bus)│  │  cache)    │                  │
│  └────────────┘  └────────────┘                  │
│  NATS primary runs on VPS-1 only; VPS-2 connects  │
│  as a leaf node. A 3rd NATS node ($5-10/mo) is    │
│  deployed from Day 1 to form a Raft quorum,       │
│  enabling JetStream HA (eliminates event bus       │
│  SPOF). Valkey runs as Managed Valkey HA           │
│  (automatic failover replica) from Day 1.          │
│  See Section 12 Dual-VPS Architecture for         │
│  failure modes and mitigations.                                     │
│                                                  │
│  NATS Leaf Node Warnings:                         │
│  (a) Leaf nodes may accept publishes during hub   │
│      unavailability without persisting to          │
│      JetStream — messages silently lost.           │
│  (b) JetStream acks do not guarantee durability   │
│      (Jepsen 2024: writes flushed every 2 min).   │
│      Mitigation: Set sync_interval=always in      │
│      JetStream config (forces fsync per write).   │
│      Throughput drops to ~hundreds msg/s —         │
│      acceptable for agent event bus volume.        │
│  (c) All critical events use outbox pattern to    │
│      guarantee delivery.                          │
└──────────┬───────────────────────────────────────┘
           │ SQL + RLS
           ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  SUPABASE PRO                                    │
│  PostgreSQL + Row-Level Security                 │
│  ├── tenants, agents, executions, events         │
│  ├── Encrypted secrets (tenant API keys)         │
│  ├── Realtime DB change notifications            │
│  └── Auth (better-auth adapter)                  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

2. VPS Cost Comparison — DigitalOcean vs Hetzner

SpecDigitalOceanHetznerNotes
4 vCPU / 8GB (x2 dual)$80/mo each = $160/mo$5.49/mo each = $10.98/mo1–50 customers (dual VPS from Day 1). DO prices = CPU-Optimized (dedicated vCPU); Hetzner prices = CX series (shared vCPU)
DO Load Balancer$12/moN/A (use Hetzner LB)Health-check failover between nodes
DO Managed Valkey HA$30/mo (1GB HA) / $45/mo (2GB HA) / $90/mo (4GB HA)N/A (self-hosted Redis)Managed Valkey HA with automatic failover replica (Day 1 requirement). Replaces discontinued Managed Redis (June 2025). Used for BullMQ, Socket.io adapter, caching. Eliminates cache/queue SPOF.
8 vCPU / 16GB (x2 dual)$160/mo each = $320/mo$19.99/mo each = $39.98/mo50–100 customers
16 vCPU / 32GB (x2 dual)$320/mo each = $640/mo$28.49/mo each = $56.98/mo100+ customers
Serverless GPU Rendering (Modal)~$10–30/moK&B server-side rendering at launch (10K renders/mo). Per-second billing, scale to zero, no idle cost. Scales linearly with render volume.
Day 1 dual-VPS + LB$172/mo$23/mo2 nodes ($160) + LB ($12) — eliminates single-node VPS as SPOF. NATS HA via 3rd node ($5–10/mo) eliminates event bus SPOF. Valkey HA (automatic failover replica) eliminates cache/queue SPOF. Frontend/dashboard availability depends on Vercel’s managed infrastructure
NATS 3rd Node (Raft quorum)$5–10/mo$3–5/moLightweight VPS running only NATS for JetStream HA. Day 1 requirement — eliminates event bus SPOF
Total Monthly Infrastructure (Day 1)$262–$317/mo2x droplets ($160) + LB ($12) + NATS 3rd node ($5–$10) + Managed Valkey HA ($30–$45) + Cloudflare Pro ($20) + Supabase Pro ($25) + Serverless GPU Rendering ($10–$30) + KEK Secrets Manager ($0–$10)

DigitalOcean advantages: Better UI, US data centers (NYC/SFO), App Platform, managed DBs, 1-click Docker, larger community.

Hetzner advantages: ~70% cheaper when comparing shared vCPU (Hetzner CX series) vs dedicated vCPU (DO CPU-Optimized) — not a like-for-like comparison. Hetzner dedicated vCPU (CCX series) starts at ~€14.49/mo for 2 dedicated cores/8GB — the cost advantage narrows significantly on like-for-like comparison. US data centers available (Ashburn VA + Hillsboro OR since 2021), EU data centers, Hetzner Cloud API. Note: Hetzner announced a 30–37% price increase effective April 1, 2026, which further narrows the gap.

Recommendation: Start with two DigitalOcean CPU-Optimized droplets (4vCPU/8GB, $80/mo each) behind a DigitalOcean Load Balancer ($12/mo) = $172/mo for compute + LB. Add NATS 3rd node ($5–$10/mo), Managed Valkey HA ($30–$45/mo), Cloudflare Pro ($20/mo), Supabase Pro ($25/mo), Serverless GPU Rendering via Modal ($10–$30/mo), and KEK Secrets Manager ($0–$10/mo) for a Day 1 total of ~$262–$317/mo. Dual-VPS + NATS HA + Valkey HA from Day 1 — intentionally over-engineered for reliability with no single points of failure. Migrate to Hetzner later if cost pressure grows. The architecture is VPS-agnostic — Docker Compose deploys anywhere.

3. Tech Stack (Final)

LayerTechnologyWhy
FrontendNext.js 16 (App Router) on VercelLatest stable (shipped October 2025, current 16.2 as of March 2026), global CDN. Default to Server Components; only add 'use client' for interactive elements (forms, real-time displays, Socket.io consumers). Route protection via middleware.ts (edge-level, never client-side guards). Granular <Suspense> boundaries per async component (never wrap root layout in a single Suspense). loading.tsx files for every data-fetching route segment.
UI Componentsshadcn/ui + Tailwind CSS + TanStack TableZero lock-in, accessible (Radix), best B2B dashboard choice
Agent VisualizationReact Flow v12Interactive mind map + live agent status
ChartsRechartsCEO dashboard financials
Authbetter-auth (Organization plugin)Self-hosted, free, built-in multi-tenant RBAC, team management, invitations
BackendHono + TypeScript on Node.jsTypeScript-first, fastest benchmarks, native SSE streaming for AI. DO Load Balancer idle timeout configured to 300s for SSE/streaming routes. SSE keepalive: Send : keep-alive\n\n comment every 15 seconds (Cloudflare has a 100s idle timeout, and LLM inference latency can reach 60–90s — a 60s keepalive is unsafe). SSE anti-buffering (CF-01): SSE routes MUST return the X-Accel-Buffering: no response header AND, if using Cloudflare Tunnel, set the --no-chunked-encoding flag in Tunnel config. Topology clarification (CF-05): The production architecture uses Cloudflare DNS proxy mode (orange-cloud) with traffic routed to the DigitalOcean Load Balancer — NOT Cloudflare Tunnel. Cloudflare Tunnel is an alternative topology for environments without a public LB. Choose one: (a) Cloudflare DNS proxy → DO Load Balancer (production default), or (b) Cloudflare Tunnel → direct to VPS (no LB needed). Do not mix both. Without both, Cloudflare buffers SSE tokens and delivers them in bursts instead of streaming. DO LB note (DO-02): The idle_timeout_seconds setting on DigitalOcean Load Balancers cannot be configured via the dashboard — must use the DO API or Terraform. Implementation: Hono SSE middleware. This is a Wave 2 build item alongside the streaming infrastructure.
Agent FrameworkVercel AI SDK (AI SDK v6) (@ai-sdk/anthropic + @ai-sdk/openai) on Hono backendthorchh/agent-starter referenced for UI/chat patterns only — not used as backend runtime. Agent execution uses bare generateText, streamText, generateText({ output: Output.object({ schema }) }) with Zod schemas inside BullMQ workers. AI SDK v6’s ToolLoopAgent abstraction is available as a base class for agent workers but bare generateText/streamText is preferred for full control over retry logic, cost tracking, and human-in-the-loop approval. Mastra patterns (graphs, MCP) adopted selectively as orchestration complexity grows
AI GatewayVercel AI Gateway (managed) + direct API keysZero markup (BYOK), managed service, 40+ providers, automatic fallback routing, built-in prompt caching, zero ops overhead
Event BusNATS JetStreamHub-and-spoke, 10K–50K msgs/sec with JetStream persistence (workload-dependent; 100K+ for core NATS pub/sub without persistence), 2 vCPU/4GB RAM footprint
Job QueueBullMQ (Valkey)Cron, retries, scheduled agent tasks. DigitalOcean Managed Valkey is wire-compatible with Redis; BullMQ works unchanged
CacheValkey + Node.js LRUL1 in-process, L2 distributed (Managed Valkey)
Real-timeSocket.io on Hono backendFull control, native NATS integration, no per-connection billing (vs Supabase Realtime’s 500 default on Pro, expandable to 10K at $10/1K connections). NATS → backend → Socket.io → client
DatabaseSupabase Pro (PostgreSQL + RLS)$25/mo, managed, real-time. RLS optimized with indexed tenant_id + initPlan pattern
SecretsAES-256-GCM encrypted in SupabasePer-tenant JobTread/QB/Google/Microsoft keys with rotation support
Microsoft Auth@azure/msal-node + @microsoft/microsoft-graph-clientMSAL handles OAuth 2.0 + PKCE flow, token caching, and silent refresh. Graph client provides typed access to Mail, Calendar, and Subscriptions APIs. Day 1 integration alongside Google.
MonitoringPrometheus + Grafana (self-hosted Loki on VPS for log retention compliance; Grafana Cloud free tier insufficient for SOC 2’s 1-year requirement) + SentryMetrics, alerting, error tracking from Day 1. Sentry note (MON-06): Sentry provides official Hono support via @sentry/node. The community @hono/sentry middleware is deprecated — use the official Sentry SDK directly.
LoggingPino (structured JSON) → Grafana Alloy → Grafana Loki + OpenTelemetrySearchable agent logs + distributed tracing across agent execution flows. Log collector (MON-02): Use Grafana Alloy (not Promtail, which is deprecated) as the log shipping agent to Loki.
CI/CDGitHub ActionsLint, type-check, test, deploy on every PR. Vercel auto-deploys frontend.
WAF / CDNCloudflare Pro ($20/mo)DDoS protection, WAF rules, SSL termination for dual VPS

Next.js App Router Conventions (KB-Enforced):

  • Server/Client Component boundary: Default to Server Components for all pages and data-fetching components. Only add 'use client' for: form components with useState/useFormState, Socket.io real-time consumers (useSocket hook), React Flow interactive canvas, client-side search/filter controls, toast/modal providers. Explicit client component boundaries: components/providers/*.tsx (auth, socket, theme), components/forms/*.tsx, components/realtime/*.tsx, components/charts/*.tsx (Recharts).
  • Route protection: ALL route protection MUST use Next.js middleware.ts at the edge — NOT client-side route guards. Client-side guards flash protected content before redirect. Middleware intercepts at the edge before any HTML is sent. The middleware.ts performs ONLY lightweight, synchronous checks: cookie presence, JWT decode (without database calls), and redirect logic. It checks the better-auth session cookie, redirects unauthenticated users to /login, and handles tenant-scoped routing. PPR preservation rule: Do NOT perform database lookups in middleware.ts (e.g., checking tenant_members.onboarding_completed, role verification, feature flags). Database calls in middleware add ~150ms blocking latency before any HTML is sent, killing Partial Prerendering (PPR). All authorization checks that require database access (role verification, onboarding status, feature flags) MUST happen in Server Components behind granular <Suspense> boundaries, preserving PPR’s ability to send the static shell immediately.
  • Suspense strategy: Do NOT wrap the root layout in a single <Suspense> boundary (this blocks the entire page). Use granular <Suspense> boundaries around individual async Server Components (e.g., dashboard stats card, agent list, execution history table). Each boundary gets its own skeleton loader matching the component shape.
  • loading.tsx files: Required for every route segment that fetches data: /dashboard/loading.tsx, /escalations/loading.tsx, /settings/team/loading.tsx, /settings/billing/loading.tsx, etc. Next.js uses these for instant loading states during navigation.
  • redirect() safety: redirect() from next/navigation throws a NEXT_REDIRECT error internally. NEVER call redirect() inside a try/catch block — it will be caught and swallowed. Either call redirect() outside try/catch, or use the isRedirectError() guard to re-throw redirect errors in catch blocks.
  • Data fetching cache control: Server Components that call Supabase MUST specify explicit cache control. Use export const revalidate = 0 (no cache) for tenant-scoped dynamic data. Use export const revalidate = 3600 for static reference data. Never rely on Next.js default caching for authenticated, tenant-scoped queries.
  • generateMetadata: Required on all public-facing pages (/login, /forgot-password, /invite/*, /signup/*, marketing pages) for SEO. Authenticated pages use a shared default metadata export.
  • useSearchParams(): Must be wrapped in its own <Suspense> boundary in client components. Without this, it opts the entire page out of static generation. Wrap the component using useSearchParams() in a <Suspense fallback={null}>.

3b. Key Stack Decisions (Researched)

Each technology choice was evaluated against 3–5 alternatives. Below are the decisions that diverge from “obvious defaults” and why.

Hono over Express

Express is the Node.js default but community-maintained types, no built-in validation, and showing its age. Hono is TypeScript-first with 2–3x better benchmarks, native SSE streaming (critical for AI responses), and strongly typed routes. Runs on Node.js — not Bun (compatibility issues with native modules in production). (HONO-04) Minimum version: Hono ≥4.12.0 (well past the CORS + WebSocket fix where CORS middleware incorrectly intercepted WebSocket upgrade requests in earlier versions).

Hono Production Constraints (KB-Verified):

  • (HONO-01) BANNED: c.notFound(). Use c.json({ error: "Not found" }, 404) instead. c.notFound() returns Response instead of a typed response, silently breaking Hono RPC type inference with no compile error. Enforce via custom ESLint rule (no-restricted-properties on c.notFound).
  • (HONO-02) WebSocket setup: @hono/node-ws requires injectWebSocket(server) on the raw Node.js HTTP server returned by @hono/node-server. Without this call, WebSocket upgrade requests silently fail (no error, no connection). This applies to any native Hono WebSocket routes (Socket.io has its own upgrade path).
  • (HONO-03) SSE disconnect handling: Hono’s streamSSE does NOT detect client disconnects. Must use c.req.raw.signal + stream.onAbort() to cancel in-flight LLM inference when a client disconnects. Without this, disconnected clients waste compute on abandoned streaming responses. Required for all agent streaming endpoints.
  • (HONO-05) Logging middleware: clone before reading. Response body must be cloned via c.res.clone() before reading in logging middleware. Reading the body directly marks the stream as “disturbed,” sending an empty response to the client.
  • (HONO-06) RPC + path aliases: TypeScript path aliases (@/lib/types) break hc<typeof app>() client type inference across packages. Route signatures must use relative imports or node_modules imports for types consumed by the RPC client.
  • (HONO-07) hcWithType pattern: Direct hc<typeof app>() with a large app causes slow IDE type-checking (~10s+ per keystroke). Use the hcWithType pattern to separate the type layer from the runtime client, keeping IDE responsiveness under 1s.
  • (HONO-08) JSX pragma in monorepo: Hono files must use /** @jsxImportSource hono/jsx */; React files must use /** @jsxImportSource react */. Without explicit pragmas, the monorepo’s shared tsconfig picks one JSX namespace globally, causing type errors in the other.

better-auth over next-auth

next-auth (Auth.js v5) has no built-in multi-tenancy, organizations, or RBAC. Building these from scratch adds 2–3 weeks of work. better-auth provides all three via its Organization plugin: team management, invitations, custom roles/permissions, member lifecycle — self-hosted, free, zero per-user fees. Clerk ($0.02/MAU) was evaluated but creates vendor lock-in and costs scale linearly.

better-auth Security Hardening (KB-Enforced):

  • CRITICAL — Minimum version: better-auth >=1.4.5. CVE-2025-61928 (CVSS 9.3, October 2025) allows unauthenticated API key creation for arbitrary users when the API keys plugin is enabled. Fixed in v1.3.26. GHSA-x732-6j76-qmhm (path normalization bypass) fixed in v1.4.5. Pin to >=1.4.5 and add to Wave 1 security audit checklist.
  • CSRF protection: better-auth has built-in CSRF protection enabled by default. Verify advanced.disableCSRFCheck is NOT set to true in the better-auth server config. This is a Wave 1 security checklist item.
  • Rate limiting on auth endpoints: better-auth does NOT rate-limit by default. Rate limiting on login, signup, and password reset endpoints (5–10 req/min/IP) MUST be implemented via Hono middleware (Valkey-backed sliding window). This is already specified in Security Hardening (Section 12) but must be enforced at the better-auth handler level.
  • Session token storage: Verify better-auth is configured with httpOnly: true, secure: true, sameSite: 'lax' cookie settings. better-auth handles this by default but the configuration must be explicitly verified in Wave 1 security audit.
  • Account lockout: better-auth does NOT include account lockout after failed login attempts. Implement lockout after 5 consecutive failures (15-minute lockout) at the application layer. Track failed attempts in Valkey (key: auth:lockout:{email}, TTL: 15 min). This is already specified in the UX spec (Section 13a) but requires explicit implementation — it is not a better-auth built-in.
  • OAuth state parameter validation: better-auth handles OAuth CSRF protection via the state parameter. Verify this is active in the OAuth provider configuration. Do not implement custom OAuth state handling that could conflict with better-auth’s built-in mechanism.
  • Session revocation on password change: better-auth does NOT automatically revoke sessions on password change. The password change handler MUST explicitly call auth.api.revokeAllSessions() after a successful password change. Without this, stolen sessions remain valid after password reset.
  • Session revocation on member removal: Removing a member from an organization does NOT auto-clear their session in better-auth. The removal handler MUST explicitly revoke all sessions for the removed user via auth.api.revokeAllSessions({ userId }). Without this, removed users retain access until their session cookie expires.
  • Drizzle ORM compatibility: If using Drizzle ORM as the better-auth database adapter, pin the Drizzle version and test adapter compatibility before upgrading. Drizzle v1.0 may introduce breaking changes to the better-auth adapter. Run the better-auth test suite against any Drizzle upgrade before merging.
  • Multi-tab session behavior: better-auth sessions are cookie-based and SHARED across all browser tabs for the same domain. Logging out in one tab invalidates the session for all tabs on the next request. Do NOT build per-tab session isolation features using localStorage — the httpOnly cookie is the single source of truth. Tabs that are actively open will not receive a real-time logout signal from better-auth itself; use Socket.io to broadcast session invalidation events to all connected tabs.

Vercel AI Gateway over LiteLLM and OpenRouter

vs. LiteLLM: No self-hosted Python proxy to maintain, no memory leaks requiring nightly restarts, no 800+ open GitHub issues, no DB bottleneck at scale. Frees 1,024 MB RAM per VPS node. Vercel AI Gateway is a managed service — zero ops overhead.

vs. OpenRouter: Zero markup (OpenRouter charges 5.5% on credit purchases), deeper AI SDK integration (native generateText/streamText compatibility), automatic Anthropic prompt caching support.

Trade-off: Vercel vendor dependency, mitigated by the internal AIGateway abstraction layer. All AI calls go through an AIGateway interface — swapping Vercel AI Gateway for direct provider API calls requires a single-line change in the factory function.

Socket.io over Supabase Realtime

Supabase Realtime defaults to 500 concurrent connections on Pro (expandable to 10,000 at $10/1,000 connections with no spend cap). At 1,000 customers with 2 concurrent users each = 2,000 connections, Socket.io chosen for full control, native NATS integration, and to avoid per-connection billing at scale. Socket.io on the Hono backend has no connection limits, integrates naturally with NATS (agents → NATS → backend → Socket.io → client), and keeps real-time infrastructure under full control.

Socket.io Integration Pattern: Socket.io attaches to the raw Node.js http.Server returned by @hono/node-server, not to Hono itself. This is standard Node.js — fully proven and documented. Hono handles HTTP routes; Socket.io handles WebSocket connections on the same port. No compatibility risk.

Socket.io Production Hardening (KB-Enforced):

  • Transport mode (CRITICAL): Configure transports: ['websocket'] (WebSocket-only, disable HTTP long-polling fallback). Without sticky sessions, HTTP long-polling requests hit different servers behind the DigitalOcean Load Balancer and fail. Since we use a stateless LB without sticky sessions, WebSocket-only transport is mandatory. If long-polling fallback is ever needed, sticky sessions MUST be configured on the LB first.
  • Memory leak prevention: Set perMessageDeflate: false on the Socket.io server. The default (true) leaks ~300KB of native (un-GC-able) memory per connection. At 2,000 concurrent connections, this is 600MB of un-reclaimable memory. Disable unless benchmarking proves compression savings outweigh the leak.
  • Ping tuning for long-lived connections: Set pingInterval: 25000 and pingTimeout: 10000. Default values cause false disconnections under network jitter. These values must stay within Cloudflare’s 100-second idle timeout (25s + 10s = 35s max silence, well under the limit).
  • Per-tenant rate limiting: Implement per-tenant Socket.io event rate limiting to prevent a single tenant from flooding the WebSocket. Max events per tenant: 100/sec for dashboard updates, 10/sec for user-initiated actions. Enforced in the Socket.io middleware layer.
  • Connection count limits: Enforce per-tenant connection count limits to prevent resource exhaustion. Default: 50 concurrent connections per tenant (adjustable per plan tier). Reject new connections with a connection_limit_exceeded error when the limit is hit.
  • Reconnection strategy: Client-side reconnection MUST use exponential backoff with jitter (not linear). Default Socket.io linear reconnection causes thundering herd after server restart. Configure: reconnectionDelay: 1000, reconnectionDelayMax: 16000, randomizationFactor: 0.5.
  • Binary data handling: Socket.io binary transport adds significant overhead. For large payloads (file uploads, data exports >100KB), use HTTP endpoints instead of Socket.io. Socket.io is for lightweight real-time events only (status updates, notifications, presence).
  • Room cleanup on disconnect: Implement explicit room cleanup in the disconnect event handler. Stale room memberships leak memory over time. On disconnect: remove socket from all tenant rooms, decrement tenant connection counter in Valkey, log disconnection with tenant context.

Cross-node adapter: Socket.io uses @socket.io/redis-adapter for cross-node event delivery (using DigitalOcean Managed Valkey for cross-node delivery — decouples real-time dashboard updates from the NATS dependency, ensuring WebSocket delivery survives primary VPS failure).

Client reconnection protocol: Socket.io reconnection with max 5 retries, exponential backoff with jitter (reconnectionDelay: 1000, reconnectionDelayMax: 16000, randomizationFactor: 0.5 — jitter prevents thundering herd after server restart). On reconnect, client sends last-received event timestamp; server replays missed events from event log. Room membership re-validated against current auth token on every reconnection. After 5 failed retries, show ‘Connection lost’ banner with manual retry button.

Socket.io Room Naming Convention:

  • Room naming: tenant:{tenantId}:{channel} (e.g., tenant:abc-123:dashboard, tenant:abc-123:agent:estimate-engine, tenant:abc-123:escalations)
  • Room joins are SERVER-INITIATED only — server places socket into rooms after auth validation.
  • Validation: parseTenantRoom(roomName) extracts tenant ID and verifies it matches the authenticated session.
  • Clients cannot request arbitrary room names.

Agent Framework: Lightweight Tool Calling (over Pure Custom Loops)

Five boilerplates were evaluated for production-ready agent tool calling (verified March 2026 via each repo’s README + visible code):

BoilerplateTool Calls?Details
mastra-ai/mastraYes — strongestNative createTool, multi-step reasoning, agent graphs, MCP tool servers, Vercel AI SDK UI hooks. Full-featured but heavy — graphs, RAG tiers, multi-agent orchestration add complexity beyond what’s needed at launch.
thorchh/agent-starterYes — lightweightNext.js 16 + Vercel AI SDK (AI SDK v6) + shadcn/ui + Tailwind v4. Ships multi-step tool calling (tool() + Zod), streaming, chain-of-thought, file uploads, message branching. Zero extra abstractions. Tools live in src/lib/ai/tools/.
vercel/chatbotPartial (SDK-level)README references AI SDK tool calling capability. No concrete tool examples in the template itself — clean streaming chat UI. Full tools addable in ~15–30 min via official cookbook.
supabase-community/vercel-ai-chatbotNoPure streaming chat with Vercel AI SDK + Supabase. Zero tool/function-calling implementation.
jabirdev/nextjs-better-authNoAuth + Drizzle + Supabase starter only. No Vercel AI SDK, no LLM features, no tools.
inngest/vercel-ai-o1-preview-crm-agentNoVercel AI SDK + Inngest for background steps. Workflow orchestration, not agent tool calls.

Decision: Two-track approach. Use thorchh/agent-starter as a UI/chat reference pattern only. Agent execution runs on the Hono backend using the bare ai package (generateText, streamText, generateText({ output: Output.object({ schema }) }) with Zod schemas) inside BullMQ workers. The boilerplate’s Next.js route handlers, streaming hooks (useChat, useCompletion), and React components are not used in the backend runtime. The actual agent framework is a custom implementation using @ai-sdk/anthropic + @ai-sdk/openai provider packages called directly from Hono route handlers and BullMQ workers. Mastra templates (particularly deep-search and CedarCopilot/cedar-mastra-starter which includes built-in React Flow) serve as reference implementations for complex orchestration patterns to adopt selectively as agent complexity grows.

Composition strategy: thorchh/agent-starter base + better-auth (Organization plugin) for multi-tenant RBAC + Supabase Pro for database + Vercel AI Gateway for model routing. Layer in Mastra patterns (graph workflows, MCP tool servers) only when proven orchestration complexity demands them. This delivers working tool-calling agents faster than building from scratch while preserving full control over retry logic, cost tracking, human-in-the-loop approval, and per-tenant model routing.

AI SDK v6 import paths: useChat must import from @ai-sdk/react, NOT ai. Core functions (generateText, streamText, tool, Output, stepCountIs) import from ai. Note: convertToModelMessages is async in v6 — all call sites need await.

Why not Mastra as the base? Mastra (22K stars, from the Gatsby team) is the strongest agent framework available — but its full feature set (graphs, MCP servers, memory tiers, RAG, multi-agent orchestration) adds complexity beyond what’s needed at launch. thorchh/agent-starter gives 100% of the Vercel AI SDK tool calling surface (streamText, generateText, tool() + Zod, multi-step tool calling via stopWhen: stepCountIs(N)) in a clean Next.js template. AI SDK v6 note: The v5 maxSteps parameter is replaced by stopWhen: stepCountIs(N) in v6. The codemod handles this automatically; using old maxSteps without migration produces a type error. Explicit stopWhen setting is mandatory. Construction-domain logic lives in agent prompts and tool definitions regardless of which base is used — start lean, upgrade to Mastra patterns when the orchestration justifies it. Mastra v1.14 update (March 2026): Mastra v1.14 ships with AI SDK v6 support and Hono server adapters, making it a viable replacement for hand-rolled agent execution in Wave 2+. Evaluate as orchestration complexity grows — the Hono adapter alignment significantly reduces integration friction compared to earlier versions.

Model routing implementation: Each agent’s tool call sites have an explicit model parameter set at development time based on task type — not dynamically classified at runtime. Example: Leads Agent classification → Nano; email drafting → Haiku; Sales Agent analysis → Sonnet. This eliminates the meta-cost of a routing classifier and is fully deterministic for cost modeling.

Model Escalation Safety Net (AI-26 — Wave 2a)

While model tiers are set at development time (above), a lightweight executeWithEscalation() wrapper adds automatic retry at a higher tier when the assigned model fails. This prevents agent execution failures from becoming user-visible outages. Inspired by pi-mono’s cross-provider model handoff system, but simplified for Kiptra’s task-oriented (not conversational) agents.

Escalation ladder: nano ($0.40/1M out) → fast/Haiku ($5/1M) → standard/Sonnet ($15/1M) → frontier/Opus ($75/1M)

4 automatic escalation triggers:

  1. retry_after_error — API error (rate limit, 500, timeout). Escalate to different provider/tier.
  2. output_quality_low — Structured output Zod validation fails after 2 retries at current tier.
  3. context_overflow — Message history exceeds 85% of current model’s context window. Escalate to model with larger window (or truncate if no larger model available).
  4. tool_failure_threshold — Tool execution failed 2+ times at current tier (suggests model isn’t generating valid tool arguments).

Guards: maxEscalations per call (default: 2 — e.g., nano → fast → standard, never reaches frontier without explicit opt-in). Budget gate checks Valkey daily spend counter before each call. Per-tier daily call limits enforced per tenant. Per-provider daily spending caps (AI-28) prevent cost spikes when one provider goes down and traffic cascades to another — the escalation system skips capped providers entirely. Frontier tier disabled by default for Starter/Professional plans.

Cost tracking: Every LLM call produces a ModelCallCost record (model, tier, tokens, latency, USD). Accumulated into ExecutionCostSummary with per-tier breakdown. Feeds into the per-tenant spending limits (enforced via AI SDK middleware + Valkey counters) and the CEO dashboard cost panel. All escalation decisions are logged with EscalationDecision { fromTier, toTier, reason, timestamp } for cost analysis.

Integration with Vercel AI SDK v6: executeWithEscalation() wraps generateText() / streamText(). Each call gets a fresh model instance resolved via resolveModelForTier(tier, tenantPrefs), which checks tenant-level overrides before falling back to defaults. Vercel AI SDK handles cross-provider message normalization via convertToModelMessages — no custom message transformation needed (unlike pi-mono, which maintains its own transformMessages engine for interactive model switching).

Key difference from pi-mono: Pi-mono solves interactive model switching (user switches mid-conversation, full history replayed with thinking block transformation). Kiptra solves autonomous agent execution where each step starts with curated context, not replayed multi-turn history. This makes Kiptra’s implementation simpler — no thinking block transformation, no tool call ID renormalization, no orphaned tool call repair.

Tenant model preferences: TenantModelPreferences stored in tenant config JSONB: tier overrides (e.g., swap Haiku for GPT-4.1 Mini), daily spend cap (USD), per-tier call limits, frontier enable/disable. Managed via admin panel.

Supabase RLS: Optimized for Scale

Naive RLS policies show up to 1,000x+ degradation without optimization (verified by Supabase's own benchmarks — naive RLS on a 100K row table: 178,000ms vs 12ms with optimizations). The following optimizations are mandatory, not optional:

  1. B-tree index on every tenant_id column + composite indexes on (tenant_id, created_at), (tenant_id, status)
  2. Wrap current_setting('app.tenant_id', true) calls in a SELECT subquery to trigger PostgreSQL's initPlan optimizer (54–57% improvement)
  3. Use immutable functions for JWT claim extraction (caches results per-transaction)
  4. Never use JSON extraction in RLS policies — denormalize into indexed columns

With these optimizations, RLS is essentially a WHERE tenant_id = X injection — PostgreSQL handles this at 1,000+ tenants without issue.

Connection pooling compatibility: Supabase’s Supavisor (formerly PgBouncer — Supabase migrated to Supavisor) transaction mode is incompatible with auth.uid() session variables. RLS policies use current_setting('app.tenant_id') set via a helper function at the start of each transaction, ensuring compatibility with transaction-mode pooling. Backend connection pool size is explicitly configured per VPS node to prevent Supavisor slot exhaustion under concurrent agent load. Connection pool sizing (Supabase Pro Small = 90 max connections): Budget across services per VPS node: API server pool = 20, BullMQ workers pool = 10, cron jobs pool = 5. Total per node = 35, total for 2 nodes = 70, leaving 20 connections as headroom for migrations, admin queries, and Supabase dashboard access. Connection pool validation: Supabase Pro default is 200 Supavisor connections in transaction mode. The per-tenant semaphore (3 concurrent DB connections per tenant per node) bounds theoretical maximum to: 100 tenants × 3 connections × 2 nodes = 600, but Supavisor multiplexes transactions so actual concurrent backend connections are much lower. Validate with load testing: simulate max concurrent agent executions (100 tenants × 5 executions) in Wave 4 k6 suite. Set Prometheus alert at 80% of Supavisor max_client_conn. If pool exhaustion occurs, reduce per-tenant semaphore to 2 or upgrade Supabase plan.

RLS policy rule: Policies reference only the tenant_id column and pre-computed, indexed permission columns. Role-based access control (RBAC) is enforced at the application layer (Hono middleware), not in RLS policies. Mixing role conditions into RLS creates the 1000x+ degradation the initPlan pattern is designed to prevent.

Enforcement: All database access must go through a withTenantContext(tenantId, callback) wrapper that calls set_tenant_context() and runs the callback within a transaction. A custom ESLint rule flags direct supabase.from() calls outside this wrapper. This is a Wave 1 implementation item.

Supavisor Transaction-Mode RLS Safety (KB-Enforced):

  • Integration test requirement (CRITICAL): Write an integration test that verifies SET LOCAL does NOT leak across transactions in Supavisor transaction-mode pooling. This is the RLS security boundary — it must be tested, not assumed. Test: open two concurrent connections via Supavisor, set different app.tenant_id values, verify each connection only sees its own tenant’s data. Run this test in CI against the actual Supabase instance (not a local mock). This is a Wave 1 security gate.
  • Filtered vector query LIMIT gotcha: When combining pgvector similarity search with RLS row filtering, the LIMIT clause is applied BEFORE RLS filtering. This means a LIMIT 10 query may return fewer than 10 results after RLS filters out rows belonging to other tenants. Mitigation: over-fetch by 2–3x (e.g., LIMIT 30) and trim client-side, or use a subquery that applies the tenant filter before the LIMIT. This affects the Wave 3 knowledge retrieval layer (AI-16).

AI Gateway Architecture

By replacing the self-hosted LiteLLM proxy with Vercel AI Gateway, the entire AI routing layer becomes a managed service with zero operational overhead. ~300 lines of TypeScript middleware replaces the LiteLLM container + config, freeing 1,024 MB RAM per VPS node.

Architecture responsibilities:

  1. Vercel AI Gateway handles: provider routing, automatic fallbacks, built-in prompt caching, observability dashboards, and model availability monitoring.
  2. AI SDK v6 streaming metadata — AI SDK v6 natively returns token counts on streaming responses via response metadata. No gateway-level configuration needed for per-tenant billing.
  3. Per-call timeout — Per-call timeout via AI SDK timeout option (default: 120s to match Sonnet timeout). No global gateway config required.
  4. AI SDK costTrackingMiddleware — Every LLM call produces a ModelCallCost record (model, tier, tokens, latency, USD). The middleware writes cost data directly to Supabase’s agent_executions table and updates Valkey per-tenant spending counters in real time.
  5. Abstraction layer — All AI calls go through an internal AIGateway interface. The Vercel AI Gateway adapter can be swapped for direct provider API calls with a single-line change in the factory function. Zero agent code modifications.
  6. Direct API fallback — If Vercel AI Gateway is unavailable, the AIGateway adapter can route directly to provider APIs by swapping the provider constructor. Per-tenant cost tracking continues via the AI SDK middleware layer (which is independent of the gateway).
  7. Model lifecycle management — AI SDK provider registry + Vercel AI Gateway model configuration must be stored in an external config file (not hardcoded in application code) that can be updated without a code deploy. Store model routing rules in a database table that the admin panel can update. Add a health check that tests each configured model with a minimal completion request daily — if a model returns 404 (deprecated by provider), alert on-call immediately. Model deprecation must not require a code deploy.

Spending Limit Enforcement:

  • Per-tenant spending limits enforced via AI SDK budgetEnforcementMiddleware + Valkey counters, checked before every LLM call.
  • Valkey counter is updated by the costTrackingMiddleware regardless of routing path (Vercel AI Gateway or direct provider).
  • Emergency global spending cap on shared API keys: set at 2x the sum of all active tenant monthly limits.
  • Per-provider daily spending caps (AI-28): Valkey counters track daily spend per provider (provider:anthropic:daily_spend:{YYYY-MM-DD}, provider:openai:daily_spend:{YYYY-MM-DD}). Default cap per provider: 150% of that provider’s expected daily share based on the model routing table (e.g., if Anthropic handles ~70% of spend at $X/day, cap at $1.5X). Updated by the same costTrackingMiddleware that updates per-tenant counters — one additional INCRBYFLOAT per call, negligible overhead. When a provider cap is hit: (a) the model escalation system (AI-26) skips that provider’s models entirely, (b) single-step calls route to the cheapest available alternative provider, (c) multi-step agents already in-flight on the capped provider complete their current execution but new executions are blocked, (d) Slack/email alert fires immediately to on-call. This prevents the scenario where Provider A goes down, all traffic escalates to Provider B via fallback routing, and Provider B costs spike unbounded. Caps reset at midnight UTC via Valkey key expiry (EXPIREAT set to next midnight). Admin panel displays per-provider daily spend alongside per-tenant spend. Cap values stored in system_config table (platform-scoped, not tenant-scoped) and editable via admin panel without code deploy.
  • If Valkey is also down (double failure), agent execution is suspended entirely — fail closed, not open.
  • Co-failure scenario: Valkey can fail under memory pressure on VPS. When Valkey is down, the direct API fallback path has no spending enforcement. Mitigation: The AIGateway MUST maintain an in-process spending cap using a Node.js Map with atomic per-tenant counters AND per-provider counters, seeded from the last known Valkey values at process startup. Cap at 110% of each tenant’s monthly limit and 150% of each provider’s daily limit. This in-process fallback activates only when Valkey is unreachable, providing a last-resort guard rail independent of any external service.

Shared state: Spending limits are enforced via Valkey counters (shared across both VPS nodes via managed Valkey) with Supabase as durable fallback. Per-tenant spending state is persisted to Supabase on each cost update for durability and cross-node consistency. If Valkey is unavailable, the middleware falls back to Supabase for spending limit reads (higher latency but functional). If both are unavailable, agent execution is suspended (fail closed).

Cache hit rate model: Cache hit rate modeled conservatively at 20–30% blended (Starter customers sharing standard templates achieve ~50–60% due to common system prompt prefixes; Enterprise customers with custom prompts achieve ~10–20%). System prompts are structured with invariant platform instructions first (cacheable prefix) followed by tenant-specific configuration (non-cached suffix) to maximize Anthropic’s cache breakpoint efficiency. Cache write cost ($3.75/1M tokens for Sonnet) is included in the cost model — caching breaks even after ~2 reuses of the same prefix. Implementation requirement: The prepareStep callback with addCacheControlToMessages pattern is REQUIRED infrastructure for multi-step cost savings. Without it, Sales Agent pays full input cost on every step instead of the 86% savings the cost model assumes. See KB file 06-prompt-caching-patterns.md for implementation spec.

Architecture Pattern: Monolith-First

Teams under 20 engineers ship significantly more features per sprint with monoliths vs microservices. Microservices require dramatically more operational overhead than monoliths. The plan uses a modular monolith: clear module boundaries in code (agents, integrations, billing, auth) with a shared database. Services are extracted only when proven scaling bottlenecks emerge AND engineers exist to maintain them.

TypeScript Configuration & Coding Standards (KB-Verified)

tsconfig Requirements (Non-Negotiable):

  • (TS-01) verbatimModuleSyntax: true supersedes esModuleInterop. Do NOT use both. verbatimModuleSyntax is the correct modern choice — it enforces explicit import type syntax and aligns with the TypeScript 7.0 migration path.
  • (TS-02) noUncheckedIndexedAccess: true and exactOptionalPropertyTypes: true are NOT included in strict: true. Must be added explicitly. Without noUncheckedIndexedAccess, arr[0] has type T instead of T | undefined — a silent source of runtime crashes on empty arrays/objects.
  • (TS-06) isolatedDeclarations should be enabled per-package only (when using oxc/esbuild for builds), NOT globally. Requires explicit return types on all exported functions — too strict for app code but correct for shared library packages.

TypeScript Coding Standards (Enforced via ESLint):

  • (TS-03) NO TypeScript enum. Use as const objects with typeof instead. Example: const Status = { Active: 'active', Completed: 'completed' } as const; type Status = typeof Status[keyof typeof Status];. Enums emit runtime JavaScript and are incompatible with --erasableSyntaxOnly (the TS 7.0 migration path). Enforce via no-restricted-syntax ESLint rule.
  • (TS-04) TypeScript project references required for monorepo. Without them, TypeScript type-checks the entire monorepo as one program (~40s full check vs 3–8s incremental per package). Each package (@kiptra/shared, @kiptra/backend, @kiptra/frontend) must have its own tsconfig.json with "composite": true and "references" pointing to dependencies.
  • (TS-05) Ban barrel exports (index.ts re-exporting everything). Use direct imports: import { UserSchema } from '@kiptra/shared/schemas/user' not from '@kiptra/shared'. Barrel files force TypeScript to load every module in the barrel on any single import, destroying tree-shaking and slowing IDE type-checking. Enforce via no-restricted-imports ESLint rule on barrel paths.

3c. Reference Boilerplates (Evaluated March 2026)

All repos below are from reputable sources (Vercel, Supabase community, Mastra/Gatsby team, or high-star production templates), use our exact stack, and deploy to Vercel. Organized by role in the architecture.

Primary: Chat UI + Agent Tool Calling

RepoStarsStack MatchRole in Our Architecture
thorchh/agent-starter~120Next.js 16 + AI SDK (AI SDK v6) + shadcn + Tailwind v4Primary fork. Multi-step tool calling (tool() + Zod), streaming, chain-of-thought, file uploads, message branching. Tools in src/lib/ai/tools/. Model switching built-in (trivial to plug Vercel AI Gateway routing). Running agent chat with tools in <10 min.
vercel/chatbot~19.9KNext.js 16 + AI SDK + shadcn + Tailwind v4UI reference. Official Vercel template. Cleanest streaming chat UI. Tool calling addable in ~15–30 min via cookbook. Fork → replace Neon with Supabase → add React Flow + Socket.io.

Auth + Multi-Tenant Foundation

RepoStarsStack MatchRole in Our Architecture
jabirdev/nextjs-better-auth~94Next.js 16 + better-auth + Drizzle + SupabaseAuth layer reference. Our exact auth choice (better-auth Organization plugin). Clone for RBAC/team-invites/multi-tenancy patterns, merge into primary fork.
supabase-community/vercel-ai-chatbot~800AI SDK + Supabase Auth + PostgresSupabase integration reference. Pre-wired Supabase chat persistence + auth. Use as bridge if prioritizing Supabase RLS setup before better-auth swap.

Advanced Agent Orchestration (Upgrade Path)

Repo / TemplateStarsStack MatchRole in Our Architecture
CedarCopilot/cedar-mastra-starter—Next.js 16 + Mastra + React FlowReact Flow + agent status reference. Built-in interactive mind maps + live streaming status + tool calling + workflows. Closest match to our architecture visualization + live agent status needs. One-click Vercel deploy.
mastra-ai/template-deep-research
npx create-mastra@latest --template deep-search		22K (mono)Mastra + AI SDKComplex orchestration reference. Multi-agent research with self-evaluation, web tools, human-in-the-loop. Reference for when agent graphs/MCP tool servers become needed.
koji/mastra-app-template—Next.js + Mastra + Assistant UIAlternative UI pattern. Assistant UI + Mastra agents + web search tool. Reference for Assistant-style interface if needed.

Workflow + Background Jobs

RepoStarsStack MatchRole in Our Architecture
inngest/vercel-ai-o1-preview-crm-agent—AI SDK + Inngest + Next.jsDurable workflow reference. Background steps, retries, step orchestration. Study alongside our NATS JetStream + BullMQ patterns for cron and inter-agent events.

Visualization

RepoStarsStack MatchRole in Our Architecture
xyflow/react-flow—React + Next.jsMind map foundation. Official React Flow repo. Examples folder has Next.js + shadcn integrations. Combine with streaming for real-time agent node status.

Recommended Build Path

Step 1: Fork thorchh/agent-starter → instant chat UI + AI SDK streaming + tool calling (<10 min). thorchh/agent-starter serves as the UI/chat reference. Agent execution patterns (tool definitions, Zod schemas) are ported to the Hono backend; the boilerplate is not used as the backend runtime.

Step 2: Pull in jabirdev/nextjs-better-auth patterns for multi-tenant RBAC + Supabase RLS.

Step 3: Add xyflow/react-flow for the architecture mind map + live agent status (reference CedarCopilot/cedar-mastra-starter for integration patterns).

Step 4: Wire up NATS JetStream + BullMQ for event bus + job queue (reference Inngest patterns for durable workflows).

Step 5: Layer Mastra graph patterns (deep-search template) only when agent orchestration complexity demands it.

Note: The canonical domain model (Project, Estimate, EstimateLine, Task, Contact, Invoice, Payment, Document) must be defined as TypeScript interfaces in src/domain/ before writing any agent or adapter code. This is the prerequisite for the Anti-Corruption Layer’s “one adapter file per integration” claim.

Change Order Extensibility

Change orders are a critical construction workflow — they affect 80%+ of remodeling projects. Scope changes mid-project impact estimates, schedules, budgets, and client relationships.

Phase 1 (Waves 1–5): (Note: ‘Phase’ refers to customer-scale bands per Section 5; ‘Wave’ refers to build stages per Section 9.) Change order tracking is manual — logged in JobTread or the contractor’s existing tool. The canonical domain model should include a ChangeOrder interface stub (linked to Project and Estimate) to ensure the data model is extensible.

Phase 2 add-on (post-launch): Change Order Agent — scope change detection (from project update events), cost impact analysis (leveraging the Sales Agent’s pricing data), and client approval workflow (draft change order → human review → client-facing document). The Bookkeeping Agent and Sales Agent should be designed with change order extensibility in mind — specifically, the Sales Agent’s pricing lookup tools and the Bookkeeping Agent’s budget tracking should accept change-order-scoped queries.

Lightweight-first principle: thorchh/agent-starter gives 100% of the Vercel AI SDK tool-calling surface with zero framework overhead. Mastra templates are reference material for selective adoption — not the base. Every boilerplate listed deploys to Vercel and integrates with our Supabase + better-auth + Vercel AI Gateway stack with <1 hour of configuration.

4. AI Gateway & Cost Model

Architecture Decision: Vercel AI Gateway over LiteLLM and OpenRouter

The AI gateway uses Vercel AI Gateway (managed, zero markup, BYOK). OpenRouter charges a 5.5% fee on credit purchases. LiteLLM is self-hosted with 800+ open GitHub issues, memory leaks requiring nightly restarts, and a DB bottleneck at 1M+ logs. Vercel AI Gateway eliminates both: zero markup (same as LiteLLM), zero ops overhead (unlike LiteLLM), automatic Anthropic prompt caching, and native AI SDK v6 integration. At 100 customers, this saves ~$193/mo vs OpenRouter (5.5% of ~$3,500 AI spend) while also freeing 1,024 MB RAM per VPS node vs LiteLLM.

Per-Request Model Routing Strategy

Task ComplexityModelInput $/1MOutput $/1MUse Case
Simple (classify, extract)GPT-4.1 Nano$0.10$0.40Lead scoring, status checks (pricing verified March 2026)
Medium (summarize, draft)Claude Haiku 4.5$1.00$5.00Email drafts, daily summaries
Complex (reasoning, analysis)Claude Sonnet 4.6$3.00$15.00Estimates, financial analysis, CEO briefings
Frontier (deep analysis, complex reasoning)Claude Sonnet 4.6$3.00$15.00Multi-step financial analysis, cross-agent orchestration, complex reasoning chains

Note: Complex and Frontier route to the same model (Sonnet). The distinction is functional (Estimates vs CEO briefings), not cost-based. Frontier tasks are additionally capped at 1x/day per tenant to control frequency.

Note: Full 4-tier routing specification with per-agent cost breakdown in Appendix B.

Note: Opus ($25/1M output) is reserved exclusively for Frontier-tier CEO strategic briefings (1x/day max) — all other production tasks route to Sonnet 4.6 or lower. If Sonnet quality proves sufficient for strategic briefings during Wave 4 testing, the Frontier tier collapses into Complex and Opus is eliminated entirely.

Note: Model pricing verified March 2026. Re-validate against Vercel AI Gateway supported models and provider pricing pages before launch and at each quarterly planning cycle.

Cost at Scale (100 customers)

MetricUnoptimizedWith Routing + Caching
Monthly AI cost$5,400$3,500
Per customer/mo$54.00$35.00
Annual$64,800$42,000

Calculated using 20–30% blended cache hit rate (Starter: ~50–60%, Enterprise: ~10–20%). See Appendix B for full derivation.

Pricing Strategy (SaaS Revenue)

TierSetup FeeMonthlyIncludesAI Margin
Starter$5,000$275/mo3 operational agents + Support Agent, 1K calls/day~85%
Professional$10,000$500/mo6 operational agents + Support Agent, 5K calls/day~80%
Enterprise$20,000$750/mo6 operational agents + Support Agent, unlimited, priority. Enterprise tier capped at $200/mo AI spend per tenant; usage above cap billed at cost.~75%
Enterprise Unit Economics Note

At $750/mo with a dedicated success manager commitment, Enterprise is a cost center unless each CSM carries 15–20+ Enterprise accounts. At 10% Enterprise mix (Year 3 = ~19 Enterprise customers), one CSM at $55–70K/year is viable ($3.6K/year per Enterprise customer vs $9K/year revenue per customer). The dedicated CSM commitment should be scoped as “priority support queue + quarterly business review”, not a full-time assignment per account.

At 100 customers (60/30/10 blended mix): $39,000/mo recurring revenue — $3,500 AI cost — $979 infra (VPS $320 + Supabase $599 + Vercel $20 + Cloudflare $20 + SendGrid $20) — ~$624 payment processing (blended ACH/card, ~1.6%) = $33,897/mo contribution margin (86.9%). This uses the actual infrastructure costs from the scaling table and the blended tier mix used throughout the P&L.

Payment Method Strategy

Both ACH/bank transfer and credit/debit cards are accepted for all payments. ACH is presented as the default/recommended option. A 2.9% + $0.30 card processing surcharge is applied to card payments, displayed transparently at checkout. This is legal in 48 US states (restricted only in Connecticut and Massachusetts — verify customer’s state before applying surcharge).

Why ACH is recommended (shown to customer):

  • No processing fee (vs 2.9% surcharge on cards)
  • Lower fraud/dispute risk
  • Standard for B2B construction payments

Checkout display:

Text
Select Payment Method:
  ● Bank Account (ACH)  — $5,275.00  [RECOMMENDED]
  ○ Credit/Debit Card   — $5,427.98  (includes 2.9% processing fee)

Checkout Flow:

  1. Customer selects plan tier (Starter/Professional/Enterprise)
  2. Checkout shows: Setup fee ($5K–$20K) + First month ($275–$750) = Total due today
  3. Payment method: Customer chooses ACH (recommended, no surcharge) or credit/debit card (2.9% + $0.30 surcharge applied). ACH is pre-selected as default.
  4. If card is selected, surcharge line item appears before confirmation
  5. Monthly subscription renewals: customer’s choice of ACH or card. Same surcharge applies to card renewals. Customer can switch payment method at any time in billing settings.

Stripe Configuration:

TypeScript
// Checkout session — ACH preferred, cards accepted
const session = await stripe.checkout.sessions.create({
  mode: 'subscription',
  payment_method_types: ['us_bank_account', 'card'], // ACH preferred, cards accepted
  // Card surcharge applied via Stripe's automatic_tax or line item adjustment
  line_items: [
    { price: setupFeePrice, quantity: 1 },      // One-time setup fee
    { price: monthlySubscriptionPrice },          // Recurring monthly
  ],
  payment_method_options: {
    us_bank_account: {
      financial_connections: { permissions: ['payment_method'] },
      verification_method: 'instant', // Plaid instant verification
    },
  },
});

ACH Timeline:

  • Instant verification via Plaid: customer connects bank account in ~30 seconds
  • Fallback: micro-deposit verification (2 business days)
  • ACH settlement: 4–5 business days for initial payment
  • Subsequent ACH debits: 4–5 business days each

Cost Model / P&L Note: Blended processing rate assumes 70% ACH / 30% card (based on construction industry payment patterns). At 70/30 split: blended rate ~1.5–1.8% of revenue. If card adoption is higher, processing costs increase but are offset by the surcharge passed to customers. The surcharge means card processing fees are revenue-neutral — customers pay the fee, not Kiptra. P&L processing costs remain based on ACH rates only (the portion Kiptra absorbs).

Edge Cases: Customer disputes card surcharge — Surcharge is clearly disclosed before payment confirmation. Stripe receipt includes surcharge as a separate line item. Refunds include the surcharge amount.

Backup Processor: Helcim maintained as backup. If Stripe account is ever restricted, Helcim processes payments while Stripe issue is resolved.

Daily call caps: The tier-level daily cap (1K for Starter, 5K for Professional) is the binding constraint. Per-agent limits are configured per tier to respect the tier cap.

Tier limit configuration: All tier-level limits (seat counts, daily agent call caps, integration count, feature flags per tier) must live in a single configuration table in the database (plan_tiers), not in application code. The Stripe subscription.metadata carries the tier name, and the plan_tiers table maps tier names to their limit values. Tier limit changes (e.g., increasing Professional daily calls from 20 to 30) require only a database update, not a code deploy. The Hono rate-limiting middleware reads limits from a Valkey-cached copy of plan_tiers, refreshed every 5 minutes.

Cost Controls (Non-Negotiable)

  1. Per-tenant spending limit enforced via Valkey counters (checked in AI SDK budgetEnforcementMiddleware before every LLM call). When a tenant reaches 80%, an in-app alert and email notification are triggered. At 100%, Starter/Professional agents are paused with a dashboard notification; Enterprise tier switches to metered Stripe billing (usage above cap billed at cost) rather than hard cutoff. All spending events are logged in the audit trail.
  2. Model cascade: route by task complexity, not one-size-fits-all
  3. Prompt caching: system prompts cached (90% per-token discount when a cache hit occurs. Blended cache hit rate is 20–30% across all tiers (see Appendix B) — effective cost reduction is ~18–27%, not 90%)
  4. Token budgets: max_tokens per agent invocation (prevent runaway)
  5. Loop detection: Loop prevention via per-agent stopWhen: stepCountIs(N) limits (see Section 12) and per-execution token budget. Per-agent step budgets (includes +2 structured output buffer for AI SDK v6): Leads Agent: stopWhen: stepCountIs(7), CEO Agent: stopWhen: stepCountIs(12), Sales Agent: stopWhen: stepCountIs(17). The stopWhen approach is preferred over consecutive-call heuristics, which produce false positives for agents that legitimately call the same tool multiple times (e.g., Sales Agent pricing lookups).
  6. Daily usage sync: track per-tenant spend in Supabase for billing dashboard
  7. Pre-flight spending check: Before queuing an agent execution in BullMQ, the API endpoint MUST check the tenant’s current month spend against their plan limit. If spend >= 100%, return HTTP 429 with a user-facing message (‘Monthly AI budget reached — upgrade your plan or wait for the next billing cycle’) and do NOT queue the job. This prevents the scenario where a customer clicks ‘Run Estimate’, waits 2 minutes for the agent to execute, and then gets a mid-execution failure when the spending limit is enforced mid-execution at step 12 of 17. The 80% spending alert should be driven by the AI SDK costTrackingMiddleware (real-time Valkey counter), not the daily sync job. Add a BILLING_PAUSED state to the agent execution state machine to distinguish spending-limit halts from actual failures.

5. Multi-Tenancy Architecture

Scale Target

  • Phase 1: 10-50 customers (Slate + early adopters)
  • Phase 2: 50-500 customers (construction vertical)
  • Phase 3: 1,000-10,000+ customers (multi-vertical expansion)
  • Tenant types: Individual users (solopreneurs) AND team accounts (companies with employees/roles)
  • Supabase compute planning: Phase 1 (10–50 tenants) requires Small compute add-on (90 direct / 400 pooler connections). Phase 2 (50–500) requires Medium or Large compute. Connection budget must account for per-tenant semaphore (3 connections/tenant/node) plus background workers.

Database Schema (Supabase + RLS)

SQL
-- Core tables (all have tenant_id + RLS)
tenants          -- id (uuid PK), name, industry_vertical (kitchen_bath|hvac|general_construction|landscaping), tenant_type (contractor|dealer|sales_rep|manufacturer), company_size, plan (starter|professional|enterprise), billing_status (active|past_due|canceling|canceled), stripe_customer_id, stripe_subscription_id, onboarding_step, onboarding_completed (boolean), feature_flags (JSONB), timezone, created_at, updated_at
tenant_members   -- users per company (RBAC: owner, admin, manager, designer, bookkeeper, viewer)
agents           -- active agents per tenant (instantiated from templates)
agent_executions -- run history, input/output, cost tracking. status column: QUEUED | RUNNING | COMPLETED | FAILED | TIMED_OUT | AWAITING_APPROVAL | REJECTED | CANCELLED | DEAD_LETTER (CHECK constraint, indexed on tenant_id + status)
event_bus_log    -- full audit trail
integrations     -- OAuth tokens for external tools (encrypted)
escalations      -- human-in-the-loop approval queue
drafts           -- agent-generated drafts pending approval
conversation_turns    -- AI conversation history per tenant
tenant_usage_daily    -- daily usage aggregation per tenant
webhook_events        -- webhook deduplication per tenant
invitations           -- team invitations per tenant (managed by better-auth Organization plugin)
notification_preferences -- user notification settings (tenant-scoped via user)
support_conversations     -- Support Agent chat history, messages, and feedback per tenant user

-- Table classification: Tenant-scoped tables (RLS enforced): all tables listed above.
-- Platform-scoped tables (service-role access, no tenant RLS):
--   agent_templates      -- industry-specific agent configs (shared across tenants by design)
--   agent_prompt_versions -- prompt history (platform-scoped, shared across tenants)
--   tenant_keys           -- envelope encryption DEKs (service-role only, never exposed via RLS)
--   prompt_versions       -- prompt version tracking
--   system_config         -- platform-wide configuration
CI Migration Check (Mandatory)

Every new table migration MUST include either an RLS policy or an explicit platform-scoped whitelist entry. CI lint rule enforces this — migrations without RLS classification are rejected.

RLS Performance Optimization (Mandatory)

ALL RLS policies MUST wrap current_setting() in a subselect: tenant_id = (SELECT current_setting('app.tenant_id', true))::uuid. This triggers PostgreSQL’s InitPlan optimization, evaluating the setting once per query instead of per-row. Without the (SELECT ...) wrapper, 10–100x performance degradation occurs on tables with >10K rows.

RLS Indexing Requirement (Mandatory)

CI migration lint: every tenant-scoped table MUST have CREATE INDEX on tenant_id. Composite indexes for frequently queried combinations (e.g., (tenant_id, status), (tenant_id, created_at)).

RLS WITH CHECK Clause (Mandatory)

All tenant-scoped RLS policies include both USING (...) for reads AND WITH CHECK (...) for inserts/updates. This prevents a tenant from inserting rows with a different tenant_id.

FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY (Mandatory)

Every tenant-scoped table MUST include ALTER TABLE ... FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY in the migration. Without FORCE, table owners bypass RLS policies entirely.

Agent Template System (Key to Scaling Beyond Slate)

  • Templates are keyed on two axes: industry_vertical (kitchen_bath, hvac, etc.) × tenant_type (contractor, dealer, sales_rep, manufacturer). See Section 6a.
  • Each combination has a template (set of pre-configured agents with persona-tuned prompts)
  • Customers can enable/disable agents from their template
  • Templates define: agent roles, trigger conditions, escalation rules, integration requirements, dashboard layout, onboarding content
  • Slate's 7-agent config = “Kitchen & Bath × Contractor” template
  • Future templates: Kitchen & Bath × Dealer, Kitchen & Bath × Manufacturer, HVAC × Contractor, etc.

Team Hierarchy & Role-Based Access

Every tenant is a team (company). Within each team:

RoleAccessExample (Slate)
Owner/CEOFull visibility: all agents, all employee activity, all financials, CEO AgentAdam
AdminManage integrations, view all agents, manage team membersOffice Manager
Manager/PMInteract with assigned agents (Project Management Agent, Sales Agent), approve drafts/escalationsProject Manager
DesignerDesign Agent only, submit selections, view own project specsDesigner
BookkeeperBookkeeping Agent only, billing memos, AP/AR views, lien waiversBookkeeper
ViewerRead-only dashboards, no agent interactionSubcontractor portal (future)

Key behaviors:

  • CEO sees a unified dashboard across all agents + employee activity log
  • Employees see only agents relevant to their role (scoped by RBAC permissions)
  • Agent outputs are scoped: CEO Agent outputs → CEO only (spec requirement)
  • Employee actions are logged and visible to CEO (who approved what, when)
  • Onboarding flow: CEO signs up → invites employees → assigns roles → employees get scoped access

Tenant Isolation

  • Database: RLS policies use current_setting('app.tenant_id', true) (with missing_ok parameter) set via a set_tenant_context() PostgreSQL function called at the start of each transaction. This function reads the user’s activeOrganizationId from the better-auth session (injected into a custom JWT claim via a session hook). A custom accessToken.customClaim hook is required since better-auth’s Organization plugin stores this in the session table, not JWT claims by default. The extracted organization ID is then mapped to the internal tenant_id. When the setting is unset, current_setting returns NULL — the RLS predicate tenant_id = current_setting(...)::uuid evaluates to NULL (falsy), returning zero rows rather than raising an exception. Adversarial test required: verify that queries without set_tenant_context() return zero rows, not PostgreSQL exceptions. This approach is compatible with Supavisor (which replaced PgBouncer on the Supabase platform) transaction-mode pooling and avoids the auth.uid() session-variable limitation. Supavisor SET LOCAL requirement: set_tenant_context() MUST use SET LOCAL (transaction-scoped), NOT session-scoped SET. This prevents tenant context leaking across Supavisor connection reuse. All database queries must be wrapped in explicit transactions — no bare queries allowed. Connection state isolation relies on SET LOCAL (transaction-scoped) semantics. Verify Supavisor’s connection return behavior clears session state — Supavisor does not document a server_reset_query parameter. Per-tenant safeguards: statement_timeout set per-transaction alongside set_tenant_context() (30s default, 120s for Sales Agent). Application-layer semaphore limits concurrent database connections to 3 per tenant per node, preventing connection pool exhaustion from expensive queries.
  • API Keys: Per-tenant spending enforcement via Valkey counters + AI SDK middleware (all tenants share provider API keys; spending isolation is at the application layer)
  • Secrets: AES-256-GCM encryption for stored OAuth tokens
  • Event Bus: NATS subject namespacing (TENANT_{id}.agent.{name}.{event}) with per-tenant authorization rules (user/NKey credentials scoped to TENANT_{id}.> publish/subscribe permissions). Simplification: Since tenants never connect to NATS directly (only backend services publish/subscribe), a single internal NATS user for backend services is sufficient. Subject namespacing (TENANT_{id}.agent.{name}.{event}) is enforced at the application layer. Per-tenant NKey credentials are deferred to post-100-tenant scale if multi-service isolation requires it.

Canonical NATS subject format: TENANT_{tenantId}.{domain}.{name}.{event}. All publish/subscribe calls MUST use the buildSubject(tenantId, domain, name, event) helper. The buildSubject() helper validates that the tenantId parameter matches the current request context — preventing a compromised or buggy code path from publishing to another tenant’s subject namespace. CI lint rule rejects raw string subjects.

  • Job Queue: BullMQ uses shared priority queues (agent-jobs:high, agent-jobs:medium, agent-jobs:low) with tenant_id as job data, not queue name. Tenant isolation is enforced at the worker level: each job’s tenant_id is validated before processing, with per-tenant rate limiting within the queue processor. This avoids the O(N) Valkey overhead of N×7 individual queues at scale. Fair scheduling: Workers process at most 5 consecutive jobs from the same tenant before yielding to the next tenant in the queue, preventing high-volume Enterprise tenants from starving Starter tenants.
  • Real-time: Socket.io server-side room join validation checks authenticated tenant_id before allowing room subscription — clients cannot join another tenant’s rooms. Forced room eviction: When tenant_members is updated (user removed or role changed), publish an event that triggers the WebSocket server to forcibly remove that user’s socket from tenant rooms and disconnect the socket. This prevents removed users from receiving real-time updates on existing connections.
  • Cache: Redis 6+ ACLs with keyspace pattern permissions (~tenant:{id}:*) enforce isolation at the engine level. Each logical tenant group receives a Redis ACL user with restricted key patterns — not purely application-layer prefix enforcement. A CI lint rule prevents cache calls without the tenant prefix wrapper as an additional defense layer. A dedicated provisioning service (not the application tier) holds the Redis admin credential for ACL SETUSER. ACL lifecycle includes provisioning on tenant creation, credential rotation schedule, and revocation on tenant offboarding. Implementation note: DigitalOcean Managed Valkey (Redis-compatible) does NOT expose ACL configuration. Per-tenant Redis ACLs require self-hosted Redis/Valkey, which contradicts the managed Valkey mandate. Resolution: Use application-layer key prefix enforcement (tenant:{id}:*) with CI lint rules as the PRIMARY isolation mechanism. Redis ACLs are deferred to a future self-hosted Redis migration if tenant isolation requirements demand kernel-level enforcement. The CI lint rule scanning for unprefixed Valkey key access is the Wave 1 deliverable.
  • Agent Execution: Tenant context injected at request boundary, never trusted from client

API Rate Limiting (per tenant): Hono middleware enforces per-tenant rate limits via Valkey sliding window counters — 100 req/min/tenant for standard endpoints, 10 req/min/tenant for agent execution endpoints, 30 req/min/user for escalation approvals. Rate limit headers are included in API responses for integration back-off. This is distinct from the per-agent AI spending limits, which control cost, not request volume.

Co-design note (rate limit intersection): The inbound execution rate limit (10/min/tenant) × per-execution outbound call depth (up to stopWhen: stepCountIs(17)) can produce up to 170 outbound API calls/min/tenant. The per-tenant JobTread token bucket (60 req/min starting budget) must account for this multiplier. AI SDK v6 warning: If stopWhen is not explicitly set, v6 defaults to stepCountIs(20), changing this calculation to 200 outbound calls/min/tenant — exceeding the JobTread budget. Explicit stopWhen is mandatory. Workers check remaining token budget before making outbound calls and back-pressure the execution if budget is exhausted.

Webhook routing: Webhook endpoints use cryptographic URL tokens (/webhooks/:service/:webhookToken) rather than tenant IDs in the URL path. The webhookToken is a 32-byte random hex value mapped to tenant_id server-side at integration setup time. Signature validation is performed per-service before any tenant routing — see Section 7 for per-service validation mechanisms.

Webhook deduplication: A webhook_events table with a unique index on (service, external_event_id, tenant_id) deduplicates at-least-once delivery from all webhook providers. On receipt: INSERT with ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING — if no row inserted, the event is a duplicate and returns 200 without publishing to NATS. Retention: 7-day TTL on webhook_events rows (matching DLQ retention). A pg_cron job purges rows older than 7 days. Partial index WHERE created_at > NOW() - INTERVAL '7 days' keeps deduplication lookups fast.

Tenant Offboarding

On churn, a TenantOffboardingService executes: (1) mark tenant as churned, (2) export all tenant data to a signed DigitalOcean Spaces URL delivered to the tenant (Export encryption: The tenant data archive is encrypted with AES-256 using a one-time key. The download link (signed DigitalOcean Spaces URL, 24-hour expiration) and the decryption key are delivered via separate channels (download link via in-app notification, decryption key via email to the account owner)), (3) queue a 90-day deletion job per the data retention policy, (4) immediately revoke OAuth tokens and clear Valkey spending counters, (5) purge Valkey keys matching tenant:{id}:*, (6) expire NATS credentials, (7) verify Valkey key prefix cleanup (all tenant:{id}:* keys deleted), (8) Queue backup purge — pg_dump backups (every 6 hours) containing tenant data are retained for the standard backup window (30 days) then automatically aged out; document this retention as a CCPA-permitted exception under the ‘unreasonable effort’ clause, (9) Anonymize tenant PII in Grafana Loki logs at ingest time: a Promtail pipeline stage strips PII fields (names, emails, phone numbers) from log entries before they reach Loki. Tenant IDs are replaced with opaque hashes in logs. This eliminates the need for post-hoc deletion, which Loki does not reliably support, (10) The 90-day deletion queue is processed by a BullMQ cron job that performs hard DELETE operations on all tenant rows in Supabase.
