Supabase Pro (pgvector + tsvector)$25
Extraction LLM (classifier, GPT-4.1-nano)~$6
Extraction LLM (extractor, GPT-4.1-mini)~$72
Embedding generation (text-embedding-3-small)~$1
Retrieval compute~$4
Valkey cache (hot context)$15
Total~$123/mo

Scaling: ~$440–480/mo at 500 tenants; ~$1,500–1,600/mo at 1,000 tenants (includes Supabase Team plan upgrade at $599/mo triggered around 500+ tenants).

Compared to the naive approach (GPT-4o on every message): $123/mo vs. $9,000/mo — a 98.7% cost reduction with validated accuracy.

Implementation Timeline

WeekDeliverable
1Database schema, RLS policies, basic CRUD API
2Extraction pipeline (classifier + extractor + BullMQ queue), embedding generation
3Retrieval + validation layer, Valkey caching, context injection into agents
4Failure handling (circuit breakers, stateless fallback), monitoring, testing
5Consolidation jobs, memory audit, confidence decay, admin dashboard

7e. Anti-Hallucination Architecture

Non-Negotiable Safety Architecture

This section summarizes the corrected anti-hallucination system (v2). The v1 architecture had eight critical flaws: LLM-based arithmetic, circular RAGAS evaluation, arbitrary confidence thresholds, missing citation verification, and no financial guardrails. V2 addresses every finding. Complete specification: kiptra-anti-hallucination-v2.md

Core Principle: “Never hallucinate” is a false promise

No AI system achieves zero hallucination. Any vendor or architecture document claiming otherwise is dishonest. Kiptra is transparent about what AI can and cannot guarantee, and designs safety around this reality rather than pretending it away.

Honest Accuracy Targets (Per-Agent)

AgentOutput TypeTargetMethod
Sales AgentArithmetic<0.1% errorDeterministic pipeline, zero LLM math
Sales AgentScope interpretation85–90% accuracyLLM + scope confidence scoring + human review gate
Sales AgentNarratives<3%RAG + factored verification
Support AgentKB answers<5%RAG + citation enforcement + NLI verification
Support AgentEscalation0%Deterministic routing rules
Scheduling AgentDate/time math<0.1%Deterministic calendar logic (date-fns/Temporal)
CRM AgentData entry<1%Structured extraction + Zod validation
CRM AgentSummaries<5%RAG + source attribution

Sales Agent: Deterministic Estimation Pipeline

The Iron Rule: the LLM NEVER performs arithmetic, lookups, or financial calculations. Every number in an estimate flows through deterministic TypeScript code using Decimal.js for financial precision. The LLM’s role is strictly limited to:

  • Interpreting natural language scope into structured parameters (via Zod schema, temperature 0.0)
  • Matching ambiguous material descriptions to catalog SKUs (top-3 candidates with confidence)
  • Generating narrative descriptions grounded in calculated line items (read-only access to numbers)

Pipeline flow:

  1. Scope Interpreter (LLM) → parses user input into structured JSON with scopeConfidence score (0.0–1.0)
  2. Scope Confidence Gate → routes based on confidence (see below)
  3. Material Matcher (LLM) → matches to catalog SKUs via embeddings, flags if confidence < 0.80
  4. Deterministic Calculator (TypeScript/Decimal.js) → ALL math: quantity × unitPrice, labor, overhead, markup, tax, total
  5. Validation Layer (TypeScript) → historical comparable check, unit price bounds, tax rate validation, markup policy
  6. Narrative Generator (LLM) → describes work items, inclusions, exclusions. Cannot change any numbers.
  7. Citation Verification (DeBERTa NLI) → decomposes narrative into atomic claims, verifies each against source data

Scope Confidence Scoring & Routing Gates

The Scope Interpreter outputs a confidence score driven by: explicit quantities provided (+0.15–0.25), prior project data (+0.10–0.15), design files attached (+0.10–0.20), ambiguous terms present (-0.05–0.15 each), missing critical dimensions (-0.15–0.25).

Scope ConfidenceAction
< 0.80Route to human review. Do NOT generate estimate. “We need a bit more detail about your project.”
0.80–0.89Generate estimate, flag as “AI Estimate — Review Recommended”
≥ 0.90Generate estimate, deliver normally

Mandatory Human Review Gates (Non-Negotiable)

Certain estimates ALWAYS require human review before delivery. No exceptions, no overrides.

TriggerRationale
Estimate total > $25,000High financial exposure — errors are costly
First estimate per project type per tenantNo comparable baseline exists for this tenant/type combination
Scope confidence < 0.80AI is uncertain about what was requested
Tenant has < 10 total estimatesBootstrap period — insufficient data for validation

User experience during review: “Your estimate is being finalized by our team. Expected delivery: [SLA].” SLA: 2 hours during business hours, next business day otherwise.

Bootstrap Problem Mitigation

New tenants have zero historical comparables, disabling the strongest safety net. Explicit mitigation:

Tenant Estimate CountPolicy
0–9 estimatesALL estimates require human review before delivery
10–49 estimatesHigh-value (>$10K) still require review; building comparable baseline
50+ estimatesFull automation; comparable-based validation activates

New tenants are seeded with anonymized industry comparables (same region, same project type), clearly marked as “industry average.” Phased out as tenant-specific data accumulates.

Tiered Comparable Deviation Thresholds

Tighter bounds for higher-value estimates because the dollar impact of percentage errors scales linearly:

Estimate ValueMax Deviation from Comparables
< $10K30%
$10K–$50K20%
> $50K15%

DeBERTa NLI Citation Verification

Every generated response is decomposed into atomic claims, and each claim is verified independently using a dedicated NLI model — NOT the same LLM that generated the response (avoids circular evaluation).

  1. Claim Decomposer (Haiku 3.5) → breaks response into atomic factual claims, tags type (FACTUAL, NUMERICAL, PROCEDURAL, OPINION)
  2. NLI Verifier (cross-encoder/nli-deberta-v3-large) → classifies each claim as ENTAILED, CONTRADICTED, or NEUTRAL against cited source
  3. Numerical Verifier → exact string match of all numbers against source text
  4. Response Adjudicator → all ENTAILED = deliver; any CONTRADICTED = remove/correct; any NEUTRAL = flag as unverified

Total verification overhead: ~415–465ms (claim decomposition ~300ms, NLI batch ~100–150ms, numerical extraction ~5ms, adjudication ~10ms). Acceptable for async responses; for real-time chat, verification runs async with “verifying...” indicator.

Financial Guardrails

  • Hard limits (block delivery): total within company min/max bounds, markup within policy (10–60%), tax rate within legal max, total matches line item sum exactly, no negative line items
  • Statistical bounds (flag for review): comparable deviation (tiered), unit price within ±20% of catalog mean, labor rate within ±25%
  • External price validation: compare matched prices against manufacturer published price lists; flag >5% deviation to catch stale catalog data

Support Agent Safety

  • RAG grounding: all factual answers grounded in retrieved KB content via hybrid retrieval (pgvector + BM25 + RRF) with Cohere Rerank 3.5
  • Relevance gate: top chunk < 0.35 = OUT_OF_DOMAIN (deterministic refusal, no LLM); 0.35–0.55 = LOW_CONFIDENCE; > 0.55 = PROCEED
  • Citation enforcement: every factual claim must cite a KB source, verified by DeBERTa NLI
  • Financial blocklist: regex patterns block pricing commitments, discounts, guarantees. Defense-in-depth: NLI catches creative phrasings regex misses; system prompt prohibits quoting prices; monthly adversarial test suite validates coverage
  • Out-of-domain: deterministic refusal responses (no LLM involved) + human escalation

Post-Delivery Feedback Loop

When actual project costs are entered via JobTread integration, line-by-line comparison identifies which scope items were over/under estimated. Deviations >15% feed back into the golden test set and adjust comparable baselines. Systematic errors (e.g., “consistently under-counting wall cabinets”) trigger alerts. Tenants providing <30% feedback data get tightened scope confidence thresholds (+0.05).

17 Cataloged Failure Modes

The full specification catalogs 17 failure modes with detection and mitigation for each, including: invalid LLM JSON (F1), wrong SKU match (F2), stale catalog prices (F3), irrelevant RAG context (F4), LLM ignoring grounding prompt (F5), wildly off estimate total (F6), Support Agent pricing commitment (F7), LLM API failure (F8), embedding drift (F9), calibration drift (F10), incorrect tax rate (F11), adversarial prompt injection (F12), systematic scope errors (F13), zero comparables (F14), manufacturer price staleness (F15), low scope confidence (F16), and full database outage (F17).

Evaluation Framework (Non-Circular)

Four layers, each independent: (1) Deterministic checks — Zod validation, numerical consistency, citation existence, guardrails; (2) NLI-based verification — DeBERTa entailment per claim; (3) Statistical monitors — response consistency (10% sample), cross-model agreement (5% sample), RAGAS as signal not judge; (4) Human evaluation — 50 random responses/agent/week, monthly golden test set (200+ cases). RAGAS is one signal among many, never the sole evaluator.

Cost Model

OutputCostBreakdown
Estimate (complex)~$0.068–0.078Scope interpreter + material matcher + narrative + claim verification + NLI + reranker + embedding
Support response~$0.029RAG generation + claim verification + NLI + reranker + embedding
Help Bot response~$0.005Haiku-only RAG generation + reranker + embedding (no NLI, bounded scope). Est. $10–20/mo across all tenants.

Monthly at 100 tenants (~10K estimates, ~100K support responses): ~$4,700 LLM/infra + ~$1,000 human evaluation = ~$5,700/month.

Full Specification Reference

See kiptra-anti-hallucination-v2.md for complete pipeline diagrams, Zod schemas, TypeScript implementations, failure mode catalog, monitoring metrics with thresholds, calibration methodology, drift detection, cost projections at scale, and implementation roadmap (4 phases, 16 weeks).

8. Standalone Architecture Mind Map (Shareable Visual)

A completely standalone project — NOT part of the Kiptra AI webapp. This is a separate, independent visual built purely as a communication/sales tool for non-technical people (Adam, investors, potential customers, partners). It has its own repo and deployment. Think of it as a pitch deck in interactive form.

Hosting: Its own Vercel deployment (e.g., kiptra-architecture.vercel.app) or a static HTML file you can email/share directly.

Design Goals

  • Non-technical audience: No jargon. Visual metaphors over technical labels.
  • Beautiful: Polished design with gradients, shadows, animations. Not a wireframe.
  • Interactive: Hover for details, click to expand, zoom/pan, animated data flows.
  • Shareable: Public URL, works on mobile, exportable as image/PDF.

Node Types

  • Platform Core (center): “Kiptra AI” — the brain
  • Agent Nodes (7–9 per persona, 28 total agent types across 4 personas): Each with icon, name, one-line description
  • Integration Nodes: JobTread, QuickBooks, Google, CRM, Weather — with real logos
  • Data Flow Arrows: Animated dashed lines showing how data moves between agents
  • Human Touchpoints: Where humans review/approve (escalations, drafts)
  • Customer Layer: Show how 1 customer → 100 customers → 1000 customers scale visually

Layout

  • Hub-and-spoke: Platform core at center
  • Agents in inner ring (color-coded by domain)
  • Integrations in outer ring (with brand logos)
  • Animated particles flowing along edges to show data movement
  • Zoom levels: zoomed out = high-level overview, zoomed in = agent details

Interactivity

  • Hover any node: Tooltip with plain-English explanation
  • Click agent: Expand to show its responsibilities, triggers, outputs
  • Scale slider: Drag to show 1 customer → 100 → 1000 (nodes multiply visually)
  • Dark mode with glassmorphism aesthetic

Tech Stack

  • @xyflow/react v12 (NOT reactflow — renamed in v12)
  • framer-motion for node entrance animations, hover effects, transitions
  • lucide-react for agent/integration icons (1000+ tree-shakable SVG icons)
  • tailwindcss for styling custom nodes (glassmorphism: backdrop-blur-md bg-white/10 border-white/20)
  • html-to-image v1.11.11 (PINNED — newer versions have export bugs) for PNG/PDF export
  • React Flow colorMode="dark" for built-in dark mode
  • CSS <animateMotion> on SVG <circle> for particle effects along edges
  • Next.js OG image generation for link preview when sharing URL
  • Standalone deployment — own Vercel project, NOT a route in the main app
  • Can also export as single HTML file for offline sharing

Performance Notes

  • 6 agent nodes + ~10 integration nodes + edges = well under 50-node threshold (no optimization needed)
  • Memoize all custom nodes with React.memo() anyway (best practice)
  • SSR compatible — render static on server, hydrate animations on client
  • Mobile: fitView auto-adjusts, pinch-zoom native, increase handle sizes for touch

9. Build Order (Revised — 34–38 Weeks to Production (Realistic))

Timeline: 26–28 Weeks (Optimistic) / 34–38 Weeks (Realistic)

The original 26–28 week estimate assumes zero rework, no integration delays, and maximum parallelism. Based on industry benchmarks for solo/duo developer teams building distributed systems, a 1.5–2x calendar time overrun is standard. The wave structure below reflects the 26–28 week optimistic schedule; add 30–40% buffer for realistic planning.

The original 14-week plan prioritized speed over production-readiness. This revised plan adds CI/CD, testing, security hardening, and monitoring from Day 1 — and launches with 2–3 agents instead of 7 to ensure quality. Remaining agents are built post-launch. Every successful construction SaaS (Procore, Buildertrend) started by solving ONE problem well. Additional weeks allocated for Supabase RLS optimization (Wave 1), Stripe integration complexity (Wave 2), and risky third-party integrations in Wave 5 (JobTread Pave, QuickBooks OAuth).

Timeline Risk

The 26–28 week build order assumes two full-time developers with no context-switching. Historical data on projects of this complexity suggests 1.5–2x calendar time overrun is common. The wave structure is designed so each wave ships independently usable functionality — if timelines slip, Wave 3 (first demo) remains the hard gate for sales activity.

Wave 0: Architecture Mind Map (Complete)

  1. Standalone interactive mind map — own repo + own Vercel deployment
    • Deployed at kiptra-architecture.vercel.app
    • Beautiful visual for non-technical stakeholders (Adam, TJ, investors)
    • Shows agents, integrations, data flows, scale model, cost analysis

Wave 1: Foundation + DevOps (Week 1–5)

  1. Next.js 16 app scaffolding (shadcn/ui, Tailwind, better-auth with Organization plugin)
  2. Supabase schema + RLS policies (optimized: indexed tenant_id, initPlan pattern, adversarial tests). Include feature_flags JSONB DEFAULT '{}' column on tenants table for per-tenant agent activation control (referenced in Wave 3). Include industry_vertical (default: 'kitchen_bath') and tenant_type (default: 'contractor') columns with CHECK constraints on tenants table — added now to avoid data migration later even though only contractor persona is functional in Waves 1–4 (see Section 6a).
  3. Dual VPS setup (2x DigitalOcean droplets + Load Balancer, Docker Compose: Hono backend, NATS JetStream; Valkey is DigitalOcean Managed Valkey, external)
  4. Tenant + team member signup/auth flow with RBAC (owner, admin, manager, designer, bookkeeper, viewer — full 6-role set from Section 5)
  5. CI/CD pipeline: GitHub Actions — lint, type-check, test, build on every PR
  6. Monitoring: Sentry (error tracking, manual Hono middleware — see MON-06), UptimeRobot (uptime), Pino + Grafana Alloy + Loki (structured logging — Alloy replaces deprecated Promtail as the log shipping agent, see MON-02). Deploy Grafana Loki self-hosted on VPS (not Grafana Cloud free tier) to meet 90-day hot + 1-year cold log retention requirement from Day 1. Budget ~1.5GB RAM for the monitoring stack (MON-05).
  7. Security headers: HSTS, CSP, X-Frame-Options, CORS allowlist, rate limiting on auth endpoints
  8. Cloudflare: Pro ($20/mo) WAF + DDoS protection in front of dual VPS
  9. Environments: Local dev (Docker Compose), staging (Supabase branch + staging VPS), production (dual VPS + LB)
  10. DigitalOcean Spaces: Object storage bucket setup (avatars, backups, data exports) with IAM credentials and CORS configuration. Bucket policy: private by default, presigned URLs for client uploads. Required for Wave 4 data export and customer-facing file storage.
  11. Audit logging: All data access logged with tenant_id, user_id, timestamp, action. Audit identity model: User-triggered actions log actual user_id. System-triggered actions (cron, webhook) log a system identity plus trigger_source (e.g., system:cron:daily-briefing). Chain-triggered actions (agent A triggers agent B) log the originating agent ID and the context_ref from the execution chain.
  12. Canonical domain model (PRIORITY — build alongside or immediately after Supabase schema): Define TypeScript interfaces in src/domain/ (Project, Estimate, EstimateLine, Task, Contact, Invoice, Payment, Document). Prerequisite for all agent and adapter code. This must be completed before any Wave 2 work begins — agent tool definitions, integration adapters, and the anti-corruption layer all depend on these types.
  13. Data sourcing (parallel track — starts Wave 1): Begin sourcing Sales Agent pricing data immediately. Adam/Slate to compile material pricing from 3+ suppliers (target: 500 materials). This is a non-code workstream that runs in parallel with engineering and must be complete before Wave 3a Sales Agent go-live.
  14. API versioning: Implement from Day 1: URL prefix /api/v1/ for all REST endpoints. Webhook payloads include a version field from first delivery. Near-zero effort at build time but prevents breaking-change management issues post-launch.
  15. API deprecation policy: When a v1 endpoint is superseded by v2, the v1 endpoint must: (1) Return a Deprecation response header with the sunset date. (2) Log a deprecated_endpoint_called metric with tenant_id (to identify which customers still use it). (3) Continue functioning for a minimum of 90 days after the deprecation notice. (4) After 90 days, return HTTP 410 Gone with a migration guide URL. During the rolling deploy window where VPS nodes run different code versions, route signatures must be backward-compatible — enforce via CI check that diffs the exported AppType across commits.
Wave 1 Scope Note (SCALE-18)

Wave 1 contains foundation, auth, DevOps, monitoring, and security across 10 items. Realistically 4–5 weeks for two developers. If compressed to 3 weeks, defer audit logging and Cloudflare WAF setup to early Wave 2.

Wave 2a: AI Gateway + Agent Executor (Week 6–7)

Wave 2 Density Warning (SCALE-04)

Wave 2 scope is the densest in the plan. Consider splitting into Wave 2a (AI gateway + agent executor framework, 2 weeks) and Wave 2b (Stripe + error patterns + observability, 2–3 weeks). Stripe webhook integration (idempotency, replay, event ordering, subscription state machine) is routinely underestimated.

  1. Vercel AI Gateway configuration + AI SDK middleware (budgetEnforcement, costTracking, logging) + model routing via provider registry + model escalation safety net (see AI-26 below) + per-provider daily spending caps (AI-28): Valkey counters track daily spend per provider to prevent provider outage cascading into unbounded cost on fallback providers. $0 additional cost — see Section 4 Spending Limit Enforcement for full spec.
  2. Agent executor framework (thorchh/agent-starter base + Vercel AI SDK (AI SDK v6) for tool calling, streaming, structured output) + AgentPluginFactory registration API (Section 6) + 13-event streaming protocol (AI-25, Section 7b)
  3. Agent Event Protocol (13-event system, pi-mono inspired): Design and implement the 13-event KiptraAgentEvent union type for real-time agent status streaming to dashboards. Events: started, thinking, tool_call, tool_result, streaming, completed, failed, paused, resumed, escalated, approval_requested, approval_received, cancelled. Defined in packages/shared/src/events/ before the first agent is built (Wave 3a) so all agents emit consistent events from day one.
  4. AgentPlugin Factory + Registration Pattern (pi-mono inspired): Standardize agent creation through a factory pattern with runtime registration. All 7 agents build on the AgentPluginFactory interface — getting this right before Wave 3a prevents rework. Factory receives AgentRegistrationAPI for declarative capability registration (metadata, tools, triggers, permissions, dashboard components). See Section 6 for full spec.
  5. Agent template system (Slate “Construction Remodeling” = first template)
  6. NATS event bus (pub/sub per tenant, subject namespacing) + BullMQ job queue. Initial concurrency bounds (validated via Wave 2 load testing): Per-node worker concurrency: 10 concurrent jobs per priority level. Per-tenant concurrent job limit: 5 simultaneous agent executions. Priority mapping: escalations = high, user-triggered = medium, scheduled/cron = low. Write coordination: With up to 5 concurrent agent executions per tenant, race conditions on shared state are possible. BullMQ does not deduplicate jobs by default. Mitigations: (1) Use deterministic jobId values scoped to ${tenantId}:${agentId}:${triggerType}:${windowId} where windowId is a time-bucket (floor to 5-minute intervals for scheduled jobs) to prevent duplicate queuing. (2) Document the consistency model: dashboard data is eventually consistent with a bounded staleness of the last agent execution cycle. Display last_updated timestamps on dashboard panels. (3) For user-triggered executions, use a Valkey SET NX lock to prevent double-submission of the same agent for the same tenant within a 30-second window.
Pi-Mono Inspired Patterns — Soft-Add in Wave 3a

The following patterns are informed by pi-mono OSS analysis and should be soft-added in Wave 3a when the first agents are built: (1) TypeBox tool schemas for runtime validation (AI-27) — TypeBox generates JSON Schema at compile time for tool parameters, ensuring LLM tool calls are validated at runtime without manual schema maintenance. (2) Model escalation safety net for cross-provider failover mid-task (AI-26) — lightweight executeWithEscalation() wrapper that retries at a higher tier on model failure, preventing agent execution failures from becoming user-visible outages.

Wave 2b: Billing + Error Patterns + Observability (Week 8–10)

  1. Stripe Checkout + webhooks (ACH-preferred with card option): Subscription creation with ACH and card payment methods (payment_method_types: ['us_bank_account', 'card']), Plaid instant bank verification, card surcharge logic (2.9% + $0.30, exempt in CT/MA), customer portal for payment method management. Stripe webhook signature verification + idempotency (event ID deduplication via webhook_events table). Helcim configured as backup processor.
  2. Error handling patterns: Exponential backoff, circuit breakers, dead letter queues, idempotency keys
  3. Socket.io: Real-time WebSocket layer — NATS → backend → Socket.io → client dashboards. Note (XREF-17): Socket.io attaches to the Hono backend built in Wave 1. The Wave 1 backend ships without WebSocket support; real-time capability is added in Wave 2.
  4. OpenTelemetry: Distributed tracing across agent execution flows
  5. Integration tests: RLS adversarial queries, API endpoint tests, webhook handler tests
  6. QuickBooks OAuth app submission (INT-18): Submit QuickBooks OAuth app for Intuit production review (1–3 week approval process). Development uses sandbox; production access requires advance submission. Start early to avoid blocking Wave 5.
  7. Microsoft 365 OAuth integration (INT-19): Microsoft Entra ID app registration, OAuth 2.0 + PKCE flow via @azure/msal-node, Microsoft Graph client setup (@microsoft/microsoft-graph-client). Implements MicrosoftEmailProvider (Mail.Send, Mail.Read) and MicrosoftCalendarProvider (Calendars.Read) behind the provider abstraction interfaces. Includes Microsoft Graph subscription management for webhook-based change notifications (mail + calendar), with BullMQ cron renewal every 2 days. Admin consent endpoint (/adminconsent) as fallback for organizations that disable user consent.
  8. Provider abstraction layer (INT-20): EmailProvider and CalendarProvider interfaces with Google and Microsoft implementations. Factory resolves the correct provider from the tenant’s integrations table at runtime. Agent code calls provider-agnostic methods; never Google or Microsoft APIs directly.
  9. CPP/MPN publisher verification enrollment (PRE-LAUNCH): Start Microsoft Cloud Partner Program publisher verification — 2–6 week lead time, free. Without this, Microsoft consent screen shows “unverified publisher” warning. Must complete before first customer onboarding.
  10. Baseline load testing (SCALE-10): Test concurrent LLM calls through Vercel AI Gateway at 10/50/100 simultaneous executions. Measure AI Gateway response latency P50/P95/P99, Valkey connection pool exhaustion. This data is needed to set correct BullMQ concurrency limits before Wave 3 agents are built.
  11. Tenant isolation build items (XREF-24): NATS per-tenant subject namespace setup, BullMQ shared priority queue configuration with tenant validation, Socket.io tenant room validation middleware, Valkey key prefix enforcement via withTenantPrefix() wrapper + CI lint rule (application-layer isolation; Valkey ACLs deferred to post-managed-Valkey phase)
  12. Envelope encryption service: Implement tenant_keys table, DEK provisioning on tenant creation, AES-256-GCM encrypt/decrypt service for OAuth tokens. Required before any OAuth token is stored (Wave 5 integrations).
  13. SendGrid Inbound Parse (EMAIL-01): MX record configuration, webhook endpoint for inbound email receipt, Haiku input classifier for content sanitization (strip signatures, detect spam/phishing). Prerequisite for Wave 3 Leads Agent email intake.
  14. XREF-24 expanded: (a) BullMQ worker implementation with tenant context injection via withTenantContext() wrapper and per-tenant rate limiting, (b) Valkey key prefix enforcement wrapper + CI lint rule for unprefixed key access, (c) Integration test: submit jobs from two tenants simultaneously, verify tenant B’s worker context never reads tenant A’s data.

Wave 3a: First 3 Agents + Core Infrastructure (Week 11–14)

  1. Leads Agent for Slate (CRM + email intake + lead scoring). First agent implemented using AgentPluginFactory pattern (Section 6) + TypeBox tool schemas (AI-27) + streaming event protocol (AI-25).
  2. Sales Agent (highest-value agent — directly saves customers money)
  3. Sales Agent knowledge base seeding (HARD GATE): The Sales Agent MUST NOT go live until the knowledge base is seeded with verified pricing data. Minimum threshold: 500 materials with prices sourced from 3+ suppliers, validated against Slate’s actual project data. Seeding is a Wave 3a prerequisite, not a parallel task — the Sales Agent cannot be demonstrated or shipped without it. Without verified pricing data, the Sales Agent hallucinates material prices, which is the single highest-risk failure mode for the product (contractors will immediately lose trust if prices are wrong).
  4. CEO Agent + CEO dashboard (Recharts) + morning briefing generation
  5. Escalation queue UI + human-in-the-loop approval workflow
  6. Agent execution logs + real-time status dashboard
  7. Feature flags: Per-tenant agent activation control (JSONB column on tenants table, set up in Wave 1). Graduate to Unleash or PostHog post-100 tenants when non-engineer flag management is needed.
  8. Knowledge retrieval layer (AI-16): Supabase pgvector extension for semantic search over project history and reference documents. Standard retrieve(query, tenant_id, knowledge_base_type) tool available to relevant agents. For structured data (price lists, material catalogs): PostgreSQL full-text search. Required for Sales Agent accuracy — without retrieval, the agent hallucinates material prices. RLS enforcement: The pgvector embeddings table MUST have tenant_id column with RLS enabled. Vector similarity searches MUST use the same connection pool with set_tenant_context() as all other queries. Defense-in-depth: add explicit WHERE tenant_id = current_setting('app.tenant_id', true)::uuid to all vector search queries. Adversarial test: verify cross-tenant embedding retrieval returns zero results.
  9. Embedding pipeline (AI-16b): OpenAI text-embedding-3-small ($0.02/1M tokens). Chunking: 512-token chunks with 50-token overlap. Embeddings generated on document write/update via BullMQ background job. Index: HNSW for <1M rows per tenant. Estimated cost: ~$2–5/month per tenant — included in AI cost model as a minor line item.
  10. Demonstrate 3 operational agents to Adam as proof of concept

Wave 3b: Support Agent + Dashboard + E2E Tests (Week 15–17)

  1. Support Agent: 7th agent for AI-first customer support (Section 7c). Includes:
    • Support KB content creation (HARD GATE): Minimum viable KB of 57–83 articles covering 5 categories: platform features (~20 articles), billing/subscription (~10 articles), integrations/OAuth (~15 articles), troubleshooting/error resolution (~18 articles), getting started/onboarding (~15 articles). Content sourced from: product documentation, internal FAQs, integration guides, and common onboarding questions. KB must be seeded before the Support Agent goes live — without grounding content, the agent hallucinates answers.
    • In-app chat widget: Floating chat widget (shadcn/ui Sheet component) with persistent conversation history, typing indicators, markdown rendering, and contextual quick-action chips.
    • Content authoring admin panel: Markdown editor with auto-embedding on publish, diff-based re-embedding on updates, and article versioning for Owner/Admin roles.
    • Support analytics dashboard: CSAT, resolution rate, containment rate, CES, cost per resolution, and topic clustering for content gap detection.
  2. Agent quality metrics layer (AI-11): Track human approval rate per agent (% of drafts approved vs rejected vs modified), escalation rate per agent, and weekly quality review (sample 5 random outputs per agent for manual evaluation). Modified drafts store the diff to identify systematic agent errors.
  3. E2E tests: Critical flows — login, trigger agent, approve escalation, view dashboard
  4. Shadow mode (Week 16–17, overlaps with Wave 4 early onboarding): Before going live, the Support Agent runs in shadow mode using real customer queries — it generates responses for every incoming query but humans review and approve/reject before the response is sent to the customer. Shadow mode data is used to: calibrate confidence thresholds, identify content gaps, validate safety rails, and establish baseline metrics. The agent goes live only after achieving ≥ 80% human approval rate on shadow responses.
  5. RAGAS evaluation pipeline: Automated evaluation of RAG quality using RAGAS framework, run during shadow mode. Minimum thresholds before go-live: faithfulness ≥ 0.90 (responses grounded in retrieved context), answer relevancy ≥ 0.85 (responses address the actual question), context precision ≥ 0.80 (retrieved chunks are relevant to the query). Evaluation runs against a curated test set of 100+ question-answer pairs covering all 5 KB categories.
  6. Help Bot UI + Kiptra product KB (40–60 articles): In-app help panel reusing Support Agent RAG infrastructure with a separate Kiptra-product-specific knowledge base. Haiku-only, ~$0.005/response, estimated $10–20/mo across all tenants. See Section 13m.
  7. Product tour infrastructure: Driver.js integration, tour_progress table + RLS policies, Platform Orientation tour (5 steps, role-based content), onboarding checklist component with auto-complete triggers. See Section 13l.
Parallel Workstream Note

KB article authoring can begin as a parallel non-code workstream during Wave 2. Content authors (founders, domain experts) can draft articles in markdown while Wave 2 infrastructure is being built. This de-risks the Wave 3b Support Agent hard gate on KB content availability.

Content Authoring Workstream (Parallel — Starts Wave 1)

Content authoring is a non-code parallel workstream that must begin during Wave 1 and run continuously. Without this content, multiple hard gates are blocked.

  • Support KB articles: 57–83 articles × 2–4 hrs each = 140–280 hrs of content creation. Covers platform features (~20), billing/subscription (~10), integrations/OAuth (~15), troubleshooting (~18), and onboarding (~15). Hard gate for Wave 3b Support Agent go-live.
  • Sales Agent pricing data: 500 materials with prices sourced from 3+ suppliers, validated against Slate’s actual project data. Owner: Adam/Slate. Hard gate for Wave 3a Sales Agent go-live.
  • RAGAS test set: 100+ question-answer pairs covering all 5 KB categories. Required for Wave 3b RAGAS evaluation pipeline before Support Agent go-live.

Wave 4: Hardening + First Customer (Week 18–21)

  1. Pre-launch verification suite (XREF-13, SCALE-22): (1) Create 3 test tenants with different roles, (2) attempt all CRUD operations across tenant boundaries, (3) verify JWT claim extraction produces correct tenant_id, (4) verify spending limits block agent execution and produce alerts, (5) kill primary VPS during active agent execution and verify failover, (6) verify Socket.io reconnection re-validates room membership, (7) Stripe webhook lifecycle test, (8) AI Gateway fallback routing test, (9) prompt injection defense validation. Also: k6 at 10/50/100/500 concurrent users, agent execution under load.
  2. Security audit: OWASP checklist, RLS policy review, penetration testing basics
  3. Backup verification: Test Supabase restore + dual-VPS Docker volume recovery
  4. Customer onboarding flow (remote via Google Meet/Zoom, OAuth connection wizard). Includes industry vertical and business type (persona) selection step — see Section 6a and Section 13d. Phase 1: only contractor persona is functional; other persona selections show “Coming soon — join waitlist.”
  5. Admin panel basics (tenant management, usage dashboard, team invitations)
  6. Data retention policy: Documented — subscription duration + 90 days. Note (XREF-19): Data retention policy is Wave 4 per this build order — Section 9 is the source of truth for scheduling.
  7. Incident response plan: 1-page doc — who is called, communication protocol, remediation
  8. Cyber liability insurance: $1K–$3K/year, purchased before first customer
  9. AI liability insurance (PRE-LAUNCH GATE): Obtain written E&O confirmation that AI-generated output claims are covered AND evaluate standalone AI liability insurance (Munich Re / Armilla / Testudo, $5–$15K/year) before first paying customer. Without confirmed AI liability coverage, every AI-generated estimate is an uninsured liability. This is a hard launch gate — no first customer until coverage is confirmed in writing.
  10. ToS + Privacy Policy + customer DPA drafting: Engage legal counsel ($3K–$5K). ToS: AI liability disclaimers, limitation of liability, SLA terms, data ownership, acceptable use. Privacy Policy: CCPA-compliant disclosure, cookie policy, third-party processor list. Customer-facing DPA template for B2B customers. All three documents must be published before first paying customer — this is a hard launch gate.
  11. Contractor licensing review: Legal review of top 10 target states’ contractor licensing requirements as they relate to AI-generated estimates. Label all estimate outputs as “decision support — requires licensed contractor review.”
  12. Launch to first 3–5 paying customers (Slate + Tier A leads)
  13. Module-specific tours (BOS, K&B Design Canvas, Integration Setup): 3–5 steps each, self-triggered on first module visit. Everboarding system: feature micro-tours, role change tours, “What’s New” infrastructure with changelog beacon. See Section 13l.
Wave 4 Integration Prerequisite (INT-22)

JobTread Pave GraphQL API adapter + anti-corruption layer: Implement the JobTread Pave GraphQL adapter following the IntegrationAdapter interface (Section 3b). Includes GraphQL query builder, pagination handling, rate limit compliance (token bucket), and anti-corruption layer mapping JobTread domain objects to canonical domain model types (Project, Task, Contact). Prerequisite for Wave 5 Project Management Agent and any agent needing live project data from JobTread.

Wave 5: Remaining Agents + Scale (Week 22–28)

(Professional/Enterprise tier agents — completes the 6-agent set. See Appendix A for tier-to-agent mapping.)

  1. Design Agent (Google Drive folder hierarchy, submittals/cut sheets)
  2. Project Management Agent (JobTread schedule sync, weather API, drift detection)
  3. Bookkeeping Agent (QuickBooks read-only integration, AR tracking, lien waivers)
  4. Usage metering + per-tenant AI cost dashboards
  5. Agent template builder (create templates for new industry verticals). Note (XREF-26): The underlying template system (database rows + JSONB config) is built in Wave 2. This Wave 5 item is the admin UI for configuring templates without direct database access.
  6. Full multi-tenant admin panel + team management (invite employees, assign roles)
  7. Referral program infrastructure
  8. Performance optimization based on real usage data from Wave 4 customers

Key change from original plan: Launch with 3 agents (Leads Agent, Sales Agent, CEO Agent) + Support Agent instead of building all 7 before first customer. This recovers 4 weeks for hardening and gets real customer feedback faster. Remaining agents (Design Agent, Project Management Agent, Bookkeeping Agent) ship in Wave 5 based on actual usage priorities.

Post-Launch Roadmap

  • Month 5–6: Batch onboarding optimization, Loom walkthrough library, self-serve onboarding option
  • Month 7–9: Second vertical template (Design-Build or HVAC), API versioning strategy. Dealer persona template (K&B × Dealer) if adoption signals warrant — ~1–2 weeks of template creation + testing (see Section 6a).
  • Month 10–12: SOC 2 readiness assessment (if enterprise deals demand it), direct API negotiations with Anthropic/OpenAI at volume. Manufacturer and Sales Rep persona templates as adoption grows.
  • Year 2: Third vertical, second integration: Procore or Jobber adapter (multi-PM-tool support, consistent with Section 7a recommendation), horizontal scaling beyond dual-VPS to multi-node cluster. B2B network layer (cross-tenant ordering, shared catalogs) when cross-company ordering demand materializes.
  • Month 8–14 (parallel): Business Operating System — Phase 1 (core modules: scorecards, quarterly goals, weekly sync, org chart) ships Month 8–12; Phase 2 (advanced: issue tracker, knowledge base, VTO) ships Month 12–14. See Section 15.
  • Month 10–16 (parallel): Kitchen & Bath AI Design Tool — 6-month build starting after core platform stabilizes. Three.js rendering pipeline, AI layout generation, manufacturer catalog integration. See Section 16.

Data Import & Export (Wave 4)

Data Portability Requirement

Data import and export are both Wave 4 deliverables. Import reduces onboarding friction for customers migrating from spreadsheets or other tools. Export is required for CCPA data portability compliance and builds customer trust (“your data is yours”).

Data Import

  • CSV import adapter: Support CSV import for projects, contacts, and estimates. Mapping UI allows the user to map CSV columns to canonical domain model fields (Section 3c / Wave 1 domain interfaces).
  • Validation: Pre-commit validation with error report — show row-level errors (missing required fields, type mismatches, duplicate detection) before any data is written. User reviews and confirms before import executes.
  • Historical backfill: For JobTread customers, initial data sync via Pave API pulls historical projects, contacts, and estimates into the local data mirror (Section 7a Anti-Corruption Layer). This is part of the onboarding flow, not a separate import step.
  • Implementation: BullMQ background job for large imports (>100 rows). Progress indicator via Socket.io real-time updates. Rollback on critical failure (transactional import per batch).

Data Export

  • “Download all my data” button: Available in account settings for Owner/Admin roles. Exports all tenant-scoped data as a ZIP archive containing JSON (machine-readable) + CSV (human-readable) files.
  • Scope: All data from tenant-scoped tables — projects, contacts, estimates, agent execution history, conversation threads, drafts, escalations, audit logs.
  • Implementation: BullMQ background job assembles the archive, uploads to DO Spaces with a signed URL (24-hour expiration). User notified via email + in-app notification when export is ready. Rate limited to 1 export per 24 hours per tenant.
  • CCPA compliance: This export capability satisfies CCPA data portability requirements. Combined with the data deletion capability in the tenant offboarding flow (Section 5), this completes the CCPA data rights implementation.

10. Cost & Revenue Model (Scaling Path)

Note: These figures illustrate unit economics at various scale points. For the full P&L projection with churn modeling, acquisition pacing, and operating costs, see Section D.

Infrastructure Cost by Scale

Component10 Customers100 Customers1,000 Customers
Vercel Pro$20$20$150 (Team)
Dual VPS + LB (DO)$172$320$2,100 (cluster)
DigitalOcean Managed Valkey$15$30$60
Supabase$25$599 (Team — verify current pricing at supabase.com/pricing; triggered at 50–75 customers based on ~250MB/mo DB growth)$599 (Team)
AI Compute (Vercel AI Gateway)$350$3,500$35,000
Payment Processing (ACH + card blend, ~1.6%)*$64$624$6,240
SendGrid$0$20$90
Cloudflare Pro$20$20$20
Monitoring (self-hosted Loki compute + Sentry team upgrade)$50$100$200
Total Platform Cost$768/mo$5,770/mo$49,829/mo

*Payment processing uses ACH-first strategy: setup fees always via ACH (0.8% capped at $5/txn). Monthly renewals are a mix of ACH ($5 cap) and card (2.9% + $0.30). Blended rate estimate: ~1.6% of revenue. See ACH-First Payment Strategy section for details.

Pricing Tiers (From Spec + Adjusted)

TierSetup Fee (One-Time)Monthly RecurringAgentsTarget
Starter$5,000$275/mo3 operational agents + Support AgentSmall contractors, solopreneurs
Professional$10,000$500/mo6 operational agents + Support AgentMid-size remodelers (like Slate)
Enterprise$20,000$750/mo6 operational agents + Support Agent + customLarge GCs, multi-location

Revenue Projection (Blended Mix: 60% Starter, 30% Pro, 10% Enterprise)

Conservative mix assumes majority Starter-tier customers, reflecting that early-market SMB remodelers skew toward the entry-level plan. Blended setup fee: 0.6×$5K + 0.3×$10K + 0.1×$20K = $8,000. Blended monthly: 0.6×$275 + 0.3×$500 + 0.1×$750 = $390/mo.

Setup Fee Sensitivity

The blended $8,000 assumes 60/30/10 tier mix (Starter/Professional/Enterprise). Year 1 reality may skew Starter-heavy as Enterprise sales cycles run 3–6 months. At 75/25/0 mix (no Enterprise in Y1): blended setup = $6,250. Y1 setup revenue drops from $376K to ~$156K (25 customers × $6,250). Combined with the 25-customer conservative scenario: Y1 total revenue = $156K + $88K = $244K, Y1 profit = ~$212K. Still profitable, but a materially different cash position than the $442K headline.

Steady-State Snapshot: 50 Customers (MRR-Only Unit Economics)

This table shows recurring revenue economics at a hypothetical 50-customer steady state — not a Year 1 annual projection. For the actual Year 1 P&L (which includes setup fees and accounts for customer ramp timing), see Appendix D.

Revenue StreamCalculationAnnual
Setup fees30×$5K + 15×$10K + 5×$20K$400,000
Monthly recurring(30×$275 + 15×$500 + 5×$750) × 12$234,000
Total Year 1$634,000

Bull Case Scenario: 200 Customers

This is an aspirational upside scenario assuming accelerated growth beyond the base-case P&L. The base-case churn model (Appendix C) projects 104 active customers at end of Year 2, not 200. For base-case Year 2 projections, see Appendix D.

Revenue StreamCalculationAnnual
New setup fees (150 new)90×$5K + 45×$10K + 15×$20K$1,200,000
Monthly recurring (200 active)(120×$275 + 60×$500 + 20×$750) × 12$936,000
Total Year 2 (Bull Case)$2,136,000

Bull Case Scenario: 1,000 Customers

This scenario requires 800 new customers in Year 3 — approximately 4.8x the base-case acquisition rate of 168 new customers. It assumes significant sales team expansion, inbound marketing channels, and possibly Series A investment. The base-case churn model projects 190 active customers at end of Year 3. For base-case projections, see Appendix D.

Revenue StreamCalculationAnnual
New setup fees (800 new)480×$5K + 240×$10K + 80×$20K$6,400,000
Monthly recurring (1,000 active)(600×$275 + 300×$500 + 100×$750) × 12$4,680,000
Total Year 3 (Bull Case)$11,080,000

Steady-State Scenario: Zero New Acquisition

Recurring Revenue at Maturity

When customer acquisition drops to zero (steady state), economics depend entirely on recurring revenue. At 190 active customers (end of Year 3): 190 × $390 blended ARPU = $74,100/mo = $889,200/yr recurring revenue. This represents the floor revenue absent any new sales — sufficient to cover all operating costs including full founder salaries and hired staff. Setup fees constitute 66–79% of total revenue during growth years; at steady state, the business must sustain itself on recurring revenue alone.

Net Profit Projection (60/30/10 Mix)

EXCLUDES LABOR

All figures in this table exclude founder salaries, non-founder hires, and onboarding labor. See Appendix D for fully-loaded P&L with personnel costs.

ScaleMonthly RevenueMonthly Cost (All-In)Monthly Net ProfitNet Margin
50 customers$19,500$3,311†$16,189~83%
200 customers$78,000$10,242$67,75886.9%
1,000 customers$390,000$65,029*$324,97183.3%

† Assumes Supabase Pro at $25/mo. If the Supabase Team upgrade triggers at exactly 50 customers, cost increases to ~$3,885/mo.

* All rows exclude founder salaries and non-founder hires. See Appendix D for fully-loaded P&L.

*The $65,029 all-in cost at 1,000 customers includes operating expenses (ops, insurance, monitoring) beyond the $49,829 platform cost table above. The difference (~$15,430/mo) covers ops tooling, legal/misc, and insurance at scale.

Why margins remain strong at 83–87%:

  • AI cost per customer = $24–35/mo at median usage (ceiling: $144.90/mo at full utilization, no caching — see Section B). You charge $275–750/mo. That's 8–21x markup at median.
  • Payment processing is the second largest cost — significantly reduced by the ACH-first strategy. Setup fees processed via ACH at 0.8% capped at $5/txn ($5 on a $20K fee vs $580 via card). Monthly renewals are a mix of ACH ($5 cap) and card (2.9% + $0.30). Blended processing rate: ~1.6% of revenue (vs ~2.9% card-only). Year 1 estimated processing costs: ~$5,900 (vs ~$13,900 card-only).
  • Infrastructure scales sublinearly (adding customer 101 costs ~$3/mo incremental, not $320). Dual-VPS architecture costs only $80/mo more than a single node.
  • Setup fees ($5K-$20K) are high-margin after initial template creation (effective margin ~65–75% at Starter tier, higher at Pro/Enterprise — see Appendix A for breakdown). Templates are reusable.
  • No physical goods, no inventory, no shipping. Pure software margins.
  • The value delivered (replacing $50-80K/yr of admin labor) justifies the price easily.

Conservative stress test (all costs 3x expected):

ScaleMonthly RevenueMonthly Cost (3x)Net ProfitNet Margin
1,000 customers$390,000$195,087$194,91350.0%

Even at 3x cost overrun, margins stay at 50% — still strongly profitable.

Note: The all-in cost figures exclude founder salaries and non-founder hires. At 1,000 customers, Section E hiring triggers dictate full-time Customer Success, a developer, an SDR, and potentially senior roles — adding $200–400K/year in payroll not reflected here. The stress test models platform costs only.

Key Insight

AI cost and payment processing fees are the two dominant expenses at scale (~70% and ~15% of total costs respectively). The ACH-first strategy reduces payment processing from ~23% (card-only) to ~15% (blended ACH/card). Infrastructure (dual VPS, DB) is negligible. The model routing + caching strategy directly impacts margin.

Cost Stress Test (What If 5x Expected Usage?)

ScenarioMonthly AI CostPer CustomerAction
Baseline (75 calls/day/agent)$3,500$35.00Normal operations
2x usage (150 calls/day)$7,000$70.00Still profitable at $275–$750/mo pricing
5x usage (375 calls/day)$17,500$175.00Margin drops to ~50–52% fully loaded — still healthy
10x usage (750 calls/day)$35,000$350.00Margin ~4–5% fully loaded — trigger usage alerts
Runaway agent (infinite loop)$500+/day/tenant—Hard kill via per-key spending limit

Guardrails against cost blowout:

  1. Per-tenant daily spending limit (Valkey counter, enforced in AI SDK budgetEnforcementMiddleware): hard cap at 2x expected spend → tenant AI access suspended
  2. Per-agent invocation limit: max 500 calls/day per agent (configurable per tier)
  3. The tier-level daily cap (1K for Starter, 5K for Professional, unlimited for Enterprise) is the binding constraint and overrides per-agent defaults.
  4. Per-request token cap: max_tokens=4096 for standard, 8192 for complex agents
  5. Circuit breaker: if agent spends >$5 in a single execution, abort + alert
  6. Monthly billing reconciliation: if tenant exceeds plan limits, auto-notify + suggest upgrade
  7. Overage billing option (Enterprise): billed at cost (pass-through of actual AI spend) instead of hard cutoff

Worst case at 100 customers (all 10x): $35,000/mo AI cost + ~$3,500/mo infrastructure/ops vs $39,000/mo revenue (100 customers × $390 blended) = barely profitable at ~4–5% margin after all platform costs. Usage alerts would trigger long before reaching 10x, and per-agent circuit breakers ($5 max per execution) prevent runaway costs.

Anthropic/OpenAI Provider Rate Limits

Rate Limit Scaling Plan

At 100 customers (~7,500 LLM calls/day = ~5.2 RPM average, ~52 RPM peak during morning briefing window): Anthropic’s default rate limits (60 RPM for Sonnet on standard API tier) are sufficient. No action needed.

At 500+ customers (~37,500 LLM calls/day = ~26 RPM average, ~260 RPM peak): Request rate limit increase from Anthropic and OpenAI, or provision multiple API keys per provider with round-robin distribution in the AIGateway adapter. Vercel AI Gateway handles provider-level failover automatically — configure via the provider registry with multiple API key entries per provider.

At 1,000+ customers: Direct volume agreement with Anthropic/OpenAI includes custom rate limits. This aligns with the direct API negotiation trigger in the post-launch roadmap (Month 10–12).

Direct Provider Volume Discounts

At ~$60K/mo AI spend (~1,700 customers), negotiate direct volume discounts with Anthropic/OpenAI for 15-20% savings = $9-12K/mo saved. The AIGateway abstraction makes provider switching a configuration change.

Total Cost of Ownership for Customers

Kiptra AI is an AI agent layer that sits on top of existing project management software — it does not replace tools like JobTread, Buildertrend, or QuickBooks. Customers should budget for both:

SoftwareMonthly CostPurpose
Existing PM tool (JobTread, Buildertrend, etc.)$149–$499/moProject management, scheduling, client portal
Kiptra AI$275–$750/moAI agents for automation, estimating, lead mgmt
Total software spend$424–$1,249/mo

ROI justification: A remodeling company with 2 office staff ($50K salary each = $8,333/mo combined) that automates 50% of admin tasks saves ~$4,167/mo. Against $424–$1,249/mo total software cost, the ROI is 3.3x–9.8x. Payback period on setup fee: 1.2–5 months depending on tier and automation level.

The value proposition is not “replace your PM software” — it is “make your existing team 2–3x more productive by automating the tasks your PM software can’t do.”

Unit Economics: LTV:CAC

MetricCalculationValue
Average Monthly Revenue per Customer$390 (blended 60/30/10)
Average Customer Lifetime1 / 30% annual churn = 3.33 years = 40 months
Gross LTV$390 × 40 months$15,600
AI Cost over Lifetime$35/mo × 40 months$1,400
Infrastructure Cost over Lifetime~$5/mo × 40 months$200
Net LTV$15,600 - $1,400 - $200$14,000
Setup Fee Revenue$8,000 (blended)Collected Day 1
Total Customer Value$14,000 + $8,000$22,000
CAC (Ambassador model)Adam’s time (~4 hrs/customer × $0 salary) + $1,250 average referral cost (see Section E)~$1,250
LTV:CAC Ratio$22,000 / $1,25017.6:1

Note: The 17.6:1 ratio is high because founder sales labor is uncosted. With a salaried salesperson ($65K/yr, closing 3 customers/month), CAC rises to ~$3,050 and LTV:CAC becomes 7.2:1 — still excellent (benchmark: 3:1 is considered healthy per Bessemer Venture Partners).

Fully-Loaded CAC

The headline CAC of $1,250 (referral cost only, founder labor at $0) understates the true customer acquisition cost. Including onboarding labor: Sales (4 hours) + Onboarding (8–12 hours) = 12–16 hours founder time per customer. At an imputed $150/hr loaded cost: $1,800–$2,400 labor + $1,250 referral = $3,050–$3,650 fully-loaded CAC. LTV:CAC at fully-loaded CAC: $22,000 / $3,350 = 6.6:1 (still well above the 3:1 benchmark). Present both figures to investors: $1,250 marginal CAC (referral only) and $3,350 fully-loaded CAC (including founder time).

Stress test at 45% churn (worst case): Average lifetime = 1/0.45 = 2.22 years = 26.7 months. Gross LTV = $390 × 26.7 = $10,413. AI cost = $35 × 26.7 = $935. Infra cost = $5 × 26.7 = $134. Net LTV = $10,413 − $935 − $134 = $9,344. Total Customer Value = $9,344 + $8,000 setup = $17,344. LTV:CAC = $17,344 / $1,250 = 13.9:1. Still healthy, but significantly lower than the 30% base case.

At 40% industry-median churn rate: lifetime = 1/0.40 = 2.5 years = 30 months. Gross LTV = $390 × 30 = $11,700. AI cost = $35 × 30 = $1,050. Infra cost = $5 × 30 = $150. Net LTV = $11,700 − $1,050 − $150 = $10,500. Total Customer Value = $10,500 + $8,000 setup = $18,500. LTV:CAC = $18,500 / $1,250 = 14.8:1 (still healthy). The 30% figure used above is the optimistic planning base case.

11. Verification

Every verification item has a quantitative pass/fail threshold, assigned wave, owner, and automation status. Items without a passing result block deployment of the associated wave.

11.1 Infrastructure & Deployment

#VerificationPass CriteriaWaveOwnerAutomated?
V-01Docker Compose deploys cleanly on both VPS nodesExit code 0 on docker compose up -d on primary + secondary; all containers healthy within 60sWave 1JosephCI (deploy script)
V-02Deployment health check (rolling)Rolling deploy to secondary → health endpoint returns 200 within 30s → deploy to primary → health 200 within 30s; zero downtime confirmed via LB health logWave 1JosephCI (deploy script)
V-03Failover testKill primary VPS process; LB routes 100% traffic to secondary within 90s; active agent execution in progress on primary resumes on secondary via BullMQ job recovery (stalled job picked up within 120s); NATS JetStream processing confirmed suspended (no false delivery); zero HTTP 5xx during failover windowWave 2JosephManual (quarterly)
V-04BullMQ job recovery after failoverIn-progress jobs on killed node marked stalled within 30s; secondary worker picks up stalled jobs within 120s; zero jobs lost (DLQ count unchanged)Wave 2JosephManual (quarterly)
V-05NATS Raft cluster failoverWhen any single NATS node is killed, remaining 2 nodes maintain Raft quorum within 10s; JetStream consumers continue processing on surviving nodes; zero message loss during single-node failure; recovered node rejoins cluster and catches up via Raft log replay; zero duplicate deliveriesWave 2JosephManual (quarterly)
V-06Socket.io cross-node deliveryClient connected to VPS-2 receives event published from VPS-1 within 500ms; verified with 10 sequential test events, 100% delivery rateWave 2JosephIntegration test

11.2 Security & Tenant Isolation

#VerificationPass CriteriaWaveOwnerAutomated?
V-07Supabase RLS blocks cross-tenant data access3 test tenants; each tenant’s SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE returns only own data; cross-tenant query returns 0 rows (not error); tested across all RLS-protected tablesWave 1JosephIntegration test
V-08Cross-tenant RLS adversarial test3 test tenants; SQL injection via RPC, direct REST with forged JWT, and Supabase client with wrong tenant context — all return 0 rows or 403; 0 cross-tenant leaks across all CRUD operationsWave 2JosephIntegration test
V-09Per-tenant NATS subject namespace isolationTenant A subscribes to tenant.B.* — receives 0 messages; 10 events published to Tenant B’s namespace, Tenant A receives noneWave 2JosephIntegration test
V-10Prompt injection defense10 crafted injection payloads (OWASP LLM Top 10 categories) submitted; Layer 1 classifier blocks ≥9/10 before reaching agent; 0 reach agent execution unfilteredWave 3aJosephIntegration test
V-11OAuth token refresh (QuickBooks)QB refresh token exchange produces new access + refresh token pair; both persisted atomically (single DB transaction); old tokens invalidated; verified with 3 consecutive refresh cyclesWave 2JosephIntegration test
V-29Microsoft OAuth end-to-endUser connects Outlook account via OAuth 2.0 + PKCE; sends test email via Mail.Send; receives calendar events via Calendars.Read; refresh token persisted with AES-256-GCM encryption; admin consent fallback tested (simulate AADSTS65001 error, verify admin consent URL displayed); Microsoft Graph subscription created and webhook notification received within 60s of calendar changeWave 2JosephIntegration test
V-30Provider abstraction paritySame agent workflow (draft email → approve → send) succeeds via both GoogleEmailProvider and MicrosoftEmailProvider; same calendar availability check returns consistent format via both GoogleCalendarProvider and MicrosoftCalendarProvider; provider swap requires zero agent code changesWave 2JosephIntegration test

11.3 Agent & AI Quality

#VerificationPass CriteriaWaveOwnerAutomated?
V-12Agent quality baseline≥85% accuracy on 5-sample rubric per agent, scored by 2 independent reviewers; inter-rater agreement ≥80% (Cohen’s kappa ≥0.6)Wave 3aJosephManual
V-13End-to-end agent executionTrigger agent → executes → result appears in dashboard; total latency <30s for single-step agents; result matches expected schema (Zod validation passes)Wave 3aJosephE2E test
V-14AI Gateway fallback routingPrimary model returns 503 → fallback model fires within 2s for single-step calls; multi-step agents return graceful error (not hang); verified with 5 consecutive fallback triggersWave 2JosephIntegration test
V-15Spending limit enforcementAgent execution paused when tenant hits spending limit (within $0.50 of limit); tenant notified via dashboard toast + email within 60s; no further LLM calls made after limit hitWave 3aJosephIntegration test
V-16Per-tenant spending limit enforcement via Valkey counters (AI SDK middleware)Per-tenant Valkey counter tracks cumulative spend; exceeding limit returns 429 (not 500); dashboard shows accurate spend within $0.10 of actualWave 2JosephIntegration test

11.4 Frontend & Real-Time

#VerificationPass CriteriaWaveOwnerAutomated?
V-17React Flow mind map renders correctlyMind map renders 7–9 agents (persona-specific, 28 total types) + integration nodes; all edges visible; no overlapping nodes; renders in <2s on 4G throttled connectionWave 3aJosephE2E test
V-18WebSocket agent status updatesAgent status change pushed to dashboard in <500ms (measured P95 over 20 events); UI updates without page refreshWave 2JosephE2E test
V-19BullMQ cron fires daily briefingCron job triggers within ±60s of configured time; briefing email delivered within 5 minutes; content includes all active project summaries for tenantWave 3aJosephIntegration test
V-20Stripe webhook processingSubscription create → invoice → payment → active status verified end-to-end; webhook signature validation rejects tampered payloads; idempotency key prevents duplicate processing (replay same event 3x, only 1 DB write)Wave 2JosephIntegration test
V-31Help Bot returns context-aware responsesHelp Bot returns route-contextual response on 3+ different routes (dashboard, billing, integrations); deep links in responses navigate to correct route; ThumbsUp/ThumbsDown feedback captured in support_messages table with source: 'help_bot'Wave 3bJosephE2E test
V-32Platform Orientation tour completes on first loginPlatform Orientation tour triggers on first login for all roles; role-based content matches user role (Owner sees team management, Designer sees canvas); tour_progress record created with completed=true and correct tour_id='platform'; tour does not re-trigger on subsequent loginsWave 3bJosephE2E test
V-33Onboarding checklist auto-completes itemsChecklist items auto-complete when trigger conditions are met: connecting QuickBooks creates checkmark, inviting team member creates checkmark, running first agent creates checkmark; progress percentage updates in real-time via WebSocket; checklist dismissible after 80% completionWave 3bJosephE2E test
V-34Module tour triggers on first visitBOS tour triggers on first visit to /bos; K&B Canvas tour triggers on first visit to /design/kitchen-bath; tours do NOT trigger if user has already completed that tour (tour_progress.completed=true); 24-hour cooldown enforced between tours; dismissed tours respect escalation policy (7d/30d/never)Wave 4JosephE2E test

11.5 BullMQ & Event Processing

#VerificationPass CriteriaWaveOwnerAutomated?
V-21NATS events route to correct tenantPublish 10 events across 3 tenants; each tenant’s subscriber receives only its own events; 0 cross-tenant deliveries; 0 dropped eventsWave 2JosephIntegration test
V-22BullMQ dead letter queueFailed job (after max retries) appears in DLQ within 30s; admin panel surfaces DLQ jobs with error message and stack trace; DLQ jobs are re-processable via admin actionWave 2JosephIntegration test

11.6 Business Operating System

#VerificationPass CriteriaWaveOwnerAutomated?
V-23Core 5 Business OS modules render data correctly with RLS isolationScorecards, Quarterly Goals, Weekly Sync, Org Chart, and Issue Tracker each render correct data for 3 test tenants; cross-tenant queries return 0 rows; all CRUD operations function with <500ms response timeWave 4JosephIntegration test

11.7 K&B Design Tool

#VerificationPass CriteriaWaveOwnerAutomated?
V-24DXF export validated against AutoCAD importExport 5 representative kitchen/bath layouts as DXF; import each into AutoCAD 2025; all geometry, layers, and dimensions render correctly with 0 import errors; file size <5 MB per layoutPhase 2JosephManual
V-253D render performanceThree.js 3D render completes in <3 seconds on Modal serverless GPU (A10/L4); scene with 50+ cabinet objects, lighting, and materials; 4K output; GPU memory <512 MB; no impact on VPS CPU/memoryPhase 2JosephManual

11.8 Load & Performance

#VerificationPass CriteriaWaveOwnerAutomated?
V-26Concurrent user load test50 concurrent users (simulated via k6 or Artillery); P95 response time <3s across all API endpoints; server memory <85% of 8GB (6.8 GB); BullMQ queue depth stabilizes (not growing) within 60s of load start; zero 5xx errors during sustained 10-minute testWave 3bJosephLoad test script

11.9 Compliance & Legal

#VerificationPass CriteriaWaveOwnerAutomated?
V-27Terms of Service + Privacy Policy publishedToS and Privacy Policy pages live at /legal/terms and /legal/privacy before first paying customer onboards; reviewed by legal counsel; last-updated date within 30 days of launchWave 4 (gate)JosephManual
V-28CCPA data deletion end-to-endTrigger deletion request for test tenant; verify complete removal from all 6 data stores (Supabase Postgres, Valkey, NATS, Supabase Storage, DigitalOcean Spaces backups, Stripe metadata) within 30 days; deletion audit log entry created; re-query all stores returns 0 records for deleted tenantWave 4JosephManual (semi-annual)

11.10 GTM Verification Gates

Go-to-market milestones with quantitative thresholds. Missing a gate triggers the specified corrective action.

#GTM GatePass CriteriaDeadlineOwnerCorrective Action if Missed
G-01Outbound reply rate≥40% reply rate from first 50 Tier A contacts (20+ replies)Month 1JosephRevise messaging; A/B test 3 subject lines; consult sales advisor
G-02Demo-to-proposal conversion≥40% of demos result in a proposal sent (minimum 8 demos conducted)Month 2JosephReview demo script; add ROI calculator to demo flow; survey non-converters
G-03First closes≥3 signed contracts (any tier)Month 3JosephOffer founder pricing ($199/mo first year); extend free trial to 30 days; consider pilot program
G-04Active customer count≥15 active paying customersMonth 6JosephIf below 10: activate paid channels (Google Ads for “construction project management”, LinkedIn Ads targeting GC owners); allocate $2,000/mo ad budget

11.11 Acceptance Criteria (Given/When/Then)

BDD-style acceptance criteria for all critical user flows. These scenarios serve as the source of truth for integration test implementation and manual QA verification. Each scenario maps to a specific verification item (V-xx) or feature area defined above.

Agent Execution (Trigger → Result)

Gherkin
Feature: Agent Execution

Scenario: Successful user-triggered agent execution
  Given a tenant "Slate" with an active Professional subscription
  And the "Sales Agent" agent is enabled for this tenant
  And the tenant's monthly AI spend is below 100% of their limit
  When a user with role "Manager" clicks "Run Estimate" for project "Kitchen Reno"
  Then a BullMQ job is queued with tenant_id, agent_type, and project context
  And an agent_executions record is created with status "QUEUED"
  And status transitions to "RUNNING" when the worker picks up the job
  And the agent executes with stopWhen: stepCountIs(17) and max_tokens: 8000
  And on success, status transitions to "COMPLETED" with output stored
  And a NATS event is published on "TENANT_{id}.agent.estimate-engine.completed"
  And the user receives a real-time update via Socket.io

Scenario: Agent execution blocked by spending limit
  Given a tenant whose monthly AI spend is >= 100% of their plan limit
  When any user triggers an agent execution
  Then the API returns HTTP 429 with error code "AGENT_BUDGET_EXCEEDED"
  And no BullMQ job is queued
  And the dashboard shows "Monthly AI budget reached — upgrade your plan"

Scenario: Agent execution timeout
  Given an agent execution that has been running for 300 seconds
  When the wall-clock timeout fires
  Then the execution status is set to "TIMED_OUT"
  And the BullMQ job is moved to the dead letter queue
  And the user is notified via Socket.io with error code "AGENT_EXECUTION_TIMEOUT"
  And the event is logged to Sentry with the agent_execution_id

Escalation Approval Flow

Gherkin
Feature: Escalation Approval

Scenario: Agent creates an escalation
  Given the Leads Agent detects a lead mentioning a legal dispute
  When the agent triggers an escalation
  Then an escalation record is created with severity, context, and recommended action
  And the agent_execution status transitions to "AWAITING_APPROVAL"
  And the escalation appears in the dashboard's approval queue
  And the Owner/Admin receives a Socket.io notification
  And an email notification is sent if enabled in notification_preferences

Scenario: Owner approves an escalation
  Given a pending escalation in the approval queue
  When the Owner clicks "Approve"
  Then the agent executes the recommended action
  And the escalation status updates to "approved"
  And the agent_execution status transitions to "COMPLETED"
  And an audit log entry is created with approver, timestamp, and action taken

Scenario: Owner rejects an escalation
  Given a pending escalation in the approval queue
  When the Owner clicks "Reject" with a reason
  Then the agent_execution status transitions to "REJECTED"
  And the rejection reason is stored in the escalation record
  And an audit log entry is created

Draft Approval Flow

Gherkin
Feature: Draft Approval

Scenario: Agent creates an email draft
  Given the Leads Agent has scored a lead and composed a follow-up email
  When the agent stores the draft
  Then a record is created in the drafts table with type "email", content, and metadata
  And the draft appears in the user's pending approvals queue
  And a Socket.io notification is sent to the user's dashboard

Scenario: User approves a draft email
  Given a pending email draft in the approval queue
  When an authorized user (Owner/Admin/Manager) clicks "Send"
  Then the email provider API (Gmail or Microsoft Graph, based on tenant's connected provider) is called with the draft content using the tenant's OAuth credentials
  And the draft status updates to "sent" with a timestamp
  And if the email API fails, the draft status updates to "send_failed"
  And a new escalation is created with the failure context

Scenario: User edits and approves a draft
  Given a pending email draft
  When a user modifies the draft content and clicks "Send"
  Then the original draft content is preserved in version history
  And the modified content is sent via the tenant's email provider API (Gmail or Microsoft Graph)
  And the draft record reflects both original and edited versions

User Login + MFA

Gherkin
Feature: User Authentication

Scenario: Successful login without MFA
  Given a registered user with verified email and MFA disabled
  When they submit valid email and password
  Then better-auth creates a session with httpOnly cookie
  And the user is redirected to their tenant's dashboard
  And a Socket.io connection is established in room "tenant:{tenant_id}"

Scenario: Successful login with MFA
  Given a registered user with MFA enabled
  When they submit valid email and password
  Then they are prompted for their TOTP code
  When they submit a valid TOTP code
  Then the session is created and they are redirected to the dashboard

Scenario: Failed login — locked account
  Given a user who has failed login 5 times in 15 minutes
  When they attempt to log in again
  Then the API returns error code "AUTH_ACCOUNT_LOCKED"
  And the lockout duration is 15 minutes
  And a security email is sent to the user's registered email

Team Invitation + Acceptance

Gherkin
Feature: Team Invitation

Scenario: Owner invites a new team member
  Given an Owner of tenant "Slate" with member count below plan limit
  When they enter an email and select role "Designer"
  Then an invitation record is created via better-auth Organization plugin
  And an invitation email is sent with a unique token
  And the invitation appears as "pending" in the team management page

Scenario: Invitee accepts invitation
  Given a valid, non-expired invitation token
  When the invitee clicks the link and creates their account
  Then they are added to the tenant with the assigned role ("Designer")
  And the invitation status updates to "accepted"
  And the Owner receives a notification that the new member joined
  And the new member sees only agents permitted for their role (Design Agent)

Scenario: Invitation expired
  Given an invitation token older than the configured TTL
  When the invitee clicks the link
  Then they see error "AUTH_INVITATION_EXPIRED"
  And they are prompted to request a new invitation from their organization

Stripe Subscription Creation (ACH-Preferred with Card Option)

Gherkin
Feature: Stripe Subscription (ACH-Preferred, Cards Accepted)

Scenario: New tenant subscribes to Professional plan via ACH
  Given a new tenant who has completed onboarding
  When they select the Professional plan ($500/mo)
  Then the checkout page shows both ACH and credit/debit card as payment options
  And ACH is pre-selected as the recommended default
  And the total due today is displayed as $10,500 (setup fee $10,000 + first month $500) for ACH
  When they connect their bank account via Plaid instant verification
  Then a Stripe Customer is created and linked to the tenant record
  And a Stripe Subscription is created for the Professional price with ACH payment method
  And the tenant's plan column updates to "professional"
  And the tenant's billing_status updates to "active"
  And 6 operational agents + Support Agent are enabled
  And an AI SDK spending limit is configured for the Professional tier

Scenario: Customer at checkout selects ACH
  Given a customer at checkout
  When they select ACH
  Then no surcharge is applied and total shows base price

Scenario: Customer at checkout selects credit/debit card
  Given a customer at checkout
  When they select credit/debit card
  Then a 2.9% + $0.30 surcharge is added and displayed before confirmation

Scenario: Customer in Connecticut or Massachusetts selects card
  Given a customer in Connecticut or Massachusetts
  When they select credit/debit card
  Then no surcharge is applied (state restriction)

Scenario: Stripe webhook confirms ACH payment
  Given a Stripe "invoice.paid" webhook event for an ACH payment
  When the webhook is received and signature is verified
  Then the tenant's billing_status is confirmed as "active"
  And the event is deduplicated via webhook_events table (idempotency key = event ID)

Scenario: Customer switches payment method for renewals
  Given a tenant with an active subscription
  When the Owner navigates to billing settings
  Then they can switch between ACH and credit/debit card for future renewals
  And the same surcharge rules apply to card renewals
  And payment method changes take effect on the next billing cycle

Scenario: Customer disputes card surcharge
  Given a customer who paid via credit/debit card with surcharge
  When they dispute the surcharge
  Then the surcharge is clearly documented on the Stripe receipt as a separate line item
  And refunds include the surcharge amount

Plan Upgrade/Downgrade

Gherkin
Feature: Plan Change

Scenario: Upgrade from Starter to Professional
  Given a tenant on Starter plan (3 agents, 1K calls/day)
  When the Owner selects "Upgrade to Professional"
  Then Stripe prorates the remaining Starter billing period
  And the new Professional subscription is activated immediately
  And the tenant's plan updates to "professional"
  And 3 additional agents are enabled
  And the daily API call limit increases to 5K
  And the AI SDK spending limit is updated

Scenario: Downgrade from Professional to Starter
  Given a tenant on Professional plan with 6 agents active
  When the Owner selects "Downgrade to Starter"
  Then the downgrade is scheduled for end of current billing period
  And a warning is shown: "3 agents will be disabled at period end"
  And at period end, the 3 lowest-priority agents are disabled
  And the tenant's plan updates to "starter"

JobTread Grant Key Connection

JobTread Authentication (INT-14)

JobTread uses grant key authentication (not OAuth). Customers create a grant key at app.jobtread.com/grants and paste it into Kiptra. A guided onboarding walkthrough with direct link and video minimizes friction. Kiptra applies to the JobTread Integration Partner Program for a native OAuth-style connection (roadmap item). A BullMQ cron job makes a lightweight API call every 30 days to prevent the 3-month inactivity expiration.

Gherkin
Feature: JobTread Integration

Scenario: Successful grant key connection
  Given a tenant with no JobTread integration connected
  When the Admin navigates to Settings → Integrations → JobTread
  And follows the guided walkthrough (direct link to app.jobtread.com/grants + video)
  And pastes the grant key into the Kiptra UI
  Then the grant key is encrypted with the tenant's DEK (AES-256-GCM)
  And stored in the integrations table with key_version
  And an initial full sync is triggered via BullMQ job
  And the integration status shows "Connected" with last_synced_at timestamp

Scenario: Grant key keepalive
  When the BullMQ cron job runs every 30 days
  Then a lightweight JobTread API call is made for each connected tenant
  And the grant key inactivity timer is reset (preventing 3-month expiration)

Scenario: Grant key expiry alert
  Given a JobTread grant key that has not been used for 75 days (85-day threshold)
  When the proactive health check runs
  Then an in-app banner warns "Your JobTread connection expires in 15 days"
  And a lightweight API call is made to keep the key alive

QuickBooks OAuth Connection

Gherkin
Feature: QuickBooks Integration

Scenario: Successful OAuth connection
  Given a tenant with no QuickBooks integration
  When the Admin completes the QB OAuth 2.0 flow
  Then tokens are encrypted (AES-256-GCM) and stored with key_version
  And refresh_token_obtained_at timestamp is recorded
  And an initial financial data sync is triggered

Scenario: Refresh token rotation
  Given a connected QuickBooks integration needing token refresh
  When the refresh endpoint is called
  Then both new access_token AND new refresh_token are received (QB rotates refresh tokens)
  And BOTH tokens are atomically updated in a single database transaction
  And if the DB write fails after the token endpoint returns 200, the old refresh token is permanently revoked
  And a re-authorization prompt is surfaced to the tenant immediately

Scenario: 100-day inactivity warning
  Given a QB refresh_token_obtained_at that is 85 days old
  When the daily health check runs
  Then an alert fires: "Your QuickBooks connection expires in 15 days of non-use"
  And an in-app banner with "Reconnect" link is displayed

Leads Agent Email Intake

Gherkin
Feature: Email Lead Intake

Scenario: New lead email received
  Given an email arrives at leads@customer-domain.com
  And SendGrid Inbound Parse forwards it to the webhook endpoint
  When the webhook is received with valid URL path token
  Then the email content is sanitized (HTML stripped, whitespace normalized, character limits enforced)
  And the Haiku input classifier scans for prompt injection
  And if clean, a NATS event is published on "TENANT_{id}.agent.discovery-concierge.email_received"
  And the Leads Agent scores the lead (0-100) using the tenant's qualification criteria
  And a lead record is created in the CRM (Supabase)
  And a draft acknowledgment email is stored in the drafts table

Scenario: Prompt injection detected in email
  Given an inbound email containing injection patterns (e.g., "ignore previous instructions")
  When the Haiku classifier flags it as potential injection
  Then the email is routed to a human review queue (NOT silently dropped)
  And an audit log entry records the detection
  And the Leads Agent does NOT process the email

Sales Agent Cost Generation

Gherkin
Feature: Estimate Generation

Scenario: Successful estimate generation
  Given project "Kitchen Reno" with complete specifications in JobTread
  And the tenant's cost catalog contains all required line items
  When the Manager triggers "Generate Estimate"
  Then the Sales Agent queries project specs via query_jobtread_projects
  And cross-references historical costs via query_quickbooks_summary
  And generates a structured estimate with line items, quantities, and pricing
  And applies the tenant's configured markup and margin rates
  And stores the estimate draft via create_estimate_draft
  And the draft appears in the approval queue with confidence indicator

Scenario: Missing cost catalog entry
  Given a project spec requiring "custom marble countertop" not in the cost catalog
