  When the Sales Agent encounters the missing item
  Then the line item is marked as "MISSING: custom marble countertop"
  And the estimate is still generated with available items
  And the overall confidence indicator is set to "Low"
  And an escalation is created flagging the missing catalog entry

CEO Agent Morning Briefing

Gherkin
Feature: Morning Briefing

Scenario: Successful daily briefing generation
  Given tenant "Slate" with timezone "America/New_York"
  And the BullMQ repeatable job is scheduled for 7:00 AM ET + random(0, 30min) jitter
  When the scheduled time arrives
  Then the CEO Agent fetches last 24h of agent_executions outputs
  And summarizes them to 2K tokens via Haiku
  And queries financial KPIs from QuickBooks, pipeline from lead data, project status from JobTread
  And generates a structured morning briefing (TL;DR → Financial → Pipeline → Projects → Agents → Actions)
  And stores the briefing in the drafts table
  And pushes a Socket.io notification to the Owner's dashboard
  And optionally sends an email briefing if enabled in Owner's notification_preferences

Scenario: Data source unavailable
  Given QuickBooks integration is temporarily down (circuit breaker open)
  When the morning briefing runs
  Then the financial section states "Financial data unavailable — QuickBooks integration is down (last synced: {{timestamp}})"
  And the remaining sections are generated normally
  And the briefing includes an action item: "Reconnect QuickBooks integration"

Support Agent Conversation

Gherkin
Feature: Support Agent Chat

Scenario: High-confidence KB answer
  Given a user on the Integrations settings page asks "How do I reconnect JobTread?"
  When the Support Agent searches the knowledge base
  And the top result has confidence score >= 0.85
  Then the agent responds with step-by-step instructions
  And includes source attribution: "Based on [Integration Troubleshooting Guide]"
  And ends with "Was this helpful?"

Scenario: Low-confidence triggers escalation
  Given a user asks about an unreleased feature
  When the Support Agent searches the KB and all results have confidence < 0.60
  Then the agent responds: "I'm not sure about this. Let me connect you with our team."
  And escalate_to_human is called with conversation transcript, sentiment tag, and P3 severity
  And the user sees "Your request has been escalated to our team"

Scenario: Proactive support — failure streak
  Given tenant "Slate" has 3+ agent execution failures in the past 24 hours
  When the proactive support BullMQ worker runs (every 15 minutes)
  Then an in-app notification appears: "We noticed some agents encountered errors. Need help?"
  And clicking the notification auto-opens the support chat with diagnostic context pre-loaded

Tenant Spending Limit Enforcement

Gherkin
Feature: Spending Limit Enforcement

Scenario: Pre-flight spending check blocks execution
  Given tenant "Slate" on Professional plan with $200/mo AI budget
  And current month spend is $205
  When any user triggers an agent execution
  Then the API returns HTTP 429 with "AGENT_BUDGET_EXCEEDED"
  And no BullMQ job is queued
  And the dashboard shows a spending limit banner

Scenario: 80% spending alert
  Given tenant spend reaches 80% of monthly limit
  When the AI SDK costTrackingMiddleware detects the threshold
  Then an in-app alert is triggered
  And an email notification is sent to the Owner
  And the alert is logged in the audit trail

Scenario: Enterprise tier metered billing above cap
  Given an Enterprise tenant with $200/mo AI spend cap
  And current month spend reaches $200
  When an agent execution is triggered
  Then the execution proceeds (NOT blocked)
  And usage above $200 is tracked for metered Stripe billing at cost
  And the dashboard shows "Usage above cap will be billed at cost"

Agent Failover (Primary Model Fails)

Gherkin
Feature: Model Failover

Scenario: Anthropic API down — single-step call failover
  Given the Sales Agent makes a stateless classification call using Claude Haiku
  And the Anthropic API returns 503
  When the Vercel AI Gateway fallback logic activates
  Then the call is retried with GPT-4.1 (OpenAI) as fallback
  And the execution completes using the fallback model
  And the cost is tracked under the fallback model's pricing

Scenario: Provider failure mid-execution — multi-step agent
  Given the Sales Agent is on step 8 of 17 with tool calls already executed
  And the Anthropic API fails
  When the AI SDK circuit breaker opens
  Then the execution is aborted (NOT retried — side effects already occurred)
  And the execution status is set to "FAILED"
  And the job is dead-lettered to BullMQ DLQ
  And the user is notified via Socket.io with "AGENT_EXECUTION_FAILED"
  And a human escalation is created for non-idempotent executions

Scenario: AI SDK circuit breaker half-open
  Given the circuit breaker has been open for 60 seconds
  When the half-open test call succeeds
  Then the circuit closes and normal routing resumes
  And queued single-step calls in BullMQ are processed

Webhook Idempotency

Gherkin
Feature: Webhook Idempotency

Scenario: Duplicate webhook event received
  Given a Stripe "invoice.paid" event with ID "evt_123" was already processed
  And a record exists in webhook_events with dedup_key "evt_123"
  When the same event is received again (Stripe retry)
  Then the webhook returns HTTP 200 (acknowledge receipt)
  And no duplicate processing occurs
  And the duplicate is logged for monitoring

Scenario: First-time webhook event
  Given a new JobTread webhook event with no matching dedup_key
  When the event is received with valid signature
  Then a record is inserted into webhook_events atomically before processing
  And the event payload is published to NATS for the appropriate agent
  And if processing fails, the webhook_events record remains (preventing re-processing of side-effected events)

Real-Time Agent Status Update (Socket.io)

Gherkin
Feature: Real-Time Status Updates

Scenario: Agent execution status streamed to dashboard
  Given a user has an active Socket.io connection
  And they are in room "tenant:{tenant_id}:agent:estimate-engine"
  When the Sales Agent transitions from QUEUED → RUNNING → COMPLETED
  Then each state transition is pushed to the client in real-time
  And the dashboard agent status card updates without page refresh

Scenario: Connection lost and reconnected
  Given the user's Socket.io connection drops
  When the connection is re-established within 30 seconds
  Then the client automatically re-joins its tenant/agent rooms
  And any status changes during disconnection are fetched via REST API catch-up
  And the connection indicator returns to green

Scenario: Connection lost > 30 seconds
  Given the Socket.io connection has been disconnected for 30+ seconds
  When auto-reconnection continues to fail
  Then the connection indicator shows gray with "Refresh" link
  And clicking "Refresh" performs a full page reload

Data Export (CCPA)

Gherkin
Feature: CCPA Data Export

Scenario: User requests data export
  Given a user in tenant "Slate" requests a data export
  When the Owner submits the CCPA export request
  Then a BullMQ job is queued to compile all tenant-scoped user data
  And the export includes: profile, conversation history, agent executions, drafts, escalations
  And PII is included (it is the user's own data)
  And the export is generated as a ZIP of JSON files
  And the user is notified via email when the export is ready for download
  And the download link expires after 7 days
  And an audit log entry records the export request

Scenario: Data deletion request
  Given a CCPA deletion request for a specific user
  When the Owner confirms the deletion
  Then all user-specific records are soft-deleted (marked deleted, not purged)
  And the user's session is invalidated immediately
  And hard deletion occurs after a 30-day grace period
  And an audit trail of the deletion is retained (without PII)

Tenant Offboarding

Gherkin
Feature: Tenant Offboarding

Scenario: Owner cancels subscription
  Given an Owner of tenant "Slate" initiates cancellation
  When they confirm cancellation with reason
  Then the Stripe subscription is set to cancel at period end
  And the tenant's billing_status updates to "canceling"
  And agents continue to function until period end
  And a CCPA-compliant data export is automatically generated
  And the Owner receives confirmation email with data export link

Scenario: Subscription period ends after cancellation
  Given tenant "Slate" billing_status is "canceling" and the period has ended
  When the Stripe "customer.subscription.deleted" webhook fires
  Then the tenant's billing_status updates to "canceled"
  And all agents are disabled
  And OAuth tokens for integrations are revoked
  And the AI SDK spending limit is removed
  And tenant data is retained for 90 days (compliance), then hard-deleted
  And an audit log records the offboarding completion

K&B Design Generation (AI Loop)

Gherkin
Feature: Kitchen & Bath AI Design Generation

Scenario: Successful design generation
  Given a project with room dimensions (12x10 kitchen), client preferences, and NKBA constraints
  When the user triggers "Generate Design"
  Then the Design Layout Agent initiates the AI loop:
    Step 1: Claude generates a layout proposal (cabinet placement, appliance positions)
    Step 2: Z3 constraint solver validates NKBA compliance (clearances, work triangle, ADA)
    Step 3: If constraints violated, Claude receives violations and regenerates
    Step 4: Loop continues until constraints pass (max 5 iterations)
    Step 5: Gemini renders the validated layout into a photorealistic preview
  And the final design is stored with all iterations logged
  And the Catalog Match Agent matches specified items to manufacturer catalog via pgvector RAG
  And a BOM (Bill of Materials) is generated for the Sales Agent

Scenario: Constraint solver rejects all iterations
  Given a room layout that cannot satisfy NKBA constraints after 5 iterations
  When the max iteration limit is reached
  Then the best-effort layout is presented with constraint violations highlighted
  And an escalation is created for the designer to manually adjust
  And the specific violations are listed (e.g., "Sink-to-stove clearance: 28in, required: 36in")

Specification Contradiction Resolutions

Two schema conflicts identified during the DDL formalization process, resolved with rationale.

notification_preferences Schema Conflict (Resolved)

Resolution: Adopt Section 13 Schema (Denormalized Columns-per-Channel)

Conflict: Section 5 defined notification_preferences with a normalized row-per-channel model (user_id, category, channel, enabled), while Section 13 used a denormalized columns-per-channel model (user_id, notification_type, in_app, email, push, digest_frequency).

Resolution: Adopt the Section 13 schema. Rationale: (1) Fewer rows (1 per user per type vs 3), (2) Single query returns all channel preferences, (3) Easier application code, (4) digest_frequency column needed for batched email digests, (5) Matches better-auth notification patterns.

ORM Recommendation: Drizzle ORM

Recommendation: Drizzle ORM (ADR Candidate)

Rationale: (1) First-party better-auth adapter, (2) Type-safe queries without codegen, (3) SQL-first philosophy — critical for RLS debugging, (4) Plain SQL migrations compatible with Expand & Contract pattern, (5) Native pgvector column support, (6) ~7x smaller bundle than Prisma, (7) No query engine overhead.

Integration: drizzle-kit introspect generates schema from existing Supabase database. withTenantContext() wrapper pattern preserved via Drizzle transactions with set_config('app.tenant_id', tenantId, true).

12. Infrastructure, Security & DevOps

Production-readiness requirements derived from Google SRE Launch Checklist, OWASP Multi-Tenant Security Cheat Sheet, and AWS Well-Architected SaaS Lens.

CI/CD Pipeline (Wave 1, Day 1)

  • Every PR: Lint (ESLint) → type-check (tsc) → unit tests (Vitest) → build
  • Merge to main: Integration tests → deploy to staging → smoke tests → deploy to production
  • Frontend: Vercel auto-deploys from main (preview deploys on PRs for free)
  • Backend: GitHub Actions SSH deploy to both VPS nodes (secondary first, then primary), docker compose pull && docker compose up -d
  • Rollback discipline: During the deploy step, after pulling and starting the new image, explicitly tag the outgoing image as ‘previous’ and do NOT prune it. Both the current and previous image tags must be resident on each VPS node at all times. Rollback command: IMAGE_TAG=previous docker compose up -d --force-recreate. Verification: after rollback, confirm docker compose ps shows all services healthy and curl -f http://localhost:3000/health/live returns 200 within 30 seconds. Rollback SLA: under 90 seconds (matching LB failover window). This requires images to be pre-pulled — pulling a new image at rollback time adds 2–5 minutes, which is unacceptable during an incident.
  • Branch protection on main: require passing checks before merge
  • All third-party actions pinned to full SHA. Workflow-level permissions: {} with per-job scoping. persist-credentials: false on checkout.
  • Dependency management: Configure Renovate (preferred over Dependabot for monorepo support) as a Day 1 GitHub Actions item. Configuration: weekly minor/patch PRs grouped by layer (frontend, backend, infra), semantic versioning constraint pinning for known breaking-change packages (Drizzle, AI SDK, better-auth), auto-merge for patch updates that pass CI. For AI SDK specifically: all AI SDK usage must be encapsulated behind a thin adapter layer (src/lib/ai/) so version upgrades only require changing the adapter, not every tool definition and agent file across the codebase.

CI Quality Gates (All Enforced — PRs Blocked on Failure)

  1. ESLint: Zero errors (warnings allowed but tracked). Custom rules: no-restricted-properties for c.notFound(), no-restricted-syntax for enum, no-restricted-imports for barrel exports.
  2. TypeScript: tsc --build --noEmit with zero errors.
  3. Vitest: All tests pass, 80% line coverage minimum.
  4. Migration compatibility: Custom CI check that diffs the previous and current migration files against the running code to detect column removal/rename that would break the previous code version during rolling deploy.
  5. CI time target: Under 8 minutes for the full pipeline (lint + typecheck + test + build). Use Turborepo remote caching to achieve this.

Dual-VPS Architecture (Day 1)

Two identical DigitalOcean CPU-Optimized droplets (4vCPU/8GB, $80/mo each = $160/mo total) behind a DigitalOcean Load Balancer ($12/mo). Intentionally over-engineered for reliability from Day 1.

  • Primary VPS: Runs the full Docker Compose stack (Hono backend, NATS). Valkey is DigitalOcean Managed (external service, not in Docker Compose). BullMQ is a library within the Hono backend, not a separate service
  • Secondary VPS: Hybrid active-active for HTTP request handling and event processing. Both nodes serve HTTP traffic simultaneously via the Load Balancer. The secondary handles API requests, runs BullMQ workers (coordinated via managed Valkey HA), and participates as a full NATS Raft cluster member. Shared state coordination via external managed Valkey HA (DigitalOcean Managed Valkey HA — mandated, not optional) and NATS 3-node Raft cluster. Receives the same Docker image deployments. Health checks every 30 seconds.
  • JetStream HA (Day 1): A 3rd NATS node ($5–10/mo lightweight VPS) is deployed from Day 1 to form a 3-node Raft quorum, enabling JetStream HA. The secondary VPS connects as a full cluster member (not a leaf node). If VPS-1 dies, the remaining 2 nodes maintain quorum and JetStream processing continues — event-driven agent execution is NOT interrupted. The 3rd node runs only NATS (no application workload), keeping cost minimal. This eliminates the event bus SPOF that existed in the original 2-node leaf-node topology.
  • Load Balancer: DigitalOcean managed LB distributes traffic. If primary fails health check (3 consecutive failures = 90 seconds), traffic automatically routes to secondary.
  • Shared State: PostgreSQL (Supabase) is fully external. Valkey runs as Managed Valkey HA (automatic failover replica, $30–$45/mo) — not per-node. Socket.io uses @socket.io/redis-adapter for cross-node event delivery. NATS runs as a 3-node Raft cluster (VPS-1, VPS-2, and a dedicated lightweight 3rd node) providing JetStream HA from Day 1. State divergence risk is minimal — Raft consensus ensures JetStream durability across node failures.
  • Cost: $160/mo (2x VPS) + $12/mo (LB) = $172/mo. Only $92/mo more than single VPS. Insurance against total platform outage for all customers.
  • Failure Modes: If primary VPS fails, the secondary continues serving HTTP traffic via the Load Balancer (90-second health check failover). NATS JetStream continues operating via the 3-node Raft cluster — the remaining 2 nodes maintain quorum and event-driven agent execution is NOT interrupted. Valkey HA (managed with automatic failover replica) provides improved availability over a single instance — automatic promotion of the replica on primary failure. During a rare Valkey double-failure, BullMQ job processing, Socket.io cross-node delivery, and distributed cache are all degraded. Agent execution designed to be re-triggerable from BullMQ job state. Note: With @socket.io/redis-adapter, Socket.io cross-node delivery is NOT affected by NATS failure — real-time dashboard updates continue via Valkey pub/sub independently. Database failure (Supabase) is the remaining SPOF — mitigated by Supabase’s managed HA infrastructure + pg_dump backups every 6 hours to DigitalOcean Spaces (6-hour RPO).

Docker Compose Hardening Requirements (Wave 1)

Production Docker Compose configuration must include the following for all services. Without these, the 8GB droplet is vulnerable to disk exhaustion, OOM kills, and silent container failures.

  • Log limits (INFRA-01, HIGH): Every service MUST have logging: { driver: "json-file", options: { max-size: "10m", max-file: "3" } }. Without log rotation, a verbose service fills the 8GB droplet disk and takes down all services.
  • Memory limits (INFRA-02, HIGH): Every service MUST set deploy.resources.limits.memory. Node.js containers (Hono backend) MUST also set --max-old-space-size to 75% of the container memory limit (e.g., 1536m for a 2GB container). Without explicit limits, OOM kills are silent and undiagnosable.
  • Health check conditions (INFRA-03, HIGH): All depends_on declarations MUST use condition: service_healthy, not just container start. Without health check conditions, the Hono backend starts before NATS is actually ready to accept connections, causing startup race conditions.
  • Named volume ownership (INFRA-04, MED): Docker named volumes are created with root ownership (Docker bug #3270). Non-root containers (see INFRA-05) need an explicit chown in the entrypoint script to fix volume permissions on first run.
  • Dockerfile requirements (INFRA-05, MED): All Dockerfiles MUST use: (a) multi-stage builds (builder + production stages to minimize image size), (b) USER node (non-root execution), (c) tini or dumb-init as PID 1 init process for proper signal handling. Without an init process, docker stop sends SIGTERM but Node.js does not forward it to child processes — after 10s Docker sends SIGKILL, which can cause BullMQ data loss (in-progress jobs marked as stalled instead of gracefully completed).
  • Restart policy (INFRA-06, MED): All services MUST have restart: unless-stopped. Without a restart policy, a crashed container (e.g., NATS OOM) stays down permanently until manual intervention.
  • Docker network isolation (ARCH-01, HIGH): Production Compose files must define named networks with tier segmentation. Create three networks: kiptra-frontend (LB → Hono API only), kiptra-backend (Hono → Valkey, NATS, Postgres — internal: true, not externally reachable), and kiptra-monitoring (Prometheus, Grafana, Alloy — isolated from application traffic). Without network segmentation, a compromised frontend container can directly query Valkey or the database. Each service should only be attached to the networks it needs.

Python Services Infrastructure (K&B Design Tool — Section 16)

Infrastructure Gap: Python Services Not in Section 12 (ADR-INFRA-001)

Section 16 specifies three Python dependencies (OR-Tools CP-SAT, ezdxf, IfcOpenShell) and one ML dependency (sentence-transformers) that run server-side. Section 12 does not account for these in the Docker Compose stack, memory budget, or network topology. This addendum closes that gap.

Timing: The Python service is a Phase 2 addition (Month 10–16, K&B Design Tool build). It does NOT affect Wave 1–4 infrastructure. The Day 1 Docker Compose stack (Hono + NATS) is unchanged.

1. Docker Container Specification

Dockerfile
# services/kb-python/Dockerfile
# --- Stage 1: Builder ---
FROM python:3.12-slim-bookworm AS builder

RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    build-essential \
    libgeos-dev \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --prefix=/install -r requirements.txt

# --- Stage 2: Production ---
FROM python:3.12-slim-bookworm AS production

# Install tini for PID 1 signal handling (INFRA-05 compliance)
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    tini \
    libgeos-dev \
    curl \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/* \
    && groupadd -r appuser && useradd -r -g appuser appuser

COPY --from=builder /install /usr/local
WORKDIR /app
COPY . .

# Non-root execution (INFRA-05 compliance)
USER appuser

EXPOSE 8100

ENTRYPOINT ["tini", "--"]
CMD ["uvicorn", "main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8100", "--workers", "2"]

Base image: python:3.12-slim-bookworm (~150 MB base). Multi-stage build strips build tools. Final image ~400–500 MB (dominated by OR-Tools ~180 MB and sentence-transformers model ~90 MB).

Python dependencies (requirements.txt):

requirements.txt
fastapi==0.115.*
uvicorn[standard]==0.34.*
ortools==9.11.*
ezdxf==1.4.*
ifcopenshell==0.8.*
sentence-transformers==3.4.*
pydantic==2.10.*
httpx==0.28.*

Notes:

  • sentence-transformers model: The all-MiniLM-L6-v2 model (~90 MB) is downloaded at build time via a Dockerfile RUN step (not at runtime). This ensures deterministic builds and avoids Hugging Face download failures in production.
  • OR-Tools CP-SAT: Pure Python package, no native compilation needed at runtime. The solver has a 2 GB protobuf model size limit, which is far above K&B BOM optimization needs (typical models are <1 MB).
  • IfcOpenShell: Requires libgeos-dev at runtime for geometry operations.

2. Docker Compose Entry

YAML
# Add to docker-compose.yml under 'services:'
# Phase 2 only -- not present in Wave 1-4 deployments

  kb-python:
    build:
      context: ./services/kb-python
      dockerfile: Dockerfile
    image: ghcr.io/kiptra-ai/kb-python:${GIT_SHA:-latest}
    restart: unless-stopped
    networks:
      - kiptra-backend
    expose:
      - "8100"
    # NOT in 'ports:' -- internal service only, not exposed to host (ARCH-01)
    environment:
      - DATABASE_URL=${DATABASE_URL}
      - EMBEDDING_MODEL=all-MiniLM-L6-v2
      - LOG_LEVEL=info
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          memory: 768M
        reservations:
          memory: 384M
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:8100/health"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 10s
      retries: 3
      start_period: 30s
    logging:
      driver: "json-file"
      options:
        max-size: "10m"
        max-file: "3"
    depends_on:
      hono-backend:
        condition: service_healthy

Compliance with Section 12 hardening requirements:

  • INFRA-01 (log limits): JSON file driver with 10m/3 files.
  • INFRA-02 (memory limits): 768 MB hard limit. Uvicorn workers configured to stay within budget (2 workers x ~200 MB each + model loaded once via fork = ~500 MB typical).
  • INFRA-03 (health check): depends_on with condition: service_healthy.
  • INFRA-05 (non-root + tini): Runs as appuser, tini as PID 1.
  • INFRA-06 (restart policy): restart: unless-stopped.
  • ARCH-01 (network isolation): Connected to kiptra-backend only. No ports: mapping — not reachable from outside Docker network.

3. API Contract (Internal HTTP — REST)

Protocol: Internal HTTP (REST over JSON). Not gRPC. Rationale: the K&B Python service handles 3 low-frequency endpoints (optimization runs are <10/min even at scale). gRPC adds protobuf compilation to the build pipeline and a Go/Node gRPC client dependency for negligible latency benefit at this call volume. Internal HTTP on the Docker network has sub-millisecond overhead.

API Contract
## Base URL (internal): http://kb-python:8100
## All endpoints require X-Tenant-Id header for audit logging

POST /api/v1/optimize
  Purpose:  OR-Tools CP-SAT BOM optimization
  Request:  { budget: number, items: [{ sku, price, score, constraints }], rules: [] }
  Response: { selected: [{ sku, quantity, price }], total_cost: number, solve_time_ms: number }
  Timeout:  5s (CP-SAT solves K&B problems in 200ms–2s)

POST /api/v1/export/dxf
  Purpose:  Generate DXF file from scene graph
  Request:  { scene_data: JSONB, options: { scale, layers } }
  Response: Binary DXF file (application/octet-stream)
  Timeout:  15s

POST /api/v1/export/ifc
  Purpose:  Generate IFC file from scene graph
  Request:  { scene_data: JSONB, options: { schema_version } }
  Response: Binary IFC file (application/octet-stream)
  Timeout:  15s

POST /api/v1/embed
  Purpose:  Generate embeddings for catalog products (batch)
  Request:  { texts: string[] }  (max 100 per batch)
  Response: { embeddings: number[][] }  (384-dim vectors)
  Timeout:  10s

GET  /health
  Purpose:  Liveness + readiness (combined — single-purpose service, no external deps to check)
  Response: { status: "healthy", version: string, uptime_seconds: number }

Hono backend integration: The Hono backend calls the Python service via fetch('http://kb-python:8100/api/v1/optimize', ...) using Node.js native fetch. The kb-python hostname resolves via Docker DNS on the kiptra-backend network. A thin KbPythonClient class in the Hono backend wraps these calls with timeout handling, retry (1 retry on 503), and structured error mapping.

4. Health Check

Python
# FastAPI health endpoint in main.py
import time
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI()
_start_time = time.time()

@app.get("/health")
async def health():
    return {
        "status": "healthy",
        "version": "1.0.0",
        "uptime_seconds": round(time.time() - _start_time, 1)
    }

Docker HEALTHCHECK (defined in Compose above): curl -f http://localhost:8100/health every 30s. Three consecutive failures = unhealthy. The 30-second start period allows Uvicorn workers to load the sentence-transformers model before health checks begin.

Monitoring integration: The Hono backend’s /health/ready endpoint (MON-03) should add a check for kb-python reachability when the K&B module is enabled. If kb-python is unreachable, /health/ready still returns 200 (K&B is a feature module, not core platform) but includes "kb_python": "unavailable" in the response body for observability.

5. Memory Budget Update

Updated Memory Budget — 8 GB Droplet (Post-K&B Addition)

Section 12 (MON-05) allocates ~1.5 GB to the monitoring stack and ~6.5 GB to application services. Adding the kb-python service requires revising this allocation.

ServiceBefore K&BAfter K&BNotes
OS + Docker overhead~500 MB~500 MBKernel, systemd, Docker daemon
Monitoring stack~1,500 MB~1,300 MBPrometheus (512 MB) + Loki (384 MB) + Grafana (256 MB) + Alloy (128 MB) — enforced via Docker deploy.resources.limits.memory. Reduce Loki chunk cache from 512 MB to 256 MB when K&B active.
Hono backend (Node.js)~2,048 MB~1,792 MBReduce --max-old-space-size from 1536m to 1280m. Still 75% of container limit.
NATS JetStream~512 MB~512 MBUnchanged — max_mem in NATS config
kb-python (NEW)—~768 MBOR-Tools + ezdxf + IfcOpenShell + sentence-transformers model
Headroom~3,632 MB~1,320 MBComfortable headroom. 8GB→16GB upgrade recommended before K&B activation for production safety margin.

Total after K&B: ~6,872 MB / 8,192 MB (84% allocated).

Manageable but worth monitoring. The 1,320 MB headroom is adequate for initial K&B rollout. Two mitigations for scale:

  1. Lazy loading (recommended for Phase 1): The kb-python service starts with profiles: ["kb"] in Docker Compose, meaning it only runs on VPS nodes where the K&B feature is activated. For Phase 1 (1–50 customers), if K&B adoption is <20% of tenants, run kb-python on the secondary VPS only, leaving the primary with full headroom for core platform services.
  2. Upgrade trigger (MON-05 update): When K&B Design Tool ships (Month 10+), upgrade both VPS nodes from 4vCPU/8GB ($80/mo each) to 8vCPU/16GB ($160/mo each). Total cost goes from $172/mo to $332/mo. This should be planned as a line item in the K&B Design Tool budget, not treated as a surprise infrastructure cost.

Alternative: Offload embeddings. The sentence-transformers model (~200 MB RAM at runtime) is the largest single consumer. If memory pressure is critical, replace local embeddings with Supabase’s built-in pgvector + pg_embedding extension or an external embedding API (OpenAI text-embedding-3-small at $0.02/1M tokens). This reduces kb-python memory to ~512 MB but adds per-request cost and external dependency.

Docker Compose Profile (Lazy Loading)

YAML
# Updated kb-python entry with profile for conditional activation

  kb-python:
    profiles:
      - kb
    # ... rest of service definition as above ...

# Start without K&B: docker compose up -d
# Start with K&B:    docker compose --profile kb up -d

Network Topology Update

Add kb-python to the kiptra-backend network (ARCH-01). The service connects ONLY to the backend network — it is never reachable from the frontend network or the public internet. The Hono backend routes requests to it via Docker DNS (http://kb-python:8100). No new firewall rules needed — the service has no host port mapping.

Diagram
                  +---------------------------------------------+
                  |  kiptra-backend network (internal: true)       |
                  |                                              |
   LB --> Hono ---|---> NATS                                     |
                  |---> kb-python:8100 (NEW)                     |
                  |    |-- POST /api/v1/optimize  (OR-Tools)     |
                  |    |-- POST /api/v1/export/*  (ezdxf/IFC)    |
                  |    +-- POST /api/v1/embed     (sentence-tx)  |
                  +---------------------------------------------+

DigitalOcean Platform Notes

  • Managed Valkey version cap (DO-03, MED): DigitalOcean Managed Valkey replaces discontinued Managed Redis (June 2025). Wire-compatible with Redis; BullMQ and Socket.io adapters work unchanged. Plan accordingly — verify feature parity for any Redis 7.4+ features relied upon.
  • Real client IP behind Cloudflare (DO-04, MED): Use the CF-Connecting-IP header for real client IP in rate limiting and audit logs. X-Forwarded-For returns the Cloudflare edge IP, not the client IP, when traffic flows through Cloudflare.
  • Proxy Protocol conflict (DO-05, MED): Proxy Protocol MUST NOT be enabled on both Cloudflare and the DigitalOcean Load Balancer simultaneously. Enable it on one or the other — double-wrapping corrupts the connection headers.

Rolling Deployment — Sequential Node Update (Wave 2)

Deploy to secondary VPS first, verify health, then deploy to primary. This is a rolling deployment, not true blue-green (which requires two complete, identically-sized environments with atomic traffic switching). Constraint: All database migrations must be backward-compatible with the previous code version, since both versions run simultaneously during the rollout window. A migration compatibility check in CI blocks deploys if a migration removes or renames columns still referenced by the previous code version.

  • Deploy sequence: secondary VPS first → verify health → primary VPS. Near-zero-downtime (brief period of mixed versions).
  • Health check endpoints (MON-03) split into three to prevent restart loops when a dependency is briefly unreachable: (a) /health/live — process liveness only, never checks external dependencies (used by Docker HEALTHCHECK and LB liveness probe), (b) /health/ready — checks database connected, NATS connected, Valkey connected, Socket.io Valkey adapter connected (used by LB readiness probe — returns 503 until all dependencies are reachable), (c) /health/startup — one-time initialization check (schema migrations applied, NATS subjects created, BullMQ workers registered). A single /health endpoint that checks all dependencies causes restart loops when a dependency is briefly unreachable — the LB marks the node unhealthy even though the process is fine.
  • If errors spike on newly deployed node, Load Balancer automatically removes it from rotation within 90 seconds.
  • WebSocket connections: existing connections drain naturally (30-second grace period). New connections route to healthy nodes.
  • Deployment frequency: multiple times per day if needed. No maintenance windows.
  • Graceful shutdown (SIGTERM handler): SIGTERM handler sequence (order is critical): (1) worker.close() — stops accepting new BullMQ jobs and waits for in-progress jobs to complete (up to lockDuration). (2) io.close() — sends disconnect to all Socket.io clients with 30-second drain. (3) Track in-flight SSE streams and send close events to trigger client reconnection to the surviving node. (4) server.close() — stops accepting new HTTP connections, drains existing ones. (5) process.exit(0). The BullMQ lockDuration must be >= 120,000ms (matching the longest LLM timeout) so in-progress jobs are not marked stalled during shutdown. This is a required Wave 2 implementation item, not an assumed behavior of the DigitalOcean Load Balancer.
  • SSE stream drain: On SIGTERM, track all in-flight SSE streams (agent execution streams). Send a stream close event with a retry directive (retry: 5000) so clients automatically reconnect to the surviving node. If an LLM inference is mid-flight, the onStepFinish callback should check a shutdown flag and gracefully complete the current step before yielding. Do not kill active LLM calls — wait for the current step to finish, then close the stream.
  • Deploy-safe webhook handling: Configure the DO Load Balancer to remove a node from rotation before deploying to it (pre-drain), not after. SendGrid retries failed webhooks for up to 72 hours, then permanently drops the email. A deploy window where the webhook endpoint returns 5xx causes lead email loss. Additionally: set up a SendGrid webhook alert — if no inbound emails are processed for a tenant that normally receives 5+/day, page on-call. Document MX propagation delay (up to 48 hours) in the onboarding flow with a ‘your email setup is pending DNS propagation’ status indicator.
  • Code canary deployment: Before promoting a deploy to both VPS nodes, deploy new code to secondary only and configure the DO Load Balancer to route 10% of traffic to secondary for a 15-minute observation window. Monitor error rate, P99 latency, and BullMQ stalled job count during the canary window. If any metric exceeds threshold, roll back secondary to the previous image. If metrics are clean, promote to primary. This is separate from the prompt canary (10% of tenants) — code canary validates infrastructure changes, prompt canary validates AI behavior changes.

Docker Image Registry

Docker image registry: GitHub Container Registry (ghcr.io). Image tagging: every merge to main produces an image tagged with the git SHA (ghcr.io/kiptra-ai/backend:abc123f). The deploy script also tags the image as ‘latest’. On deploy, the outgoing image is re-tagged as ‘previous’. Both ‘latest’ and ‘previous’ tags are always resident on each VPS node. Image pruning policy: keep the 5 most recent images per VPS node.

Testing Strategy (Phased)

Test TypeWhenWhat to TestTool
UnitWave 1, Day 1Business logic, billing calculations, agent routing, RLS policiesVitest
IntegrationWave 2API endpoints, webhook handlers, Supabase RLS adversarial queriesVitest + Supertest
E2EWave 3Critical flows only: login, create agent, approve escalation, view dashboardPlaywright
LoadWave 410/50/100/500 concurrent users, agent execution under loadk6

Testing Conventions

  1. Coverage threshold: 80% line coverage enforced in CI via Vitest coverage reporter. PRs that decrease coverage below 80% are blocked.
  2. AI SDK mocking: Use Vercel AI SDK’s MockLanguageModelV1 and simulateReadableStream test utilities for deterministic agent logic tests without burning real API tokens. Mock AI responses at the AIGateway adapter layer.
  3. Webhook testing: Stripe CLI (stripe listen) for local webhook replay. SendGrid Inbound Parse: fixture multipart/form-data payloads stored in test/fixtures/sendgrid/ for replay via supertest.
  4. Multi-tenant isolation tests: Every integration test creates 2 test tenants and verifies cross-tenant data isolation via RLS adversarial queries.
  5. Agent execution tests: Deterministic tool call sequences with mocked external APIs (JobTread, QuickBooks fixtures). Verify step count, token budget, and escalation triggers.

Security Hardening (Wave 1 — Non-Negotiable)

  • Headers: HSTS, CSP, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options — built into Hono middleware
  • Auth: Rate limiting on all auth endpoints (5–10 req/min/IP), account lockout after 5 failed attempts, MFA via TOTP
  • CORS: Explicit allowlist (not wildcard) — only Vercel frontend domain
  • Tenant isolation: Never trust client-supplied tenant IDs — derive from auth token server-side
  • Input validation: Zod schemas on all API endpoints, parameterized queries everywhere
  • Secrets: Environment variables only (never in code), Vercel env vars for frontend, Docker secrets for both VPS nodes
  • WAF: Cloudflare Pro ($20/mo) in front of dual VPS (via DO Load Balancer) — DDoS protection, bot filtering, SSL termination. Page Rules deprecated (CF-03, MED): Cloudflare Page Rules are deprecated as of 2025. Use Cache Rules + Configuration Rules instead for all URL-based routing and caching configuration. WebSocket keepalive (CF-04, MED): Socket.io pingInterval must be ≤30s to stay within Cloudflare's 100s idle timeout for WebSocket connections. This is separate from the SSE keepalive interval.
  • Rate limiting scope: Auth endpoints = 5–10 req/min/IP. Agent execution endpoints = 10 req/min/tenant. Escalation approval = 30 req/min/user. All authenticated endpoints = 100 req/min/user global cap. Implemented via Hono middleware using Valkey-backed sliding window rate limiter.
  • Audit logging: Every data access logged with tenant_id, user_id, timestamp, action — SOC 2 readiness from Day 1
  • Network segmentation: NATS and Valkey ports are NOT exposed to the host interface in Docker Compose (use expose: not ports: for internal services). DigitalOcean Cloud Firewall: only ports 80 and 443 open to Cloudflare IP ranges. All other ports closed. VPS-to-VPS communication uses DigitalOcean’s private networking (VPC). Wave 1 security checklist: verify nmap -p 4222,6222,8222,6379,8080 <vps-public-ip> returns closed/filtered for all non-web ports.
  • (ARCH-01) Docker network isolation (production): Docker Compose must define named networks with segmentation: (1) frontend network: Load Balancer → Hono API containers only; (2) backend network: Hono → Valkey, NATS, Postgres (internal services); (3) monitoring network: Prometheus, Loki, Grafana (isolated from application traffic). Without named network segmentation, all containers share the default bridge network — a compromised frontend container can directly query Valkey and Postgres. Each service connects only to the networks it requires (e.g., Hono connects to both frontend and backend; Valkey connects only to backend).
  • Cloudflare SSL: Mode = Full (Strict) — mandatory. Install Cloudflare Origin CA certificates (not Let’s Encrypt) on both VPS nodes and the DigitalOcean Load Balancer. (CF-02): Let’s Encrypt renewal can fail behind Cloudflare proxy (DNS challenge required). Cloudflare Origin CA certs have 15-year validity and are purpose-built for this topology. Enable “Always Use HTTPS” and “Minimum TLS Version: 1.2”. Wave 4 security audit must verify Cloudflare SSL mode is Full (Strict), not Flexible.
  • Secret rotation: Application implements a SIGHUP handler that re-reads secrets from Docker secrets files without restarting. For immediate rotation (compromise): update Docker secret → rolling restart (secondary first, then primary). Annual rotation schedule for all provider API keys, documented in incident response plan.

Alerting Thresholds (Wave 1)

  • UptimeRobot (Day 1, free tier): External uptime monitoring for https://api.kiptra.io/health/live and https://app.kiptra.io. 5-minute check interval. SMS + Slack on downtime. Provides external validation independent of self-hosted monitoring (catches scenarios where Prometheus itself is down).
  • API P99 latency > 2s for 5 minutes → Slack alert
  • Error rate > 1% over 5 minutes → SMS to on-call founder
  • Supabase connectivity: 3 consecutive health check failures (90 seconds) → SMS alert. Aligned with LB health check threshold to prevent alert fatigue from transient connection blips
  • AI Gateway P95 > 5s → Slack alert (managed service, no restart needed)
  • BullMQ dead letter queue depth > 10 → Slack alert
  • Valkey memory policy (MON-01, HIGH): DigitalOcean Managed Valkey MUST be configured with maxmemory-policy noeviction. With any eviction policy (e.g., allkeys-lru), BullMQ jobs silently disappear under memory pressure — no error, no DLQ entry, just lost work. Monitor Valkey memory usage and alert at 80% capacity.
  • On-call rotation: Joseph and TJ alternate weeks. Escalation: if no acknowledgment within 15 minutes, page both founders.
  • BullMQ custom metrics (MON-04, MED): Export custom Prometheus metrics for BullMQ queue health: bullmq_jobs_waiting, bullmq_jobs_active, bullmq_jobs_failed, bullmq_stalled. These are not available out-of-the-box — implement via BullMQ's Queue.getJobCounts() exposed on a /metrics endpoint scraped by Prometheus.
  • Monitoring stack memory budget (MON-05, MED): The self-hosted monitoring stack (Prometheus + Grafana + Loki + Grafana Alloy) consumes 600MB–2GB RAM on the 8GB droplet. This MUST be accounted for when setting container memory limits (INFRA-02). Budget: ~1.5GB for monitoring, leaving ~6.5GB for application services (Hono backend, NATS).

Prompt Injection Defense (Wave 2 — Critical)

Three-layer defense against prompt injection attacks. Agents process inbound emails, webhooks, and user input — all are untrusted attack surfaces.

Layer 1 — Architectural Defense + Input Sanitization

  • Primary defense is architectural, not classifier-based: (a) User input is never concatenated into system prompts — a hard structural separator (<user_input> tag) marks untrusted content. (b) Vercel AI SDK’s system parameter enforces separation at the framework level. (c) Agent tool permissions are statically defined — injected prompts cannot invoke tools not in the agent’s registered tool set.
  • All external inputs (inbound emails via SendGrid, webhook payloads, user-submitted text) are sanitized before reaching agent prompts
  • Strip control characters, limit input length, detect known injection patterns (e.g., “ignore previous instructions”, “system prompt override”)
  • The Haiku classifier (Haiku 4.5, cost: negligible) provides a secondary probabilistic defense layer — scores injection probability before routing to agent — but is not the primary gate.

Layer 2 — Prompt Architecture

  • System prompts use delimiter-based separation (XML tags) between instructions and user content
  • Agent instructions are marked as immutable; user content is explicitly sandboxed
  • Agents cannot modify their own system prompts or access other agents’ prompts
  • Tool-use permissions are statically defined per agent — an injected prompt cannot grant new tool access

Layer 3 — Output Validation

  • All agent outputs pass through a validation layer before action execution
  • Structured outputs validated against JSON schemas (Zod) — malformed responses are rejected
  • Actions with side effects (sending emails, creating records, modifying data) require schema-conformant output AND human approval for client-facing actions
  • Anomaly detection: if an agent’s output is statistically different from its historical pattern (token count, action type, referenced entities), it is flagged for review

AI Output Validation Pipeline (Wave 2)

Every agent output passes through a validation pipeline before execution:

AgentResult includes full execution trace: { finalOutput: T, toolCallHistory: ToolCall[], modelUsage: ModelUsageByStep[], escalationsTriggered: Escalation[] }. The validation pipeline inspects toolCallHistory for anomalous patterns (unexpected tool calls, out-of-scope invocations), not just the final output.

  1. Schema validation — output must conform to the agent’s defined output schema (Zod). Malformed JSON, missing required fields, or unexpected field types → reject and retry (max 2 retries)
  2. Boundary checks — after 30 days of tenant operation, statistical bounds (mean ± 3 standard deviations) are computed from the tenant’s own historical data per project type. For new tenants, platform-wide quartiles serve as initial reference, tightening as customer-specific data accumulates. Computed by a nightly background job per tenant. An estimate of $0 or $10M for a kitchen remodel → flagged as anomalous
  3. Action authorization — outputs requesting side effects (send email, create record, modify schedule) checked against the agent’s permission set. Actions outside scope → reject
  4. Consistency check — for financial agents (Sales Agent, Bookkeeping Agent), outputs cross-referenced against source data. If the AI claims an invoice is overdue but QuickBooks shows it’s paid → flagged
  5. Rate limiting — rate limits apply to side-effecting actions only (email sends, record creates, data modifications) — not read-only tool calls. Tier-specific: Starter = 50 side-effecting actions/hour, Professional = 200/hour, Enterprise = 500/hour
  6. Loop prevention — stopWhen: stepCountIs(N) set per agent type (Leads Agent = stepCountIs(7), Sales Agent = stepCountIs(17), CEO Agent = stepCountIs(12)) via Vercel AI SDK. Note: Step budgets are +2 vs. v5 values because AI SDK v6 consumes a step when generating structured output. Total token budget per execution as secondary guard. This is more permissive for legitimate multi-tool workflows and more robust against true loops than a consecutive-call heuristic.

Tool Interface Definitions (Top 5 Critical Tools)

Concrete interface sketches for the most critical agent tools. All tools enforce tenant context via withTenantContext() and validate inputSchema with Zod schemas (v6 renamed parameters to inputSchema).

TypeBox Tool Schema Migration (AI-27 — Wave 3a)

When implementing the first agent tools in Wave 3a, adopt TypeBox (@sinclair/typebox) alongside Zod for tool parameter definitions. TypeBox produces compile-time TypeScript types AND runtime JSON Schema from a single source — eliminating the Zod-to-JSON-Schema conversion step that Vercel AI SDK performs internally. Tools registered via AgentRegistrationAPI.registerTool() (Section 6) use AgentToolDefinition<TParams extends TSchema> with TypeBox schemas.

Dual-schema approach: TypeBox for tool parameters (input to the LLM — needs JSON Schema), Zod for tool outputSchema (output validation in the AI Output Validation Pipeline — needs runtime parsing). This avoids forcing a single schema library where each has a natural fit.

promptGuidelines on tools: Each AgentToolDefinition declares an optional promptGuidelines: string[] array. These guidelines are automatically injected into the agent’s system prompt when the tool is active, making tools self-documenting to the LLM. Example: the query_jobtread_projects tool includes ["Always filter by status when the user asks about active projects", "Limit results to 50 unless explicitly asked for more"]. This pattern ensures tool-specific LLM guidance stays co-located with the tool definition rather than buried in monolithic system prompts.

TypeScript
// 1. Query integration data
tool('query_jobtread_projects', {
  inputSchema: z.object({
    tenantId: z.string().uuid(),
    status: z.enum(['active', 'completed', 'all']),
    limit: z.number().max(50)
  }),
  returns: z.array(ProjectSchema)
})

// 2. Create estimate draft
tool('create_estimate_draft', {
  inputSchema: z.object({
    projectId: z.string().uuid(),
    lineItems: z.array(EstimateLineSchema),
    notes: z.string().optional()
  }),
  returns: z.object({ draftId: z.string().uuid(), totalAmount: z.number() })
})

// 3. Send email draft (requires approval)
tool('draft_email', {
  inputSchema: z.object({
    to: z.string().email(),
    subject: z.string(),
    body: z.string(),
    escalate: z.literal(true)
  }),
  returns: z.object({ escalationId: z.string().uuid() })
})

// 4. Query financial summary
tool('query_quickbooks_summary', {
  inputSchema: z.object({
    tenantId: z.string().uuid(),
    dateRange: z.object({ start: z.string(), end: z.string() })
  }),
  returns: FinancialSummarySchema
})

// 5. Check schedule availability
tool('check_calendar_availability', {
  inputSchema: z.object({
    tenantId: z.string().uuid(),
    dateRange: z.object({ start: z.string(), end: z.string() }),
    resourceIds: z.array(z.string()).optional()
  }),
  returns: z.array(AvailabilitySlotSchema)
})

Agent-Tool Permission Matrix

Each agent is restricted to its authorized tool set. “Read-only” means the tool is available but parameterized to prevent mutations. All tools enforce tenant context via withTenantContext().

ToolLeads AgentSales AgentBookkeeping AgentProject Management AgentCEO AgentDesign AgentSupport Agent
query_jobtread_projects✓✓✗✓✓✓Read-only
create_estimate_draft✗✓✗✗✗✗✗
draft_email✓✗✓✓✓✗✗
query_quickbooks_summary✗✓✓✗✓✗Read-only
check_calendar_availability✓✗✗✓✓✗✗
query_execution_logs✗✗✗✗✓✗✓
query_lead_pipeline✓✗✗✗✓✗✗
search_knowledge_base✗✗✗✗✗✗✓
escalate_to_human✗✗✗✗✗✗✓
check_account_status✗✗✗✗✗✗✓
check_integration_health✗✗✗✗✗✗✓

Enforcement (two layers): (1) Static: Tools registered via AgentRegistrationAPI.registerTool() are validated against the permission matrix at registration time. Any tool call not in the agent’s registered tool set is rejected before execution and logged as an anomalous invocation (see AI Output Validation Pipeline step 3). (2) Dynamic: The before_tool_call event handler (Section 6, factory pattern) enables runtime permission gating — agents can block tool calls based on tenant usage limits, feature flags, business rules, or real-time conditions. Dynamic gates complement, not replace, the static matrix.

Error Handling Patterns (Wave 2 — Distributed Systems)

PatternImplementationUse Case
Exponential backoff + jitterAll external API calls (Vercel AI Gateway, JobTread, QuickBooks)Transient failures, rate limits
Circuit breakersPer-integration (JobTread, QB, Google)Prevent cascade failures when a service is down
AI provider circuit breakersAIGateway wrapperProvider-level circuit breakers for Anthropic and OpenAI independently. Vercel AI Gateway handles cross-provider failover automatically. The AIGateway wrapper implements per-provider health tracking. Circuit opens after 5 consecutive failures in 30s per provider. Half-open after 60s (test with one call). Multi-step agents queue in BullMQ until circuit closes.
Dead letter queuesNATS + BullMQDead Letter Queue policy: Retention = 7 days. Alert threshold: DLQ depth > 10 jobs triggers Slack alert. Remediation: failed jobs surfaced in admin panel with retry/dismiss actions. Customer-visible failures: if an approved action fails (email send, record creation), an escalation is created in the escalations table so the user sees it in the dashboard — never silent failure. Correlation: BullMQ job ID and NATS message ID logged together in agent_executions for cross-system debugging.
Idempotency keysAll agent actions, especially payments/approvalsPrevent duplicate charges/emails on retry
Saga / compensating transactionsMulti-step agent workflows touching external systemsRoll back partial changes when a step fails
Key saga patterns: (1) Email draft approved + email provider API (Gmail/Microsoft Graph) send fails → mark draft as ‘send_failed’, create escalation ticket. (2) Estimate created in Supabase + JobTread sync fails → mark estimate as ‘sync_pending’, retry via BullMQ with exponential backoff. (3) Multi-agent chain where downstream agent fails → mark upstream output as ‘downstream_failed’, notify user via NATS event.

Database Migration Strategy

Uses the Expand & Contract pattern to prevent downtime:

  1. Add new columns/tables first (expand) — no breaking changes
  2. Backfill data with migration scripts
  3. Update application code to use new schema
  4. Remove old columns later (contract)

Critical rule: RLS policies must NEVER reference newly-added columns until backfill is complete — a policy referencing a NULL column silently denies all access for existing rows. Sequence: (1) add column with DEFAULT NULL, (2) backfill, (3) add NOT NULL constraint, (4) update RLS policy. Additionally, RLS policy changes affect ALL tenants simultaneously. Test every migration against a snapshot of production data before applying. Use Supabase's built-in tooling: supabase db diff, supabase migration new.

Column addition convention: All new columns on existing tables must be nullable with DEFAULT NULL. Never add a non-null column with a default value to a high-traffic table (e.g., agent_executions) — this rewrites the entire table and takes a full table lock. Non-null constraints and default values must be added in a separate migration after the column is backfilled. This is enforced by CI lint rule.

Backup & Disaster Recovery

  • Database: Supabase Pro automated daily backups + independent pg_dump cron every 6 hours to DigitalOcean Spaces (6-hour RPO from Day 1; 3-2-1 rule: 3 copies, 2 media, 1 offsite). $0 additional cost — cron schedule change only.
  • Dual VPS: Both nodes maintain identical Docker volumes (NATS persistent state). Secondary serves as live backup of primary.
  • Valkey backup: DigitalOcean Managed Valkey HA (automatic failover replica from Day 1) includes automated daily backups with 7-day retention. The HA replica provides real-time data redundancy; backups are an additional safety net. For additional safety, schedule weekly RDB snapshots to DO Spaces via a BullMQ cron job (backup size: ~50MB at 100 tenants). Restore procedure: provision new Managed Valkey HA from DO backup, or import RDB snapshot via redis-cli --pipe.
  • NATS JetStream backup: Snapshot of JetStream data directory (/data/jetstream/) to DO Spaces via cron every 6 hours. RPO: 6 hours for the event store. Restore procedure: stop NATS, replace data directory from snapshot, restart. Note: With the 3-node NATS Raft cluster (Day 1), JetStream data is replicated across nodes in real-time — backups are an additional safety net, not the primary recovery mechanism.
  • DR testing cadence: Quarterly restore drill — test Supabase restore, Valkey RDB import, NATS JetStream data directory restore, and dual-VPS Docker volume recovery. Results documented in incident runbook. First drill must complete before first paying customer.
  • Test restores before first customer — most teams never verify this works
  • RTO target: 1 hour. RPO target: 6 hours (pg_dump every 6 hours from Day 1)
  • DR Runbook (to be documented before first customer): (1) Detect: UptimeRobot + Grafana alerts notify on-call founder via SMS. (2) Assess: Determine scope — VPS failure, Supabase outage, or full datacenter loss. (3) VPS failure: LB auto-fails over in 90 seconds; NATS JetStream continues operating via 3-node Raft cluster (remaining 2 nodes maintain quorum) — event-driven agent triggers continue uninterrupted. BullMQ jobs (backed by managed Valkey HA) survive with automatic failover. (4) Supabase outage: Application returns 503 for data operations; in-flight agent executions fail gracefully to DLQ. Supabase outage also degrades spending-limit enforcement — the AIGateway falls back to Valkey-cached per-tenant rate limits for the duration of the outage. (5) Full restore: Restore from latest Supabase backup (pg_dump every 6 hours, 6-hour RPO) + Docker volume snapshots. Estimated RTO: 1–2 hours. (6) Post-incident: Verify data integrity, replay DLQ jobs, notify affected tenants. RPO/RTO is disclosed in customer Terms of Service.

Log Retention Policy

  • Hot storage: 90 days in Grafana Loki (via paid tier or self-hosted)
  • Cold storage: 1 year in compressed cold storage (DigitalOcean Spaces, ~$0.02/GB/month). (ARCH-02) Loki must be explicitly configured with an S3-compatible storage backend pointed at DO Spaces for cold tier — this does not happen automatically. Required Loki config: storage_config.aws.s3 with DO Spaces endpoint (https://{region}.digitaloceanspaces.com), access key, and bucket name. Without this, Loki retains all logs on local disk, which fills VPS storage and causes log loss. (ARCH-03) Cost optimization note: Cloudflare R2 (zero egress fees) is an alternative to DO Spaces for log archival. R2 is S3-compatible, so the Loki config is identical except for the endpoint URL. Evaluate at scale when egress costs from DO Spaces become material.
  • Audit logs: Stored in Supabase with data retention policy (subscription duration + 90 days)
  • SOC 2 note: SOC 2 requires 1-year log retention — Grafana Cloud free tier’s 30-day limit is insufficient

Structured Log Field Standard

Every Pino log line MUST include these mandatory fields:

  • tenant_id (string) — from auth context or job context
  • request_id (string) — generated per HTTP request via crypto.randomUUID()
  • trace_id (string) — W3C traceparent propagated across services
  • agent_id (string) — null for non-agent requests
  • service (string) — 'api' | 'worker' | 'socket'

These fields are injected via Pino child loggers created per-request in Hono middleware and per-job in BullMQ worker context. Without consistent field conventions, Loki queries and OpenTelemetry traces cannot correlate frontend actions to backend agent executions.

Distributed Trace Propagation

Traces flow from Next.js frontend (optional browser instrumentation) → Hono API (W3C traceparent header) → NATS message headers (traceparent injected as NATS header) → BullMQ job data (traceparent stored in job.data.traceContext) → Vercel AI Gateway (traceparent via AI SDK telemetry). Each service creates child spans under the propagated trace. This enables Grafana Tempo or Loki-based trace visualization for end-to-end debugging of agent execution flows.

RPO improvement path: Day 1 RPO is 6 hours (pg_dump every 6 hours, $0 additional cost). Upgrade to Supabase Team ($599/mo — verify current pricing at supabase.com/pricing before budgeting, as Supabase adjusts plan pricing periodically; triggered at 50–75 customers) enables Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR), reducing RPO from 6 hours to minutes. Until PITR is enabled, the 6-hour RPO is the accepted risk for the pre-50-customer phase.

Environment Management

  • Local dev: Docker Compose with local Valkey, NATS, PostgreSQL
  • Staging: 1 Basic droplet (4vCPU/8GB, $48/mo) + Supabase branch (included in Pro plan) = ~$48/mo compute. With staging Managed Valkey ($15/mo), total staging cost is ~$65–$80/mo. Reflected in the 'Ops tooling' P&L line item ($6K Y1). Staging uses separate API keys for all integrations (Anthropic test key, Stripe test mode, SendGrid sandbox) — never production keys. Supabase branch migrations require explicit approval before promotion to production.
  • Production: Dual VPS (hybrid active-active) + DO Load Balancer + Supabase Pro + Vercel
  • Seed data strategy for staging using anonymized production-like data
  • Staging infrastructure: Staging Valkey: separate DigitalOcean Managed Valkey instance (not shared with production). Staging NATS: local Docker Compose instance. Staging tests single-node behavior; production multi-node behavior is validated via the Wave 4 pre-launch verification suite.
  • Feature flags (JSONB column on tenants table for Phase 1; graduate to Unleash or PostHog post-100 tenants when non-engineer flag management is needed)

Local Development Environment

The monorepo includes a docker-compose.local.yml for local development with:

  1. Valkey 8.x container (wire-compatible with Redis; matches production Managed Valkey)
  2. NATS with JetStream enabled (single node)
  3. Local dev calls Vercel AI Gateway directly with development API keys. No local proxy container needed.
  4. Supabase local via supabase start (local PostgreSQL + Auth + Storage)

All external service dependencies (Stripe, SendGrid, JobTread, QuickBooks, Google Calendar, Microsoft Graph) use test/sandbox modes locally: Stripe CLI (stripe listen --forward-to localhost:3000/webhooks/stripe) for webhook testing, SendGrid fixture payloads for Inbound Parse simulation, JobTread sandbox credentials.

Target: git clone → pnpm install → docker compose -f docker-compose.local.yml up → supabase start → pnpm dev = running app in under 10 minutes.

Environment Variable Manifest

The repository includes a .env.example file enumerating every required environment variable with comments on where to obtain each value. Minimum required variables:

  • SUPABASE_URL, SUPABASE_ANON_KEY, SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY [all]
  • BETTER_AUTH_SECRET [all]
  • VERCEL_AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY [all] (AI Gateway uses the Vercel project’s built-in auth for production)
  • NATS_URL, REDIS_URL [all]
  • STRIPE_SECRET_KEY, STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET [staging, production]
  • SENDGRID_API_KEY [staging, production]
  • NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL, NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY [all]
  • ENCRYPTION_KEK (for envelope encryption) [all]

Each variable is tagged with its environment scope: [local], [staging], [production], or [all].

Staging Environment

One 4vCPU/8GB DO droplet ($48/mo) running the same Docker Compose as production. Supabase branch database (included in Pro plan) for isolated staging data. Staging-specific Stripe test mode keys, SendGrid sandbox, and Vercel AI Gateway with separate provider API keys for staging. Staging URL: staging.kiptra.io. Every merge to main auto-deploys to staging. Production deploy requires manual approval after staging smoke tests pass. Staging cost is included in the Ops line item ($6K Y1).

Compliance Readiness

ItemWhenImplementation
Audit loggingWave 1All data access logged with tenant context
Encryption at restWave 1Supabase default (AES-256)
Encryption in transitWave 1TLS 1.3 everywhere (Cloudflare + Vercel)
MFAWave 1TOTP via better-auth
RBACWave 1better-auth Organization plugin
Data retention policyWave 4Data retained for subscription duration + 90 days
Incident response planWave 41-page doc: who is called, communication protocol, remediation steps
Data Processing Agreements (DPAs)Wave 3–4Execute DPAs with Anthropic and OpenAI — required before processing customer financial data. Start DPA process in Wave 3; gate Wave 4 launch on execution completion (typically 1–2 weeks).
Cyber liability insuranceWave 4E&O + Cyber liability combined: $3K/Y1, $5K/Y2, $8K/Y3 — matches P&L line items. Purchase before first paying customer.
AI liability insurance (PRE-LAUNCH GATE)Wave 4 (hard gate)Obtain written E&O confirmation covering AI-generated outputs + evaluate standalone AI liability insurance (Munich Re / Armilla / Testudo, $5–$15K/year). Must be confirmed in writing before first paying customer. Without coverage, every AI-generated estimate is an uninsured liability.
Terms of ServiceWave 4AI liability disclaimers, limitation of liability, SLA terms, data ownership, acceptable use. Must be published before first paying customer. Budget: $3K–$5K legal counsel (shared with Privacy Policy + DPA).
Privacy PolicyWave 4CCPA-compliant data collection disclosure, cookie policy, third-party processor list (Anthropic, OpenAI, SendGrid, Stripe, DigitalOcean), data retention periods. Published alongside ToS.
Customer-facing DPAWave 4Template Data Processing Agreement for B2B customers. Covers data processing scope, sub-processor list, breach notification timeline (72 hours), data deletion obligations.
Contractor licensing reviewWave 4Legal review of top 10 target states’ contractor licensing requirements for AI-generated estimates. All estimate outputs labeled as decision support requiring licensed contractor review.
SOC 2 Type IIPost-Series A$10K–$50K. SOC 2 readiness defined: Day 1 controls include audit logging, encryption at rest/in transit, access control (RLS + RBAC), and incident response plan. The 3–6 month observation period for Type II begins when all controls are implemented (target: Wave 4 completion). Earliest achievable Type II certification: Month 10–12 post-launch.

Construction-specific: Small/mid contractors (your initial market) care about “is my data safe” and “does it work” — not certifications. MFA, encryption, and access controls are sufficient. SOC 2 becomes relevant only when enterprise GCs or government contracts enter the pipeline.

Error Taxonomy (Platform-Wide)

Standardized error codes across all API endpoints, agent executions, and integration callbacks. Every error includes a machine-readable code, human-safe message, HTTP status, retry guidance, and correlation IDs for support debugging.

Base Error Interface

TypeScript
interface ApiError {
  code: string;           // e.g., "AGENT_EXECUTION_TIMEOUT"
  message: string;        // Human-readable, safe to display to end users
  statusCode: number;     // HTTP status code
  details?: unknown;      // Additional context (never contains PII)
  retryable: boolean;     // Whether the client should retry
  retryAfterMs?: number;  // Suggested retry delay if retryable
  documentationUrl?: string;
  requestId: string;      // Correlation ID for support tickets
  traceId?: string;       // OpenTelemetry trace ID for debugging
}

Error code format: DOMAIN_TYPE_DETAIL — e.g., AUTH_SESSION_EXPIRED, AGENT_EXECUTION_TIMEOUT.

AUTH Domain (Authentication & Authorization)

CodeMessageHTTPRetryableNotes
AUTH_CREDENTIALS_INVALIDInvalid email or password401NoGeneric message to prevent enumeration
AUTH_SESSION_EXPIREDYour session has expired. Please sign in again401Nobetter-auth cookie expired
AUTH_SESSION_REVOKEDYour session was revoked by an administrator401NoAdmin or cross-tab logout via Socket.io
AUTH_TOKEN_INVALIDInvalid authentication token401NoMalformed or tampered JWT/cookie
AUTH_TOKEN_REFRESH_FAILEDUnable to refresh your session401NoRefresh token invalid or expired
AUTH_ROLE_INSUFFICIENTYou do not have permission to perform this action403NoRBAC role check failed
AUTH_TENANT_MISMATCHYou do not have access to this organization403NoCross-tenant access attempt
AUTH_MFA_REQUIREDMulti-factor authentication is required403NoMFA not completed for sensitive action
AUTH_MFA_CODE_INVALIDInvalid verification code401NoWrong TOTP/SMS code
AUTH_ACCOUNT_LOCKEDAccount temporarily locked due to too many failed attempts423Yes (after lockout period)Rate limit on login attempts
AUTH_ACCOUNT_SUSPENDEDYour account has been suspended403NoAdmin action
AUTH_INVITATION_EXPIREDThis invitation has expired410NoTeam invitation past TTL
AUTH_INVITATION_INVALIDThis invitation is invalid or has already been used400NoInvalid token or already accepted
AUTH_EMAIL_NOT_VERIFIEDPlease verify your email address403NoEmail verification pending

AGENT Domain (Agent Execution)

CodeMessageHTTPRetryableNotes
AGENT_EXECUTION_TIMEOUTAgent execution timed out504YesExceeded 300s wall-clock limit
AGENT_EXECUTION_FAILEDAgent execution failed500YesGeneric execution error (check details)
AGENT_MODEL_UNAVAILABLEAI model temporarily unavailable503YesAI provider down, circuit open
AGENT_MODEL_FALLBACK_EXHAUSTEDAll AI model providers are unavailable503YesAll fallback models failed
AGENT_BUDGET_EXCEEDEDMonthly AI budget reached — upgrade your plan or wait for the next billing cycle429NoPre-flight spending check failed
AGENT_BUDGET_WARNINGYou have used 80% of your monthly AI budget200N/AWarning, not error — included in response headers
AGENT_INPUT_INVALIDInvalid input for agent execution400NoZod validation failed on input
AGENT_INPUT_REJECTEDInput rejected by safety filter400NoPrompt injection detected
AGENT_TOOL_FAILEDAgent tool execution failed500YesExternal tool call (JobTread, QB) failed
AGENT_TOOL_UNAUTHORIZEDAgent attempted to use an unauthorized tool403NoTool not in AgentPlugin.permissions
AGENT_STEP_LIMIT_REACHEDAgent reached maximum execution steps200NostopWhen triggered — normal completion
AGENT_TOKEN_LIMIT_REACHEDAgent exceeded token budget200Nomax_tokens enforced — truncated output
AGENT_ESCALATION_REQUIREDThis request requires human review200NoRouted to escalation queue
AGENT_ALREADY_RUNNINGAn execution for this agent is already in progress409YesPrevent duplicate concurrent runs
AGENT_DISABLEDThis agent is not enabled for your account403NoAgent not in tenant's template
AGENT_QUEUE_FULLAgent execution queue is full. Please try again shortly429YesBullMQ backpressure

BILLING Domain (Stripe & Subscriptions)

CodeMessageHTTPRetryableNotes
BILLING_PLAN_LIMIT_REACHEDYou have reached the limit for your current plan403NoAgent count, API calls, etc.
BILLING_PAYMENT_FAILEDPayment method failed. Please update your billing information402NoStripe charge declined
BILLING_SUBSCRIPTION_EXPIREDYour subscription has expired403NoPast grace period
BILLING_SUBSCRIPTION_CANCELEDYour subscription has been canceled403NoVoluntary cancellation
BILLING_SUBSCRIPTION_PAST_DUEYour subscription payment is past due402NoGrace period active
BILLING_UPGRADE_REQUIREDThis feature requires a higher plan403NoFeature gated by plan tier
BILLING_METERED_OVERAGEUsage above cap will be billed at cost200N/AEnterprise tier informational
BILLING_WEBHOOK_SIGNATURE_INVALIDInvalid webhook signature400NoStripe signature verification failed

INTEGRATION Domain (OAuth & External Services)

CodeMessageHTTPRetryableNotes
INTEGRATION_OAUTH_EXPIREDYour connection to {{provider}} has expired. Please reconnect401NoOAuth token expired + refresh failed
INTEGRATION_OAUTH_REFRESH_FAILEDUnable to refresh {{provider}} connection500NoRefresh token consumed but DB write failed — requires re-auth
INTEGRATION_OAUTH_REVOKEDYour {{provider}} connection was revoked401NoUser revoked access in provider
INTEGRATION_RATE_LIMITED{{provider}} rate limit reached. Retrying automatically429YesToken bucket exhausted
INTEGRATION_PROVIDER_DOWN{{provider}} is temporarily unavailable503YesCircuit breaker open
INTEGRATION_PROVIDER_ERROR{{provider}} returned an unexpected error502YesNon-rate-limit provider error
INTEGRATION_SYNC_FAILEDData sync with {{provider}} failed500YesSync job error
INTEGRATION_SYNC_STALE{{provider}} data may be outdated (last synced {{timestamp}})200N/AWarning header, not error
INTEGRATION_WEBHOOK_INVALIDInvalid webhook payload from {{provider}}400NoSignature or schema validation failed
INTEGRATION_WEBHOOK_DUPLICATEDuplicate webhook event (already processed)200NoIdempotency key match — return success
INTEGRATION_NOT_CONNECTED{{provider}} is not connected. Set up the integration in Settings400NoNo OAuth token exists
INTEGRATION_SCOPE_INSUFFICIENTAdditional permissions required for {{provider}}403NoOAuth scope insufficient for requested operation
INTEGRATION_ENCRYPTION_FAILEDUnable to decrypt integration credentials500NoDEK/KEK issue — critical alert

SUPPORT Domain (Support Agent)

CodeMessageHTTPRetryableNotes
SUPPORT_CONVERSATION_CLOSEDThis conversation has been closed400NoAttempting to message a resolved conversation
SUPPORT_KB_NOT_FOUNDNo relevant knowledge base articles found200NoLow-confidence response, may trigger escalation
SUPPORT_CONFIDENCE_LOWUnable to provide a confident answer — escalating to our team200NoConfidence < 0.60
SUPPORT_ESCALATION_CREATEDYour request has been escalated to our team200NoSuccessful escalation
SUPPORT_RATE_LIMITEDToo many support requests. Please wait a moment429YesPer-user rate limit on support chat
SUPPORT_PII_DETECTEDSensitive information was detected and redacted for your security200NoPII found in user message
SUPPORT_INPUT_REJECTEDYour message could not be processed400NoPrompt injection or malformed input

TENANT Domain (Multi-Tenancy)

CodeMessageHTTPRetryableNotes
TENANT_NOT_FOUNDOrganization not found404NoInvalid tenant_id
TENANT_SUSPENDEDYour organization has been suspended403NoAdmin or billing suspension
TENANT_MEMBER_LIMIT_REACHEDYour organization has reached its member limit403NoPlan-based member cap
TENANT_ONBOARDING_INCOMPLETEPlease complete your organization setup403NoOnboarding steps remaining
TENANT_FEATURE_DISABLEDThis feature is not available for your organization403NoFeature flag check failed
TENANT_AGENT_LIMIT_REACHEDYour plan allows a maximum of {{limit}} agents403NoStarter = 3, Professional/Enterprise = 6

VALIDATION Domain (Input Validation)

CodeMessageHTTPRetryableNotes
VALIDATION_INPUT_INVALIDInvalid input: {{field}} — {{reason}}400NoZod schema validation failure
VALIDATION_SCHEMA_MISMATCHRequest body does not match expected format400NoWrong content-type or structure
VALIDATION_FILE_TOO_LARGEFile size exceeds the maximum of {{limit}}413NoUpload size limit
VALIDATION_FILE_TYPE_INVALIDFile type not supported. Accepted types: {{types}}415NoMIME type check
VALIDATION_FIELD_REQUIRED{{field}} is required400NoMissing required field
VALIDATION_FIELD_FORMAT{{field}} must be a valid {{format}}400NoEmail, URL, UUID, etc.
VALIDATION_IDEMPOTENCY_CONFLICTThis request conflicts with a previous request409NoIdempotency key reused with different payload

SYSTEM Domain (Infrastructure)

CodeMessageHTTPRetryableNotes
SYSTEM_INTERNAL_ERRORAn unexpected error occurred500YesCatch-all — always log to Sentry
SYSTEM_SERVICE_UNAVAILABLEService temporarily unavailable503YesVPS overloaded or deploying
SYSTEM_RATE_LIMITEDToo many requests. Please slow down429YesGlobal rate limiter
SYSTEM_MAINTENANCESystem is undergoing scheduled maintenance503YesPlanned downtime window
SYSTEM_DATABASE_UNAVAILABLEDatabase temporarily unavailable503YesSupabase outage

13. UX Specifications — Screens, Flows & Edge Cases

This section defines the product-level UX specifications that complement the architecture described in Sections 1–12. Each subsection covers a discrete functional area: screen layouts, user stories, data flows, RBAC rules, error states, and edge cases. These specs serve as the implementation contract between design and engineering.

Global UX Conventions

The following conventions apply across all screens unless a subsection explicitly overrides them.

  • Navigation shell: All authenticated pages render inside a shared layout with a collapsible left sidebar (240px expanded, 64px icon-only collapsed) and a top bar. The sidebar contains: logo, primary nav links (Dashboard, Agents, Customers, Projects, Escalations (badge), Approvals (badge), Reports, Business OS, Settings), seat/plan badge, and a collapse toggle. Contextual sidebar swap: On /bos/* routes, the sidebar replaces main navigation with Business OS grouped navigation (Home, Execution, Strategy, People) and a “← Back” button to return to the main nav. On /settings/* routes, the sidebar shows settings sub-navigation if applicable. The top bar contains: breadcrumbs (left), notification bell with unread badge (center-right), and user avatar dropdown (right: profile, settings, sign out). On mobile (<1024px), the sidebar becomes a hamburger-triggered slide-over.
  • Breadcrumbs: Shown in the top bar for all nested routes. Format: Dashboard › Agents › Leads Agent. Each segment is a clickable link. The root segment matches the sidebar section. Breadcrumbs provide consistent “navigate back” context for routes like /dashboard/agents/:id and /escalations/:id.
  • Loading states: All data-fetching screens display a skeleton loader (matching the layout shape of the expected content) during initial load. Long-running operations (agent execution, data sync, CSV export, initial integration sync) show a progress indicator: either a determinate progress bar (if progress is measurable) or an indeterminate spinner with descriptive text (e.g., “Running agent…”, “Exporting data…”). Buttons that trigger async operations enter a disabled + spinner state until completion.
  • Toast notifications: Success, error, warning, and info toasts appear in the bottom-right corner. Auto-dismiss after 5 seconds (success/info) or persist until dismissed (error/warning). Toasts stack vertically (max 3 visible). Used for: form saves, action confirmations, error feedback, real-time event alerts.
  • Confirmation dialogs: All destructive actions require a confirmation modal before execution. This includes: deactivate/remove team member, disconnect integration, cancel subscription, disable agent, reject escalation, revoke session, and transfer ownership. The modal states what will happen and includes a “Cancel” button (secondary) and a red-styled “Confirm” button. High-impact actions (remove member, transfer ownership, cancel subscription) require typing a confirmation phrase.
  • Date/time display: Relative timestamps (“3 hours ago”, “yesterday”) for events less than 7 days old. Absolute timestamps (“Mar 16, 2026 2:30 PM”) for anything older. Hovering any timestamp shows the full absolute date/time in a tooltip. All times displayed in the user’s local timezone (detected via browser Intl.DateTimeFormat).
  • Pagination: All tables with potentially unbounded data use cursor-based pagination, 25 rows per page (configurable: 10, 25, 50). Controls at table footer: “Previous” / “Next” buttons, current page indicator, total row count. Applies to: execution history, escalation queue, invoice history, team member list, usage breakdown.
  • Empty states: Every data-dependent screen has a designed empty state with: an illustrative icon or graphic, a clear message explaining what will appear, and a primary action CTA where applicable (e.g., “No agents configured yet” → link to agent setup; “No integrations connected” → “Connect your first integration” button; “No team members yet” → “Invite your first team member”).
  • Keyboard accessibility: All interactive elements are reachable via Tab. Focus indicators use the accent color outline (2px solid var(--accent)). Enter/Space activate buttons and toggles. Escape closes modals and dropdowns. Arrow keys navigate within dropdowns, date pickers, and table rows. Skip-to-main-content link is the first focusable element on every page.
  • Help access: A persistent Help Bot button (MessageCircleQuestion icon) in the bottom-right corner of every authenticated page. Clicking opens a slide-out panel with two modes: Quick Help (contextual suggestions matched to current route, search bar, quick links) and AI Chat (Haiku-powered, streamed responses with deep links). Owner/Admin also see a “Schedule a call” option. Full specification in Section 13m.

Screen Inventory

AreaRouteDescription
Auth Flows/loginEmail/password & OAuth sign-in
/login/mfaTOTP multi-factor authentication
/forgot-password, /forgot-password/sentPassword reset request & confirmation
/reset-password, /reset-password/successNew password entry & success
