Invitation/invite/accept?token=xxxInvitation landing & routing
/invite/register?token=xxxNew account registration via invitation
/invite/successInvitation acceptance confirmation
Onboarding/onboard/companyCompany details entry
/onboard/profileOwner profile setup
/onboard/planPlan selection (Phase 2 only)
/onboard/paymentStripe Checkout payment — ACH recommended, cards accepted with 2.9% + $0.30 surcharge (Phase 2 only)
/onboard/teamTeam invitation wizard
/onboard/completeOnboarding completion & provisioning trigger
Account Settings/settings/profileProfile, email, connected accounts
/settings/securityPassword, MFA, active sessions
/settings/notificationsNotification channel preferences
Team Management/settings/teamMember list, search, seat counter
/settings/team/inviteInvite modal (email, role, message)
/settings/team/[memberId]Member detail, role change, deactivate/remove
Billing/settings/billingPlan overview, invoices, payment method, usage summary
/settings/billing/usageDetailed agent-level usage breakdown
Dashboard & Agents/dashboardMain dashboard (post-login landing page, role-scoped)
/dashboard/agentsAgent list with status, scheduling, enable/disable
/dashboard/agents/:idAgent detail: config, execution history, recent outputs
Customers/customersCustomer directory with search, filters, contact info, project history. Data synced from QuickBooks/JobTread + manually added contacts
/customers/:idCustomer detail: contact info, associated projects, communication history, notes
Projects/projectsProject list with status filters, timeline view, search. Data synced from JobTread + manually created
/projects/:idProject detail: tasks, documents, timeline, budget, team, linked estimates
Escalation Queue/escalationsPrioritized queue of agent actions awaiting human review
/escalations/:idEscalation detail with diff view, approve/reject/modify
Approvals/approvalsPending approval queue for agent-generated drafts, estimates, emails requiring human sign-off before delivery. Distinct from escalations (agent issues needing intervention)
Reports/reportsReport hub: agent performance, customer activity, financial summary, team productivity. Recharts visualizations
/reports/agent-roiAgent ROI analysis: time saved, cost savings, accuracy metrics per agent
Notifications/notificationsFull notification history with filters (type, read/unread, date range), mark all read, notification preferences link
Agent Sub-Pages/dashboard/agents/:id/chatConversational interface with agent. Message thread, agent tools/actions, approval buttons for drafts. Design Agent chat includes embedded K&B canvas
/dashboard/agents/:id/workspaceAgent working area: current tasks, recent outputs, knowledge base access, configuration
Business OS Hub/bosBOS landing page with module overview cards, setup wizard, getting started checklist. Stats: team members, active goals, upcoming meetings, open issues
Integrations/settings/integrationsConnected service grid with health indicators
OAuth flow (modal/redirect)Connect/disconnect third-party services
Invited Onboarding/welcomeRole-specific welcome screen (invited users, shown once)
Error & Status Pages/404Not Found page
/500Server Error page with Sentry reference
/maintenanceScheduled maintenance holding page
Help BotGlobal (all authenticated routes)Floating help panel with Quick Help + AI Chat modes (Section 13m)

13-UI. Visual Design System

This subsection defines the foundational visual language for all Kiptra AI screens. Every screen spec in Section 13 inherits these tokens, components, and conventions unless explicitly overridden. The system is built on shadcn/ui + Tailwind CSS + Radix UI primitives.

Design Foundation

Framework: shadcn/ui + Tailwind CSS + Radix UI primitives

Philosophy: Clean, professional, high-contrast for readability on job sites (bright sunlight, dusty screens). Prioritize clarity over decoration. Construction professionals need to glance and understand — no ambiguity.

Color Palette

All colors are defined as CSS custom properties and mapped into tailwind.config.ts. Dark mode is deferred to post-launch. Token architecture is ready (all colors reference CSS custom properties) but dark mode color values have not been designed. Estimated effort: 1–2 days design + 0.5 day implementation to swap custom property values via prefers-color-scheme or a manual toggle.

TokenHexTailwind KeyUsage
Primary#1E3A5Fprimary.DEFAULTPrimary buttons, active nav, links
Primary Light#2563EBprimary.lightHover states, secondary emphasis
Secondary#475569secondary.DEFAULTBody text, secondary elements
Accent#F59E0Baccent.DEFAULTAlerts, badges, attention items (escalations, warnings)
Success#16A34AsuccessConnected, healthy, approved, completed
Warning#EA580CwarningApproaching limits, expiring, needs attention
Error / Danger#DC2626errorFailed, disconnected, past due, destructive actions
Background#FFFFFFbackground.DEFAULTPrimary page background
Background Secondary#F8FAFCbackground.secondaryCard surfaces, secondary panels
Surface#FFFFFFbackground.surfaceWhite cards on gray background for depth separation
Border#E2E8F0border.DEFAULTCard borders, dividers
Border Hover#CBD5E1border.hoverInteractive element borders on hover
Text#0F172Atext.DEFAULTHeadings, high-emphasis text
Text Secondary#334155text.secondaryBody copy
Text Muted#64748Btext.mutedLabels, placeholders, help text

Typography

Font family: Inter (Google Fonts) — clean, highly legible, excellent at small sizes on mobile. Line height: 1.5 for body, 1.25 for headings.

RoleTailwind ClassesRendered Size
Page titletext-2xl font-bold24px / 700
Section headingtext-xl font-semibold20px / 600
Card headingtext-lg font-semibold18px / 600
Body (default)text-sm14px / 400
Small / labelstext-xs12px / 400
Monospace (IDs, code)font-mono text-xs12px / 400

Why 14px base? Construction workers often view on phones at arm’s length or tablets on job sites. 14px with high contrast is more readable than 13px in bright outdoor conditions.

Spacing & Layout

  • Base unit: 4px (Tailwind default)
  • Page padding: px-6 py-4 (24px / 16px)
  • Card padding: p-4 (16px) standard, p-6 (24px) for detail views
  • Grid: 12-column CSS Grid. Sidebar (240px fixed) + main content area.
  • Max content width: 1280px centered for readability on wide monitors
  • Card gap: gap-4 (16px) in grid layouts
  • Section spacing: space-y-6 (24px) between major sections

Component Specifications

All components map to shadcn/ui primitives with Kiptra-specific overrides.

Componentshadcn/ui BaseCustom Notes
Button (primary)Button variant="default"Deep blue bg, white text, rounded-md
Button (secondary)Button variant="outline"Border only, used for secondary actions
Button (destructive)Button variant="destructive"Red bg, requires confirmation dialog
CardCardWhite bg, border, rounded-lg, subtle shadow-sm
TableTableStriped rows (gray-50 alternate), sticky header, responsive horizontal scroll on mobile
Badge (status)BadgeGreen = active, Amber = pending, Red = error, Gray = inactive
Dialog (confirmation)AlertDialogCentered modal with overlay, focus trap
ToastSonner (shadcn toast)Bottom-right, stacked, auto-dismiss 5s
InputInputFull width within form, label above, error message below in red
Select / DropdownSelectRole dropdowns, filter controls
TabsTabsSettings page navigation (Profile / Security / Notifications)
Sidebar navCustom (Radix)Collapsible, icon + label, active state highlight
AvatarAvatarCircular, fallback to initials with bg color derived from name hash
TooltipTooltipFor icon-only buttons, truncated text, datetime details
SkeletonSkeletonPulse animation, matches component dimensions
ProgressProgressUsage meters — colored by threshold: green / amber / red

Iconography

  • Icon set: Lucide Icons (ships with shadcn/ui)
  • Sizes: 16px inline with text, 20px in buttons/nav, 24px for feature icons
  • Style: Stroke icons (not filled), 1.5px stroke weight for consistency
AgentLucide IconRationale
AI / General IntelligenceBrainCognitive processing
Estimating AgentCalculatorEstimates and numbers
Leads AgentMailLead intake and communication
Executive AnalystBarChart3Reporting and analytics
Operations AgentWrenchOperations tooling
OrchestratorCalendarScheduling and coordination
Support AgentHeadsetCustomer-facing support

Data Visualization (Recharts)

  • Chart colors: Primary Blue, Accent Amber, Success Green — max 3 colors per chart for clarity
  • Chart types: Line charts for daily usage trends and cost over time. Bar charts for per-agent execution breakdown and per-user activity. No donut/pie charts (hard to read; use bar charts instead).
  • Grid lines: Light gray (#E2E8F0), horizontal only
  • Labels: Always-visible axis labels; tooltip on hover with exact values
  • Responsive: Charts resize to container width. On mobile (<768px), charts stack vertically and simplify to essential data only.

Responsive Breakpoints

BreakpointRangeLayout Behavior
Mobile<768pxSingle column, hamburger nav, stacked cards, tables render as card view (no horizontal rows)
Tablet768px – 1024pxCollapsed sidebar (icon-only), 2-column grid where applicable
Desktop>1024pxFull sidebar, multi-column layouts, data-dense tables

Construction-specific: All touch targets are minimum 44×44px (WCAG). Action buttons on mobile are oversized for gloved or dirty hands.

Responsive Table Pattern

All data tables use a ResponsiveDataTable wrapper component that renders as a standard <Table> on desktop (>768px) and switches to a vertically stacked card layout on mobile (<768px). Each card shows the row’s key fields with labels, preserving sort and filter controls at the top. This pattern applies to: execution history, escalation queue, team member list, invoice history, and usage breakdown tables.

Animation & Motion

  • Principle: Subtle and functional, never decorative. Construction workers don’t want to wait for animations.
  • Transitions: 150ms ease-out for hover states, 200ms for panel open/close
  • Loading: Skeleton pulse (shadcn default) for content areas — NOT spinners. Spinners are used only for async button states.
  • Real-time updates: Brief highlight flash (200ms amber border) on cards/rows that receive live updates via Socket.io
  • Page transitions: None (instant navigation via Next.js App Router)

Error Boundary Strategy

Next.js App Router error boundaries catch rendering errors and display fallback UI instead of crashing the entire page. The following error.tsx files are required:

  • /app/error.tsx — catches errors in any route not covered by a more specific boundary
  • /app/dashboard/error.tsx — catches errors in dashboard and agent views
  • /app/settings/error.tsx — catches errors in settings, billing, and team management
  • /app/escalations/error.tsx — catches errors in the escalation queue
  • /app/global-error.tsx — root-level boundary that catches errors in the root layout itself (must include its own <html> and <body> tags)

Each error boundary displays: a user-friendly error message, a “Try again” button (calls reset()), and a “Go to dashboard” fallback link. Error details are reported to Sentry automatically via the useEffect hook in the error component.

Accessibility (WCAG 2.1 AA)

  • Color contrast: All text meets 4.5:1 ratio. Verified: #0F172A on #FFFFFF = 15.4:1; #334155 on #FFFFFF = 8.6:1.
  • Color independence: Status indicators use icon + color + label (never color alone). Usage meters show percentage text alongside colored bar.
  • Focus indicators: 2px blue outline on keyboard focus (not visible on mouse click via :focus-visible)
  • Screen reader: All images have alt text, all icons have aria-label, all form inputs have associated <label> elements.
  • Reduced motion: Respect prefers-reduced-motion — disable all transitions and animations.
  • Live regions for real-time updates: All Socket.io-driven content updates (agent status changes, escalation alerts, execution progress) must use aria-live="polite" regions so screen readers announce changes without requiring page refresh.
  • Role attributes for custom widgets: Custom components not using native HTML elements must include appropriate role attributes: React Flow canvas (role="img" with aria-label), agent status cards (role="status"), and toast notifications (role="alert").
  • Accessible chart names: All Recharts visualizations must include aria-label describing the chart content (e.g., “Monthly revenue trend, January through March 2026”) and a visually hidden data table fallback for screen reader users.
  • Keyboard patterns for TanStack Table: Arrow key navigation between cells, Enter to activate row actions, Escape to exit cell focus. Column sort toggleable via Enter on header cells.
  • 320px reflow: All content must be usable at 320px viewport width (WCAG 1.4.10) without horizontal scrolling for text content. Data tables may scroll horizontally but must provide a card-based alternative view on mobile (see Responsive Table Pattern below).

Performance Budgets

Core Web Vitals targets enforced via Vercel Analytics and CI checks:

  • LCP (Largest Contentful Paint): <2.5s on 4G connection
  • INP (Interaction to Next Paint): <200ms
  • CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift): <0.1
  • Initial JS bundle (gzipped): <200KB for the main entry point. Enforced via next.config.ts bundle analyzer and CI size check. Exceeding this budget blocks the PR.

Monitoring: Vercel Speed Insights (included in Pro) provides real-user metrics. Weekly review of P75 LCP and INP in the Vercel dashboard. Alert if any metric crosses the “needs improvement” threshold for 3 consecutive days.

Brand Identity (Phase 1)

  • Logo: “Kiptra AI” wordmark in primary blue + amber accent on the “A”. Placed in sidebar header.
  • Favicon: Amber “A” on transparent background
  • Tone: Professional, competent, no jargon. Button labels use plain English (“Approve”, “Reject”, “Connect QuickBooks” — not “Execute Action”, “Dismiss”, “Initiate OAuth Flow”).
  • Voice: Direct and brief. Error messages explain what happened and what to do: “Payment failed. Update your card to continue.” — not “An error occurred processing your request.”

Design Token Structure

Tokens are organized in tailwind.config.ts under theme.extend.colors. All values reference CSS custom properties for future dark mode toggle.

tailwind.config.ts// theme.extend.colors
primary:    { DEFAULT, light, dark }
secondary:  { DEFAULT, light }
accent:     { DEFAULT, light }
success:    "var(--color-success)"
warning:    "var(--color-warning)"
error:      "var(--color-error)"
info:       "var(--color-info)"
background: { DEFAULT, secondary, surface }
border:     { DEFAULT, hover }
text:       { DEFAULT, secondary, muted }

13a. Login Page

Routes: /login, /login/mfa

User Stories

  • Returning user signs in with email/password.
  • Returning user signs in via Google or Microsoft OAuth.
  • MFA-enabled user enters TOTP code after password authentication.
  • User clicks “Forgot password?” to start the reset flow.

Screen: /login

Layout: Centered card (max-width 420px). Company logo + tagline at top. Clean, single-column form.

  • Email input — standard text field with validation.
  • Password input — with show/hide toggle (eye icon).
  • “Sign in” primary button — full-width, accent color.
  • Divider — “or continue with” horizontal rule with centered text.
  • OAuth buttons — Google and Microsoft, each with provider icon. Outlined style to distinguish from primary CTA.
  • “Forgot password?” link — below password field, routes to /forgot-password.
  • “Don’t have an account? Contact sales” — Phase 1: no self-serve signup. Phase 2: becomes a /signup link.

Error States

ConditionMessage ShownNotes
Invalid email or password“Invalid email or password.”Generic message — never reveal which field is wrong (enumeration prevention).
Account locked (5 failed attempts)“Account locked. Try again in 15 minutes or contact support.”Lockout duration: 15 min. Counter resets on successful login. Audit log entry written on lock.
OAuth provider error“Could not connect to [Provider]. Please try again.”Log full error server-side; surface only generic message to user.
Network error“Network error. Check your connection and try again.”Client-side detection via fetch failure / timeout.

Screen: /login/mfa

Shown after successful email/password authentication when MFA is enabled on the account.

  • “Enter your 6-digit code” heading — clear instruction.
  • 6-digit TOTP input — individual digit boxes with auto-advance on each keystroke. Auto-submit on 6th digit.
  • “Verify” button — primary CTA, also triggered by auto-submit.
  • “Use a recovery code instead” link — switches to a single text input for the recovery code.
  • “Back to login” link — clears partial auth state, returns to /login.

MFA Error States

ConditionMessage ShownNotes
Invalid code“Invalid code. Please try again.”Clear input, refocus first digit.
Expired code (TOTP window passed)“Code expired. Enter your current code.”TOTP window: 30s. Accept current + previous window to handle clock skew.
Too many attempts (5 failures)“Too many attempts. Please sign in again.”Invalidate partial auth session. Redirect to /login. Audit log entry.

Data Flow

Reads: users table (better-auth), tenant_members for org membership lookup.

Writes: Session token (better-auth managed), audit log entry (login success/failure, IP, user agent).

Post-auth routing: If user belongs to a single organization → redirect to /dashboard. If multiple organizations (future) → org picker screen before dashboard.

Multi-org session scoping: Selected organization_id is embedded in the session token at selection time. Switching tenants issues a NEW session token (not a client-side header swap). withTenantContext() derives tenant_id exclusively from the session token. Multi-tab session model: better-auth sessions are cookie-based and SHARED across all tabs in the same browser. Logging out in one tab logs out all tabs. The plan does NOT claim per-tab session isolation. For multi-org access in separate tabs, users must use different browser profiles (or the future multi-org session switcher must re-scope the shared session cookie).

Edge Cases

ScenarioBehavior
Multiple organizationsOrg selector shown post-auth. User picks which org to enter. Session scoped to selected org.
Account locked“Try again in 15 minutes” message with support contact link. No hint about which credential failed.
OAuth-only accountPassword field hidden. Only OAuth buttons rendered. If user somehow reaches password form, show “This account uses Google/Microsoft sign-in.”
Social login email matches existing accountbetter-auth handles account linking with a confirmation dialog: “An account with this email already exists. Link your [Provider] account?”

13b. Password Reset Flow

Routes: /forgot-password, /forgot-password/sent, /reset-password?token=xxx, /reset-password/success

User Stories

  • User who forgot their password initiates a reset via email and sets a new password.

Screen: /forgot-password

  • Email input — single field.
  • “Send reset link” button — primary CTA.
  • “Back to login” link — returns to /login.

Security: Always shows the success screen regardless of whether the email exists in the system. This prevents email enumeration attacks. The server returns 200 OK in all cases.

Screen: /forgot-password/sent

  • “Check your email” message — includes the email address submitted (masked: j***@example.com).
  • “Resend link” button — rate limited to 1 request per 60 seconds. Button disabled with countdown timer during cooldown.
  • “Back to login” link.

Screen: /reset-password?token=xxx

  • Token validation on page load — server verifies token existence, expiry (1 hour), and single-use status before rendering the form.
  • New password input — with strength meter (zxcvbn or equivalent). Shows requirements inline.
  • Confirm password input — must match.
  • “Reset password” button — primary CTA. Disabled until both fields valid and matching.

Token expiry: 1 hour from generation. If the token is invalid or expired, show an error page with a “Request new link” CTA that routes back to /forgot-password. Never reveal why the token failed (expired vs. invalid vs. already used) — just “This link is no longer valid.”

Screen: /reset-password/success

  • “Password updated” confirmation — clear success state.
  • “Sign in” button — routes to /login.
  • All existing sessions revoked — server-side, on password change via explicit auth.api.revokeAllSessions() call (better-auth does NOT auto-revoke sessions on password change). User must re-authenticate on all devices.

Edge Cases

ScenarioBehavior
Token already used“This link is no longer valid. Request a new one.” — same message as expired (no information leakage).
Token expired“This link is no longer valid.” + “Request new link” CTA.
Email not foundStill shows “Check your email” on /forgot-password/sent. No email sent server-side.
OAuth-only userEmail sent says: “Your account uses Google/Microsoft sign-in. Use that provider to access your account.” No reset link included.
Same password as currentOptionally block with “New password must be different from your current password.” Configurable per tenant policy.

13c. Invitation Acceptance Flow

Routes: /invite/accept?token=xxx, /invite/register?token=xxx, /invite/success

User Stories

  • Invited team member accepts an invitation and creates a new account.
  • Existing user accepts an invitation to join an additional organization with one click.

Screen: /invite/accept?token=xxx

Landing page for all invitation links. Shows invitation details and branches based on user state.

  • Invitation details — Company name, inviter name, role being assigned (e.g., “Sarah invited you to join Slate Design as a Project Manager”).
  • If logged in with matching email → “Accept Invitation” button (one-click join). No further steps required.
  • If not logged in but account exists → “Sign in to accept” button. Routes to /login with redirect=/invite/accept?token=xxx. After login, returns here for one-click accept.
  • If no account exists → “Create account to accept” button. Routes to /invite/register?token=xxx.

Screen: /invite/register?token=xxx

Registration form scoped to the invitation. The token is validated on load — if invalid/expired, show error instead of form.

  • Email — pre-filled from invitation record, not editable. Displayed as read-only text.
  • Full name input — required.
  • Password input — with strength meter (same component as reset flow). Shows requirements inline.
  • OAuth buttons — “or sign up with Google / Microsoft” as alternative to password creation.
  • “Create account & join [Company]” button — primary CTA. Company name pulled from invitation record.

Screen: /invite/success

  • “You’ve joined [Company] as [Role]” message — confirmation with company name and assigned role.
  • Brief role description — one sentence explaining what the role can do (e.g., “As a Project Manager, you can manage projects, view estimates, and coordinate with agents.”).
  • “Go to Dashboard” button — primary CTA, routes to /dashboard.

Edge Cases

ScenarioBehavior
Invitation expired (72h)“This invitation has expired. Ask [Inviter Name] to send a new one.” Inviter name pulled from invitation record.
Already accepted“You’ve already joined [Company].” + “Go to Dashboard” link. No error styling — treat as success.
Invitation revoked“This invitation is no longer valid.” No further detail (don’t reveal revocation).
Email mismatch (logged in as different user)“This invitation was sent to [invited@email]. Sign in with that email or sign out first.” Show both options.
Seat limit reached“[Company] has reached its member limit. Contact the account owner to upgrade.” Invitation remains valid — can be accepted once a seat opens.

Data Flow

Reads: Invitation record (token, org_id, role, inviter_id, email, expires_at, status). User session (if logged in).

Writes: users table (if new account created), tenant_members (new membership row with assigned role), invitation status updated to accepted, audit log entry (invitation accepted, by whom, when).

External: better-auth — organization.acceptInvitation() handles the membership creation and role assignment atomically.

Cross-references

Related sections: Section 5 (RBAC roles and team invitation mechanics), Section 12 (rate limiting on auth endpoints and MFA via TOTP), Wave 1 item 4 (invitation system as part of initial auth buildout).

13d. Signup & Onboarding Flow

Phase Context

Phase 1 is founder-led onboarding (remote via Google Meet/Zoom, per Wave 4). Self-serve signup is deferred to Month 5–6 of the GTM timeline. This specification covers both Phase 1 (founder-assisted) and Phase 2 (self-serve) flows. Screens marked (Phase 2 only) are not built until the self-serve milestone.

User Stories

  • CEO/owner (Phase 1): Completes account setup after founder-led sales call. Plan is pre-selected; payment is handled offline via ACH/bank transfer or via a pre-configured Stripe link (ACH recommended, cards accepted with surcharge).
  • CEO/owner (Phase 2): Self-registers, selects a plan, pays via Stripe Checkout (ACH recommended, cards accepted with 2.9% + $0.30 surcharge), and begins onboarding without human assistance.
  • First user: Sets up profile and security credentials during the onboarding wizard.

Onboarding State Machine

Flow
company_details → industry_persona → profile_setup → plan_selection → payment → team_invite → complete
                                                            (Phase 1: skip)   (Phase 1: skip)

Each step stores progress to the tenants.onboarding_step column. Users can navigate backward freely but cannot skip forward past incomplete steps. If the browser is closed mid-flow, the next login resumes at the last completed step (keyed off tenants.onboarding_completed boolean).

Industry + Persona selection (new step): After company details, the user selects their industry vertical and supply chain role. These two selections determine the template that configures the entire tenant experience. The confirmation banner reads: “Based on your selections, we’ve configured Kiptra for [Industry] [Persona]. You can customize this later in Settings.” See Section 6a for the full multi-persona architecture.

Screen Inventory

1. /onboard/company — Company Details
FieldTypeRequiredNotes
Company nameText inputYesNo uniqueness constraint — tenant_id (UUID) is the unique key
Industry verticalDropdownYesKitchen & Bath pre-selected. Options: Kitchen & Bath, HVAC, General Construction, Landscaping. Maps to tenants.industry_vertical.
Business typeRadio cardsYesContractor/Remodeler (“I design and install for homeowners”), Dealer/Showroom (“I sell to contractors”), Sales Representative (“I represent manufacturers”), Manufacturer (“I make products”). Maps to tenants.tenant_type. Determines template. Phase 1: only Contractor is functional; others show “Coming soon — join waitlist.”
Company sizeRadio groupYes1–5, 6–15, 16–50, 50+
PhoneTel inputNoUsed for onboarding follow-up
AddressAddress fieldsNoRequired later for JobTread/QuickBooks integration setup

Primary action: “Continue” button. Writes to tenants table on submission.

2. /onboard/profile — Owner Profile
FieldTypeRequiredNotes
Full nameText inputYes—
EmailEmail inputYesPre-filled if OAuth signup; validated unique against users table
PasswordPassword + strength meterConditionalSkipped entirely if user authenticated via OAuth (Google/Microsoft)
PhoneTel inputNo—
AvatarFile uploadNoMax 2MB, JPEG/PNG/WebP; stored in Supabase Storage

Primary action: “Continue” button. Writes to users table and creates tenant_members row with owner role.

3. /onboard/plan — Plan Selection (PHASE 2 ONLY)

Phase 1: Plan is pre-selected by the founder during the sales call and stored on the tenant record before the user begins onboarding. This screen is bypassed entirely.

Phase 2: Three plan cards displayed side by side:

FeatureStarterProfessionalEnterprise
Monthly price$275/mo$500/mo$750/mo
Setup fee$5,000$10,000$20,000
Agent count3 agents6 agents (all core)6 agents + custom
Integration limit1 integrationAll integrationsUnlimited
Support levelEmail (48h SLA)Priority email (24h SLA)Dedicated Slack + phone
UsersUp to 5Up to 15Unlimited

“Most Popular” badge applied to Professional card. Feature comparison matrix below cards for detailed line-item comparison. See Appendix A for full pricing tier breakdown.

4. /onboard/payment — Payment (PHASE 2 ONLY)

Stripe Checkout Session (redirect mode or embedded via @stripe/react-stripe-js). ACH recommended, cards accepted with surcharge. ACH is pre-selected as the default payment method. Credit/debit cards are available with a transparent 2.9% + $0.30 processing surcharge (exempt in CT and MA). Displays setup fee as a one-time line item plus first month’s recurring subscription. Bank account verification via Plaid instant verification (primary) or micro-deposit fallback (2 business days).

  • Payment method: payment_method_types: ['us_bank_account', 'card'] — ACH preferred, cards accepted. ACH is pre-selected as default. Card surcharge (2.9% + $0.30) applied via line item adjustment, displayed before confirmation. Surcharge exempt in Connecticut and Massachusetts (state restriction).
  • On success: Stripe fires checkout.session.completed webhook → backend handler creates Stripe subscription record, updates tenants.stripe_customer_id and tenants.stripe_subscription_id, sets tenants.plan and tenants.billing_status = 'active'.
  • On failure: User remains on payment page with a contextual error message (“Bank account verification failed”, “Insufficient funds”, etc.). Retry is immediate — no cooldown.
  • ACH settlement: 4–5 business days. Tenant is provisioned immediately on checkout completion; ACH settlement is asynchronous. If ACH payment fails after settlement attempt, invoice.payment_failed webhook triggers the billing state machine.
  • Webhook idempotency: Deduplicate on checkout.session.id to handle Stripe’s at-least-once delivery.
5. /onboard/team — Team Invitations

“Invite your team” heading with dynamic invitation rows:

  • Each row: email input + role dropdown (admin, manager, designer, bookkeeper, viewer). The owner role is auto-assigned to the signup user and not selectable.
  • “Add another” button appends a new row (no upper limit enforced in UI; plan user cap enforced server-side).
  • “Skip for now” link advances to completion without sending invitations.
  • “Send invites & finish” button: calls better-auth Organization plugin inviteMember() per row, which generates invitation tokens and triggers SendGrid transactional emails.
  • Invitation records are written to the invitations table with status = 'pending', expires_at = NOW() + 72 hours.
6. /onboard/complete — Onboarding Complete
  • Success animation (Lottie or CSS confetti).
  • Summary card: company name, selected plan, number of invitees.
  • “Go to Dashboard” CTA button → redirects to /dashboard.
  • Phase 1 addendum: Banner below CTA — “Your onboarding specialist will contact you within 24 hours to schedule your setup session.”

Provisioning Trigger: On reaching the “complete” step, the tenant provisioning saga fires: (1) Supabase row finalization with all onboarding data, (2) NATS subject namespace creation (TENANT_{tenantId}.{domain}.{name}.{event}), (3) Valkey key prefix verification (tenant:{tenant_id}:*). See Section 5 for the full provisioning saga specification.

Data Requirements

DirectionTargetDetails
WritestenantsCompany info, plan, stripe_customer_id, stripe_subscription_id, onboarding_step, onboarding_completed
WritesusersProfile (name, email, avatar URL, hashed password if non-OAuth)
Writestenant_membersOwner role assignment linking user to tenant
WritesinvitationsPending invitation records with token, role, and expiry
ReadsStripe price IDsLoaded from environment config to construct Checkout Session
ExternalStripe APICheckout Session creation, webhook receipt
ExternalSendGridTeam invitation transactional emails

Edge Cases

ScenarioBehavior
Email already registeredShow inline error: “This email is already registered. Sign in instead.”
ACH payment fails at checkoutStay on payment page. Display reason (“Bank account verification failed”, “Insufficient funds”). Allow immediate retry or micro-deposit fallback.
ACH payment fails after settlementStripe fires invoice.payment_failed webhook. Billing state machine transitions to past_due. Dashboard banner: “Payment failed. Update payment method to avoid service interruption.”
Browser closed mid-onboardingState persisted via onboarding_step column on tenants. Next login redirects to last incomplete step.
OAuth signup (Google/Microsoft)Skip password creation on profile step. Email pre-filled and non-editable.
Company name already existsAllowed — tenant_id (UUID) is the unique key, not company name.
Invitation exceeds plan user capServer-side validation rejects. UI shows: “Your plan supports up to N users. Remove invitees or upgrade your plan.”
Invitation email bouncesSendGrid webhook updates invitations.status = 'bounced'. Owner notified on dashboard.
Cross-References

Wave 1 item 4 (authentication), Wave 4 “Customer onboarding flow”, GTM Month 5–6 “self-serve onboarding option”, Section 5 (tenant provisioning saga), Appendix A (pricing tiers).

13e. Account Settings Dashboard

/settings/profile — Profile Information

  • Avatar: Circular preview with upload/change overlay. Accepts PNG/JPG/WebP, max 2 MB, cropped to 256×256 on upload. Stored in DigitalOcean Spaces under avatars/{userId}.webp.
  • Full name: Editable text field. Persisted on save.
  • Email: Editable, but changes require re-verification. On submit: (1) send confirmation link to the new address via SendGrid, (2) email remains unchanged until the user clicks the link, (3) link expires after 24 hours.
  • Phone number: Editable text field with E.164 format validation.
  • Connected accounts: Section listing OAuth providers (Google, Microsoft). Each row shows provider icon, connected email, and a Connect or Disconnect button.
  • “Save changes” button: Disabled until a field is modified. Shows inline success/error toast on submit.
  • “Restart product tour” button: Resets tenant_members.tour_completed = false and launches the role-specific tour on next dashboard visit. See Section 13l.
  • “View my permissions” link: Expands an inline panel showing the user’s current role and a summary of what they can access (mirrors the role summary from Section 13l welcome screen). Useful after a role change.

RBAC: All roles can edit their own profile. No cross-user profile editing.

/settings/security — Password, MFA, Sessions

Password Section
  • Fields: Current password, New password, Confirm new password.
  • “Change password” button. Validates current password server-side before accepting the new one.
  • Password strength meter (zxcvbn-based) with minimum score of 3.
  • OAuth-only users (no password set): Section displays “Set a password” instead, with only New + Confirm fields. Setting a password enables email+password login alongside OAuth.
MFA Section (TOTP)
  • Enable flow: Toggle → display QR code (otpauth:// URI) + manual entry key → user enters first TOTP code to verify → on success, display recovery codes (8 single-use codes, one-time display, user must copy/download). MFA is not active until the first code is verified.
  • Disable flow: Requires both current password AND a valid TOTP code. This prevents an attacker with only a stolen session from disabling MFA.
  • Recovery codes: Each code is single-use. The UI shows remaining count (e.g., “6 of 8 recovery codes remaining”). Users can regenerate all codes (requires password + TOTP), which invalidates previous codes.
Active Sessions
  • Table listing all active sessions: Device type (icon), Browser, IP address, Last active timestamp, Approximate location (GeoIP city/country).
  • Current session highlighted with “This device” badge.
  • “Revoke” button per session row (except current).
  • “Revoke all other sessions” button at the top — invalidates every session except the current one.

RBAC: All roles manage their own security settings. No cross-user security management.

/settings/notifications — Notification Preferences

Toggle matrix: rows are notification categories, columns are delivery channels.

CategoryEmailIn-AppDefault
Agent alerts (errors, completions)ToggleToggleBoth on
Escalation requestsToggleToggleBoth on
Daily briefingToggleToggleEmail on
Team activity (joins, role changes)ToggleToggleIn-app on
Billing alertsToggleToggleBoth on (Owner/Admin locked on)
Security alerts (new login, MFA changes)ToggleToggleBoth on (locked on, non-dismissible)

RBAC: All roles manage their own notification preferences. Owner and Admin receive billing alerts by default and cannot disable them. Security alerts are locked on for all roles.

In-App Notification Center

In-app notifications are delivered via Socket.io and displayed through a notification bell icon in the top bar (part of the global navigation shell). The bell shows an unread count badge (red circle with number, max “99+”).

  • Clicking the bell opens a dropdown panel (max-height 480px, scrollable) listing recent notifications in reverse chronological order.
  • Each notification row: Category icon (color-coded), title, summary (truncated to 2 lines), relative timestamp, unread indicator (blue dot).
  • Click-through: Clicking a notification navigates to the relevant screen (e.g., escalation notification → /escalations/:id, agent error → /dashboard/agents/:id).
  • “Mark all as read” link at the top of the panel.
  • “View all notifications” link at the bottom → future dedicated notifications page (Phase 2).
  • Real-time push: New notifications animate into the panel if open, and increment the badge count if closed. A brief toast appears for high-priority notifications (escalation requests, agent errors, security alerts) even if the panel is closed.
  • Persistence: Notifications stored in a notifications table (id, user_id, tenant_id, category, title, body, link, read, created_at). Retained for 90 days, then auto-pruned.

Real-Time Update Indicators

Socket.io delivers real-time data updates to the UI. The following patterns indicate new data availability:

  • Dashboard: Agent status cards update in-place with a brief highlight animation (border flash) when status changes. New escalations trigger both the notification bell and the escalation alert banner count increment.
  • Escalation queue: New items push to the top of the list with a slide-in animation and a “New” badge that fades after 10 seconds.
  • Agent detail: New execution results append to the history table in real-time. A “New results available” pill appears above the table if the user has scrolled down, clicking it scrolls to the top.
  • Integration cards: Health status badges update reactively when the backend pushes a status change via Socket.io.
  • Connection status: A persistent indicator in the bottom-right corner of the app shows Socket.io connection state: green dot (connected), red dot with “Reconnecting…” text (disconnected, auto-reconnecting), gray dot (offline — show “Refresh” link after 30s of failed reconnection).

Data & Dependencies

  • Database: users table (profile fields, password hash), better-auth sessions table, notification_preferences table (new — user_id, category, channel, enabled, unique on (user_id, category, channel)).
  • External: SendGrid (email verification flow, notification delivery), TOTP generation/verification via better-auth’s built-in MFA plugin, GeoIP service for session location (MaxMind GeoLite2 or equivalent).

Edge Cases

  • Email change to existing email: Block with “This email is already associated with another account.” Do not reveal which account.
  • Disconnect last OAuth without password: Block with “You must set a password before disconnecting your last sign-in provider.” Prevents account lockout.
  • MFA recovery codes single-use: Each code is consumed on use. If all 8 are consumed and the user loses their TOTP device, account recovery requires manual identity verification (support flow).
  • Revoke current session: Triggers immediate logout. The user is redirected to the login page with a “Session ended” message.
  • Email verification link expiry: After 24 hours, the pending email change is discarded. The user must re-initiate the change. Only one pending email change is allowed at a time.
  • Concurrent session revocation: If another session is revoked while the user is active, the revoked session receives a Socket.io disconnect event and is redirected to login on next API call (401).

13f. Team Management Panel

/settings/team — Member List

  • Table columns: Avatar, Name, Email, Role (color-coded badge), Status (active / pending / deactivated), Last active, Actions (dropdown).
  • “Invite member” button in the header (visible to Owner and Admin only).
  • Search & filter: Text search by name/email, dropdown filters for role and status.
  • Loading state: Skeleton table rows during initial fetch.
  • Empty state: When only the owner exists: “You’re the only team member. Invite colleagues to start collaborating.” with prominent “Invite member” CTA.
  • Pagination: 25 members per page. Shows total member count in the table footer.
  • Seat count indicator: “X of Y seats used” displayed below the table header. Seat limits per plan:
    • Starter: 5 seats
    • Professional: 15 seats
    • Enterprise: unlimited
RBAC Visibility Rules
RoleAccess Level
OwnerFull access. All actions (invite, edit roles, deactivate, remove, transfer ownership).
AdminInvite, edit roles, deactivate members. Cannot remove the Owner, cannot change own role.
ManagerView-only. Can see the team list but cannot modify anything.
Designer / Bookkeeper / ViewerNo access. Attempting to navigate to /settings/team redirects to the dashboard.

Invite Modal (/settings/team/invite)

  • Email address: Text input with real-time format validation.
  • Role: Dropdown selector. Options: Admin, Manager, Designer, Bookkeeper, Viewer. Owner is not available — one Owner per tenant is enforced at the database level (unique constraint on (tenant_id, role) where role = 'owner').
  • Personal message: Optional textarea (max 500 characters). Included in the invitation email body.
  • “Send invitation” button.

Validation (server-side):

  • Email format (RFC 5322 via Zod).
  • Not already an active or pending member of this tenant.
  • Seat limit not exceeded — if at capacity, the invite button is disabled with tooltip: “Seat limit reached. Upgrade your plan to invite more members.”

Flow: Calls better-auth organization.inviteMember() → creates a pending row in tenant_members → sends invitation email via SendGrid with a signed acceptance link (72-hour expiry). Accepting the link: if the invitee has an existing Kiptra account, they are added to the tenant immediately; if not, they are directed to a signup form pre-filled with their email, and upon registration are auto-joined to the tenant.

Member Detail Modal (/settings/team/[memberId])

  • Profile summary: Name, email, avatar, role badge, date joined, last active timestamp.
  • Role change: Dropdown (Owner and Admin only). Changing a role takes effect immediately — the affected user’s permissions update on their next API call, and a Socket.io event notifies their active sessions to refresh permissions client-side.
  • “Deactivate member” → Sets status to deactivated, explicitly revokes all active sessions for that user via auth.api.revokeAllSessions({ userId }) (Socket.io forced room eviction per Section 5), retains all historical data. Deactivated users cannot log in but their past activity remains visible in audit logs.
  • “Reactivate member” (visible for deactivated members) → Restores status to active. The user can log in again with their existing credentials. Counts toward seat limit.
  • “Remove member” (Owner only) → Permanent removal from the tenant. All sessions explicitly revoked via auth.api.revokeAllSessions({ userId }) (better-auth does NOT auto-clear sessions on member removal — without explicit revocation, removed users retain access until session expiry). The user retains their Kiptra account but loses access to this tenant’s data. Confirmation modal: “This will permanently remove [Name] from your team. This cannot be undone.”
  • “Transfer ownership” (Owner only, on their own profile) → Requires password confirmation + TOTP code (if MFA enabled). Transfers the Owner role to a selected Admin. The original Owner is demoted to Admin. Only one Owner per tenant at all times — the transfer is atomic (single transaction).

RBAC Permission Matrix

ActionOwnerAdminManagerDesignerBookkeeperViewer
View team list✓✓✓✗✗✗
Invite members✓✓✗✗✗✗
Change roles✓✓*✗✗✗✗
Deactivate members✓✓✗✗✗✗
Remove members✓✗✗✗✗✗
Transfer ownership✓✗✗✗✗✗

*Admin cannot change the Owner’s role or their own role.

Edge Cases

  • Invite existing user in different tenant: Allowed. The user can belong to multiple tenants (future multi-org support). They select their active tenant from a tenant switcher after login.
  • Last Admin changes own role: Blocked. The system requires at least one Admin (or Owner) per tenant. If the last Admin attempts to change their role to Manager or below, the request is rejected with “At least one Admin is required.”
  • Owner removes self: Blocked. The Owner must transfer ownership first. The “Remove member” action is hidden on the Owner’s own profile — only “Transfer ownership” is available.
  • Seat limit reached: The “Invite member” button is disabled. A banner displays: “You’ve used all X seats on your [Plan] plan.” with an “Upgrade plan” link to /settings/billing.
  • Pending invitation: Shown in the member table with status badge “Pending.” Actions available: “Resend invitation” (resets the 72-hour timer) and “Revoke invitation” (deletes the pending row, frees the seat).
  • Expired invitation (72 hours): Status changes to “Expired.” The row remains visible with a “Resend” action. Expired invitations do not count toward the seat limit.
  • Deactivated member reactivation at seat limit: Blocked. Reactivating a deactivated member counts as filling a seat. If at capacity, the reactivation is rejected with the same upgrade prompt.
Cross-References

Section 5 — Six RBAC roles (Owner, Admin, Manager, Designer, Bookkeeper, Viewer), team hierarchy, tenant isolation, forced Socket.io room eviction on member removal/role change. Section 12 — MFA (TOTP) implementation via better-auth, referenced by the ownership transfer confirmation flow. Wave 1 Item 4 — Team invitation and role assignment as a Day 1 deliverable. Wave 5 — “Full multi-tenant admin panel + team management” as the complete implementation milestone.

13g. Billing & Subscription Management

User Stories

  • Owner/admin views current plan and usage
  • Owner manages subscription (upgrade/downgrade/cancel) via Stripe Customer Portal
  • Owner/admin sees invoice history and downloads receipts
  • Owner sees AI usage meters and approaching-limit alerts

/settings/billing — Billing Overview

Current plan card: Plan name, monthly price, setup fee (paid/pending), renewal date, status (active / past_due / canceled).

Quick actions:

  • “Manage subscription” → Stripe Customer Portal (handles upgrade, downgrade, cancel, payment method)
  • “Download latest invoice” button

Usage summary: Agent execution count (this period), AI token usage vs limit, seat usage (X/Y).

AI spending alert indicators:

ThresholdColorBehavior
< 60% of limitGreenNormal — no indicator
60–79%YellowSubtle usage badge in sidebar
80–99%Orange“Approaching limit” banner at page top
100%RedStarter/Pro: “Limit reached” — agent execution blocked. Enterprise: “Overage billing active”

Invoice history table: Date, Amount, Status (paid / pending / failed), PDF download link. Paginated (25 per page). Filterable by status and date range. Sortable by date and amount.

Payment method: Bank account (ACH) or credit/debit card (last 4 digits, bank name or card brand/expiry). Customer can switch payment method at any time. “Update” → Stripe Customer Portal. Card payments include 2.9% + $0.30 surcharge (exempt in CT/MA). Monthly subscription renewals: customer’s choice of ACH or card. Same surcharge applies to card renewals.

RBAC

Owner: full access (view + manage subscription + portal redirect). Admin: view-only (usage, invoices, plan details — no mutation). All other roles: no access; /settings/billing is hidden from navigation.

/settings/billing/usage — Detailed Usage Breakdown

  • Date range picker: Current period, last 30/60/90 days
  • Agent-level breakdown table: Agent name, execution count, token usage, estimated cost
  • Daily usage chart (Recharts line chart): tokens per day with cursor tooltip
  • Per-user breakdown: Which team members triggered how many executions
  • Empty state: When no usage data exists (new tenant, first billing period): “No usage data yet. Usage will appear here after your agents run their first executions.” with a link to the agent configuration page.
  • Loading state: Skeleton chart placeholder and skeleton table rows during data fetch. The daily usage chart shows a pulsing placeholder until data loads.
  • “Export CSV” button: Exports the current filtered view (date range + breakdown) as a CSV file. Shows a brief spinner during generation.
RBAC

Owner and Admin only. Enforced via Hono RPC middleware requireRole(['owner','admin']).

Stripe Customer Portal Integration

Portal sessions are created server-side and the user is redirected:

TypeScript
const session = await stripe.billingPortal.sessions.create({
  customer: tenant.stripe_customer_id,
  return_url: `${APP_URL}/settings/billing`,
});
// redirect user to session.url

Portal handles: payment method update, plan changes (with proration preview), cancellation (with survey), invoice downloads.

Portal configuration:

  • Enable plan switching (between Starter / Professional / Enterprise products)
  • Enable cancellation with exit survey
  • Disable subscription pause (not supported in our billing model)

Webhook events to handle:

  • customer.subscription.updated
  • customer.subscription.deleted
  • invoice.payment_failed
  • invoice.paid

Billing State Machine

State Machine
active → past_due          // payment failed
  past_due → canceled       // after 3 retry attempts over 7 days

active → canceling          // user-initiated, runs until period end
  canceling → canceled      // period end reached

active → upgraded/downgraded // via portal, immediate proration

Webhook-Driven State Sync

Stripe EventSystem Action
invoice.paid Update tenant status = active, record payment in invoice_history
invoice.payment_failed Set tenant status = past_due, send alert email to owner, restrict non-essential features after 3 days
customer.subscription.updated Sync plan tier, seat limits, and feature flags from Stripe subscription metadata
customer.subscription.deleted Set tenant status = canceled, trigger offboarding countdown (90-day data retention per Section 5)

Usage Tracking Architecture

  • Ingestion: Agent executions logged to agent_executions table with token counts (populated via AI SDK response metadata + costTrackingMiddleware)
  • Aggregation: Daily aggregation job (BullMQ scheduled, 0 2 * * *) computes per-tenant usage summaries
  • API: Usage data exposed via internal endpoint: GET /api/v1/usage?period=current — tenant_id derived from authenticated session via withTenantContext(). Never accept client-supplied tenant IDs in URLs for data queries.
  • Alerting: Approaching-limit check runs hourly; triggers Socket.io notification + email at 80% and 100% thresholds
SQL
-- Daily aggregation query (runs via BullMQ cron)
INSERT INTO tenant_usage_daily (tenant_id, date, execution_count, token_count, estimated_cost)
SELECT
  tenant_id,
  DATE(created_at) AS date,
  COUNT(*) AS execution_count,
  SUM(input_tokens + output_tokens) AS token_count,
  SUM(cost_usd) AS estimated_cost
FROM agent_executions
WHERE created_at >= NOW() - INTERVAL '1 day'
GROUP BY tenant_id, DATE(created_at)
ON CONFLICT (tenant_id, date) DO UPDATE SET
  execution_count = EXCLUDED.execution_count,
  token_count     = EXCLUDED.token_count,
  estimated_cost  = EXCLUDED.estimated_cost;

Data Requirements

DirectionSourceDetails
Reads Supabase tenants (plan, stripe_customer_id, status), agent_executions (aggregation), tenant_usage_daily
Reads Stripe API Subscription details, invoices list, upcoming invoice preview
Reads Vercel AI SDK Real-time token usage data (via response metadata, persisted to agent_executions)
Writes None from UI Stripe Customer Portal handles all subscription mutations. Webhooks update the tenants table server-side.
External Stripe Customer Portal session URL, Invoice PDF URLs (hosted by Stripe)

Edge Cases

ScenarioUI Behavior
Payment failed (past_due) Red banner: “Payment failed. Update payment method to avoid service interruption.” + CTA button to Stripe Portal
Canceled but active until period end “Cancels on [date]” with “Reactivate” button (creates new portal session with reactivation flow)
Mid-cycle plan change Stripe handles proration automatically via portal; webhook syncs new plan to tenants table
Enterprise overage Show real-time accumulating overage amount: “Current overage: $X.XX” with link to usage detail
No invoices yet (new tenant) “First invoice on [date]” placeholder in invoice history section
Setup fee separate from recurring Clearly labeled in invoice history: “Setup Fee” vs “Monthly Subscription” line items
Free trial (future) “Trial ends on [date], X days remaining” banner above plan card
Cross-References

Wave 2 item 15 (Stripe Checkout + webhooks + customer portal) — implements the backend infrastructure this screen depends on. Section 5 (AI spending limits, daily usage sync, tenant offboarding) — defines the limit thresholds and offboarding flow triggered by cancellation. Appendix A (pricing tiers) — source of truth for plan names, prices, and feature gates.

13h. Dashboard & Agent Overview

User Stories

  • Owner/admin views a comprehensive dashboard with all agent statuses, escalation queue summary, and revenue snapshot.
  • Manager views assigned agent statuses and their scoped escalation queue.
  • Designer checks Design Agent status and pending reviews.
  • Bookkeeper reviews financial summary from Bookkeeping Agent.
  • Viewer sees a read-only dashboard with agent status and project summaries.
  • First-time user sees a welcome message with a setup checklist.

/dashboard — Main Dashboard

Layout: Role-scoped dashboard layout. Content adapts based on the authenticated user’s role within the current tenant.

Role-Scoped Layout
RoleVisible Components
Owner / AdminAll agent status cards, escalation queue summary, daily briefing card, revenue snapshot (from Bookkeeping Agent), team activity feed
ManagerAssigned agent status cards, escalation queue for their scope, task list
DesignerDesign Agent status, active project specs, pending reviews
BookkeeperBookkeeping Agent financial summary, invoice status, payment alerts
ViewerRead-only dashboard with agent status and project summaries (no action buttons)
Dashboard Components
  • Agent status cards: Agent name, status (active / paused / error), last execution time, today’s execution count. Color-coded border: green (active), gray (paused), red (error).
  • Escalation alert banner: “X items need your attention” with link to /escalations. Shown when pending escalation count > 0. Amber background, dismissible per session.
  • Daily briefing card: AI-generated morning summary from CEO Agent. Collapsed by default after first read. Timestamp of generation shown.
  • Real-time updates: Via Socket.io — agent status changes, new escalations, and completed drafts push updates to the dashboard without page reload. Connection status indicator in the bottom-right corner (green dot = connected, red dot = disconnected with auto-reconnect).
Empty State (First Login)

Layout: Welcome message with tenant name. Setup checklist card with progress indicator.

  • Checklist items: Connect integrations (link to /settings/integrations), invite team members (link to /settings/team), review first agent output (link to /escalations).
  • Each item shows a checkbox that auto-completes when the action is performed. Checklist persists until all items are completed or the user explicitly dismisses it.

/dashboard/agents — Agent List View

  • Table columns: Agent name, Status (color-coded badge), Last run (relative timestamp), Next scheduled run, Execution count (today), Actions.
  • Enable/disable toggle: Per agent. Writes to feature_flags JSONB column. Toggling off shows confirmation: “Pausing [Agent] will stop all scheduled executions. Continue?”
  • Click-through: Row click navigates to /dashboard/agents/:id.
  • Search & filter: Text search by agent name, dropdown filter by status (all / active / paused / error).
  • Empty state: When no agents are configured: illustration with “No agents set up yet. Your onboarding specialist will configure your first agents.” (Phase 1) or “No agents configured. Contact support to get started.” (Phase 2).
  • Loading state: Skeleton cards matching the agent card layout during initial data fetch.
RBAC

Owner / Admin: Full access including enable/disable toggles and configuration. Manager: View assigned agents only; no toggle access. Designer / Bookkeeper: View agents relevant to their scope only. Viewer: Read-only view of all agents.

/dashboard/agents/:id — Agent Detail

  • Agent header: Name, description, current status badge, enable/disable toggle (Owner/Admin only).
  • Configuration panel (Owner/Admin only): Prompt version selector, model routing configuration, execution limits (max runs/day, max tokens/run). Changes require confirmation and are audit-logged.
  • Execution history table: Timestamp, trigger type (scheduled / manual / webhook), duration, token cost, status (success / failed / timeout). Paginated (25 per page), sortable by any column. Filterable by status and trigger type. Date range picker for historical queries. “Export CSV” button (Owner/Admin only) exports filtered results with a progress indicator during generation.
  • Recent outputs: Last 10 drafts/actions. Each shows a summary, timestamp, and status. Pending items show Approve / Reject buttons (for users with escalation permissions). Clicking an output expands inline to show full content.

Error States

ConditionMessage ShownNotes
Agent in error stateRed status badge with “Error” label. Expandable error detail below the agent card.Auto-recovered errors clear after next successful execution.
No execution history“No executions yet. This agent will run on its next scheduled trigger.”Shown for newly configured agents.
Socket.io disconnected“Live updates paused. Reconnecting…” banner at top of dashboard.Auto-reconnect with exponential backoff. Manual refresh link provided.
Agent not foundRedirect to /dashboard/agents with toast: “Agent not found.”Handles deleted agents or invalid IDs.
Cross-References

Section 7b (Agent Execution Patterns) — defines the six agents whose status is displayed here. Section 5 (Socket.io rooms, tenant isolation) — powers the real-time dashboard updates. Section 3 (Tech Stack, NATS entry) and Section 7b (NATS event bus patterns) — agent execution events that feed the execution history table.

13i. Escalation Queue

User Stories

  • Owner/admin reviews all pending agent escalations and approves, rejects, or modifies them.
  • Manager reviews escalations within their scope and takes action.
  • User views the full audit trail of an escalation to understand what the agent proposed and what happened.

/escalations — Escalation Queue

  • Table columns: Priority (high / medium / low — color-coded: red / amber / blue), Agent name, Summary (truncated to 120 chars), Created (relative timestamp), Status (pending / approved / rejected / modified).
  • Filters: Agent (multi-select dropdown), Priority (multi-select), Status (multi-select), Date range (date picker).
  • Bulk actions toolbar (visible when rows selected): “Approve selected”, “Reject selected” (with reason modal). Available to Owner, Admin, and Manager (scoped).
  • Sort: Default by priority (high first), then by created date (oldest first). Sortable by any column.
  • Pagination: 25 items per page.
  • Real-time: New escalations push to the top of the list via Socket.io with a subtle highlight animation.
RBAC

Owner / Admin: See all escalations, full action permissions. Manager: See scope-relevant escalations only, can approve/reject within scope. Designer / Bookkeeper / Viewer: No access. Navigating to /escalations redirects to /dashboard.

/escalations/:id — Escalation Detail

  • Agent-generated draft: Full content of the proposed action, displayed in a structured format. For multi-field changes, a diff view highlights what the agent wants to modify (before/after columns).
  • Source context panel: What triggered this escalation — source event type (email received, scheduled job, webhook event), original payload summary, timestamp.
  • Action buttons:
    • “Approve” — Execute the agent’s proposed action as-is. Confirmation modal: “This will execute the action immediately. Continue?”
    • “Modify & Approve” — Opens an inline editor to adjust the draft before execution. Modified content is stored alongside the original for audit purposes.
    • “Reject” — Opens a reason modal (required text field, 500 char max). Rejection reason is stored and visible in audit trail.
    • “Reassign” — Dropdown of eligible team members (Owner, Admin, Manager). Sends a notification to the assignee.
  • Audit trail: Timeline of all actions taken on this escalation — who created it (agent), who viewed it, who approved/rejected/modified it, timestamps, and any modifications made. Immutable log.

Error States

ConditionMessage ShownNotes
Escalation already actioned“This escalation was [approved/rejected] by [Name] on [date].” Action buttons disabled.Prevents double-action race conditions.
Escalation expired (configurable TTL)“This escalation has expired and can no longer be actioned.”Expired escalations are auto-rejected with reason “Expired”.
Execution failure after approval“Approved action failed to execute. Error: [details]. Retry?”Retry button re-queues the action. Failure is logged in audit trail.
Empty queue“All clear! No escalations need your attention.”Illustrated empty state with a checkmark icon.
Cross-References

Section 7b (Agent escalation patterns) — defines when and why each agent escalates. Section 5 (Socket.io notifications) — powers real-time escalation alerts. Section 7b (NATS subject naming) — agent.*.escalation subjects route escalation events.

13j. Integration Setup

User Stories

  • Owner/admin connects a third-party service (QuickBooks, Google Calendar, Gmail, Outlook Email, Outlook Calendar, SendGrid) via OAuth, or connects JobTread via grant key (paste from app.jobtread.com/grants with guided walkthrough). For email and calendar, customers choose their provider: “Connect your email — Google or Microsoft?” and “Connect your calendar — Google or Microsoft?”
  • Owner/admin monitors integration health and reconnects if a token has expired.
  • Non-admin users view integration connection status but cannot modify connections.

/settings/integrations — Connected Integrations

Layout: Card grid (responsive: 3 columns on desktop, 2 on tablet, 1 on mobile). One card per integration.

  • Per card: Service logo, service name, connection status badge (connected / disconnected / error), last sync timestamp (relative), primary action button.
  • Health indicators:
    • Green — Healthy. Last sync within expected interval.
    • Yellow — Sync delayed > 1 hour. “Sync delayed” subtitle.
    • Red — Auth expired or error. “Reconnection required” subtitle with “Reconnect” button.
  • “Connect” button (disconnected state) — Initiates OAuth flow.
  • “Disconnect” button (connected state) — Confirmation modal: “Disconnecting [Service] will stop all automated syncing. Existing data will be retained. Continue?” Requires typing the service name to confirm.
  • Empty state: When no integrations are available for the current plan: “Your Starter plan includes 1 integration. Choose which service to connect.” When all available integrations are already connected: “All integrations connected” with health status summary.
  • Loading state: Skeleton cards matching the integration card layout during initial data fetch. Individual cards show a spinner overlay during connect/disconnect operations.
RBAC

Owner / Admin: Full access — connect, disconnect, reconnect. All other roles: View connection status only. Connect/disconnect buttons are hidden.

OAuth Connection Flow

Modal-initiated flow with provider redirect:

  • Step 1: User clicks “Connect [Service]” → client calls backend endpoint that generates the OAuth authorization URL with required scopes → user is redirected to the provider’s OAuth consent screen.
  • Step 2: User grants consent → provider redirects to callback URL → backend exchanges authorization code for access/refresh tokens → tokens stored with envelope encryption (per Section 12).
  • Step 3: Initial sync triggered → progress indicator shown on the integration card (“Syncing…” with spinner) → on completion: “Connected successfully” toast notification, card updates to green/connected state.

Error States

ConditionMessage ShownNotes
OAuth denied by user“Connection was not authorized. You can try again at any time.”No tokens stored. Card remains in disconnected state.
Token refresh failure“[Service] connection expired. Please reconnect.”Card switches to red/error state. “Reconnect” button initiates fresh OAuth flow.
Initial sync failure“Connected but initial sync failed. Retrying automatically…”Auto-retry with exponential backoff (3 attempts). After 3 failures: “Sync failed. Please disconnect and reconnect.”
Provider API outage“[Service] is experiencing issues. Sync will resume automatically.”Health indicator switches to yellow. No user action required.
Cross-References

Section 7 (Integration architecture) — defines the OAuth flows, token storage, and sync mechanisms. Section 12 (Envelope encryption) — covers how OAuth tokens are encrypted at rest. Wave 4 (JobTread adapter) and Wave 5 (QuickBooks integration) — implementation milestones.

13k. Error & Status Pages

User Stories

  • User navigates to a non-existent page and sees a helpful 404 page with navigation options.
  • User encounters a server error and sees a friendly message with a support reference ID.
  • User visits during scheduled maintenance and sees an informative holding page.
  • User sees a contextual banner when a backend service is degraded.

/404 — Not Found

Layout: Centered card, consistent with auth page styling.

  • Heading: “Page not found”
  • Subheading: “The page you’re looking for doesn’t exist or has been moved.”
  • Navigation links: “Go to Dashboard” (primary button), “Contact Support” (text link).
  • Search bar: Optional — text input with “Search” button for finding content.

/500 — Server Error

Layout: Centered card, same styling as 404.

  • Heading: “Something went wrong”
  • Subheading: “Our team has been notified and is working on it.”
  • “Try again” button: Reloads the previous route.
  • “Go to Dashboard” link: Fallback navigation.
  • Sentry error ID: Displayed in small text at the bottom: “Reference: [error-id]”. Useful for support tickets.

/maintenance — Maintenance Mode

Layout: Full-screen centered content, no navigation chrome.

  • Heading: “Scheduled Maintenance”
  • Subheading: “We’re performing scheduled maintenance to improve your experience. We’ll be back shortly.”
  • Estimated completion: “Expected back by [time] [timezone]” (if available). Otherwise: “Check back soon.”
  • Status page link: External link to status page (if configured).

Degraded State Banners

Injected via the global layout component. Displayed at the top of every page when a backend dependency is degraded. Non-dismissible while the condition persists.

DependencyBanner MessageBanner Color
Supabase“Some features are temporarily unavailable. Your data is safe.”Amber
NATS“Agent automation is temporarily paused. Manual operations are available.”Amber
AI Gateway“AI features are temporarily limited. We’re working on it.”Amber

Detection: Health check endpoints polled every 30 seconds by the backend. Degraded status pushed to connected clients via Socket.io. Banner renders/hides reactively based on a global state atom.

Cross-References

Section 10 (Health checks and monitoring) — defines the health check endpoints and alerting thresholds. Section 5 (Socket.io) — pushes degraded state events to clients. Section 12 (Sentry integration) — provides the error IDs displayed on the 500 page.

13l. Invited User Onboarding

Invited team members who accept an invitation (Section 13c) currently land on /dashboard with no guidance. This section defines a role-specific onboarding experience shown once after first login for invited users.

User Stories

  • Invited user logs in for the first time and sees a personalized welcome screen explaining their role.
  • Invited user optionally takes a guided product tour highlighting UI areas relevant to their role.
  • Invited user can skip onboarding and go directly to the dashboard.
  • Invited user can re-trigger the product tour from account settings at any time.

Screen: /welcome — Role-Specific Welcome

Layout: Centered card (max-width 560px), consistent with auth page styling. Shown once after first login for invited users (gated on tenant_members.onboarding_completed = false).

  • Heading: “Welcome to [Company Name], [First Name]!”
  • Subheading: “You’ve been added as a [Role].”
  • Role-specific summary: 3–4 bullet points explaining what the user can do, varying by role:
RoleSummary Bullets
Admin
  • Manage team members and invitations
  • Configure agents and integration connections
  • View all reports and dashboards
  • Approve or reject agent escalations
Manager
  • Approve agent drafts within your scope
  • Manage escalation queue items
  • View project status and agent activity
  • Coordinate team tasks
Designer
  • Access the Design Agent
  • Review and collaborate on design specs
  • View project timelines and status
Bookkeeper
  • View financial summaries from Bookkeeping Agent
  • Monitor invoice status and payments
  • Track payment alerts and reconciliation
Viewer
  • View dashboards and project status (read-only)
  • See agent activity summaries
  • Access reports shared with you

Actions:

  • “Take a quick tour” button — Primary CTA. Starts the role-specific product tour (overlay). Sets tenant_members.onboarding_completed = true.
  • “Skip and go to dashboard” link — Secondary action. Sets tenant_members.onboarding_completed = true and redirects to /dashboard.

Product Tour Architecture

Library: Driver.js (primary — 25K+ stars, MIT license, 5KB gzipped, React 19 compatible, framework-agnostic). Onborda as upgrade path if React 19 compatibility issues resolve (verify GitHub issues #28, #34 before adopting). Tour runs as an overlay on top of live pages — not separate pages. Loaded via dynamic(() => import(...), { ssr: false }) to avoid hydration mismatches.

Tour infrastructure cost: $0 (Driver.js is MIT, tour state in existing Supabase, no third-party SaaS). Engineering time: ~1 week for tour infrastructure + Platform Orientation tour, ~0.5 week per module tour.

See Product Tour Engineering KB and SaaS Onboarding UX KB for complete implementation patterns, library gotchas, and benchmarks.

4 Distinct Tours
TourTriggerStepsTarget RolesContent Focus
Platform OrientationFirst login (after onboarding wizard)5All rolesDashboard, agents, help bot, navigation, notifications
Business OSFirst visit to /bos5Owner, Admin, ManagerHub, KPIs, meetings, goals, issues
K&B Design CanvasFirst visit to /design/kitchen-bath5Owner, Admin, DesignerToolbar, 2D/3D toggle, AI chat, properties panel, BOM preview
Integration SetupFirst visit to /settings/integrations3Owner, AdminConnect QuickBooks/Calendar/Email, verify sync
Role-Based Tour Content
RolePlatform Tour FocusSkips
OwnerDashboard overview, agent ROI, reports, team management—
AdminSystem configuration, integrations, team invites, approvals—
ManagerTeam dashboard, escalations, approvals, project oversightSettings, billing
DesignerAgent chat, K&B canvas, design toolsReports, billing, team management
BookkeeperFinancial views, QuickBooks integration, reportsAgents, K&B canvas
ViewerDashboard (read-only), reports (read-only)All configuration, all creation
Tour UX Rules
  • Max 5 steps per tour (72% completion at 3 steps, drops to 16% at 7+ per Userpilot; Chameleon 2025 data: 4-step = 74% completion, 5+ = significant drop-off).
  • Platform Orientation is mandatory (auto-triggered on first login, no skip on first 2 steps). All module tours are self-triggered (2–3x higher completion per Chameleon 2025).
  • Max 1 tour per session — never chain tours back-to-back. 24-hour cooldown between tours.
  • “Resume tour” available in Help Bot if dismissed. Tour can also be re-triggered via Settings → “Restart product tour”.
  • Skip button visible on all steps except step 1 of Platform Orientation.
  • Progress indicator: “Step X of Y” shown in each tooltip (progress indicators improve completion by 12–40%).
  • On completion or skip: creates/updates tour_progress record. Tour can be re-triggered via Settings (resets completed to false).
  • Dismissal escalation: First dismiss → re-show in 7 days. Second dismiss → re-show in 30 days. Third dismiss → never auto-show again; switch to contextual tooltips only.
  • Selector validation: All target selectors validated at tour start. Gracefully skip steps with missing targets instead of crashing.
Construction-Specific UX
Construction Field-Use Requirements (Non-Negotiable)

These rules apply to all tour UI elements, especially K&B Design Canvas tour steps used during client consultations on tablets.

  • Touch targets: 60×60px minimum for tour navigation buttons (Next, Skip, Close) — vs standard 44×44px WCAG. Primary action buttons: 72×72px. 16px spacing between adjacent buttons.
  • High contrast tooltips: 7:1 minimum contrast ratio for outdoor/bright-light viewing. Pure white backgrounds with dark text. Add borders around buttons for glare conditions.
  • Action-oriented copy: “Tap here to see your KPIs” not “This is where your KPIs are displayed.” Start with a verb. Max 15 words per step.
  • Total tour time: Under 45 seconds per tour. Each step: 5–10 seconds to read.
  • No swipe gestures for tour navigation — tap only (gloves/calloused hands).
Setup Completion Checklist

Persistent sidebar checklist visible until all items complete. Progress bar with percentage. Dismissible after 80% completion. Pre-checks “Create account” on load to trigger the Zeigarnik effect (endowed progress).

Benchmark: Target 60%+ checklist completion. Industry average is 19.2% (median 10.1%) per Userpilot 2024. Sales-led companies average 22.1%. Kiptra’s white-glove onboarding with $5K+ setup fees should far exceed this — users who already paid are motivated to extract value.

Completion celebration: Confetti animation + “You’re all set!” modal with CTA to dashboard.

#TaskAuto-Complete Trigger
1Complete company profiletenants.onboarding_completed = true
2Connect QuickBooksintegrations row with provider='quickbooks', status='connected'
3Connect Calendarintegrations row with calendar provider connected
4Connect Emailintegrations row with email provider connected
5Invite a team membertenant_members count > 1
6Run your first agentagent_executions count > 0 for tenant
7Review your first estimatedrafts with status='approved' count > 0
Tour State Persistence

Dedicated tour_progress table tracks per-user, per-tour completion state. Replaces the simpler tenant_members.tour_completed boolean to support multiple tours, versioning, and dismissal tracking.

CREATE TABLE tour_progress (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  tenant_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES tenants(id),
  user_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
  tour_id TEXT NOT NULL, -- 'platform', 'bos', 'kb_canvas', 'integrations'
  current_step INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  completed BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT false,
  completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
  dismissed BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT false,
  dismissed_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
  tour_version INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 1,
  created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  UNIQUE(user_id, tour_id)
);
-- Tenant-scoped: RLS enabled
ALTER TABLE tour_progress ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
ALTER TABLE tour_progress FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
CREATE POLICY tour_progress_tenant_isolation ON tour_progress
  USING (tenant_id = (SELECT current_setting('app.tenant_id', true))::uuid)
  WITH CHECK (tenant_id = (SELECT current_setting('app.tenant_id', true))::uuid);
Everboarding (Ongoing Onboarding)
  • Feature micro-tours: When a new feature ships, users who haven’t seen it get a 2–3 step contextual tour on first encounter. Non-intrusive — embedded tooltip, not modal.
  • Role change tours: When a user’s role is upgraded (Viewer → Manager), trigger the relevant role-specific tour for newly accessible capabilities.
  • “What’s New” modal: Monthly changelog with optional walkthrough for major features. Beacon/badge on changelog icon (not modal interrupt) — outperforms modal announcements by 40% in engagement (Apty research). “Don’t show me updates” preference in Settings.
  • Tour versioning: tour_version in tour_progress table — when a tour is updated, users with old version see the updated tour. Prevents stale tour content pointing to renamed/moved UI elements.

Data Requirements

DirectionTargetDetails
Readstenant_membersUser role, onboarding_completed
ReadstenantsCompany name (for welcome message)
ReadsusersUser first name
Readstour_progressPer-user, per-tour completion state, dismissal count, tour version
Writestenant_membersonboarding_completed (boolean)
Writestour_progresscurrent_step, completed, dismissed, tour_version

Edge Cases

ScenarioBehavior
Role changed after onboardingDo NOT re-show the welcome screen. Show a “Your permissions have been updated” toast notification on next login. A “View updated permissions” link is available in /settings/profile. If the new role grants access to previously hidden modules, trigger the relevant role-specific tour on next visit to those modules (everboarding — see Section 13l Tour Architecture).
User belongs to multiple orgs (future)Show the welcome screen per organization on the user’s first visit to each org. onboarding_completed is scoped per tenant_members row (per org).
Tour dismissed mid-waytour_progress.dismissed = true, current_step saved for resume. Do not re-show automatically — dismissal escalation applies (7d/30d/never). User can restart via Settings or resume via Help Bot.
Owner/Admin (org creator)Does NOT see this flow. Owners go through Section 13d onboarding. This flow is exclusively for invited users.
Browser closed during welcome screenonboarding_completed remains false. Welcome screen re-appears on next login.

Routing Logic

Post-login onboarding routing (Server Component — NOT middleware):

Important: This check MUST NOT run in middleware.ts. Querying tenant_members.onboarding_completed requires a database call, which adds ~150ms blocking latency and kills Partial Prerendering (PPR). Middleware should only check cookie presence and JWT decode (no DB). Instead, implement this as a Server Component check behind a <Suspense> boundary in the authenticated layout.
