The phasing follows the EOS methodology itself: the weekly execution loop (Phase 1) is the foundation upon which people management (Phase 2) and knowledge systems (Phase 3) are built.

Phase 1 — MVP (Weeks 1–12): Core Execution Loop

The EOS methodology works because of a tight weekly execution loop: set quarterly goals → track KPIs weekly → meet weekly to review progress → resolve issues → assign action items → repeat. Phase 1 must deliver this entire loop end-to-end. Shipping individual modules without the loop is useless — users cannot adopt the methodology partially.

Modules delivered:

Module Weeks Dependencies
People Directory 1–2 None (foundation for all other modules)
Responsibility Map 2–4 People Directory
Strategic Vision Plan 3–5 People Directory
Quarterly Goals 4–7 Responsibility Map (for seat-based ownership)
KPI Dashboard 5–8 Responsibility Map
Weekly Action Items 6–8 People Directory
Issue Tracker (IRP) 7–9 People Directory
Announcements 8–9 People Directory
Meeting Hub (Weekly Sync only) 8–11 All execution modules (meeting pulls from goals, KPIs, issues, action items)
Cross-cutting: Notifications, Item Links, Basic PDF Export 10–12 All modules

Phase 1 exit criteria: A team can run a complete weekly sync meeting in Kiptra — reviewing KPI dashboards, checking quarterly goal progress, resolving issues, assigning action items, and receiving an AI-generated meeting recap. The full execution loop is operational.

Phase 2 — Growth (Weeks 12–20): People & Analytics

With the execution loop running, Phase 2 adds the people management and analytics layers that drive long-term organizational health.

Module Weeks Dependencies
Team Fit Assessment 12–14 People Directory, Responsibility Map
Quarterly Reviews 13–16 Team Fit Assessment, Quarterly Goals
My Dashboard 14–16 All Phase 1 modules
Analytics Dashboard 15–18 All Phase 1 modules (materialized views)
Calendar Sync (Google + Microsoft) 16–18 Meeting Hub
Meeting Types Expansion (quarterly, annual, departmental) 18–20 Meeting Hub

Phase 2 exit criteria: Teams can conduct quarterly reviews with core values scoring, managers can assess team fit, users have personalized dashboards, and meetings sync to external calendars.

Phase 3 — Premium (Weeks 20–28): Knowledge & AI

Phase 3 delivers the premium-tier features that differentiate Kiptra from Ninety.io and create long-term competitive moats.

Module Weeks Dependencies
Knowledge Portal 20–24 People Directory, Responsibility Map
SOP Builder 22–24 Knowledge Portal (shared editor infrastructure)
Business Health Assessment 23–25 People Directory
AI Assistant 24–27 All modules (conversational access to full data)
Public API + Webhooks 26–28 All modules (REST endpoints for every entity)

Phase 3 exit criteria: Full Knowledge Portal with seat-based assignment and progress tracking, AI assistant capable of conversational SMART goal creation and meeting prep, and a public API with webhook subscriptions that surpasses Ninety.io’s zero-integration offering.

Total timeline: 18–28 weeks depending on team size and parallelization opportunities.

Timeline & Resource Dependencies

This timeline assumes AI-driven development (Claude Code, Cursor, AI agent pipelines) with Joseph as the sole developer, consistent with the core platform build approach. Business OS development begins after the Kiptra core platform achieves stability (post-launch, approximately Month 8 of the overall timeline — see Section 9 Post-Launch Roadmap). ~80–90% of Business OS modules are AI-buildable (schemas, state machines, CRUD, API routes, dashboard UI, validation, tests). The remaining ~10–20% requiring human judgment (XState edge cases, ProseMirror collaboration tuning, meeting UX polish) is handled by Joseph directly. No additional engineering hires are required — AI tooling replaces the traditional 2–4 engineer team assumption.

15.8 Integration with Kiptra Platform

The Business Operating System is not a standalone application — it is a feature module within the Kiptra AI platform. This means it shares core infrastructure while introducing targeted new dependencies.

Shared Infrastructure (Reuse Directly)

Component Kiptra Platform Layer Business OS Usage
Database Supabase (Postgres 15+) All Business OS tables, RLS policies, materialized views, ltree extension
API Server Hono (Node.js runtime) New route groups: /api/bos/* for all Business OS endpoints
Frontend Next.js 16 (App Router) New route group: /app/(bos)/* for Business OS pages
Job Queue BullMQ + Valkey Meeting timers, notification delivery, PDF generation, calendar sync, KPI data ingestion
Infrastructure Docker + DigitalOcean + Cloudflare No new infrastructure — Business OS runs in existing containers
AI Vercel AI SDK + Vercel AI Gateway Meeting recap generation, SMART goal assistance, KPI trend analysis, issue prioritization
Real-time Socket.io + NATS JetStream In-app notifications (Socket.io), agent-to-agent events (NATS), live meeting collaboration
Auth better-auth Same auth layer, extended with seat-based permissions for Business OS modules

New Infrastructure (Business OS Specific)

Dependency Version Purpose Bundle Impact
XState v5 State machines for meetings, goals, reviews, approvals ~15 KB gzipped (tree-shakeable)
TipTap v2 Rich-text editor for Knowledge Portal, meeting notes, vision plan narratives ~50 KB gzipped (with extensions)
Recharts v2 Interactive charts, sparklines, KPI trends, and analytics — all chart types (line, bar, area, combo, sparklines) + shadcn/ui Card components for KPI card layouts ~45 KB gzipped
React-PDF v4 Programmatic PDF generation for reports and recaps Server-side only
Puppeteer v23 HTML-to-PDF for complex visual reports (KPI snapshots) Server-side only (Docker sidecar)
React Grid Layout v1 Draggable/resizable dashboard widget layout ~20 KB gzipped
Google Calendar API v3 Two-way calendar sync for meetings Server-side only (googleapis)
Microsoft Graph (MSAL) v2 Outlook calendar sync for meetings Server-side only (@azure/msal-node)

Key Competitive Advantages vs. Ninety.io

# Advantage Ninety.io Reality Kiptra Approach
1 Open API + Webhooks Zero API. Zero webhooks. Zero integration capability. Complete data silo. Full REST API for every Business OS entity. Webhook subscriptions for all events. MCP server for AI agent access.
2 Automated KPI Data 100% manual data entry. Users must remember to update every measurable weekly. Automated data feeds from QuickBooks (financials), CRM (sales pipeline), and project management (job progress, change orders).
3 Construction-Specific AI Context Generic across all industries. No domain awareness. AI agents understand construction terminology, common KPIs (backlog, WIP, AR aging), and industry-specific goal templates.
4 Flat Competitive Pricing $11.20–$16/user/month across 4 tiers (Essentials, Accelerate, Thrive, Summit). Business OS features included in Kiptra subscription. No per-user Business OS surcharge. No feature gating across artificial tiers.
5 AI Meeting Summaries AI-assisted meeting preparation only (pre-meeting briefs). No post-meeting recap generation. Full AI meeting recaps: auto-generated summaries, action item extraction, issue identification, KPI commentary, and next-meeting agenda seeding.
Strategic Positioning

Kiptra AI is the first platform to combine construction project management with a complete Business Operating System. Construction companies currently pay for two separate tools (PM software + Ninety.io) that don’t talk to each other. Kiptra eliminates this gap: project data flows directly into KPI dashboards, job milestones map to quarterly goals, and AI agents have full context across both domains. This is not a feature — it is a category-defining integration.

16. Kitchen & Bath AI Design Tool

A browser-based 3D kitchen and bathroom design tool that replaces legacy Windows-only CAD software with an AI-powered, collaborative platform. This module leverages three AI engines in a closed feedback loop to generate, validate, and evaluate designs in real time — bringing professional-grade kitchen design to the browser.

Deployment model: The K&B design editor is embedded directly within the Design Agent’s chat window in the Kiptra dashboard — not a separate app or subdomain. The designer converses with the Design Agent (“Create an L-shaped kitchen, 12×14, white shaker cabinets, quartz countertops”), and the 3D viewport renders inline alongside the chat. This preserves the existing auth flow (no separate login), shares Zustand state with the dashboard, and keeps the design workflow within the agent interaction paradigm that defines the entire Kiptra platform. The kb-editor package exports a <DesignViewport /> React component consumed by the agent chat UI.

KB-Derived Amendments (March 2026)

This section is informed by the full Kiptra knowledge base corpus: kiptra-expertise-v2 (46 domains, 139 files, 92,459 lines), kitchen-bath-design-tool-v2 (31 domains, 102 files, 51,104 lines), and legal-kbs (27 domains, 81 files, 44,870 lines) — totaling 583+ files and ~350K lines (~10.5 MB) across three layers (official documentation, street knowledge, and insider knowledge). Three additional competitor KBs (Chief Architect, Revit, Cyncly/2020 Design) provide strategic vulnerability mapping. All KBs completed 3 audit rounds with a verified quality score of 9.3/10. Key findings from these KBs are woven into the subsections below.

Cyncly IP Risk

Cyncly’s 2020 Design format files are proprietary. While they are listed as a data source (Section 16.6), direct parsing or reverse-engineering of these files may expose Kiptra to intellectual property claims. Mitigation: (1) Prioritize non-proprietary data sources — direct manufacturer feeds (CSV/XML), manufacturer-specific order formats, PDF spec sheet extraction, and aggregator APIs (KitchenDEV/Kitchen365). (2) Do NOT reverse-engineer 2020 Design file formats. (3) If manufacturer data is only available via 2020 Design exports, negotiate explicit written data portability rights through Adam’s manufacturer relationships. (4) Legal review of Cyncly’s EULA terms regarding data export before using any 2020 Design-sourced data. See also Risk Register items #27 and #28.

Electronic Cabinet Ordering Pipeline

Manufacturer-specific electronic ordering enables direct order submission from design software to manufacturer systems. The BOM export (Section 16.7.1) supports this pipeline. The full ordering pipeline: (1) Designer finalizes layout → (2) BOM generated from scene graph → (3) Electronic order export packages line items with manufacturer SKUs, quantities, finishes, and pricing in the manufacturer’s required format (CSV, custom XML, or portal upload) → (4) Dealer submits order directly to manufacturer ordering system. This eliminates manual order entry (currently 30–60 min per order) and is a significant differentiator vs. consumer-grade tools (Planner 5D, Coohom, Spacely) that have no ordering integration. Adam’s dealer relationships are required to validate format compatibility with each manufacturer’s ordering system.

Interactive Design Canvas (March 2026)

The K&B design tool features an interactive 2D/Elevation/3D canvas with a UI deliberately matching 2020 Design conventions to minimize designer retraining:

  • Canvas interaction: Drag-and-drop object placement, snap-to-grid at 1″ increments, zoom range 0.25x–4.0x with smooth scroll/pinch, pan via middle-click or spacebar+drag.
  • Ribbon toolbar (6 tabs): FILE (new/open/save/export), ROOM (walls/doors/windows/room shape), ITEMS (cabinets/appliances/fixtures/countertops), ANNOTATE (dimensions/notes/labels), PRESENT (render/walkthrough/client view), VIEW (2D/elevation/3D toggle, grid, snap settings).
  • 2020 Design-matching UI: Green grid background (#e8f5e9), dimension chains with leader lines, double-line wall rendering, catalog thumbnail sidebar with drag-to-canvas, properties panel with real-time dimension editing.
  • NKBA constraint violations overlay: 20 rules validated in real-time via Z3 solver, displayed as colored overlays — P1/Critical (red, blocks export), P2/Warning (amber, requires acknowledgment), P3/Advisory (blue, informational). Violation badges show count per severity on the status bar.
  • Catalog browser: 100+ cabinet SKUs from Fabuwood, KraftMaid, and Merillat. Filterable by type (base/wall/tall/vanity), width, finish, and door style. Drag from catalog to canvas with automatic snap and NKBA validation.
  • BOM preview: Live bill-of-materials panel updated on every scene change, with export to CSV and PDF. Includes item counts, dimensions, SKUs, unit costs, and subtotals by category.
  • Door style configurator: 7 styles (Shaker, Slab, Raised Panel, Recessed Panel, Beadboard, Glass Front, Louvered) with real-time 3D preview. Per-cabinet override or apply-to-all.
  • Collaboration: Multi-user presence indicators (colored cursors with user names), real-time co-editing via CRDT (Yjs), autosave every 30 seconds with manual save trigger.

16.1 Overview & Strategic Rationale

The Kitchen & Bath AI Design Tool is a ground-up reimagining of how kitchen and bathroom spaces are designed. It replaces Cyncly’s 2020 Design — the incumbent tool that dominates the K&B industry — with a modern, browser-based alternative powered by AI.

Key insight: 2020 Design is Windows-only, crash-prone, and costs $2,500–$3,000/year after private equity extraction. Designers hate it but remain locked in because manufacturer catalogs are only available through its ecosystem. Catalog data is available through manufacturer relationships, aggregators (KitchenDEV, Kitchen365), and public spec sheets. While Cyncly’s ecosystem creates friction, underlying product data is not exclusively controlled by any single vendor — meaning the lock-in is largely artificial.

Why Kiptra builds this:

  • Co-founder validation: Adam runs a K&B design and remodel business with direct manufacturer relationships. He has confirmed demand from peers who would switch immediately given a viable alternative.
  • Market disruption: Legacy CAD tools (Revit, 2020 Design, ProKitchen) are desktop-bound, expensive, and have not innovated in a decade. A browser-based, AI-powered tool with real-time collaboration is a generational leap.
  • Catalog data is accessible: Manufacturer product data (dimensions, finishes, pricing, 3D models) is available through multiple channels — dealer portal exports, FTP data drops, partnership-negotiated access (requires Adam’s manufacturer relationships), 2020 Design format files, PDF spec sheets, and aggregators like KitchenDEV/Kitchen365.
  • AI makes it possible now: LLMs can generate spatial layouts from natural language. SMT solvers can formally verify building code compliance. Vision models can evaluate rendered designs for aesthetics and ergonomics. None of this was feasible two years ago.

16.2 Three-Model AI Loop Architecture

The core innovation is three AI engines operating in a closed feedback loop. Each model has a distinct role, and together they produce code-compliant, aesthetically sound kitchen layouts from natural language input.

Flow
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    THREE-MODEL AI LOOP                              │
│                                                                     │
│   ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────────┐     │
│   │   CLAUDE      │    │   Z3 SMT     │    │   GEMINI 3.0     │     │
│   │   Sonnet/Opus │    │   Solver     │    │   Pro            │     │
│   │              │    │              │    │                  │     │
│   │  NL Input → │    │  Formal NKBA │    │  Rendered image  │     │
│   │  JSON Layout │    │  Validation  │    │  evaluation      │     │
│   └──────┬───────┘    └──────┬───────┘    └────────┬─────────┘     │
│          │                   │                     │               │
│          ▼                   ▼                     ▼               │
│   ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
│   │                   ITERATION PROTOCOL                      │     │
│   │                                                           │     │
│   │  1. Claude generates layout (cabinet runs → coordinates)  │     │
│   │  2. Z3 validates NKBA constraints (UNSAT cores on fail)   │     │
│   │  3. Three.js renders 2D floor plan image                  │     │
│   │  4. Gemini evaluates spatial flow, balance, ergonomics    │     │
│   │  5. If issues → compile fix list → return to Claude       │     │
│   │  6. Max 5 rounds · Single-round: ~15-25s · Multi-round (2-5): ~30-75s             │     │
│   └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Layout Generation (Claude Sonnet/Opus)

Natural language input is converted to structured JSON kitchen layouts using a cabinet “runs” abstraction — not individual (x,y) coordinates. A run is a sequence of cabinets along a wall segment. A deterministic function converts runs to precise coordinates, eliminating the geometric errors that plague direct LLM coordinate generation.

AI Spatial Reasoning Limitation

LLM spatial reasoning has known limitations — models struggle with precise geometric placement and distance estimation. The cabinet-run abstraction with deterministic coordinate resolution works around these limitations by keeping LLMs in the semantic domain (which cabinets, in what order) rather than the geometric domain (exact x,y coordinates). The Z3 solver catches geometric constraint violations that LLMs cannot reliably avoid, ensuring NKBA compliance regardless of LLM output quality.

TypeScript
// Claude outputs cabinet runs, not coordinates
interface CabinetRun {
  wallId: string;
  startOffset: number;     // inches from wall start
  direction: 'left-to-right' | 'right-to-left';
  cabinets: RunCabinet[];  // sequential, no gaps
}

// Deterministic function — no LLM geometry errors
function resolveRunToCoordinates(
  run: CabinetRun,
  walls: Wall[]
): PlacedCabinet[] {
  const wall = walls.find(w => w.id === run.wallId);
  let cursor = run.startOffset;
  return run.cabinets.map(cab => {
    const pos = wallPointAtOffset(wall, cursor);
    cursor += cab.width;
    return { ...cab, x: pos.x, y: pos.y, rotation: wall.angle };
  });
}

Constraint Validation (Z3 SMT Solver)

The Z3 SMT solver provides formal NKBA compliance validation. Of the 31 NKBA Kitchen Planning Guidelines, 24 are fully encodable as mathematical constraints. When a layout fails validation, Z3 returns UNSAT cores — the minimal set of constraints that conflict — telling Claude exactly what to fix.

Visual Evaluation (Gemini 3.0 Pro)

After Z3 validates the layout, Three.js renders a 2D floor plan image. Gemini 3.0 Pro receives this rendered image and evaluates spatial flow, visual balance, ergonomics, aesthetics, and safety via a structured JSON scoring rubric. This catches qualitative issues that formal constraints cannot express. Note: Gemini 3.0 Pro (or latest available) is selected for its top-ranked vision capabilities (ranked #1 on LMArena Vision leaderboard). The AI evaluation step uses a lightweight 2D floor plan render (<1 sec), not the full 3D server render.

16.3 Three.js Rendering Pipeline (3-Tier)

TierMethodSpeedUse CasePhase
Level 1 — Real-time Three.js MeshStandardMaterial, environment maps, shadow maps 60fps Interactive editing, drag-and-drop Phase 1
Level 2 — Path-traced three-gpu-pathtracer with global illumination, soft shadows, caustics 5–30 sec Design review, client presentations Phase 2
Level 3 — AI Enhanced Flux/SD img2img + ControlNet + Real-ESRGAN upscaling 10–30 sec Photorealistic output for proposals Phase 2

View System

A single Three.js scene graph serves both 2D and 3D views. The 2D view uses an OrthographicCamera top-down; the 3D view uses a PerspectiveCamera walkthrough. Camera swap toggles views — no geometry rebuild, no state duplication. The / key toggles between views.

16.3.1 Low-End Device Strategy & Graceful Degradation

Construction industry users access the K&B Design Tool on a wide range of hardware — from high-end workstations in design studios to budget Android tablets on jobsites. The rendering pipeline must degrade gracefully across four capability tiers, ensuring every user gets a functional experience even on devices that cannot run WebGL. The strategy is: detect capabilities at runtime, assign a tier, enforce performance budgets per tier, and fall back to server-rendered content for the lowest tier.

16.3.1.1 Minimum Requirements & Tier Definitions

Tier Name GPU Requirement Browser Rendering Mode User Experience
Tier 1 Full 3D WebGL 2.0 + discrete GPU (NVIDIA GTX 1060+ / AMD RX 580+ / Apple M1+) with ≥2 GB VRAM Chrome 90+, Firefox 90+, Safari 16.4+, Edge 90+ Full PBR materials, shadow maps (2048×2048), environment map reflections, path-traced preview (Phase 2) Interactive 3D editing at 60fps. Full feature set. 2D/3D view toggle.
Tier 2 Simplified 3D WebGL 2.0 + integrated GPU (Intel UHD 620+, Intel Iris Xe, AMD Vega) with ≥512 MB shared VRAM Chrome 90+, Firefox 90+, Safari 16.4+, Edge 90+ Simplified materials (MeshLambertMaterial), no shadow maps, single directional light, no reflections, no path tracing Interactive 3D editing at 30–60fps. Reduced visual fidelity. 2D/3D toggle available.
Tier 3 2D Only WebGL 1.0 minimum (or WebGL 2.0 with very low GPU memory). Chromebooks, older iPads, budget Android tablets. Chrome 80+, Safari 14+ 2D orthographic floor plan only. No 3D viewport. Canvas2D fallback for rendering if WebGL unstable. Server-side 3D renders on demand. 2D floor plan editing. “Request 3D Preview” button triggers server render (5–15 sec). Static image result.
Tier 4 View Only No WebGL support, or device fails all GPU benchmarks. Any modern browser Pre-rendered static images only. No interactive canvas. Read-only project viewer with zoom/pan on images. View designs as image gallery. Add comments/annotations on static images. Cannot edit. “Open on a desktop for editing” banner.

16.3.1.2 Runtime Feature Detection

Device capability detection runs once on application load and caches the result in localStorage (key: kiptra_kb_device_tier, TTL: 7 days). Users can manually override their tier in Settings (“Graphics Quality” dropdown). The detection pipeline takes <500ms.

TypeScript
// Device capability detection — runs once on app load
interface DeviceCapabilities {
  webglVersion: 0 | 1 | 2;
  gpuRenderer: string;           // e.g., "NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3070"
  gpuVendor: string;             // e.g., "NVIDIA Corporation"
  maxTextureSize: number;        // e.g., 16384
  maxRenderbufferSize: number;
  maxViewportDims: [number, number];
  maxVertexAttribs: number;
  maxVaryingVectors: number;
  maxTextureImageUnits: number;
  gpuMemoryMB: number | null;   // WEBGL_debug_renderer_info estimate
  benchmarkFPS: number;          // from 2-second micro-benchmark
  devicePixelRatio: number;
  screenWidth: number;
  isMobile: boolean;
  isTablet: boolean;
  hardwareConcurrency: number;
  deviceMemoryGB: number | undefined; // navigator.deviceMemory
}

function detectDeviceCapabilities(): DeviceCapabilities {
  // 1. Check WebGL support
  const canvas = document.createElement('canvas');
  let gl = canvas.getContext('webgl2') as WebGL2RenderingContext | null;
  let webglVersion: 0 | 1 | 2 = gl ? 2 : 0;

  if (!gl) {
    gl = canvas.getContext('webgl') as WebGL2RenderingContext | null;
    if (gl) webglVersion = 1;
  }

  if (!gl) return { webglVersion: 0, /* ... all null/zero ... */ };

  // 2. Extract GPU info via WEBGL_debug_renderer_info
  const debugInfo = gl.getExtension('WEBGL_debug_renderer_info');
  const gpuRenderer = debugInfo
    ? gl.getParameter(debugInfo.UNMASKED_RENDERER_WEBGL)
    : 'unknown';
  const gpuVendor = debugInfo
    ? gl.getParameter(debugInfo.UNMASKED_VENDOR_WEBGL)
    : 'unknown';

  // 3. Query GPU limits
  const maxTextureSize = gl.getParameter(gl.MAX_TEXTURE_SIZE);
  const maxRenderbufferSize = gl.getParameter(gl.MAX_RENDERBUFFER_SIZE);
  const maxTextureImageUnits = gl.getParameter(gl.MAX_TEXTURE_IMAGE_UNITS);

  // 4. Estimate GPU memory (heuristic — no reliable API exists)
  const gpuMemoryMB = estimateGPUMemory(gpuRenderer, maxTextureSize);

  // 5. Run micro-benchmark (render 10k triangles for 2 seconds)
  const benchmarkFPS = runGPUBenchmark(gl);

  return { webglVersion, gpuRenderer, gpuVendor, maxTextureSize, /* ... */ };
}

// GPU memory estimation from renderer string + texture size
function estimateGPUMemory(renderer: string, maxTexSize: number): number | null {
  const known: Record<string, number> = {
    'RTX 4090': 24576, 'RTX 4080': 16384, 'RTX 3070': 8192,
    'GTX 1060': 6144,  'RX 580': 8192,   'Apple M1': 8192,
    'Apple M2': 8192,  'Apple M3': 8192,  'Intel UHD': 512,
    'Intel Iris': 1024, 'Mali': 256,       'Adreno': 512,
  };
  for (const [key, mem] of Object.entries(known)) {
    if (renderer.includes(key)) return mem;
  }
  // Fallback: estimate from max texture size
  if (maxTexSize >= 16384) return 4096;  // likely discrete GPU
  if (maxTexSize >= 8192)  return 1024;  // likely integrated
  if (maxTexSize >= 4096)  return 512;   // likely mobile
  return 256;                             // low-end mobile
}

Tier Assignment Logic

TypeScript
type RenderTier = 1 | 2 | 3 | 4;

function assignRenderTier(caps: DeviceCapabilities): RenderTier {
  // Tier 4: No WebGL at all
  if (caps.webglVersion === 0) return 4;

  // Tier 4: WebGL present but benchmark completely fails
  if (caps.benchmarkFPS < 5) return 4;

  // Tier 3: WebGL 1.0 only, OR very low GPU memory, OR very low benchmark
  if (
    caps.webglVersion === 1 ||
    (caps.gpuMemoryMB !== null && caps.gpuMemoryMB < 512) ||
    caps.benchmarkFPS < 15 ||
    caps.maxTextureSize < 4096
  ) return 3;

  // Tier 1: Discrete GPU OR Apple Silicon, high benchmark, WebGL 2.0
  const isDiscreteGPU =
    caps.gpuRenderer.match(/NVIDIA|Radeon|Apple M[1-9]/i) !== null;
  const hasEnoughMemory =
    caps.gpuMemoryMB === null || caps.gpuMemoryMB >= 2048;

  if (
    isDiscreteGPU &&
    hasEnoughMemory &&
    caps.benchmarkFPS >= 45 &&
    caps.maxTextureSize >= 8192
  ) return 1;

  // Tier 2: Everything else with WebGL 2.0 (integrated GPUs, mid-range)
  return 2;
}

Micro-Benchmark Details

The GPU benchmark renders a rotating scene with 10,000 textured triangles (a single BufferGeometry) for exactly 120 frames, measuring actual frame delivery time via performance.now(). Total benchmark time is ~2 seconds at 60fps. The benchmark canvas is 256×256 pixels (offscreen) to minimize overhead. Result is median FPS across the 120 frames, excluding the first 10 frames (warm-up). The benchmark is not Three.js — it uses raw WebGL to avoid loading the Three.js bundle for devices that will ultimately be assigned Tier 3 or 4.

16.3.1.3 Performance Budgets Per Tier

Budget Category Tier 1 (Full 3D) Tier 2 (Simplified 3D) Tier 3 (2D Only) Tier 4 (View Only)
Target Frame Rate 60 fps 30 fps (with 60fps goal) N/A (static 2D) N/A
Max Polygon Count 500,000 triangles 150,000 triangles N/A N/A
Max Draw Calls 200 per frame 80 per frame N/A N/A
Max Texture Memory 512 MB 128 MB 32 MB (2D textures only) 0 (images served as JPEG/WebP)
Max Texture Resolution 2048×2048 per texture 1024×1024 per texture 512×512 (floor/wall patterns) N/A
Shadow Map Resolution 2048×2048 (PCF soft) None (shadows disabled) N/A N/A
Max Lights 8 (2 directional + 6 point/spot) 2 (1 directional + 1 ambient) N/A N/A
Environment Map 256×256 HDR cubemap None N/A N/A
Material Type MeshStandardMaterial (PBR) MeshLambertMaterial Canvas2D fills/strokes Static images
Anti-Aliasing MSAA 4x FXAA (post-process) Canvas2D native AA N/A
Max Scene Nodes 500 200 200 (2D representations) N/A
LOD Levels 3 (high/med/low) 2 (med/low) N/A N/A
JS Bundle (3D) ~450 KB gzipped (Three.js + R3F + Drei) ~450 KB gzipped (same bundle, reduced runtime features) ~15 KB (Canvas2D renderer only) ~5 KB (image viewer)
Initial Load Time <3 sec (broadband) <3 sec (broadband) <1 sec <500ms

Key insight: The polygon budget is per-scene, not per-object. A typical kitchen with 30 cabinets, 5 appliances, countertops, and walls consumes ~80,000–120,000 triangles on Tier 1 (procedurally generated cabinets at ~2,000 triangles each). This leaves substantial headroom. LOD management only triggers when users load unusually complex scenes (large kitchens with 50+ cabinets or high-detail imported manufacturer 3D models).

16.3.1.4 Tier-Specific Rendering Configuration

TypeScript
// Rendering configuration per tier — applied at scene initialization
import type { WebGLRendererParameters } from 'three';

interface TierConfig {
  renderer: Partial<WebGLRendererParameters> & {
    shadowMapEnabled: boolean;
    shadowMapSize: number;
    pixelRatio: number;
    toneMapping: number;
  };
  materials: {
    type: 'standard' | 'lambert' | 'basic';
    envMapIntensity: number;
    maxTextureRes: number;
  };
  scene: {
    maxLights: number;
    maxDrawCalls: number;
    maxPolygons: number;
    lodBias: number;          // 0 = highest detail, 2 = lowest
    enableShadows: boolean;
    enableReflections: boolean;
    enableAO: boolean;
    enablePathTracing: boolean;
    antiAliasMode: 'msaa' | 'fxaa' | 'none';
  };
}

const TIER_CONFIGS: Record<1 | 2, TierConfig> = {
  1: {
    renderer: {
      antialias: true,
      shadowMapEnabled: true,
      shadowMapSize: 2048,
      pixelRatio: Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio, 2),
      toneMapping: 4, // ACESFilmicToneMapping
    },
    materials: {
      type: 'standard',
      envMapIntensity: 1.0,
      maxTextureRes: 2048,
    },
    scene: {
      maxLights: 8,
      maxDrawCalls: 200,
      maxPolygons: 500000,
      lodBias: 0,
      enableShadows: true,
      enableReflections: true,
      enableAO: true,
      enablePathTracing: true,  // Phase 2
      antiAliasMode: 'msaa',
    },
  },
  2: {
    renderer: {
      antialias: false,  // FXAA instead
      shadowMapEnabled: false,
      shadowMapSize: 0,
      pixelRatio: 1,  // Force 1x for performance
      toneMapping: 0, // NoToneMapping
    },
    materials: {
      type: 'lambert',
      envMapIntensity: 0,
      maxTextureRes: 1024,
    },
    scene: {
      maxLights: 2,
      maxDrawCalls: 80,
      maxPolygons: 150000,
      lodBias: 1,
      enableShadows: false,
      enableReflections: false,
      enableAO: false,
      enablePathTracing: false,
      antiAliasMode: 'fxaa',
    },
  },
};

16.3.1.5 Server-Side Rendering — Tiered Escalation (Tiers 3 & 4)

Devices assigned Tier 3 or Tier 4 cannot run interactive 3D rendering. Instead, the server renders 3D previews on demand via serverless GPU functions on Modal.com (or RunPod). This replaces the previous CPU software rendering approach — serverless GPU is 5–10x faster, produces real GPU quality (hardware-accelerated ray tracing), and costs less than dedicated GPU instances thanks to per-second billing with scale-to-zero.

Component Technology Details
Render Server Modal.com serverless GPU (NVIDIA A10/L4) BullMQ queues render requests on VPS. Each request triggers a Modal serverless function running three-gpu-pathtracer headless on a GPU container. Concurrency is cloud-scaled (not VPS-bound) — no impact on VPS CPU/memory budget. Zero idle cost (per-second billing, scale to zero).
Request Flow BullMQ job queue → Modal API Client sends POST /api/kb/render with { projectId, viewType, resolution }. API creates BullMQ job. Worker calls Modal serverless function via HTTPS API. Modal renders scene on GPU, uploads PNG/WebP to DO Spaces / R2, returns CDN URL. Client polls job status or receives Socket.io push on completion.
Render Quality Superior to Tier 1 (real GPU) Server renders with full PBR materials, shadows, environment maps, and hardware-accelerated ray tracing. Output is 1920×1080 PNG (perspective) or 2400×1800 PNG (floor plan). WebP conversion for bandwidth savings (~60% smaller). Real GPU quality — not software emulation.
Render Time 1–3 seconds (4K) GPU rendering is 5–10x faster than CPU software rendering. Typical kitchen: ~1–2 seconds. Complex scenes: ~3 seconds. Results cached per project version — re-render only on scene change.
Caching DO Spaces / R2 + Valkey Renders cached by projectId + version + viewType + resolution hash. Cache TTL: indefinite (invalidated on scene edit). Valkey stores cache keys for fast lookup. Storage cost: ~200 KB per render (WebP). CDN serves cached renders globally.
View Types 4 pre-defined camera positions (1) Top-down floor plan, (2) 3/4 perspective overview, (3) Eye-level walkthrough from kitchen entry, (4) Detail view of primary work triangle. User can request custom viewpoint via angle/position parameters.
Tiered Escalation Path
Tier Technology When Cost per Render Monthly at 10K Renders Quality
Tier 1 (Launch) three-gpu-pathtracer headless on Modal serverless GPU (A10/L4) Day 1 ~$0.001 ~$10–30 Same Three.js scene graph as client — no format conversion. Real GPU path tracing. $0.0003/sec, 1–3 sec per 4K render.
Tier 2 (v2) Blender Cycles headless on Modal/RunPod When photorealism demanded ~$0.05 ~$500–800 glTF-to-Blender conversion pipeline. Production-quality photorealistic rendering. Open source (GPL), no licensing fees.
Tier 3 (Premium) V-Ray App SDK self-hosted When revenue justifies ~$0.10 + licensing ~$1,000 + $10–50K/yr SDK Best-in-class photorealistic quality (Emmy/Academy Award-winning). Purpose-built home design variant available. Bulgarian company (Chaos Group) — EU jurisdiction, no geopolitical risk. 
Architecture
Browser: three-gpu-pathtracer (interactive preview, real-time, 60fps)
    ↓ API: "Render at 4K"
Modal/RunPod Serverless GPU (A10, per-second billing)
    ├── Tier 1: three-gpu-pathtracer headless (fast, $0.001/render)
    ├── Tier 2: Blender Cycles headless (photorealistic, ~$0.05/render)
    └── Tier 3: V-Ray App SDK (premium, ~$0.10/render + licensing)
    ↓
DO Spaces / R2 Storage → CDN → Client receives rendered image
TypeScript
// Server-side render request handler (serverless GPU)
import { Queue } from 'bullmq';

const renderQueue = new Queue('kb-server-render', { connection: redis });

app.post('/api/kb/render', async (c) => {
  const { projectId, viewType, resolution } = c.req.valid('json');
  const project = await getProject(projectId);

  // Check cache first
  const cacheKey = `render:${projectId}:${project.version}:${viewType}:${resolution}`;
  const cached = await redis.get(cacheKey);
  if (cached) return c.json({ url: cached, cached: true });

  // Queue render job — dispatched to Modal serverless GPU
  const job = await renderQueue.add('render', {
    projectId,
    sceneData: project.scene_data,
    viewType,      // 'floor_plan' | 'perspective' | 'eye_level' | 'detail'
    resolution,    // '1080p' | '1440p' | '4k'
    renderTier: 'pathtracer', // 'pathtracer' | 'blender_cycles' | 'vray'
    format: 'webp',
    quality: 85,
  }, {
    priority: 1,
    removeOnComplete: { age: 3600 },
    attempts: 2,
  });

  return c.json({ jobId: job.id, status: 'queued', estimatedSeconds: 3 });
});

16.3.1.6 Mobile & Tablet Considerations

Concern iPad (Tier 1–2) Android Tablet (Tier 2–3) Phone (Tier 3–4)
Touch Controls One-finger: select/drag. Two-finger pinch: zoom. Two-finger drag: pan. Three-finger: orbit (3D only). Long press: context menu. Double tap: center on object. Same as iPad View-only gestures: pinch-zoom, pan on static images. No editing.
Viewport Sizing Canvas fills available height minus toolbar (44px) and bottom panel (48px). ResizeObserver handles orientation changes. Minimum canvas: 600×400px. Same as iPad. If screen < 600px wide, switch to Tier 3 regardless of GPU. Full-screen image viewer with zoom. No canvas.
Orientation Landscape recommended. Portrait shows “Rotate for best experience” banner (non-blocking). Both orientations work. 2D floor plan rotates with device. Same as iPad Portrait only for view mode. Image auto-rotates.
Performance iPad Pro M-series: Tier 1. iPad Air/base iPad: Tier 2. Force pixelRatio: 1 on non-Pro iPads to maintain 30fps. Most Android tablets: Tier 2 or 3. Chromebooks with ARM GPUs: Tier 3. Samsung Tab S-series: Tier 2. Always Tier 3 or 4. No 3D rendering on phones.
Thermal Throttling Monitor requestAnimationFrame interval. If FPS drops below 20 for 10+ consecutive frames, auto-downgrade to next tier. Notify user: “Simplified rendering enabled to prevent overheating.” Same as iPad, but more aggressive: downgrade at 15fps. N/A
Memory Pressure Monitor performance.memory (Chrome) or estimate from texture allocations. If estimated GPU memory usage exceeds 70% of tier budget, start unloading LOD levels and reducing texture resolution. Same as iPad. Android has stricter memory limits — use 60% threshold. N/A
Offline Support Scene data cached in IndexedDB. Edits queue locally, sync on reconnect. Server renders unavailable offline — show last cached render with “Offline” badge. Same as iPad Cached images only. No editing offline.

16.3.1.7 Adaptive Quality at Runtime

Beyond initial tier assignment, the renderer continuously monitors performance and adapts quality dynamically during interactive sessions.

TypeScript
// Adaptive quality controller — monitors FPS and adjusts rendering
class AdaptiveQualityController {
  private frameTimes: number[] = [];
  private currentTier: 1 | 2;
  private degradationLevel = 0; // 0 = no degradation, 1-3 = progressive reduction
  private readonly TARGET_FPS: Record<1 | 2, number> = { 1: 55, 2: 28 };
  private readonly SAMPLE_WINDOW = 60; // frames

  onFrame(deltaMs: number): void {
    this.frameTimes.push(deltaMs);
    if (this.frameTimes.length < this.SAMPLE_WINDOW) return;

    const avgMs = this.frameTimes.reduce((a, b) => a + b) / this.frameTimes.length;
    const avgFPS = 1000 / avgMs;
    this.frameTimes = [];

    const target = this.TARGET_FPS[this.currentTier];

    if (avgFPS < target * 0.8) {
      // Performance too low — degrade
      this.degrade();
    } else if (avgFPS > target * 1.2 && this.degradationLevel > 0) {
      // Performance recovered — restore quality
      this.restore();
    }
  }

  private degrade(): void {
    this.degradationLevel = Math.min(this.degradationLevel + 1, 3);
    switch (this.degradationLevel) {
      case 1: // Reduce pixel ratio
        renderer.setPixelRatio(Math.max(renderer.getPixelRatio() * 0.75, 0.5));
        break;
      case 2: // Drop shadow quality or disable
        renderer.shadowMap.enabled = false;
        forceLODLevel(1); // medium LOD for all objects
        break;
      case 3: // Switch to Tier 2 materials
        swapMaterials('lambert');
        disableEnvironmentMap();
        forceLODLevel(2); // lowest LOD for all objects
        break;
    }
  }

  private restore(): void {
    this.degradationLevel = Math.max(this.degradationLevel - 1, 0);
    // Reverse the degradation steps (restore in reverse order)
  }
}

16.3.1.8 LOD (Level of Detail) Strategy

LOD Level Triangle Count (per cabinet) Texture Resolution Trigger Distance Details Included
LOD 0 (High) ~2,000 triangles 1024×1024 <5 meters from camera Full geometry: drawer faces, handles, hinges, crown molding, edge profiles, panel grooves
LOD 1 (Medium) ~500 triangles 512×512 5–15 meters from camera Simplified geometry: flat drawer faces, simplified handles, no crown molding detail, no edge profiles
LOD 2 (Low) ~100 triangles 256×256 >15 meters from camera Box geometry with color-matched material. No geometric detail. Used for distant/background cabinets.

LOD transitions use Three.js LOD objects with hysteresis (different switch-in and switch-out distances) to prevent popping at tier boundaries. LOD 0 geometry is generated procedurally from cabinet parameters; LOD 1 and LOD 2 are simplified versions generated at asset creation time using the meshopt_simplifier WASM module (Meshoptimizer).

16.3.1.9 Bundle Splitting & Lazy Loading

The Three.js bundle (~450 KB gzipped) is never loaded for Tier 3 or Tier 4 devices. The device tier is determined before the main application bundle loads, using a lightweight detection script (~3 KB) that runs inline in the HTML.

Bundle Size (gzipped) Loaded For Loading Strategy
kb-detect.js ~3 KB All devices Inline <script> in HTML head. Runs before React hydration. Sets window.__KB_TIER.
kb-editor-3d.js ~450 KB Tier 1, Tier 2 React.lazy(() => import('./KBEditor3D')). Dynamic import triggered only if tier ≤ 2.
kb-editor-2d.js ~15 KB Tier 3 React.lazy(() => import('./KBEditor2D')). Canvas2D floor plan editor. Lightweight.
kb-viewer.js ~5 KB Tier 4 React.lazy(() => import('./KBViewer')). Static image gallery with zoom/pan.
kb-pathtracer.js ~120 KB Tier 1 only (Phase 2) Dynamic import on user action (“Render Preview” button). Never pre-loaded. 
TypeScript
// Tier-based component loading — no Three.js on low-end devices
import { lazy, Suspense } from 'react';

const KBEditor3D = lazy(() => import('./KBEditor3D'));
const KBEditor2D = lazy(() => import('./KBEditor2D'));
const KBViewer   = lazy(() => import('./KBViewer'));

function DesignViewport({ projectId }: { projectId: string }) {
  const tier = useDeviceTier(); // reads window.__KB_TIER or localStorage cache

  return (
    <Suspense fallback={<LoadingSkeleton />}>
      {tier <= 2 && <KBEditor3D projectId={projectId} tier={tier} />}
      {tier === 3 && <KBEditor2D projectId={projectId} />}
      {tier === 4 && <KBViewer projectId={projectId} />}
    </Suspense>
  );
}

16.3.1.10 Texture Compression & Asset Pipeline

Format Tier 1 Tier 2 Tier 3/4 Browser Support
Basis Universal (KTX2) Primary format for all PBR textures. ~75% smaller than PNG. GPU-decompressed. Served via CDN with Accept header negotiation. Same format, 50% resolution (1024 → 512). N/A Chrome 90+, Firefox 90+, Safari 16.4+
WebP Fallback for browsers without KTX2. Server renders. Same. Primary format for 2D patterns and server renders. All modern browsers
JPEG N/A N/A N/A Tier 4 fallback for very old browsers.

The asset pipeline pre-generates textures at 3 resolutions (2048, 1024, 512) and 2 formats (KTX2, WebP) during the build process using basisu CLI. Total asset storage per material: ~800 KB (6 variants). CDN serves the appropriate variant based on device tier and browser capabilities. Three.js KTX2Loader handles decompression with a shared BasisTextureLoader Web Worker.

GPU Memory Monitoring Limitation

There is no reliable cross-browser API for querying actual GPU memory usage. WEBGL_debug_renderer_info exposes the GPU model name (from which memory can be estimated) but not live memory consumption. The performance.memory API (Chrome-only) reports JS heap, not GPU memory. Our approach: maintain a running estimate of allocated texture bytes based on what the application loads (tracked in a TextureMemoryTracker singleton). When estimated usage exceeds 70% of the tier’s texture memory budget, begin evicting least-recently-used textures and downgrading LOD levels. This is a best-effort heuristic, not a guarantee.

Mobile Safari GPU constraint: WebGL contexts on mobile Safari are limited to ~256MB GPU memory. Mitigation: TextureMemoryTracker enforces a 200MB hard cap on iOS, aggressive LOD downgrading at 150MB, and KTX2 compression reduces texture memory by 4–6x compared to uncompressed RGBA.

Decisions locked:

1. Four tiers, not three. Tier 3 (2D only) and Tier 4 (view only) are distinct because jobsite tablet users need to view designs even if they cannot edit them. A “sorry, your device isn’t supported” message is unacceptable for a construction platform.

2. Detection before bundle load. The ~3 KB detection script runs before React hydration to avoid downloading 450 KB of Three.js on devices that will never use it. This is a hard requirement — not an optimization.

3. Serverless GPU rendering, not CPU software rendering. The server-side render uses Modal.com serverless GPU (NVIDIA A10/L4) with three-gpu-pathtracer running headless. Rationale: serverless GPU costs ~$0.001 per render ($0.0003/sec, 1–3 sec per 4K render) with zero idle cost (scale to zero, per-second billing). This is 5–10x faster than CPU software rendering (1–3 sec vs 5–15 sec), produces real GPU quality (hardware-accelerated ray tracing), and has no impact on VPS CPU/memory budget. Monthly cost at 10K renders: ~$10–30. Tiered escalation to Blender Cycles (photorealistic) and V-Ray App SDK (premium) as demand warrants.

4. Phones are view-only. Screen width <600px forces Tier 3 or 4 regardless of GPU capability. Kitchen design requires precision editing that touch phones cannot provide. Phones can view designs and add comments, not edit geometry.

5. Adaptive quality is continuous. Initial tier assignment is a starting point, not a ceiling. The AdaptiveQualityController monitors every frame and will degrade or restore quality in real time. A Tier 1 device that thermal-throttles during a long session will automatically drop to Tier 2 rendering without user intervention.

16.4 Component Architecture

ComponentTechnologyResponsibility
Scene EditorReact Three Fiber + Drei2D/3D interactive canvas, wall drawing, cabinet placement, snap, measurements
State ManagementZustand + Zundo + ImmerFlat node dictionary, 50-step undo/redo, dirty node tracking
Schema ValidationZodRuntime validation: Wall, Cabinet, Appliance, Countertop, Opening, Dimension
Layout GeneratorClaude (Anthropic SDK)NL → JSON layout via structured outputs, cabinet-run abstraction
Constraint ValidatorZ3 (WASM) + TypeScriptFormal NKBA compliance, UNSAT cores, feasibility checking
Product OptimizerOR-Tools CP-SATSKU selection against budget, BOM cost optimization
Visual EvaluatorGemini 3.0 ProRendered floor plan critique — spatial flow, balance, ergonomics
Catalog RAGpgvector + sentence-transformersHybrid vector + metadata search across manufacturer catalogs
Material SystemPBR (MeshStandardMaterial)Wood grain, painted, laminate, stone textures with environment maps
CSG Enginethree-bvh-csgBoolean operations for sink/cooktop cutouts in countertops
Export PipelinejsPDF, ezdxf, web-ifc, SVG.jsPDF, DXF, IFC, SVG, PNG exports for contractors and clients

16.4.1 BOM → Sales Agent Bridge

The BOM → Estimate bridge is the data pipeline that converts a finalized K&B design into a billable project estimate. It connects the K&B design tool (Section 16) to the Sales Agent (Section 7b) via the canonical domain model (Section 14), with optional downstream sync to JobTread or other PM tools via the Anti-Corruption Layer (Section 7a). This is the subsystem that turns a 3D kitchen rendering into money.

Data Flow

Flow
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      BOM → ESTIMATE PIPELINE                          │
│                                                                     │
│  ① Design Finalized                                                │
│  │  User clicks “Generate Estimate” in Design Agent chat       │
│  │  Design status → ‘finalized’ in kb_projects                     │
│  ▼                                                                │
│  ② BOM Extraction                                                  │
│  │  Scene graph traversal → kb_project_bom rows                   │
│  │  Each scene node with a catalog ref → BOM line item            │
│  │  Countertop area computed from cabinet runs (sqft)               │
│  │  Hardware count derived from cabinet/drawer counts               │
│  ▼                                                                │
│  ③ Catalog Resolution                                               │
│  │  BOM node_id → kb_catalog_products JOIN                        │
│  │  Price lookup: dealer_cost (if available) or price_usd           │
│  │  Discontinued/out-of-stock items flagged with substitution       │
│  ▼                                                                │
│  ④ BOM → EstimateLine Mapping                                    │
│  │  Category mapping: kb category → estimate category             │
│  │  Markup/margin applied per tenant config                         │
│  │  Labor cost injected per product type                            │
│  │  Custom items added as manual line items                         │
│  ▼                                                                │
│  ⑤ Estimate Creation                                                │
│  │  Sales Agent receives mapped line items                │
│  │  create_estimate_draft tool → drafts table                     │
│  │  Status: AWAITING_APPROVAL (human-in-the-loop)                  │
│  ▼                                                                │
│  ⑥ Integration Sync (Optional)                                     │
│     Approved estimate → ACL → JobTread/Procore adapter              │
│     Canonical Estimate → adapter.write() → external PM tool        │
│     Failure → mark ‘sync_pending’, BullMQ retry (Section 12)        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

BOM Schema

The BOM is extracted from the scene graph when the designer finalizes a layout. Each scene node that references a kb_catalog_products row produces one BOM item. Countertops and hardware are computed items derived from cabinet geometry, not direct catalog references.

TypeScript
// Emitted by the K&B tool’s BOM extraction function
// Stored in kb_project_bom + returned via API

interface BomItem {
  id: string;                          // UUID, PK in kb_project_bom
  projectId: string;                   // FK to kb_projects
  nodeId: string;                      // scene graph node ID (nanoid)
  productId: string | null;           // FK to kb_catalog_products (null = custom item)
  sku: string | null;                  // manufacturer SKU, denormalized for display
  name: string;                        // human-readable (“KraftMaid B24 Base Cabinet”)
  category: BomCategory;              // discriminated union
  quantity: number;                    // integer for units, decimal for sqft/lf
  unitOfMeasure: 'each' | 'sqft' | 'lf' | 'set';
  unitCost: number;                    // dealer cost if confirmed, MSRP if not
  priceConfirmed: boolean;             // true = dealer price, false = MSRP/estimated
  dimensions: {
    width: number;                   // inches
    height: number;                  // inches
    depth: number;                   // inches
  } | null;
  manufacturer: string | null;        // “KraftMaid”, “Sub-Zero”, etc.
  finish: string | null;              // “Dove White”, “Stainless Steel”
  leadTimeDays: number | null;        // from catalog, null if unknown
  discontinued: boolean;              // flagged during catalog resolution
  metadata: Record<string, unknown>; // door style, construction, hinge type, etc.
}

type BomCategory =
  | 'cabinet_base'
  | 'cabinet_wall'
  | 'cabinet_tall'
  | 'cabinet_corner'
  | 'countertop'
  | 'backsplash'
  | 'appliance'
  | 'fixture'            // sinks, faucets
  | 'hardware'           // knobs, pulls, hinges
  | 'lighting'
  | 'filler'             // filler strips between runs
  | 'custom';            // non-catalog items (custom millwork, etc.)

interface Bom {
  id: string;                          // UUID
  projectId: string;
  projectVersion: number;             // snapshot of kb_projects.version at extraction
  items: BomItem[];
  extractedAt: string;                // ISO 8601
  totalMaterialCost: number;          // sum of (quantity * unitCost) across all items
  unresolvableItems: string[];        // nodeIds with no catalog match
  priceWarnings: PriceWarning[];      // stale prices, discontinued, unconfirmed
}

interface PriceWarning {
  bomItemId: string;
  type: 'stale' | 'discontinued' | 'unconfirmed' | 'out_of_stock';
  message: string;
  suggestedSubstitute: string | null; // product_id of suggested replacement
}

Category Mapping Layer

K&B BOM categories map to Sales Agent line item categories. The mapping is not 1:1 — the BOM is product-granular (cabinet_base vs cabinet_wall), while the estimate groups by trade/section for customer-facing quotes. The estimate_category_map is tenant-configurable JSONB stored on the tenants table.

BOM CategoryEstimate CategoryUnitLabor Rule
cabinet_baseCabinetryeach1.5 hrs/cabinet × labor rate
cabinet_wallCabinetryeach1.0 hrs/cabinet × labor rate
cabinet_tallCabinetryeach2.0 hrs/cabinet × labor rate
cabinet_cornerCabinetryeach2.5 hrs/cabinet × labor rate
countertopCountertops & Fabricationsqft0.5 hrs/sqft + $15/sqft fabrication
backsplashTile & Backsplashsqft0.75 hrs/sqft × labor rate
applianceApplianceseach1.0 hrs/unit (connect only, no rough-in)
fixturePlumbing Fixtureseach2.0 hrs/fixture (install + connect)
hardwareCabinetryset0 (included in cabinet labor)
lightingElectricaleach1.0 hrs/fixture × labor rate
fillerCabinetryeach0.25 hrs/piece × labor rate
customCustom & MillworkeachManual entry required

Markup, Margin, and Labor Injection

The mapping layer applies three cost transforms before producing estimate line items. All rates are tenant-configurable via tenants.estimate_config JSONB.

TypeScript
interface EstimateConfig {
  laborRatePerHour: number;           // e.g., $85/hr (tenant’s install crew rate)
  defaultMarkupPercent: number;       // e.g., 0.30 (30% markup on materials)
  categoryMarkupOverrides: Record<string, number>;  // per-category overrides
  contingencyPercent: number;         // e.g., 0.10 (10% contingency)
  laborHoursOverrides: Record<BomCategory, number>; // override default hrs
  taxRate: number;                    // materials tax rate (labor typically untaxed)
  includeTaxInEstimate: boolean;      // show tax as separate line or rolled in
}

// Transform: BomItem → EstimateLine
function mapBomItemToEstimateLine(
  item: BomItem,
  config: EstimateConfig,
  categoryMap: Record<BomCategory, string>
): EstimateLine {
  const markup = config.categoryMarkupOverrides[item.category]
    ?? config.defaultMarkupPercent;
  const laborHours = config.laborHoursOverrides[item.category]
    ?? DEFAULT_LABOR_HOURS[item.category];
  const materialCost = item.quantity * item.unitCost;
  const materialWithMarkup = materialCost * (1 + markup);
  const laborCost = laborHours * item.quantity * config.laborRatePerHour;

  return {
    category: categoryMap[item.category],
    description: item.name,
    sku: item.sku,
    quantity: item.quantity,
    unitOfMeasure: item.unitOfMeasure,
    materialCost: materialWithMarkup,
    laborCost,
    totalCost: materialWithMarkup + laborCost,
    sourceRef: { bomItemId: item.id, productId: item.productId },
  };
}

API Contract

The Design Agent calls a single Hono RPC endpoint to generate an estimate from a finalized K&B project. The endpoint extracts the BOM, maps to estimate lines, and invokes the Sales Agent via BullMQ. The response is async — the estimate draft is delivered via NATS → Socket.io push.

TypeScript
// Route: POST /api/v1/kb/projects/:projectId/estimate
// Auth: Bearer token (tenant context extracted from JWT)
// Permission: Design Agent or user with ‘manager’ role

// Request
interface GenerateEstimateRequest {
  projectId: string;                   // UUID, path param
  includeLabor: boolean;               // default true
  includeContingency: boolean;          // default true
  overrideMarkupPercent?: number;      // override tenant default for this estimate
  notes?: string;                      // passed to Sales Agent context
  excludeCategories?: BomCategory[];   // e.g., exclude appliances (customer-supplied)
}

// Response (synchronous acknowledgment)
interface GenerateEstimateResponse {
  bomId: string;                       // UUID of extracted BOM snapshot
  executionId: string;                 // agent_executions.id (track via WebSocket)
  itemCount: number;                   // total BOM items extracted
  warnings: PriceWarning[];            // stale/discontinued/unconfirmed prices
  estimatedTotalMaterial: number;      // pre-markup material subtotal
}

// Error responses
// 400: Project not in ‘finalized’ status
// 400: BOM extraction produced 0 items (empty design)
// 404: Project not found or not in tenant scope
// 409: Estimate generation already in progress for this project
// 422: >50% of BOM items have unresolvable catalog references
// 429: Tenant AI budget exhausted (Section 4 pre-flight check)
TypeScript
// Hono route implementation (packages/backend/src/routes/kb-estimate.ts)

const kbEstimateRoute = new Hono()
  .post('/projects/:projectId/estimate', tenantMiddleware(), async (c) => {
    const { projectId } = c.req.param();
    const body = await c.req.json();
    const input = GenerateEstimateRequestSchema.parse(body);
    const tenantId = c.get('tenantId');

    // 1. Validate project status
    const project = await getKbProject(tenantId, projectId);
    if (project.status !== 'finalized') throw new HTTPException(400);

    // 2. Check for in-flight estimate generation
    const existing = await findActiveEstimateExecution(tenantId, projectId);
    if (existing) throw new HTTPException(409);

    // 3. Extract BOM from scene graph
    const bom = await extractBom(project.scene_data, tenantId, projectId);

    // 4. Resolve catalog prices + flag warnings
    const resolved = await resolveBomPrices(bom, tenantId);

    // 5. Map BOM → EstimateLines
    const config = await getTenantEstimateConfig(tenantId);
    const lines = resolved.items
      .filter(i => !input.excludeCategories?.includes(i.category))
      .map(i => mapBomItemToEstimateLine(i, config, CATEGORY_MAP));

    // 6. Queue Sales Agent execution
    const executionId = await queueEstimateAgent({
      tenantId,
      projectId,
      bomId: resolved.id,
      lineItems: lines,
      includeLabor: input.includeLabor,
      includeContingency: input.includeContingency,
      notes: input.notes,
    });

    // 7. Publish NATS event
    await nats.publish(
      buildSubject(tenantId, 'kb', 'bom', 'generated'),
      { bomId: resolved.id, projectId, itemCount: resolved.items.length }
    );

    return c.json({
      bomId: resolved.id,
      executionId,
      itemCount: resolved.items.length,
      warnings: resolved.priceWarnings,
      estimatedTotalMaterial: resolved.totalMaterialCost,
    }, 202);
  });

NATS Event Flow

The BOM → Estimate pipeline emits 4 events using the canonical NATS subject format (TENANT_{tenantId}.{domain}.{name}.{event}) via the buildSubject() helper. All events carry the standard inter-agent payload schema (Section 7b: event_type, tenant_id, source_agent, target_agent, payload, context_ref).

Event SubjectSourceTriggerSubscribersPayload
TENANT_{id}.kb.bom.generated KB Estimate Route BOM extracted from scene graph Sales Agent, CEO Agent { bomId, projectId, itemCount, totalMaterialCost, warningCount }
TENANT_{id}.kb.bom.price_warning KB Estimate Route ≥1 BOM item has stale/discontinued/unconfirmed price Design Agent, Bookkeeping Agent { bomId, warnings: PriceWarning[] }
TENANT_{id}.estimate.draft.created Sales Agent Agent produces estimate draft via create_estimate_draft tool Dashboard (Socket.io push), Project Management Agent { estimateId, draftId, projectId, totalAmount, lineItemCount }
TENANT_{id}.estimate.approved Dashboard API User approves the estimate draft Integration Service (ACL sync), Project Management Agent, CEO Agent { estimateId, projectId, approvedBy, totalAmount }

Edge Cases

ScenarioDetectionBehavior
Catalog product has no price price_usd IS NULL AND dealer_cost IS NULL on kb_catalog_products BOM item included with unitCost: 0, priceConfirmed: false. PriceWarning of type 'unconfirmed' emitted. Estimate line item shows “PRICE TBD” — human must fill in before approval. The estimate cannot be approved while any line item has zero cost unless the user explicitly overrides with a manual price.
Product is discontinued discontinued = true on kb_catalog_products BOM item flagged with discontinued: true. Catalog RAG runs a substitution query: same manufacturer + same dimensions + same category + in-stock. PriceWarning.suggestedSubstitute populated with the closest match. User must accept the substitute or provide a manual replacement before the estimate can be approved.
BOM includes custom/non-catalog items productId IS NULL on the BOM item (scene node has no catalog reference) BOM item created with category: 'custom', unitCost: 0, priceConfirmed: false. Custom items are passed through to the estimate as manual line items. The Sales Agent generates a cost estimate based on dimensions and material type using its LLM context (Sonnet), but marks the line as “AI Estimated — Verify”. Labor hours default to 0 (manual entry required).
Design changes after estimate sent kb_projects.version > Bom.projectVersion on the linked estimate The existing estimate is not auto-invalidated. When the user re-finalizes the updated design, a new BOM is extracted and a new estimate draft is generated. The dashboard shows a “Design Updated Since Estimate” badge on the stale estimate. A diff view shows added/removed/changed BOM items between the two versions. The old estimate remains accessible for audit.
>50% of items unresolvable Count of productId IS NULL items / total items > 0.5 API returns HTTP 422. The design likely uses placeholder/generic objects that were never matched to catalog products. The user is directed back to the Design Agent to assign catalog products before generating an estimate.
Price staleness (>90 days since last sync) kb_catalog_products.updated_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '90 days' BOM item included but flagged with PriceWarning of type 'stale'. The estimate line item shows “Price may be outdated — last confirmed [date]”. Nightly BullMQ catalog sync job (Section 16.6) is the primary refresh mechanism.

Database Additions

One new table and one new column extend the existing K&B schema (Section 16.8) to support BOM snapshots and estimate linking.

SQL
-- BOM snapshots (immutable after extraction)
CREATE TABLE kb_bom_snapshots (
  id                   UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  tenant_id            UUID REFERENCES tenants NOT NULL,
  project_id           UUID REFERENCES kb_projects ON DELETE CASCADE,
  project_version      INTEGER NOT NULL,
  items                JSONB NOT NULL,             -- BomItem[] serialized
  total_material_cost  NUMERIC NOT NULL,
  price_warnings       JSONB NOT NULL DEFAULT '[]',
  unresolvable_items   TEXT[] NOT NULL DEFAULT '{}',
  estimate_execution_id UUID REFERENCES agent_executions,
  created_at           TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()
);

-- RLS: same pattern as all kb_* tables
ALTER TABLE kb_bom_snapshots ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation ON kb_bom_snapshots
  USING (tenant_id = current_setting('app.tenant_id', true)::uuid);

-- Add estimate_config to tenants table
ALTER TABLE tenants
  ADD COLUMN estimate_config JSONB NOT NULL DEFAULT '{
    "laborRatePerHour": 85,
    "defaultMarkupPercent": 0.30,
    "categoryMarkupOverrides": {},
    "contingencyPercent": 0.10,
    "laborHoursOverrides": {},
    "taxRate": 0,
    "includeTaxInEstimate": false
  }';

Sales Agent Agent Context Extension

When the Sales Agent is invoked via the BOM → Estimate pipeline, its context assembly (Section 7b, AI-08) includes additional K&B-specific data. This does not change the agent’s stopWhen: stepCountIs(17) budget or its 8K max_tokens output limit.

TypeScript
// Additional context injected when source = ‘kb_bom’
interface KbEstimateContext {
  bomId: string;
  projectName: string;
  roomDimensions: { width: number; depth: number };
  layoutType: string;                  // “U-shaped”, “L-shaped”, “galley”, etc.
  cabinetCount: number;
  applianceCount: number;
  countertopSqft: number;
  hasIsland: boolean;
  materialGrade: 'budget' | 'mid' | 'premium' | 'luxury';  // inferred from price distribution
  priceWarnings: PriceWarning[];
  preComputedLineItems: EstimateLine[]; // mapped BOM items (agent reviews, adjusts, groups)
}

// Token budget impact: ~1,500 additional tokens for K&B context
// Total Sales Agent budget remains ~17,500 tokens input (Section 7b)
Core Value Proposition

This bridge is the reason the K&B tool exists inside Kiptra rather than as a standalone product. Without it, a designer uses 2020 Design to create a layout, manually re-enters every cabinet SKU into a spreadsheet or JobTread to build an estimate, then manually creates a project in their PM tool. Three separate tools, three data entry passes, three opportunities for transcription errors. With the bridge, the designer finalizes a 3D layout and clicks one button. The BOM is extracted from the scene graph, prices are resolved from the catalog, markup and labor are applied per the contractor’s configured rates, and a complete estimate draft lands in their approval queue — traceable back to the exact design version that produced it. The estimate syncs to JobTread or Procore via the same ACL that handles every other integration. This design → estimate → project pipeline in a single platform is the moat: it eliminates the data re-entry that costs K&B contractors 2–4 hours per project and removes the pricing errors that erode margins on 30%+ of remodeling jobs.

Implementation Sequencing

The BOM → Estimate bridge depends on three prerequisites: (1) the canonical domain model’s Estimate and EstimateLine interfaces (Section 14, Wave 1), (2) the create_estimate_draft tool in the Sales Agent (Wave 2), and (3) the kb_catalog_products table populated with at least one manufacturer’s catalog data (Month 3 of K&B build). The bridge itself is a Month 4–5 deliverable — after BOM extraction exists (Month 3) and after the Sales Agent is operational (Wave 2). The NATS event flow and ACL sync are already built by the time this bridge ships.

16.4.2 K&B Design-to-Project Pipeline — End-to-End Data Flow

The complete flow from a designer’s natural language description to a billable project in JobTread. This pipeline crosses three system boundaries: the K&B design editor (Three.js/R3F), the Kiptra core platform (Hono API + agents), and the external integration layer (JobTread adapter).

Flow
SEQUENCE DIAGRAM: Design → BOM → Estimate → Project
======================================================

[Designer]        [Design Agent]     [K&B AI Loop]        [Catalog RAG]       [Sales Agent]    [JobTread Adapter]
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |  "L-shaped kitchen |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |   12x14, white     |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |   shaker, quartz,  |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |   $40K budget"     |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |------------------->|                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |              POST /api/kb/               |                     |                     |                     |
    |              designs                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |              {nl_input, room_spec,        |                     |                     |                     |
    |               project_id}                |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |  BullMQ job:         |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |  kb-design-generate  |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |-------------------->|                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |               ROUND 0: Claude (Sonnet)    |                     |                     |
    |                    |               NL → CabinetRun[] JSON     |                     |                     |
    |                    |               (structured output,         |                     |                     |
    |                    |                ~3–5 sec)                  |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |               resolveRunToCoordinates()   |                     |                     |
    |                    |               CabinetRun[] →             |                     |                     |
    |                    |               PlacedCabinet[] with        |                     |                     |
    |                    |               (x, y, rotation)            |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |               Z3 WASM validation          |                     |                     |
    |                    |               24 NKBA constraints         |                     |                     |
    |                    |               (~100–500ms)                |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |               IF UNSAT:                   |                     |                     |
    |                    |                 Extract conflict cores    |                     |                     |
    |                    |                 Feed back to Claude       |                     |                     |
    |                    |                 ROUND N+1 (max 5)         |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |               IF SAT:                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                 Three.js server render    |                     |                     |
    |                    |                 2D floor plan PNG         |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |               Gemini 3.0 Pro evaluation   |                     |                     |
    |                    |               Image → VisualEvaluation   |                     |                     |
    |                    |               (flow, balance, ergo)       |                     |                     |
    |                    |               (~5–8 sec)                  |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |               IF score ≥ 7 AND no        |                     |                     |
    |                    |               critical issues: DONE       |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |  NATS publish:       |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |  TENANT_{id}.kb.     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |  design.generated    |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |  <-- Socket.io ----|                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |  DesignViewport    |                     |                     |                     |
    |  renders layout    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |  inline in chat    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |  Designer reviews, |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |  makes adjustments |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |  (drag cabinets,   |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |   swap finishes)   |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |  "Generate BOM"   |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |------------------->|                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |              POST /api/kb/               |                     |                     |                     |
    |              designs/:id/bom             |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |  For each node in scene graph:            |                     |                     |
    |                    |  Extract: type, width, height,            |                     |                     |
    |                    |  depth, style, finish                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |----search query---->|                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |  pgvector hybrid:    |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |  vector similarity   |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |  + metadata filters  |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |  (width, category,   |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |   finish, price)     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |<--matching SKUs------|                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |  Top-5 per position  |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |               OR-Tools CP-SAT:            |                     |                     |
    |                    |               Optimize SKU selection      |                     |                     |
    |                    |               against budget constraint   |                     |                     |
    |                    |               (~200ms–2sec)               |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |               INSERT INTO kb_project_bom  |                     |                     |
    |                    |               (project_id, node_id,       |                     |                     |
    |                    |                product_id, quantity,      |                     |                     |
    |                    |                unit_price)                |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |  NATS publish:       |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |  TENANT_{id}.kb.     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |  design.bom_ready    |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |  <-- Socket.io ----|                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |  BOM panel shows   |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |  line items +      |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |  pricing           |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |  "Create Estimate"|                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |------------------->|                     |                     |                     |--(triggered)------->|
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |              POST /api/estimates         |                     |                     |                     |
    |              {source: 'kb_design',       |                     |                     |                     |
    |               design_id, bom_id}         |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |               BullMQ job:                  |
    |                    |                     |                     |               estimate-generate             |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |               READ kb_project_bom           |
    |                    |                     |                     |               JOIN kb_catalog_products      |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |               Transform BOM → estimate:    |
    |                    |                     |                     |               - Group by category           |
    |                    |                     |                     |                 (cabinets, countertops,     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                  appliances, plumbing,      |
    |                    |                     |                     |                  hardware, labor)           |
