    |                    |                     |                     |               - Apply markup %              |
    |                    |                     |                     |                 per category                |
    |                    |                     |                     |               - Calculate labor             |
    |                    |                     |                     |                 (cabinet_count * rate       |
    |                    |                     |                     |                  + complexity factor)       |
    |                    |                     |                     |               - Add contingency (10–15%)    |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |               INSERT INTO estimates         |
    |                    |                     |                     |               INSERT INTO                   |
    |                    |                     |                     |               estimate_line_items           |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |               NATS publish:                 |
    |                    |                     |                     |               TENANT_{id}.agent.            |
    |                    |                     |                     |               estimate_engine.              |
    |                    |                     |                     |               completed                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |  <-- Socket.io ----|--(estimate ready)---|---------------------|---------------------|                     |
    |  Estimate panel    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |  shows categories, |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |  line items, total |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |  Designer reviews  |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |  estimate, adjusts |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |  markup, sends to  |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |  customer          |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |  Customer approves |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |  (signature or     |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |   email confirm)   |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |  "Create Project" |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |------------------->|                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |              POST /api/projects          |                     |                     |                     |
    |              {source: 'kb_estimate',     |                     |                     |                     |
    |               estimate_id}               |                     |                     |----sync to JT------>|
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |               Anti-corruption
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |               layer maps:
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |               Kiptra Estimate →
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |               JT Project
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |               Kiptra Line Items →
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |               JT Cost Items
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |               Kiptra Contact →
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |               JT Customer
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |               Pave API:
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |               mutation {
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                 createProject(
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                   name, customer,
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                   costItems[])
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |               }
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |               NATS publish:
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |               TENANT_{id}.
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |               integration.
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |               jobtread.
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |               project_created
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |
    |  <-- Socket.io ----|  "Project created in JobTread: [link]"   |                     |                     |
    |                    |                     |                     |                     |                     |

Data Transformation Pipeline

Six distinct transformations convert a natural language description into a billable JobTread project. Each transformation has a defined input schema, output schema, and failure mode.

StepInputTransformationOutputEngineFailure Handling
1. NL → Cabinet Runs Natural language + RoomModel (walls, doors, windows, plumbing) Claude structured output converts descriptive text into ordered CabinetRun[]. Each run references a wallId, startOffset (inches), and ordered segments[] (type, width). Claude chooses cabinet sizes from standard widths (9″–48″ in 3″ increments). CabinetRun[] — abstract wall-relative layout, no absolute coordinates Claude Sonnet (Vercel AI Gateway, ~3–5s) Structured output guarantees valid JSON schema. Semantic validation (negative widths, impossible sizes) caught by Zod RunSegment schema. On schema failure: retry once with stricter prompt. On semantic failure: pass errors to Z3 step.
2. Runs → Coordinates CabinetRun[] + Wall[] (geometry) Deterministic resolveRunToCoordinates() walks each run segment-by-segment, accumulating offset along the wall vector. Produces absolute (x, y, rotation) for every cabinet. Corner cabinets trigger wall-transition logic. No LLM involved — pure geometry. PlacedCabinet[] with absolute coordinates forming a complete scene graph TypeScript (deterministic, <1ms) Cannot fail if Run schema is valid. Edge case: segments exceed wall length → validation error returned to Claude with specific overshoot measurement.
3. Coordinates → Validated Layout PlacedCabinet[] + Appliance[] + Sink Z3 WASM solver encodes 24 NKBA constraints as formal logic. Checks work triangle (legs 4’–9’, sum ≤26’), clearances (≥42″ work aisle, ≥36″ walkway), landing areas (sink: 24″/18″, range: 12″/15″, fridge: 15″ handle side), non-overlap, wall attachment, storage minimums. Returns SAT/UNSAT with specific constraint violations as UNSAT cores. SAT: validated layout. UNSAT: minimal conflict set with measured vs required values. Z3 WASM (~100–500ms) UNSAT cores identify exactly which constraints conflict. Fed back to Claude with specific measurements ("Sink left landing: 10″, need ≥18″"). Max 5 rounds. If fundamentally infeasible (room too small for layout type): return FAIL with explanation to designer.
4. Layout → Matched SKUs (BOM) PlacedCabinet[] with (type, width, style, finish) per item For each scene node: query kb_catalog_products via pgvector hybrid search (embedding similarity + metadata filters on width, category, finish). Returns top-5 matching SKUs per position. OR-Tools CP-SAT selects optimal SKU assignment across all positions: exactly one SKU per position, total cost ≤ budget. Objective: minimize cost while maintaining quality tier consistency. kb_project_bom rows: (node_id, product_id, quantity, unit_price) per design element pgvector + OR-Tools CP-SAT (~200ms–2s) No matching SKU: flag as "generic" with estimated pricing from category averages. OR-Tools infeasible (budget too low): return partial solution with "over budget by $X" warning. Stale pricing (>90 days): amber warning on affected line items.
5. BOM → Estimate kb_project_bom rows joined with kb_catalog_products Sales Agent groups BOM items by category (cabinets, countertops, appliances, plumbing fixtures, hardware, backsplash). Adds computed line items: countertop fabrication (sqft × $15/sqft), installation labor (cabinet_count × hourly_rate × complexity_factor), design fee (if applicable), permit fees (flat rate from tenant config). Applies per-category markup percentages from tenant’s estimate_config JSONB. Adds contingency (10–15%, configurable). Generates estimates row with estimate_line_items detail. estimates + estimate_line_items in Supabase. Categories: materials, labor, fees, contingency. Each line: description, quantity, unit_price, markup_pct, extended_price. Sales Agent (Sonnet, ~8–12s for analysis + comparison) If BOM has "generic" items (no SKU matched): estimate shows "estimated" pricing with disclaimer. Estimate stored as status: 'draft' in drafts table for human approval before customer delivery. Price discrepancy >20% vs budget triggers alert.
6. Estimate → JobTread Project Approved estimates + estimate_line_items + contacts (customer) Anti-corruption layer (JobTreadAdapter.write()) maps Kiptra domain types to JobTread’s Pave GraphQL schema. Estimate → Project (name, description, status). EstimateLine → CostItem (category, description, amount, unit_cost, quantity). Contact → Customer (name, email, phone, address). Adapter handles JT-specific requirements: cost item categories must match JT’s enum values, customer dedup by email, project naming convention. JobTread project with linked cost items and customer record. External ID stored as external_ref on Kiptra estimate for bidirectional sync. JobTread Pave API (REST/GraphQL, via token bucket rate limiter) API failure: mark estimate as sync_pending, queue BullMQ retry with exponential backoff (3 attempts). Partial failure (project created but cost items fail): store JT project ID, retry cost item creation only. Rate limit (429): back-pressure via token bucket, retry after Retry-After header. All failures create escalation record for manual resolution.

NATS Event Table

SubjectPublisherConsumer(s)Payload SummaryTrigger
TENANT_{id}.kb.design.generation_started Design Agent Socket.io (progress indicator) { project_id, design_id, nl_input_summary } Designer submits NL design request
TENANT_{id}.kb.design.iteration_progress K&B AI Loop (orchestrator) Socket.io (progress indicator) { design_id, round, total_rounds, z3_status, gemini_score, constraint_violations[] } Each iteration round completes
TENANT_{id}.kb.design.generated K&B AI Loop (orchestrator) Design Agent, Socket.io { design_id, project_id, layout_type, cabinet_count, nkba_pass_count, gemini_score, iteration_count } AI loop converges (SAT + score ≥ 7)
TENANT_{id}.kb.design.generation_failed K&B AI Loop (orchestrator) Design Agent, Notification Service { design_id, failure_reason, last_z3_violations[], best_gemini_score, rounds_attempted } Max rounds exhausted or fundamental infeasibility
TENANT_{id}.kb.design.updated Hono API (PATCH handler) Socket.io (collaborative awareness) { project_id, design_id, changed_nodes[], user_id } Designer manually edits layout (drag, swap finish)
TENANT_{id}.kb.design.bom_started Hono API Socket.io (progress indicator) { design_id, node_count } Designer clicks "Generate BOM"
TENANT_{id}.kb.design.bom_ready Catalog RAG + OR-Tools pipeline Design Agent, Socket.io, Sales Agent { design_id, bom_id, line_item_count, total_materials_cost, unmatched_count, budget_delta } SKU matching and optimization complete
TENANT_{id}.kb.design.bom_failed Catalog RAG pipeline Design Agent, Notification Service { design_id, failure_reason, unmatched_items[] } OR-Tools infeasible or catalog search failure
TENANT_{id}.agent.estimate_engine.started BullMQ worker (agent executor) Socket.io (agent execution dashboard) { execution_id, source: 'kb_design', design_id, bom_id } Sales Agent begins processing BOM
TENANT_{id}.agent.estimate_engine.completed Sales Agent Design Agent, Socket.io, Notification Service { execution_id, estimate_id, total_amount, category_breakdown: { cabinets, countertops, appliances, labor, contingency }, status: 'draft' } Estimate generation complete
TENANT_{id}.agent.estimate_engine.approved Hono API (approval handler) JobTread Adapter, Notification Service { estimate_id, approved_by, customer_id } Designer/PM approves estimate for customer delivery
TENANT_{id}.integration.jobtread.project_created JobTread Adapter Project Management Agent, Socket.io, Notification Service { estimate_id, jobtread_project_id, jobtread_url, cost_item_count, sync_status } Anti-corruption layer successfully creates JT project
TENANT_{id}.integration.jobtread.sync_failed JobTread Adapter Notification Service, DLQ handler { estimate_id, error_code, error_message, retry_count, next_retry_at } JT API call fails (timeout, 429, 5xx)

API Endpoints

MethodRouteRequest BodyResponseSide Effects
POST /api/kb/designs { project_id, nl_input, room_spec: RoomModel, design_brief: DesignBrief } { design_id, status: 'generating', job_id } (202 Accepted) Creates kb_projects row (status='generating'). Queues BullMQ kb-design-generate job. NATS publish kb.design.generation_started.
GET /api/kb/designs/:id — { design_id, scene_data (JSONB), status, version, nkba_compliance[], gemini_score } None (read-only). Returns full scene graph for DesignViewport rendering.
PATCH /api/kb/designs/:id { scene_data: partial JSONB, changed_nodes[] } { design_id, version (incremented) } UPDATE kb_projects.scene_data (JSONB merge). INSERT kb_project_versions snapshot. NATS publish kb.design.updated. Triggers client-side Z3 re-validation (Tier 1 TypeScript rules).
POST /api/kb/designs/:id/bom { budget_constraint?: number, preferred_manufacturers?: string[] } { bom_id, status: 'processing', job_id } (202 Accepted) Queues BullMQ kb-bom-generate job. Reads scene graph, queries catalog, runs OR-Tools. Writes to kb_project_bom. NATS publish kb.design.bom_ready on completion.
GET /api/kb/designs/:id/bom — { bom_id, items[]: { node_id, product: { sku, name, manufacturer, price }, quantity, unit_price }, totals_by_category, unmatched_items[] } None. JOIN kb_project_bom with kb_catalog_products and kb_manufacturers.
POST /api/estimates { source: 'kb_design', design_id, bom_id, markup_config?: { category: pct }[], contingency_pct?: number } { estimate_id, status: 'generating', execution_id } (202 Accepted) Queues BullMQ estimate-generate job. Sales Agent reads BOM, generates categorized estimate. Writes to estimates + estimate_line_items. Status transitions: QUEUED → RUNNING → AWAITING_APPROVAL.
POST /api/estimates/:id/approve { customer_id, send_to_customer?: boolean } { estimate_id, status: 'approved' } UPDATE estimate status. NATS publish estimate_engine.approved. If send_to_customer=true: queue email via Notification Service.
POST /api/estimates/:id/create-project { sync_to_jobtread?: boolean } { project_id, jobtread_project_id?, sync_status } Creates Kiptra project from estimate. If sync_to_jobtread=true: triggers anti-corruption layer. JobTreadAdapter.write() maps domain types and calls Pave API. NATS publish integration.jobtread.project_created.

Data Transformation Detail

Flow
TRANSFORMATION PIPELINE (data shapes at each boundary)
======================================================

1. NL Input
   "L-shaped kitchen, 12x14, white shaker, quartz, $40K"
         |
         v
2. CabinetRun[] (Claude structured output)
   [
     { wallId: "south", startOffset: 0, direction: "left-to-right",
       cabinets: [
         { type: "base_cabinet", width: 36 },
         { type: "sink_base", width: 33 },
         { type: "base_cabinet", width: 24 },
         { type: "dishwasher_gap", width: 24 }
       ]},
     { wallId: "east", startOffset: 0, direction: "left-to-right",
       cabinets: [
         { type: "corner_base", width: 36 },
         { type: "base_cabinet", width: 30 },
         { type: "range_gap", width: 36 },
         { type: "base_cabinet", width: 24 },
         { type: "tall_pantry", width: 24 }
       ]}
   ]
         |
         v  resolveRunToCoordinates()  [deterministic, no LLM]
         |
3. PlacedCabinet[] (absolute coordinates)
   [
     { id: "B1", type: "base_cabinet", x: 0, y: 0,
       width: 36, depth: 24, rotation: 0 },
     { id: "SB1", type: "sink_base", x: 36, y: 0,
       width: 33, depth: 24, rotation: 0 },
     ...
   ]
         |
         v  Z3 validation (24 NKBA constraints)
         |
4. ValidatedLayout (SAT + compliance report)
   { layout: PlacedCabinet[],
     compliance: [
       { guideline: 3, name: "Work Triangle", status: "pass",
         measured: "18'4\"", required: "≤26'" },
       { guideline: 11, name: "Sink Landing", status: "pass",
         measured: "24\"/18\"", required: "24\"/18\"" },
       ...
     ] }
         |
         v  Catalog RAG + OR-Tools SKU selection
         |
5. BOM (Bill of Materials)
   kb_project_bom rows:
   | node_id | product_id (FK) | sku          | manufacturer | unit_price | qty |
   |---------|-----------------|--------------|--------------|------------|-----|
   | B1      | uuid-001        | KM-B36-WSH   | KraftMaid    | $612       | 1   |
   | SB1     | uuid-002        | KM-SB33-WSH  | KraftMaid    | $548       | 1   |
   | CB1     | uuid-003        | KM-BC36-WSH  | KraftMaid    | $725       | 1   |
   | ...     | ...             | ...          | ...          | ...        | ... |
   | CTR1    | uuid-020        | CMB-QTZ-3CM  | Cambria      | $85/sqft   | 42  |
   | RNG1    | uuid-030        | WLF-GR364    | Wolf         | $8,200     | 1   |
         |
         v  Sales Agent (categorize + markup + labor)
         |
6. Estimate (categorized with markup)
   estimates row: { total: $43,750, status: 'draft' }
   estimate_line_items:
   | category     | description              | qty | unit   | markup | extended |
   |--------------|--------------------------|-----|--------|--------|----------|
   | Cabinets     | KraftMaid White Shaker    | 14  | $498ea | 25%    | $8,715   |
   | Countertops  | Cambria Quartz 3cm        | 42  | $85/sf | 20%    | $4,284   |
   | Fabrication  | Countertop fab + install  | 42  | $15/sf | 20%    | $756     |
   | Appliances   | Wolf/Sub-Zero package     | 4   | varies | 10%    | $16,280  |
   | Plumbing     | Kohler sink + Moen faucet | 1   | $1,850 | 15%    | $2,128   |
   | Hardware     | Cabinet pulls (qty 28)    | 28  | $12ea  | 20%    | $403     |
   | Labor        | Cabinet installation      | 14  | $150ea | 0%     | $2,100   |
   | Labor        | Plumbing rough-in         | 1   | $2,500 | 0%     | $2,500   |
   | Labor        | Countertop template+inst  | 1   | $1,800 | 0%     | $1,800   |
   | Design Fee   | K&B design services       | 1   | $2,500 | 0%     | $2,500   |
   | Contingency  | 10% of subtotal           | 1   | $4,147 | 0%     | $4,147   |
         |
         v  Anti-corruption layer (domain → JobTread mapping)
         |
7. JobTread Project (via Pave GraphQL API)
   createProject:
     name: "Kitchen Remodel - [Customer Name]"
     customer: { name, email, phone, address }
     costItems: [
       { category: "Materials", description: "KraftMaid WSH Cabinets (14)",
         amount: 8715, unitCost: 62250, quantity: 14 },
       { category: "Materials", description: "Cambria Quartz 3cm (42 sqft)",
         amount: 4284, unitCost: 10200, quantity: 42 },
       { category: "Labor", description: "Cabinet Installation",
         amount: 2100, unitCost: 15000, quantity: 14 },
       ...
     ]
   Response: { projectId: "jt-12345", url: "https://app.jobtread.com/..." }

   Kiptra stores: estimates.external_ref = "jt-12345"
   Bidirectional sync: JT webhook → Kiptra updates on project changes

Key insight: The anti-corruption layer is the critical boundary. Kiptra’s domain model (Estimate, EstimateLine, Contact) is the canonical source of truth. The JobTread adapter translates outward on create and translates inward on webhook receipt. If JobTread is replaced with Procore or Jobber, only the adapter changes — the design pipeline, BOM engine, and estimate generation are completely decoupled. The adapter implements IntegrationAdapter<TReadResult, TWritePayload> (Section 6), so this swap is verified at compile time.

16.5 NKBA Design Standards Enforcement

The National Kitchen & Bath Association publishes 31 Kitchen Planning Guidelines covering clearances, landing areas, storage minimums, ventilation, and safety.

Two-Tier Validation

Tier 1 — TypeScript Rule Engine (real-time, client-side): Simple distance and clearance checks per frame. Violations highlighted in red with inline measurements showing the shortfall.

Tier 2 — Z3 SMT Solver (WASM or server): Full formal optimization and feasibility checking. Used to validate Claude’s generated layouts and return UNSAT cores identifying exactly which constraints conflict. Runs in ~100–500ms.

Key Constraints

ConstraintNKBA RequirementTier
Work TriangleSum ≤ 26’, each leg 4’–9’Z3
Work Aisle Width≥42″ (one cook), ≥48″ (two cooks)TS + Z3
Walkway Width≥36″TypeScript
Sink Landing≥24″ on one side, ≥18″ on otherTypeScript
Range Landing≥12″ on one side, ≥15″ on otherTypeScript
Fridge Landing≥15″ on handle sideTypeScript
Dishwasher ProximityWithin 36″ of nearest sink edgeTypeScript
Cooktop SafetyNot under operable windowTS + Z3
Storage MinimumsPer NKBA formula based on kitchen sizeZ3

16.6 Manufacturer Catalog System

Data Sources

SourceFormatCoverageReliability
Direct manufacturer feedsDealer portal exports (CSV/XML), FTP data drops, or partnership-negotiated access. Requires Adam’s relationships. Not standard REST APIs.SKUs, pricing, dimensions, finishesHigh (authoritative)
2020 Design format filesProprietaryFull product librariesHigh (industry standard)
PDF spec sheetsPDF → Claude extractionDimensions, features, install specsMedium (parsing variance)
KitchenDEV / Kitchen365APIAggregated catalog dataMedium (third-party)
  • Key Manufacturers: KraftMaid, MasterBrand, Wellborn, Fabuwood (cabinets); Sub-Zero, Wolf, Thermador, Bosch (appliances); Cambria, Kohler, Moen (countertops/plumbing)
  • RAG Architecture: pgvector hybrid search (vector similarity + metadata filters for width, category, finish, price range)
  • Sync: Nightly BullMQ jobs, 90-day price staleness warnings, estimated vs confirmed pricing
  • 3D Models: Procedural cabinet generation as fallback, Blender automation for format conversion, async GLTF loading with bounding-box placeholders

16.7 New Tech Stack Additions

CategoryPackagePurpose
3D Renderingthree, @react-three/fiber, @react-three/dreiCore 3D engine, React integration, utility components
3D Utilitiesthree-bvh-csg, troika-three-text, three-mesh-bvhBoolean geometry, SDF text, BVH acceleration
3D Advanced (Phase 2)three-gpu-pathtracerProgressive path-traced rendering
Statezundo, immer, mitt, nanoidUndo/redo, immutable updates, event bus, ID generation
AI@ai-sdk/anthropic, @ai-sdk/google via Vercel AI SDK + Vercel AI GatewayClaude structured outputs, Gemini vision — routed through the Vercel AI Gateway (Section 4) for cost tracking, fallback routing, and per-tenant spending limits
Constraint Solverz3-solver (WASM)Formal NKBA constraint validation
OptimizationOR-Tools (Python)CP-SAT for SKU selection and BOM optimization
Vector Searchpgvector + sentence-transformersCatalog RAG hybrid search
Export (client)jspdf, @svgdotjs/svg.js, web-ifc, html2canvasPDF, SVG, IFC, PNG export
Export (server)ezdxf, IfcOpenShell (Python)DXF and IFC generation
Collaboration (Phase 3)yjs, y-supabaseCRDT real-time collaboration
OrderingManufacturer Order Format generatorElectronic cabinet ordering format (manufacturer-specific CSV/XML). Exports BOM in each manufacturer’s required format for direct submission to their ordering systems. Eliminates manual order entry (30–60 min/order).
3D DXgltfjsxCLI tool that converts GLTF/GLB 3D models into declarative React Three Fiber JSX components. Accelerates manufacturer model integration — imported models become typed, tree-shakeable React components with auto-generated props for animations, materials, and mesh references.
3D Performancer3f-perfReal-time performance monitoring overlay for React Three Fiber scenes. Displays FPS, draw calls, triangle count, GPU memory, and shader compilation metrics. Critical for enforcing per-tier performance budgets (Section 16.3.1.3) during development and for the adaptive quality system (Section 16.3.1.7).
OR-Tools Deferral: Greedy Heuristic for MVP

OR-Tools CP-SAT is deferred to Phase 2. For the MVP (Phase 1, Months 1–6), BOM SKU selection uses a greedy nearest-match heuristic in TypeScript: for each scene graph position, select the catalog product with the closest dimensional match within the budget ceiling, preferring the designer’s selected manufacturer and finish. This covers ~90% of real-world scenarios (single-manufacturer kitchens with consistent finishes). Full CP-SAT optimization (multi-constraint: budget + manufacturer mix + finish consistency + availability + volume pricing) is a Phase 2 deliverable when the Python microservice (services/kb-python/) is production-hardened. The greedy heuristic is ~10 lines of TypeScript vs. a full Python microservice dependency — appropriate for MVP velocity. See Section 16.11 for the existing fallback note.

16.7.1 BOM Preview Component

In-canvas BOM preview panel — a collapsible side panel within the K&B design canvas that displays the Bill of Materials in real time as the user places items. Updates live as scene objects change.

  • Item list: Catalog product name, SKU, manufacturer, finish/color variant
  • Quantities: Auto-counted from scene graph (e.g., 3× base cabinets, 1× sink)
  • Prices: Unit price and line total from kb_catalog_products, subtotal and grand total at bottom
  • Export buttons: PDF (via jspdf), CSV (native), Electronic Cabinet Order (manufacturer-specific order format)
  • Panel behavior: Toggleable via toolbar button (ClipboardList icon). Docked to right side of canvas. 320px width, scrollable. Collapses to icon-only on mobile. Panel state persisted in Zustand store.
  • Data source: Reads directly from the scene graph’s catalog references, joined against kb_catalog_products for pricing. No separate API call — derived from in-memory scene state.

16.8 Database Schema

Five new tables extend the Supabase database. All tables include tenant_id columns and RLS policies using current_setting('app.tenant_id', true) — the same set_tenant_context() pattern established in Section 5. The user_id column tracks the creator/owner within the tenant for fine-grained access (e.g., “my designs” vs “team designs”), but tenant isolation is always enforced at the RLS layer.

SQL
-- 1. Design projects
CREATE TABLE kb_projects (
  id            UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  tenant_id     UUID REFERENCES tenants NOT NULL,
  user_id       UUID REFERENCES auth.users NOT NULL,
  name          TEXT NOT NULL,
  scene_data    JSONB NOT NULL DEFAULT '{}',
  settings      JSONB NOT NULL DEFAULT '{}',
  thumbnail_url TEXT,
  status        TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'draft',
  version       INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 1,
  created_at    TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(),
  updated_at    TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()
);

-- 2. Version history
CREATE TABLE kb_project_versions (
  id          UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  project_id  UUID REFERENCES kb_projects ON DELETE CASCADE,
  version     INTEGER NOT NULL,
  scene_data  JSONB NOT NULL,
  created_by  UUID REFERENCES auth.users,
  created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(),
  UNIQUE(project_id, version)
);

-- 3. Manufacturer registry
CREATE TABLE kb_manufacturers (
  id       UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  name     TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE,
  slug     TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE,
  website  TEXT,
  logo_url TEXT
);

-- 4. Catalog products (SKUs) with full-text + vector search
CREATE TABLE kb_catalog_products (
  id              UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  manufacturer_id UUID REFERENCES kb_manufacturers,
  sku             TEXT NOT NULL,
  name            TEXT NOT NULL,
  category        TEXT NOT NULL,  -- cabinet, appliance, countertop, plumbing
  width           NUMERIC,
  height          NUMERIC,
  depth           NUMERIC,
  finishes        JSONB,
  price_usd       NUMERIC,
  price_confirmed BOOLEAN DEFAULT false,
  gltf_url        TEXT,
  search_vector   TSVECTOR,
  embedding       VECTOR(384),
  UNIQUE(manufacturer_id, sku)
);

CREATE INDEX idx_catalog_search ON kb_catalog_products USING GIN(search_vector);
CREATE INDEX idx_catalog_embedding ON kb_catalog_products USING hnsw(embedding vector_cosine_ops);

-- 5. Bill of materials
CREATE TABLE kb_project_bom (
  id         UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  project_id UUID REFERENCES kb_projects ON DELETE CASCADE,
  node_id    TEXT NOT NULL,
  product_id UUID REFERENCES kb_catalog_products,
  quantity   INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 1,
  unit_price NUMERIC
);

16.9 AI Model Requirements

ModelRoleAPI / RuntimeLatencyCost
Claude Sonnet 4.6 / Opus 4.6Layout generation, NL commandsAnthropic SDK structured outputs~3–5 secPer-token
Gemini 3.0 ProVisual evaluation of floor plansGoogle GenAI SDK~5–8 secPer-token
Z3 SMT SolverFormal NKBA validationWASM (client) or Python~100–500msFree
OR-Tools CP-SATSKU / BOM optimizationPython server-side~200ms–2secFree
Flux/SD (Phase 2)AI image enhancementReplicate / fal.ai~10–30 sec$0.02–$0.06/render
sentence-transformersCatalog embeddingsLocal Python~5ms/embedFree

16.10 Implementation Timeline (6 Months)

MonthFocusKey Deliverables
Month 1FoundationRoom drawing (walls), 2D/3D toggle, measurements, monorepo setup, scene graph store, basic PBR
Month 2Cabinet SystemCabinet runs along walls, auto-countertops, material/finish swap, PBR library, snap system
Month 3Appliances + CatalogAppliance library, drag-drop, CSG cutouts, catalog search, manufacturer import, BOM generation
Month 4NKBA + AIZ3 constraints (24 rules), violation display, Claude layout generation, iteration loop
Month 5Polish + PersistenceProject CRUD, property panel, keyboard shortcuts, elevation views, undo/redo (50 steps)
Month 6Export + GeminiPDF/DXF/SVG/IFC/PNG export, Gemini visual evaluation, full AI loop, Adam UAT

Future Phases:

  • Phase 2: Photorealistic rendering — three-gpu-pathtracer + Flux/SD img2img with ControlNet. Additionally:
    • Bathroom design: Extend room types beyond kitchens — shower/tub enclosures, vanity placement, toilet clearances, ADA compliance rules (grab bars, turning radius). Requires 8–12 additional NKBA Bathroom Planning Guidelines encoded in Z3.
    • IFC export (full): Server-side IFC generation via IfcOpenShell for interoperability with Revit, ArchiCAD, and other BIM tools. Client-side web-ifc handles lightweight preview; server handles full IFC4 schema compliance.
    • OR-Tools CP-SAT optimization: Full constraint-programming BOM optimization replaces the greedy nearest-match heuristic used in MVP (Phase 1). Required for multi-constraint scenarios: budget ceiling + preferred manufacturer + finish consistency + availability. See Section 16.11 for MVP fallback note.
    • Non-orthogonal walls: Support angled walls, bay windows, and non-90° corners. Requires updates to the resolveRunToCoordinates() function, wall mitering logic, and countertop auto-generation along non-parallel wall segments.
    • Dealer volume pricing: Tiered pricing support in kb_catalog_products — unit price varies by quantity brackets (1–9, 10–24, 25+). BOM optimization considers volume discounts when selecting SKUs. Requires manufacturer pricing tier data from Adam’s dealer relationships.
  • Phase 3: Real-time collaboration via Yjs CRDT synced through y-supabase
Staffing & AI-Buildability

This timeline assumes AI-driven development (Claude Code, Cursor, AI agent pipelines) with Joseph as the sole developer + Adam (4–8 hrs/wk domain expertise for manufacturer relationships, NKBA validation, and design review). ~80–90% AI-buildable (schemas, stores, CRUD, API, UI, validation rules, constraint encodings, tests). ~10–20% requires human 3D/geometry expertise: wall mitering, CSG edge cases, cabinet fitting, camera controllers, PBR tuning. No additional engineering hires required.

16.11 Integration with Kiptra Platform

Shared (Reuse from Kiptra)

  • Next.js (App Router), Hono API, Supabase (Postgres + Auth + Storage), Zustand, Zod, Vercel, GitHub CI/CD

New / Dedicated

  • Three.js / React Three Fiber rendering pipeline
  • Z3 WASM constraint solver
  • OR-Tools Python microservice (Fallback: if Python microservice proves operationally burdensome, CP-SAT optimization can be approximated with a greedy/heuristic algorithm in TypeScript for MVP. Full CP-SAT fidelity is only needed for complex multi-constraint BOM optimization with >20 SKU positions.)
  • Catalog RAG system (pgvector + sentence-transformers)
  • Python export services (ezdxf, IfcOpenShell)
  • PBR asset pipeline + 3D model pipeline (Blender automation + procedural generation)

Monorepo Structure

Directory
kiptra-ai/
├── packages/
│   ├── frontend/           # Existing Kiptra Next.js frontend (Section 14)
│   ├── backend/            # Hono API + BullMQ + NATS (Section 14)
│   ├── shared/             # Zod schemas, types, domain interfaces
│   ├── config/             # ESLint, TypeScript, Prettier configs
│   ├── kb-editor/          # K&B design tool frontend (Three.js/R3F)
│   ├── kb-core/            # K&B scene graph, node types, validation
│   └── kb-viewer/          # Embeddable 3D viewer (read-only)
├── services/
│   └── kb-python/          # Python services (OR-Tools, ezdxf, IfcOpenShell)
└── turbo.json
Monorepo Extension Note

The K&B tool adds kb-editor/, kb-core/, and kb-viewer/ as new workspace packages alongside the existing Section 14 structure (frontend/, backend/, shared/, config/). The services/kb-python/ directory introduces a Python microservice — the only non-TypeScript component in the monorepo. This runs as a separate Docker container (see Section 12 implications) and communicates with the Hono backend via internal HTTP, not as a Turborepo workspace package. This is an exception to the Monolith-First principle (Section 3b), justified by the fact that Z3, OR-Tools, ezdxf, and IfcOpenShell have no viable TypeScript alternatives.

16.12 Key Risks

RiskLikelihoodImpactMitigation
3D model pipeline bottleneck — inconsistent manufacturer formats High High Procedural cabinet generator covers ~90% of standard types. Blender automation for batch conversion. Async loading with bounding-box placeholders.
Claude layout quality insufficient Medium High Cabinet-run abstraction eliminates direct coordinate errors. Z3 catches formal violations. Gemini provides aesthetic feedback. Max 5 iteration rounds.
Manufacturer catalog data inconsistency High Medium Strict Zod schema validation on ingest. Data cleaning pipeline. 90-day price staleness warnings. Adam validates for launch manufacturers.
Z3 WASM bundle size too large Medium Medium Lazy-load Z3 on demand. Server-side fallback. TypeScript rules handle real-time checks without Z3.
CSG performance for cutouts Medium Low three-bvh-csg uses BVH acceleration. Pre-compute on placement (not per-frame). Cache in scene graph.

Key insight: The highest-risk items (3D model pipeline and catalog data) are both mitigated by Adam’s direct manufacturer relationships and domain expertise. His involvement is not optional — it’s a critical dependency for the entire 6-month build.

A. Revised Pricing Model

The pricing model uses a high setup fee + recurring monthly structure that front-loads revenue and reduces churn sensitivity. All personas (contractor, dealer, sales rep, manufacturer) use the same pricing tiers. The template determines the experience, not the price. Persona-specific pricing (e.g., lower-cost sales rep tier) is evaluated post-launch based on adoption data — see Section 6a and kiptra-multi-persona-architecture.md Section 9 for the full analysis.

Pricing Structure

Setup fees range from $5,000–$20,000 (blended avg $8,000 at 60/30/10 mix). Monthly recurring ranges from $275–$750/mo (blended avg $390).

Pricing Tiers

Starter
3 operational agents + Support Agent
$5,000
one-time setup
$275/mo
recurring
Limited to 1 integration connection (JobTread OR QuickBooks, not both).
 
Professional
6 operational agents + Support Agent
$10,000
one-time setup
$500/mo
recurring
All integrations + priority onboarding + 30/60/90 day check-ins.
 
Enterprise
6 operational agents + Support Agent + custom agent configuration
$20,000
one-time setup
$750/mo
recurring
Dedicated success manager + custom SLA + all integrations.
 

Setup Fee Breakdown

Tier Setup Fee Monthly Notes
Starter $5,000 $275 3 operational agents + Support Agent, limited to 1 integration connection (JobTread OR QuickBooks, not both)
Professional $10,000 $500 6 operational agents + Support Agent, all integrations, priority onboarding + 30/60/90 day check-ins
Enterprise $20,000 $750 6 operational agents + Support Agent + custom agent configuration, dedicated success manager + custom SLA + all integrations
Blended Average $8,000 $390 60/30/10 mix (conservative) — used in all projections below
Why This Works

Setup fees reflect the consulting-grade onboarding process: OAuth integration, workflow configuration, agent prompt customization, and team training. At $275/mo Starter tier, a contractor saving $2,000/mo in admin labor recoups the $5,000 setup fee in 2.5 months. The setup fee qualifies serious buyers and front-loads revenue. Churn only loses $275–$750/mo — the $5K–$20K is already banked.

Estimated onboarding effort: 8–12 hours per customer (OAuth setup, prompt tuning, team training). At founder-led rates this is uncosted in Year 1; at $150/hr loaded cost it represents $1,200–$1,800 per customer, reducing effective Starter-tier setup fee margin to ~65–75% rather than 100% (on a $5,000 fee). Pro ($10K) and Enterprise ($20K) tiers yield higher margins at the same labor hours: ~82–88% and ~91–94% respectively.

Onboarding capacity constraint: At 8–12 hours per customer, onboarding 5 customers/month consumes 40–60 hours/month of founder time. This becomes the primary sales velocity limiter before the Customer Success hire. Budget founder time allocation accordingly: 60% development, 20% onboarding, 20% sales in Months 4–8.

Onboarding labor impact: At 8–12 hours per customer and $150/hr loaded cost, Y2 onboarding labor for 116 new customers ≈ $174K, Y3 for 168 customers ≈ $252K. In Year 1, this is absorbed by founders (uncosted). In Y2–Y3, the Customer Success hire ($35K part-time, then full-time) partially absorbs this — but at 116 customers/year, onboarding alone is a full-time role (928–1,392 hours). The P&L does not include onboarding labor as a separate line item; it is partially absorbed by founder time and partially by the CS hire.

B. AI Cost Model

Critical — Existential Dependency

Model routing is not optional. Without smart routing, AI costs exceed recurring revenue per customer. This must be built in Wave 2 as a non-negotiable infrastructure component.

Per-Model Pricing (verified March 2026)

All prices per 1M tokens. Model pricing changes frequently — verify against provider pricing pages before launch.

Model Input Output Cache Read Cache Write
Claude Opus 4.6 $5.00 $25.00 $0.50 $6.25
Claude Sonnet 4.6 $3.00 $15.00 $0.30 $3.75
Claude Haiku 4.5 $1.00 $5.00 $0.10 $1.25
GPT-4.1 $2.00 $8.00 $0.50 Free
GPT-4.1 Mini $0.20 $0.80 $0.10 Free
GPT-4.1 Nano $0.10 $0.40 $0.025 Free

Model naming: All references use canonical names: Claude Haiku 4.5, Claude Sonnet 4.6, GPT-4.1 Nano, GPT-4.1 Mini. Verify exact model IDs against Vercel AI Gateway / provider documentation before launch.

Anthropic cache write costs: Anthropic charges a premium for cache writes, unlike OpenAI (free). 5-minute TTL write = 1.25x base input price; 1-hour TTL (extended caching) write = 2x base input price. Example: Sonnet 4.6 cache write at 5-min TTL = $3.75/MTok, at 1-hour TTL = $6.00/MTok. The Cache Write column above reflects the 5-min TTL rate. Factor the higher 1-hour TTL cost into any batch/scheduled agent workloads that benefit from longer cache persistence.

Cost Scenarios (Per Customer/Month, 20–30% Blended Cache Hit Rate)

Cache hit rate assumptions: 20–30% blended cache hit rate across all tiers. Starter customers sharing standard templates achieve ~50–60% cache hits; Enterprise customers with custom prompts achieve ~10–20%. Cache write cost ($3.75/1M tokens for Sonnet, 25% premium) is included. Cache TTL of 5 minutes (extendable to 1 hour) means scheduled/batch agents (e.g., daily briefings) have near-zero cache benefit — only interactive session agents benefit meaningfully.

All Expensive
$495.40/mo
LOSS on $275 Starter
Every task routed to Opus/Sonnet at maximum token volumes. Worst case. Unsustainable without higher tiers. Note: “Unoptimized” cost (~$54/customer) refers to all tasks routed to Sonnet 4.6 (not Opus) at standard token volumes. The $495 “All Expensive” scenario is a different, extreme scenario with all tasks via Opus/Sonnet at maximum volumes.
Smart Routing
$144.90/mo
$245.10 margin (63%) on blended $390
Recommended. Task-appropriate model selection. Sustainable and profitable. The $144.90/month figure represents the ceiling — all 7 agents (6 operational + Support) running daily at full token volumes. Realistic median usage (not all agents fire daily, prompt caching at 90%, smart routing) is approximately $35/customer/month. Financial projections use this median figure.
Aggressive Routing
$22.25/mo
$367.75 margin (94%) on blended $390
Maximum Nano/Mini usage. Possible quality tradeoffs. Aspirational target.

Per-Agent Daily Costs (Smart Routing)

Leads Agent
$0.28
Project Management Agent
$0.31
Design Agent
$0.62
Bookkeeping Agent
$0.74
CEO Agent
$0.80
Sales Agent
$1.93
Support Agent
$0.15

Daily total (7 agents): $4.83/day = $144.90/month per customer. Support Agent AI cost: Haiku ($0.05/day) + Sonnet for diagnostics ($0.10/day) = $0.15/day = $4.50/month.

Cost derivation: An “agent invocation” is one user-triggered or scheduled task execution. Average LLM calls per invocation varies by agent (Leads Agent = 3, Sales Agent = 6–8, CEO Agent = 4). Average tokens per call: input ~3K (including system prompt + context), output ~800 for intermediate steps, ~2K for final output. Cost = invocations/day × calls/invocation × ((input_tokens × input_rate) + (output_tokens × output_rate)). The “75 calls/day” assumption refers to total LLM API calls across all invocations, not user-initiated tasks.

Per-Tier AI Cost Breakdown (Smart Routing, realistic median usage):

TierAgents ActiveEst. AI Cost/moNotes
Starter3 operational + Support~$15–25Only 3 agents fire; lower call volume (1K/day cap)
Professional6 operational + Support~$30–45All agents active; moderate call volume (5K/day cap)
Enterprise6 operational + Support (heavy)~$40–70Full agents, heavy usage; capped at $200/mo AI spend per tenant
Blended (60/30/10 mix)—~$24Starter-dominated mix drives blended cost well below $35 ceiling

The $144.90/month figure assumes all 7 agents running daily at full token volumes — a theoretical ceiling. Actual per-tier costs are significantly lower because Starter customers use fewer agents and lower call volumes.

$24 vs $35 reconciliation: The blended AI cost at the 60/30/10 tier mix is approximately $24/customer/month at median usage. The P&L (Appendix D) deliberately uses $35/customer/month as a conservative ceiling — representing the per-professional-customer cost with all 6 agents active at above-median frequency. This ~45% buffer absorbs usage spikes, cache miss scenarios, and model price fluctuations. Both figures are correct for their respective purposes: $24 for expected blended cost, $35 for conservative P&L planning.

Sales Agent is the Cost Center

At $1.93/day, the Sales Agent accounts for ~40% of total AI spend. It requires Sonnet-class models for accurate estimate generation and comparison. Optimize here first — caching estimate templates and historical data can reduce this by 30–40%.

Model Routing Strategy

Task Complexity Target Model Cost (per 1M out) Examples
Simple GPT-4.1 Nano $0.40 Classify, extract, schedule, file
Medium Claude Haiku 4.5 $5.00 Draft emails, summaries, structured updates
Complex Claude Sonnet 4.6 $15.00 Estimates, financial analysis, synthesis
Frontier Claude Opus 4.6 $25.00 CEO strategic briefings (1x/day max). Reserved for tasks where Sonnet quality is measurably insufficient
Key Insight

Opus ($25/1M output) is reserved exclusively for the Frontier tier — CEO strategic briefings requiring maximum reasoning depth (1x/day max per customer). All other agent workloads, including complex tasks like estimate generation, run on Sonnet 4.6 ($15/1M output) or cheaper. Opus usage is budget-capped and monitored; if Sonnet quality proves sufficient for strategic briefings during Wave 4 testing, the Frontier tier collapses into Complex and Opus is eliminated entirely.

C. Churn Model

Annual churn scenarios modeled across three years of growth. Churn is measured annually and applied at year-end for simplicity.

Growth Assumptions

Year 1
47
new customers (ramp 2/mo to 5/mo)
Year 2
116
new customers (6–12/mo)
Year 3
168
new customers (12–15/mo)

Churn Impact on Active Customers

Annual Churn End Y1 End Y2 End Y3 Total Churned
0% (ideal) 47 163 331 0
10% 42 142 279 52
15% (best case — setup fee stickiness + high satisfaction) 40 133 256 75
20% 38 123 233 98
30% (optimistic base — below industry median, achievable with strong onboarding and support) 33 104 190 141
40% (industry median for SMB construction SaaS; benchmarks suggest 36–60%) 28 86 152 179
45% (stress test — high-churn environment with competitive pressure) 26 78 135 196
Effective Churn Rates vs. Stated 30%

Churn is applied at year-end to all active customers. Y1 effective rate is lower than stated because customers ramp throughout the year (avg ~22 active, 33 at year-end). Y2–Y3 converge toward the full 30% rate as the customer base matures.

Why Churn is Manageable

30% annual churn is optimistic for SMB SaaS (below the 36–60% industry median). Setup fees partially offset churn impact — revenue is banked upfront regardless of retention. A churned customer only loses $275–$750/mo in recurring revenue. The $5,000–$20,000 setup fee is already banked.

The proactive support system (failure streak detection, onboarding stall intervention, OAuth expiry warnings) directly addresses top SMB churn drivers. The 30% base-case churn rate partially reflects this proactive capability; without it, the appropriate base case would be closer to the 36–40% industry median.

Churn Scenario Framing

The P&L uses 30% as the planning base case, which is deliberately optimistic. The stress test in Appendix D uses 45%. If actual churn tracks at 40% (industry median), Year 3 active customers drop from 190 to approximately 152.

Planning Recommendation: Churn Base Case

The SMB construction SaaS industry median churn is 36–60%. While the 30% assumption is defensible given the high setup fee investment creating stickiness, the financial plan should be validated against 40% churn as the planning base case. At 40% annual churn: Year 3 active customers = 152 (vs 190 at 30%), recurring revenue = ~$550K (vs ~$688K). The 30% scenario is the bull case. The 40% scenario is the planning case. The 45% scenario is the stress test.

D. Revised P&L (3-Year Projection)

Three co-founders (Joseph, TJ, Adam). Founder salaries phased in as revenue supports it (Y1: $0 bootstrapping, Y2: $120K for 2 founders, Y3: $240K for 3 founders). Adam handles sales via his warm network. All projections use 60/30/10 tier mix (conservative), 30% annual churn (optimistic base case — below the 36–60% industry median for SMB SaaS), $35/customer/mo AI cost (smart routing), and include E&O + cyber liability insurance.

Note: This P&L uses 30% annual churn as the planning base case. This is deliberately optimistic — the industry median for SMB construction SaaS is 36–60% (see Appendix C churn scenario analysis). A 40% churn scenario reduces 3-year cumulative recurring revenue by approximately 15–20% (cumulative profit impact is ~5–6% due to setup fee dominance).

Churn Assumption Warning

The 30% base-case churn used throughout this P&L is a bull-case assumption, not the industry median. At 40% churn (industry median per Appendix C), Year 3 active customers drop from 190 to approximately 152, reducing Year 3 recurring revenue by ~$131K. If planning conservatively, use 40% churn as the base case and treat 30% as the optimistic scenario.

Year 1
47 new customers • 30% churn • ~22 avg active • Smart routing ($35/customer/mo)

Average customer derivation: Customers ramp from ~2/month early to ~6/month late, averaging ~4/month with acceleration (2/month Months 1–3, 3/month Months 4–6, 4/month Months 7–9, 6/month Months 10–12 = 6+9+12+18 = 45, plus ~2 from mid-month starts = 47 total new customers in Year 1). Monthly active counts: 2, 5, 8, 11, 14, 18, 22, 26, 32, 38, 44, 47. Average ≈ 22 across the 12-month ramp (used for all Y1 per-customer calculations). End-of-year: 47 new − 14 churned (30%) = 33 active.

Sales timing note: Month 1–3 represent pre-launch beta/Slate testing with zero revenue customers. Paid acquisition begins Month 4 (Wave 4 completion). The monthly active counts reflect paid customers only starting Month 4.

Reflects Supabase Pro→Team upgrade at ~Month 9 ($25/mo → $599/mo for the final 3 months).
