Founder Salary $0
Insurance (E&O + Cyber + AI Liability) -$8,000–$18,000
E&O + Cyber: $3K. AI Liability Insurance (Munich Re / Armilla / Testudo): $5–$15K/yr (pre-launch gate).
Payment Processing (ACH-first, blended ~1.6%) -$5,900
Setup fees via ACH ($5/txn cap). Monthly renewals: mix of ACH ($5 cap) and card (2.9%). Was $13,890 under card-only model.
Net Profit ~$450,000
Net Margin ~92%
Year 2
116 new customers • 104 active at end • ~69 avg active • Smart routing
Setup Fee Revenue $928,000
Recurring Revenue $322,920
AI Compute ($35 × ~69 avg × 12) -$28,980
Infrastructure * -$8,000
(derived from Section 10 scaling bands: mid-year blend between 33-customer and 104-customer infrastructure costs)
Ops -$12,000
Founder Salary (2 × $60K) -$120,000
Insurance (E&O + Cyber + AI Liability) -$10,000–$20,000
Payment Processing (ACH-first, blended ~1.6%) -$20,016
Setup fees via ACH ($5/txn cap). Monthly renewals: blended ACH/card. Was $36,277 under card-only model.
Customer Success (part-time) -$35,000

Support escalation staffing note: The $35K part-time Customer Success hire (Year 2) absorbs support escalations. At 20–25% AI resolution, founders handle ~2–4 escalated tickets/day through Year 1. The full-time CS hire ($55–70K) triggers at 40 active customers (mid-Year 2 in base case) and is included in the Year 2 personnel budget.

Net Profit ~$1,022,000
Net Margin ~82%
Year 3
168 new customers • 190 active at end • ~147 avg active • Smart routing
Setup Fee Revenue $1,344,000
Recurring Revenue $687,960
AI Compute ($35 × ~147 avg × 12) -$61,740
Infrastructure * -$15,000
Ops -$20,000
Founder Salary (3 × $80K) — Joseph and TJ increase from $60K to $80K each; Adam joins payroll at $80K -$240,000
Insurance (E&O + Cyber + AI Liability) -$13,000–$23,000
Payment Processing (ACH-first, blended ~1.6%) -$32,512
Setup fees via ACH ($5/txn cap). Monthly renewals: blended ACH/card. Was $58,927 under card-only model.
SDR / Sales Hire -$65,000

Adam (co-founder) leads sales strategy and closes deals. The SDR hire at 80 customers supplements Adam with outbound prospecting and lead qualification — Adam transitions from hands-on outreach to managing the sales pipeline.

Net Profit ~$1,589,000
Net Margin ~78%
3-Year Cumulative Net Profit
~$3,061,000
Fully-loaded scenario (including deferred developer and SDR hires): ~$2,851,000 cumulative — see Personnel Assumptions callout. ACH-first payment strategy saves ~$50K in processing fees over 3 years vs card-only model.
Methodology Notes

Revenue mix: 60/30/10 (Starter/Pro/Enterprise) — conservative assumption that most early SMB customers choose the entry tier. Blended setup $8,000, blended monthly $390.
AI cost: $35/customer/month based on smart model routing with 20–30% blended cache hit rate (see Section B). 30% annual churn is optimistic for SMB SaaS (below the 36–60% industry median). Setup fees partially offset churn impact — revenue is banked upfront regardless of retention.
Recurring revenue: Pro-rated using average active customers across the year (midpoint of start and end-of-year active counts), not end-of-year snapshots.
Founder salary: Y1 $0 (bootstrapping), Y2 $120K (Joseph + TJ at $60K modest salary), Y3 $240K (Joseph and TJ receive raises from $60K to $80K each; Adam joins payroll at $80K).
Insurance: E&O + cyber liability + AI liability insurance (Munich Re / Armilla / Testudo, $5–$15K/year — pre-launch gate), scaling with customer count and revenue.
Infrastructure *: Infrastructure costs represent the variable platform costs at the relevant customer scale per the Section 10 scaling table. Year 2 ($8,000) reflects the mid-year average as customer count grows from 33 to 104; Year 3 ($15,000) reflects the mid-year average from 104 to 190. These are conservative estimates — actual costs may be higher if the VPS upgrade triggers earlier. See Section 10 infrastructure table for per-customer-band pricing.
Tax note: All profit figures are pre-tax. Federal + state tax burden (estimated 25–30% effective rate for an LLC/S-Corp) would reduce net profits accordingly. Consult a CPA for tax optimization strategies.

Sales & Marketing costs: Not separately line-itemed in the P&L: Referral payments ($24K–$54K/year at scale), conference networking ($1.5K–$4K/year), CRM ($180/year). These are partially offset by the setup fee margin buffer and partially absorbed by founder time. At scale (Y3+), a dedicated Sales & Marketing budget of $30K–$60K/year should be budgeted.

P&L completeness notes: The following items are acknowledged but not separately line-itemed: (a) Referral payouts ($0 Y1, ~$15K Y2, ~$40K Y3) — budget as marketing. (b) Legal ongoing ($5–10K/year Y2+) — regulatory monitoring, contract review beyond initial ToS/Privacy. (c) Conference/travel budget ($8–15K/year for 2 founders × 3–4 events, currently budgeted at $1.5–4K in Ops line — may be underestimated). (d) DO Spaces backup storage ($10–50/month, negligible). These items total ~$30–70K/year at scale and should be separately tracked even if absorbed into the Ops line.

Onboarding Labor Cost (Not Line-Itemed)

Year 2 adds 116 new customers × 8–12 hours onboarding labor per customer × $150/hr (blended founder + CS rate) = $139K–$209K in onboarding labor. This cost is not separately line-itemed in the P&L — it is absorbed by founder time (Y1) and the $35K part-time CS hire (Y2). However, the $35K PT CS hire cannot absorb 116 onboardings at 8–12 hours each (928–1,392 hours = 0.5–0.7 FTE). Either: (a) founders absorb the majority of onboarding in Y2 (reducing time for product development), (b) the CS hire is full-time ($55–70K), or (c) onboarding hours per customer decrease significantly with template maturation. This is a known gap in the P&L model.

Personnel Assumptions & Hiring Cost Reconciliation

Year 1 = founders only ($0 salary — bootstrapping, requires 12+ months personal runway). Year 2 = $35,000 part-time Customer Success (triggered at 15–20 active customers, which occurs mid-Year 1 but hire is deferred to Year 2 start to match cash flow). Developer hire ($70–90K, triggered at 50 customers in mid-Year 2) is NOT included in the Year 2 P&L base case — if included, Year 2 net profit decreases by ~$80K. Year 3 SDR ($65K) is included; Senior Developer ($100–130K, triggered at 150 customers in Year 3) is NOT included. The base-case P&L is founder-heavy through Year 3; fully-loaded P&L with all hiring triggers would reduce Year 2 profit to ~$925K and Year 3 to ~$1,433K.

Stress Test: 45% Churn + $60/customer AI Cost

Worst-case scenario: 45% annual churn (stress test for SMB SaaS per industry benchmarks) combined with ~2.5x realistic AI cost ($60 vs $24 blended actual; ~1.7x the $35 conservative P&L input). If the business survives this, it survives anything.

40% Churn Scenario (Industry Median)

At 40% churn (industry median): Lifetime = 30 months. Gross LTV = $390 × 30 = $11,700. AI cost = $35 × 30 = $1,050. Infra cost = $5 × 30 = $150. Net LTV = $11,700 − $1,050 − $150 = $10,500. Total Customer Value = $10,500 + $8,000 setup = $18,500. LTV:CAC = $18,500 / $1,250 = 14.8:1.

CAC derivation scrutiny: The $1,250 blended CAC assumes: referral fee ($500–$1,000 per customer), founder sales time (4–6 hours × $0 imputed in Y1), demo time (1–2 hours per prospect), onboarding labor (8–12 hours per customer × $0 imputed). At zero founder labor cost, CAC is artificially low. Imputing founder time at $75/hr: sales (5hr) + demo (1.5hr) + onboarding (10hr) = $1,237.50 labor + $750 avg referral = $1,988 true CAC. Adjusted LTV:CAC = $18,500 / $1,988 = 9.3:1 — still strong but materially different from the headline 14.8:1.

Note: Year 1 stress test diverges from base case only in AI cost ($60 vs $35/customer); churn divergence appears in Year 2+. For monthly-churn modeling (more realistic but more complex), Year 1 revenue would be approximately 5–10% lower.

Year Active End Total Revenue Net Profit Margin Status
Year 1 26 $478,960 $435,530 91% Profitable
Year 2 78 $1,171,360 $919,951 79% Profitable
Year 3 135 $1,844,760 $1,366,222 74% Profitable
Business Survives All Scenarios

Even under maximum stress (45% churn + $60/customer AI costs + founder salaries + insurance), the business remains profitable in every year at 78%+ margins. Setup fees dominate early revenue and provide a substantial buffer against recurring cost overruns. The 30% base case vs 40% industry median vs 45% stress test gives a clear range: $3.01M → ~$2.85M → $2.72M cumulative 3-year profit.

Note: Year 1 recurring revenue uses conservative monthly-average methodology. 3-year cumulative figure reflects base-case personnel assumptions — see fully-loaded scenario in Personnel Assumptions callout.

Note: This stress test includes base-case personnel costs (Part-time CS $35K in Y2, SDR $65K in Y3) in the profit figures above. The fully-loaded stress scenario (including developer hires triggered by customer count) would reduce Y2 profit by an additional ~$80K and Y3 profit by an additional ~$130K.

Combined Stress Scenario

Combined stress scenario (not modeled in detail): Higher churn (45%) + higher AI cost ($60/customer) + slower acquisition (25 Y1 customers per the sales capacity callout) would reduce 3-year cumulative profit to approximately $1.5–$1.8M. The business remains profitable but at significantly lower margins (~50–55%).

Nuclear Scenario: Compound Tail Risk

Combines 45% churn + $60/customer AI cost + 75/25/0 tier mix (no Enterprise) + all deferred hires triggered + slower Enterprise adoption. At 75/25/0 mix: blended setup = $6,250, blended monthly = $343.75. Year 2 revenue drops ~22% vs base case. Year 3 with all hires (PT CS $35K, developer $80K, SDR $65K, Sr Dev $115K = $295K total personnel beyond founders): net margin compresses to ~35–40%. A simultaneous AI liability claim ($50–100K uninsured) in this scenario could force the business to draw on reserves or seek emergency funding. This scenario is unlikely but not impossible — it represents the conditions under which the “profitable in all scenarios” claim breaks down.

Recurring Revenue Floor (Zero New Sales Scenario)

Acquisition Dependency

If customer acquisition stops entirely after Year 1, recurring-only revenue from 33 active customers = $12,870/month MRR. Against Year 2’s cost structure (~$17,800/month), this produces a ~$4,930/month loss — demonstrating that the business is acquisition-dependent through Year 2. Break-even on recurring revenue alone requires approximately ~47 customers at the blended $390/month rate against Year 2 costs. This underscores the structural importance of maintaining sales velocity.

Setup fee dependency: Setup fees constitute 66–79% of total revenue across Years 1–3. At steady state (zero new customers), recurring revenue alone is 190 customers × $390 ARPU = $74,100/mo = $889K/yr — sufficient to cover operating costs but a fundamentally different business profile than the growth-phase P&L suggests.

Founder Runway Dependency

Year 1 assumes $0 founder salaries. Joseph and TJ require personal financial runway to sustain 12+ months without income. This is an unstated operational dependency — if either founder faces financial pressure, the build timeline is at risk. The first founder salary ($120K in Year 2) triggers when setup fee revenue exceeds $300K cumulative.

E. Go-to-Market Playbook

Adam brings 1,000+ warm leads from his remodeling industry network. This is not cold outreach — these are real relationships built over years. The playbook below is designed to convert warm relationships into paying customers systematically, then scale beyond Adam's direct outreach through an ambassador network where satisfied customers introduce their peers.

Warm Lead Validation Needed

Adam’s true warm network — contacts who would take a personal call and seriously evaluate a $5K–$20K purchase — is likely 150–200 (Tier A). The broader 1,000+ figure includes Tier C/D contacts who should be modeled at semi-warm/cold conversion rates (2–3% close, not 8%). The Year 1 acquisition target is achievable primarily from Tier A/B contacts; Tier C/D requires different outreach tactics.

Lead Segmentation

Tier Est. Count Profile Priority
Tier A 100–150 Closest relationships, $1M+ revenue, decision makers, tech-forward Immediate
Tier B 200–300 Good relationships, mid-size, likely have the pain Month 2–3
Tier C 300–400 Acquaintances, smaller ops, unknown readiness Month 4+
Tier D Remainder Adjacent pros, referral sources Nurture

Weekly Cadence (2 Hours/Day Dedicated)

Mon
1hr prospecting / list prep
1hr outreach (calls/texts)
Tue
1hr demos (2 slots)
1hr follow-ups
Wed
1hr outreach (new batch)
1hr proposal/close work
Thu
1hr demos (2 slots)
1hr follow-ups
Fri
1hr pipeline review
1hr relationship nurturing
Sales Capacity Constraint

Adam’s 2 hours/day cadence realistically produces 1–3 closed customers/month, not 5. The Year 1 target of 47 customers assumes the sales ramp accelerates beyond what a single part-time founder can sustain. If Adam cannot increase sales time commitment, Year 1 likely closes 20–30 customers. The plan should model this conservative scenario alongside the optimistic 47-customer target.

Acquisition Sensitivity

The base-case P&L uses 47 Year 1 customers. However, Adam’s stated 2-hour/day sales cadence realistically produces 1–3 closed customers/month over ~8 effective selling months (post-Wave 4 launch) = 8–24 customers. The conservative planning scenario is 20–25 customers. At 20 customers: $160K setup + ~$70K recurring = $230K total Y1 revenue, ~$198K Y1 profit. At 25 customers: $200K setup + ~$88K recurring = $288K total Y1 revenue, ~$255K Y1 profit. The 47-customer scenario requires either increased sales time commitment, the ambassador network producing results faster than modeled, or a higher close rate than the 20–25% assumed. Founders should plan operating costs against the 25-customer scenario and treat the 47-customer P&L as the stretch target.

Outreach Sequence (Warm — NOT Cold)

Day 1
Personal text/call: "Hey [Name], working on something new — AI tools built for remodeling companies. Got 15 min this week?"
Day 3
Follow-up if no reply. Keep it casual, one line.
Day 7
Email with Slate case study (2–3 bullet points on results).
Day 14
Final touch — call/voice note with urgency: "Locking pricing for first batch."
Day 30+
Move to nurture. Monthly LinkedIn engagement.

Expected Conversion Funnel (Warm Leads)

Conservative Base Case (25–30% Close Rate)

100 Contacts Reached
50 Replied
50%
32.5 Demo Booked
65%
27.6 Attended
85%
15.2 Proposal Sent
55%
4–5 Closed
25–30%
Conservative: 4–5 Closes Per 100 Contacts Reached

This is the primary planning scenario. An unproven product without extensive customer references should assume 25–30% proposal-to-close rate. At this rate, 100 contacts yield 4–5 customers. Plan pipeline volume and operating costs against this number.

Stretch Case (45% Close Rate)

100 Contacts Reached
55 Replied
55%
38.5 Demo Booked
70%
32.7 Attended
85%
18 Proposal Sent
55%
~8 Closed
45%
Stretch: ~8 Closes Per 100 Contacts Reached

Achievable with strong warm relationships and a compelling Slate case study with measurable ROI data. Warm lead conversion rates are 2–4x higher than cold outreach, but this rate should be earned, not assumed.

Sensitivity note: Stretch case assumes 45% proposal-to-close and 60/30/10 tier mix; conservative base case assumes 25–30%. Conservative scenario: 25% close rate and 80/15/5 mix reduces Year 1 customers from 47 to ~26 and blended monthly from $390 to $354. The business remains profitable at this level due to setup fee front-loading.

Close rate scenario range: 45% close rate is the optimistic case with strong warm relationships and a compelling Slate case study. Conservative base case: 25–30% close rate for an unproven product without extensive customer references. At 25%, 100 contacts yield ~4 customers instead of 8 — which would halve Year 1 customer count to 20–25 unless pipeline volume increases proportionally.

Timeline alignment: Wave 4 delivers first paying customers at Week 14 (~Month 3.5). The effective sales window for Year 1 is approximately 7–8 months, not 12. Sales outreach (relationship warming, curiosity-building) begins during Wave 3, but demos with commitment-ready prospects should not start until the Slate case study has measurable results (earliest Month 5–6).

Timeline note (Wave 4 + GTM reconciliation): Wave 4 launches first 3–5 paying customers at Week 14 (~Month 3.5). The GTM section’s Month 5–6 demo gate applies to outbound sales to NEW prospects — Slate and Tier A warm leads from the co-founder network can onboard at Wave 4 completion.

Sales Cycle Model

Average sales cycle: 30–45 days from first contact to closed deal (warm leads with existing relationship). Cold/semi-warm leads (Tier C/D) may take 60–90 days. First closes are expected ~6 weeks after Wave 4 launch, once the Slate case study has measurable results. The effective closing window in Year 1 is 5–6 months (not 12). At a conservative pace of 2–3 closes/month, this yields 10–18 customers in Year 1 from direct outreach alone. Ambassador referrals and inbound channels supplement this from Month 4+.

Demo Script (20 Minutes Max)

Rapport + Context (3 min)
Reconnect personally. Reference shared history.
Pain Confirmation (3 min)
Listen. Repeat their pain back to them. Do not pitch yet.
Bridge Statement (30 sec)
"That's exactly what we built this for."
Live Slate Demo (8–10 min)
Show the REAL product, not slides. Walk through their workflow.
ROI Translation (3 min)
Anchor to THEIR numbers. "You said you spend X hours on estimates..."
Close / Next Step (2 min)
Be specific. "Can I send the agreement today?" — not "let me know."

CRM: Pipedrive ($15/mo)

Pipeline stages:

Warm Lead → Contacted → Demo Scheduled → Demo Completed → Proposal Sent → Negotiating → Closed Won / Closed Lost / Nurture

CRM consideration: Evaluate using HubSpot Free (already an integration target) instead of Pipedrive for Kiptra’s own pipeline — enables dogfooding the Leads Agent on Kiptra’s own lead data, creating a live demo environment.

Dogfooding mandate (Wave 3a+): From Wave 3a onward, use Kiptra’s own agents to manage Kiptra’s sales pipeline. The Leads Agent handles Kiptra’s inbound leads, the CEO Agent generates Kiptra’s own morning briefings, and the Sales Agent (adapted) tracks Kiptra’s customer economics. This serves three purposes: (1) live demo environment with real data, (2) continuous dogfooding catches UX issues before customers do, (3) proves the product works by using it to sell itself.

Pricing Urgency (Authentic)

  • Starter: $5,000 setup + $275/mo
  • Professional: $10,000 setup + $500/mo
  • Enterprise: $20,000 setup + $750/mo
  • Scarcity: "I have two onboarding cohorts per month with 5 spots each — the next cohort starts [date]"

Batch Onboarding

  • New customers start in 2-week cohorts (1st and 15th of month)
  • Max 5 per cohort
  • All onboarding conducted remotely via Google Meet/Zoom — zero travel costs
  • Technical setup (JobTread grant key + QuickBooks + Google/Microsoft OAuth connections) takes under 1 hour — OAuth flows are standardized click-to-authorize steps; customers choose Google or Microsoft for email and calendar; JobTread uses a grant key (guided walkthrough with direct link to app.jobtread.com/grants and video), not manual configuration
  • Self-serve option: tech-forward customers can follow along and connect integrations independently
  • Productize: recorded Loom walkthroughs for common steps, live time only for custom config
  • Creates urgency and operational efficiency
  • Support Agent assists with onboarding questions 24/7, escalates to founders only for edge cases

Onboarding clarity: Technical OAuth setup takes under 1 hour (standardized click-to-authorize steps). Full onboarding including prompt tuning, team training, and go-live validation takes 8–12 hours spread over 2 weeks. Batch the 1-hour technical setup; schedule configuration and training as async/self-serve with live touchpoints for blockers.

Referral Program

  • Tiered referral payouts: Starter plan referral = $500 cash, Professional plan referral = $1,000 cash, Enterprise plan referral = $1,500 cash. This aligns payout to ~30% of the referred customer’s setup fee ACV. Cash incentives outperform account credits for B2B referral programs (HubSpot benchmark: $1,000 cash per referral).
  • $1,000 off setup fees for the referred customer
  • Ask at two moments: first "win" moment + 60-day mark
  • Target: 2–3 referrals/month from base of 20 customers
Referral Program Cost Impact (Currently Excluded from Headline P&L)

At Year 3 scale with 168 new customers, assuming 20% referral-sourced at $1,000–$1,500 cash per referral = $33K–$50K annual cost. This reduces Year 3 net profit from ~$1,563K to ~$1,513–$1,530K. Referral payments should be budgeted as a marketing line item starting Year 2 when the ambassador network is formalized.

Key Conferences (2026–2027)

Build Expo USA
Dallas + Austin
Apr 22–23 • May 27–28
JLC Live (Journal of Light Construction)
Regional events — remodeling contractors and small builders
Multiple dates
International Builders’ Show (IBS)
Las Vegas — largest US residential construction event
Feb 2027
NARI Regional Events
Join if not already member
Ongoing
IBS / KBIS 2027
Las Vegas — plan now
Jan/Feb 2027
Conference Strategy

Attend, don't exhibit. Pre-schedule meetings. Host dinner for 8–10 prospects ($500–$1K cost, massive ROI). Exhibition booths are $5K–$15K and yield fewer quality conversations than a curated dinner.

Conference selection rationale: NARI chapter events, IBS, and JLC Live are where Kiptra’s actual ICP — remodeling contractors and small builders — congregates. ServiceTitan Pantheon targets HVAC/plumbing (wrong vertical); Groundbreak by Procore targets enterprise GCs (wrong segment). Focus conference budget on events with the highest density of target buyers.

Adam's First 90 Days

Adam’s 90-day sales clock starts at Wave 4 completion (~Month 3.5), not project Week 1. Weeks 1–2 below are relative to Adam’s start date.

Weeks 1–2: Foundation
Set up Pipedrive, import/score all contacts, create Slate case study PDF, write 3 outreach templates.
Month 2: Launch Outreach
Begin Tier A outreach at 25/week. Book first demos. Close first 3–5 deals. Begin batch onboarding.
Month 3: Scale
8–12 customers cumulative. Launch referral program. Begin Tier B. Productize onboarding (Loom videos).
Inbound Demand Generation

Adam posts 2–3 LinkedIn pieces per week about AI in remodeling, leveraging industry credibility. Goal: 5,000+ following of remodeling contractors by Month 12. This warms the Tier C/D network and creates inbound leads that supplement outbound as warm contacts are exhausted.

Self-Reinforcing Loop

Kiptra solves Adam's own bandwidth problem. As Slate's first customer, the 6 operational agents automate the admin work that currently consumes his time. More agent automation at Slate → more free hours for Adam → more sales outreach → more customers → more revenue → Adam transitions to full-time Kiptra sales. The platform sells itself by freeing up its own salesperson.

Inbound Demand Generation Channels

Outbound alone cannot sustain Year 2+ growth. These inbound channels build pipeline that compounds over time.

  • SEO blog (Month 2+): Publish 2–4 articles/month targeting 10 initial keywords: “AI for remodeling contractors,” “construction estimate software,” “remodeling business automation,” “contractor project management AI,” etc. Host on kiptra.io/blog (Next.js static pages for SEO). Goal: 500 organic visitors/month by Month 6, 2,000 by Month 12.
  • YouTube case studies (monthly): 5–10 minute videos showing real contractor results with Kiptra. “How [Contractor Name] saves 15 hours/week with AI agents.” Cross-post to LinkedIn. Low production cost (screen recording + talking head).
  • Email nurture for Tier C/D contacts: Monthly email newsletter with industry insights, product updates, and customer success stories. Segment by engagement (opens/clicks). Move engaged Tier C contacts to Tier B outreach list. Use SendGrid (already in stack) for delivery.
  • NKBA partnership: Explore National Kitchen & Bath Association partnership or sponsorship for visibility with K&B-specific contractors ahead of the K&B Design Tool launch (Month 10+).
  • Monthly webinar: “AI for Remodelers” 30-minute live demo + Q&A session. Target 50–100 registrations per session. Record and repurpose as YouTube content and blog posts. Registration captures leads for nurture pipeline.

Ambassador Network Effect

Adam is the launch vehicle, not the long-term engine. His first customers become ambassadors — satisfied clients who set up meetings with peers in their network. Construction is a relationship industry: a recommendation from a trusted peer is worth more than any demo.

Active Referrers
20%
of customers become active ambassadors (not 100%)
By Month 6
3–6
referrals/quarter from ~3 active ambassadors (15 customers × 20%), generating 1–2 intros/quarter each
Compounding Effect
Y2+
meaningful but not a primary acquisition channel until the base is larger

Realistic ambassador model: meaningful but supplementary channel in Year 1. Identify 3–5 marquee “design partner” customers for intensive relationship investment. Each new cohort of customers seeds the next wave of introductions, becoming a primary channel in Year 2 when the base is larger.

Ambassador Incentive

$1,000–$1,500 cash per successful referral (already in referral program above), plus exclusive access to beta features and a quarterly Customer Advisory Board dinner. The CAB dinner serves double duty: relationship deepening + product feedback from power users. Cost: ~$1K/quarter for 8–10 attendees. ROI: each dinner generates 5–10 warm introductions.

Expansion Revenue (NRR)

Net Revenue Retention Target: 105–110% by Year 2
  • Add-on agent modules ($50–$100/month each) for vertical-specific capabilities (e.g., Permit Tracker, Subcontractor Portal)
  • Additional user seats above base tier limits
  • Enterprise usage overages billed at cost (pass-through of actual AI spend) above tier limits

Expansion revenue partially offsets gross churn, improving unit economics as the customer base matures.

Conservative Y2 expansion revenue estimate: $50–75K from upsells and add-on modules across the active customer base. Include in P&L projections with a 50% confidence discount ($25–37.5K modeled). This partially offsets gross churn and improves unit economics as the customer base matures.

Hiring Triggers

TriggerRoleEstimated CostWhy
15–20 active customersPart-time Customer Success / Technical Onboarding Coordinator$30–40K/yrHired when running more than 2 simultaneous onboarding cohorts (~15–20 active customers). Adam cannot sell AND onboard simultaneously.
40 active customersFull-time Customer Success$55–70K/yr30/60/90 check-ins, support escalations, churn prevention
50 active customersJunior/Mid Developer$70–90K/yrFeature velocity, bug fixes, integration maintenance
30–40 active customersPart-time Content Marketer$25–35K/yrBlog posts, case studies, YouTube scripts, email nurture sequences. Frees founders from content creation and accelerates inbound pipeline.
80 active customersSecond salesperson / SDR$50–65K/yr + commissionAdam’s Tier A/B leads depleting; need outbound expansion
150 active customersSenior Developer$100–130K/yrArchitecture scaling, multi-node infrastructure

Personnel costs are NOT included in the P&L base case (founders bootstrap initially). These hires are funded from operating profit as triggers are hit. At 40 customers ($15,600/mo recurring), the business generates sufficient margin to fund the first hire.

Bus Factor Mitigation

  • Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) for every major technical decision — stored in /docs/adr/ in the monorepo.
  • Mandatory code review: both developers review each other’s PRs (no self-merge except hotfixes).
  • System documentation: runbook, on-call rotation (alternating weeks), and architecture diagrams kept current.
  • Knowledge transfer: each developer must be able to operate all critical systems (NATS, RLS, encryption, deployment).
  • Wave 1 deliverable: Initial ADR template + first 5 ADRs (auth choice, database architecture, event bus, AI gateway, deployment strategy).

Key Metrics to Track Weekly

Activity
Outreach sent • Demos booked • Demos completed
Pipeline
Proposals sent • Deals closed • Referrals received
Revenue
Setup fees collected • MRR added • Total MRR

Revenue Velocity Monitoring (Early Warning System)

Structural Risk: Year 1 Revenue is 78% Non-Recurring

Setup fees dominate early revenue ($376K setup vs $103K recurring in Year 1). If customer acquisition slows, the revenue cliff is steep. Weekly velocity dashboards catch slowdowns at week 2, not month 6.

Revenue Velocity Floor

Year 1 revenue is 78% non-recurring (setup fees). If acquisition slows, there is no recurring revenue cushion. Define a weekly setup fee velocity floor: at the 25-customer planning scenario, the target is ~3 new customers/month or ~$19K setup revenue/month. If setup revenue drops below $10K/month for two consecutive months (roughly 1 new customer/month), trigger a Plan B: increase Adam’s sales time allocation, activate paid marketing channels (Google Ads targeting “construction project management software”), or accelerate the ambassador network formalization.

Leading Indicators (Weekly)
Outreach volume • Reply rate • Demo book rate • Pipeline value
Lagging Indicators (Monthly)
Close rate trend • MRR growth • Setup fees collected • Churn rate
Trigger Actions
Close rate drops → adjust messaging • Pipeline thin → hit Tier B sooner • Referrals low → activate program harder

F. Risk Register

Known risks with severity assessment and mitigation strategies. Risks are categorized by impact and likelihood.

# Risk Severity Mitigation
1 AI costs exceed monthly recurring per customer High Smart model routing (non-negotiable), per-tenant spending limits, per-provider daily spending caps (AI-28), token budgets per agent
2 Churn at 40%+ (above optimistic 30% base case) High Industry median is 36–60% for SMB SaaS (see Appendix C). Mitigations: batch onboarding for quality, 30/60/90 day check-ins, track NPS, proactive support agent intervention on usage drops. At 40% churn, Year 3 active customers drop from 190 to ~152.
3 QuickBooks OAuth / JobTread grant key expiration Low Weekly background token refresh for QB (100-day expiry), monthly lightweight API call to keep JobTread grant keys alive (3-month inactivity expiry), re-auth alert system for both
4 VPS failure Low Dual-VPS architecture from Day 1. Two identical DigitalOcean droplets behind a managed Load Balancer with 30-second health checks. If primary fails (3 consecutive failures = 90 seconds), traffic auto-routes to the secondary node (already actively serving traffic in Hybrid Active-Active mode for HTTP request handling). NATS JetStream HA via 3-node Raft cluster — event-driven agent execution continues uninterrupted during single-node failure. Valkey HA (automatic failover replica) ensures cache/queue continuity. 0% customer impact on HTTP requests, event processing, or queue operations during single-node failure. Remaining SPOF is Supabase (managed HA) — mitigated by pg_dump backups every 6 hours to DO Spaces (6-hour RPO).
5 Slate PoC doesn't deliver results High Get Slate metrics by Week 4, pivot agent priorities based on real usage
6 Model routing misconfiguration Medium Wrong model assigned to a task type degrades quality or inflates cost. Mitigation: Quarterly routing audit comparing actual token costs per agent against expected costs; automated alert if any agent’s cost-per-call deviates >30% from baseline.
7 Integration API changes/breaks Low Anti-Corruption Layer with canonical domain model (Section 7a), local data mirror with webhook + polling sync, automated Pave schema snapshot tests, version pinning, weekly integration health checks. CSV/manual import as universal fallback.
8 Adam's bandwidth split between Slate and Kiptra sales Low Kiptra's own agents automate Slate's admin work, directly freeing Adam's time. As revenue grows, Adam transitions from part-time sales to full-time. The product solves the salesperson's bandwidth problem — a self-reinforcing loop.
9 Legal & Compliance Exposure Medium
  • AI liability disclaimers in ToS: “AI outputs are decision support tools, not professional advice. All estimates, financial summaries, and recommendations must be reviewed by qualified humans before use.”
  • E&O + cyber liability insurance: Covers claims if an AI-generated estimate leads to a financial loss. Budgeted Y1: $3K, Y2: $5K, Y3: $8K. Additionally (PRE-LAUNCH GATE): obtain written E&O confirmation covering AI-generated outputs + evaluate standalone AI liability insurance (Munich Re / Armilla / Testudo, $5–$15K/year) before first paying customer.
  • Privacy Policy + DPA: Customer data governed by data processing agreements. Tenant data isolation explicitly documented. DPAs required with Anthropic and OpenAI before processing customer financial data. Both providers offer zero-data-retention API agreements.
  • No model training on customer data: Customer data is NEVER used for model training — antitrust mitigation prevents algorithmic bid-rigging claims if competing contractors use the platform.
  • Lien waiver disclaimers: AI-generated documents must be reviewed by contractor’s legal counsel before use.
  • CCPA compliance: Data access/deletion requests honored within 45 calendar days (CCPA statutory requirement), with option to extend by additional 45 days with consumer notification. Acknowledge receipt within 10 business days. Kiptra targets 30-day completion as internal SLA exceeding CCPA minimum. Data discovery spans ALL stores: Supabase (primary), Valkey (session/cache), NATS JetStream logs, Grafana Loki (structured logs), and DO Spaces backups. Identity verification required for all data access/deletion requests (email ownership confirmation + account authentication). Backup handling: daily pg_dump backups containing deleted tenant data are purged on natural rotation (30-day retention cycle), not individually scrubbed — documented as a CCPA-permitted exception under the ‘unreasonable effort’ clause. Third-party processor deletion: Anthropic and OpenAI delete conversation data per their respective DPAs (zero-data-retention API agreements already mandated).
  • Legal counsel: Adam (co-founder) manages legal counsel relationship for ongoing compliance.
  • Terms of Service & Privacy Policy (Wave 4 pre-launch gate): ToS and Privacy Policy must be drafted and published before the first paying customer. Budget: $3K–$5K for legal counsel. ToS must include: AI liability disclaimers, limitation of liability, SLA terms (RPO/RTO disclosure), data ownership clauses, and acceptable use policy. Privacy Policy must include: CCPA-compliant data collection disclosure, cookie policy, and third-party processor list (Anthropic, OpenAI, SendGrid, Stripe, DigitalOcean) with data retention periods per processor.
  • Customer-facing DPA: Template Data Processing Agreement for B2B customers (standard expectation for $5K+ annual SaaS contracts). Include in Wave 4 legal preparation alongside ToS. DPA template covers: data processing scope, sub-processor list, breach notification timeline (72 hours), data deletion obligations.
  • Contractor licensing risk: AI-generated estimates may implicate state contractor licensing requirements in certain jurisdictions. Mitigation: all estimates are labeled “decision support — requires licensed contractor review before submission.” Legal review of top 10 target states’ licensing requirements before launch. This is a Wave 4 pre-launch legal item.
10 Competitive Response (Buildertrend / Procore ship AI) Medium
  • Timeline: 12–24 months before incumbents can ship equivalent multi-agent AI
  • Assessment: Buildertrend has NO public API. Launched AI-powered Client Updates (mid-2025). Active AI roadmap under evaluation but no multi-agent orchestration capabilities announced (verified Mar 2026). Adding multi-agent AI to a monolithic legacy platform is a 2–3 year engineering effort minimum. Procore has announced “Agentic APIs” but targets enterprise GCs ($50K+/year contracts) — serving $275/mo remodelers would cannibalize their positioning. Trunk Tools ($40M Series B, $70M total funding, Jul 2025) targets large commercial GCs with document Q&A — different ICP entirely.
  • Speed moat: 21-week build to production while competitors are 2–3 years from equivalent capability
  • Lock-in via workflow: Once agents are configured for a contractor’s specific processes, switching costs are high (prompt customization, integration configuration, historical data)
  • Network effect: Ambassador network creates compounding acquisition that competitors can’t replicate with paid marketing
  • Domain depth: Agent prompts encode construction-specific knowledge (lien laws, draw schedules, AIA documents, CSI codes) that general-purpose AI tools lack
  • Pricing moat: $275–$750/mo is below enterprise competitors’ minimum deal sizes. They can’t profitably serve this segment.
  • Market size buffer: Even at 1,000 customers, Kiptra is a rounding error in the ~128K remodeling firms in the US market (NAHB data). Expansion into HVAC, landscaping, and GC verticals via the template system widens the moat further.
11 Adam dependency — Single-person sales engine High Build inbound channels by Month 6; hire SDR earlier (at 30–40 customers) if warm leads deplete faster than projected.
12 AI model pricing increases — Anthropic/OpenAI raise prices 2x Medium Vercel AI Gateway’s multi-provider routing enables rapid switching to cheaper models; $35/customer conservative buffer absorbs moderate increases.
13 Regulatory risk for AI-generated estimates — State/local government regulates AI-generated construction estimates or lien waivers Medium All AI outputs are human-reviewed drafts, never auto-executed. Legal review of output disclaimers.
14 LiteLLM production degradation — Eliminated Low Eliminated — LiteLLM replaced with managed Vercel AI Gateway. No self-hosted proxy to degrade.
15 NATS node failure — Mitigated by 3-node Raft cluster (Day 1) Low Mitigated from Day 1: 3rd NATS node ($5–10/mo) deployed in Wave 1 to form 3-node Raft quorum, enabling JetStream HA. If any single node fails, the remaining 2 maintain quorum and event processing continues. Agent execution also re-triggerable from BullMQ state as defense-in-depth.
16 Prompt injection via inbound channels — Crafted emails or support tickets manipulate agent behavior High Three-layer defense (architectural separation, static tool permissions, probabilistic classifier). All inbound content sanitized before agent processing.
17 Construction market contraction High The US remodeling market is cyclical — it contracted sharply in 2008–2009 and softened in 2023–2024 as interest rates rose. A 20% market contraction would: (1) Reduce viable target customers (fewer active contractors), (2) Cause customers to cut discretionary software spend, (3) Accelerate churn above 45% as companies downsize, (4) Reduce Adam’s Slate revenue and sales bandwidth. Likelihood: Medium (construction cycles run 5–7 years). Mitigation: (a) The $5–20K setup fee front-loads revenue, reducing exposure to mid-contract churn. (b) Multi-vertical expansion (HVAC, electrical, plumbing per Section 6) diversifies beyond remodeling. (c) AI agents that reduce overhead costs become MORE valuable during a downturn, not less — position as a cost-cutting tool when selling in a soft market. (d) Maintain 6 months of Year 1 operating reserves ($20–30K, scaling to $100K+ as monthly costs grow) from Y1 profit.
18 Business OS module complexity underestimated — 18 modules with deep business logic and real-time collaboration High Phase the build: 5 core modules first (scorecards, goals, meetings, org chart, issues), 4 advanced modules second, 9 remaining in Phase 3. Each module is a self-contained feature with its own schema, agents, and UI. Timeboxed 2-week sprints with user testing between phases. Cancel advanced modules if core adoption is weak. 18–28 week range reflects genuine uncertainty.
19 Three.js 3D rendering performance — Browser-based 3D design may not match native CAD tools Medium 3-tier rendering strategy (real-time preview → path-traced final → AI-enhanced photorealistic) manages expectations. WebGL 2.0 baseline with WebGPU optional upgrade path. Kitchen scenes are bounded complexity (<500 objects typically). Adam validates visual quality at each milestone. Fallback: export to SketchUp/Revit for final presentation if browser rendering insufficient.
20 Manufacturer catalog data acquisition — Cabinet/fixture specifications needed in structured format Medium Adam’s direct manufacturer relationships are the primary acquisition channel. Start with 3 manufacturers (one per tier: stock, semi-custom, custom). pgvector RAG search over catalog data reduces the need for perfect structure. Fallback: manual spec entry UI for missing products. Community catalog contribution model in Year 2.
21 AI Insurance Coverage Gap High Verisk January 2026 GL exclusion forms may exclude AI-generated output claims. Standard E&O policies may not cover AI-specific liability. PRE-LAUNCH GATE: (a) Obtain written E&O confirmation that AI-generated output claims are covered — must be confirmed before first paying customer. (b) Evaluate standalone AI liability insurance (Munich Re / Armilla / Testudo, $5–$15K/year) — obtain quotes and bind coverage before launch. (c) Include disclaimers on all AI outputs (“decision support only — not professional advice”). This is a hard launch gate — no first customer until AI liability coverage is confirmed in writing.
22 Data Breach High Multi-tenant SaaS storing financial data (estimates, invoices, payment records) is a high-value target. Mitigation: (a) Section 12 security controls (RLS, encryption at rest/transit, tenant isolation). (b) Incident response runbook (pre-drafted before first customer). (c) Cyber liability insurance (budgeted Y1: $3K). (d) 72-hour breach notification to affected customers and relevant regulators. (e) Quarterly security review by Year 2 (penetration testing, dependency audit, access log review).
23 AI Model Deprecation Medium AI providers deprecate models every 6–12 months (e.g., Claude 3 Opus sunset, GPT-4 version changes). Mitigation: (a) AIGateway abstraction layer enables model swaps without code changes. (b) Version-controlled prompt templates with per-model tuning. (c) Quality baseline test suite re-run on every model change (automated regression). (d) Prompt compatibility test suite validates output format/quality before production rollout. (e) Maintain fallback model configuration for immediate rollback.
24 Vendor Lock-in Low
  • Supabase: Standard PostgreSQL — exit via pg_dump to any Postgres host (RDS, Neon, self-hosted). RLS policies are standard SQL.
  • Valkey: Standard Redis protocol — drop-in replacement with any Redis-compatible service (ElastiCache, Upstash, self-hosted).
  • Vercel: Standard Next.js — deploy to any Node.js host (Cloudflare Pages, Netlify, self-hosted). No Vercel-specific APIs used.
  • better-auth: MIT licensed, forkable. Auth data stored in Supabase (portable). Migration to Auth.js or custom auth is straightforward.
25 GDPR Compliance (EU Expansion) Low Low risk in Year 1 (US-focused); Medium in Year 2+. Mitigation: (a) ToS restricts service to US customers in Year 1. (b) EU expansion requires: Supabase EU region deployment, DigitalOcean EU datacenter (AMS/FRA), Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) for AI processing of EU personal data. (c) Right to erasure (Article 17) requires same data discovery process as CCPA but with stricter 30-day timeline and no ‘unreasonable effort’ exception for backups. (d) Budget $10–15K for GDPR compliance legal review before EU launch.
26 Payment Processing Disruption — Stripe account restriction or fund holds Low Card payments accepted with surcharge (2.9% + $0.30, revenue-neutral). Stripe hold risk mitigated by: volume ramping, proactive verification, ACH as default/recommended payment method, Helcim backup. ACH remains the pre-selected default, reducing card volume and associated dispute risk. Card surcharge makes processing fees revenue-neutral — customers pay the fee, not Kiptra. Backup processor: Helcim maintained as documented backup — if Stripe account is ever restricted, Helcim processes payments while the Stripe issue is resolved. Helcim supports both ACH and card payments.
27 Cyncly IP risk — 2020 Design proprietary format parsing Medium Cyncly’s 2020 Design file format is proprietary. Reverse-engineering or parsing these files without authorization could trigger IP claims (DMCA, trade secret, EULA breach). Mitigation: (a) Do NOT reverse-engineer 2020 Design file formats — treat as off-limits. (b) Prioritize non-proprietary data sources: direct manufacturer feeds (CSV/XML), manufacturer-specific electronic ordering formats, PDF spec sheet extraction via Claude, and aggregator APIs (KitchenDEV, Kitchen365). (c) For manufacturers whose data is only available via 2020 Design exports, negotiate explicit written data portability/re-use rights through Adam’s dealer relationships. (d) Legal review of Cyncly EULA terms before using any 2020 Design-sourced catalog data. (e) Document all data provenance in kb_catalog_products.source column to ensure audit trail. Likelihood increases if Kiptra gains market traction and Cyncly perceives competitive threat.
28 Manufacturer data quality — Inconsistent dimensions, pricing, and availability across sources High Manufacturer catalog data arrives in heterogeneous formats with inconsistent units (inches vs mm), missing fields, stale pricing, and discontinued SKUs. This directly impacts BOM accuracy and estimate reliability — wrong dimensions cause NKBA validation failures; wrong prices erode contractor margins. Mitigation: (a) Strict Zod schema validation on ingest with rejection of non-conforming records (already in Section 16.12). (b) Unit normalization pipeline (all dimensions stored in inches, all prices in USD). (c) 90-day price staleness warnings with PriceWarning flags on BOM line items. (d) Cross-source price reconciliation — when the same SKU appears from multiple sources, flag discrepancies >5% for manual review. (e) Adam validates launch manufacturer data quality as a hard gate (already in Section 16.12). (f) Catalog configuration rules follow KCMA (Kitchen Cabinet Manufacturers Association) and AWI (Architectural Woodwork Institute) standards for cabinet dimensions and construction specifications — these are industry standards, not proprietary to any vendor.
29 Unaudited Engineering Spec gaps — 105 vulnerabilities identified High 105 gaps identified by KB-informed attack audit (2026-03-22). 90 fixes applied to Engineering Spec. Remaining 15 LOW-severity items deferred. See spec amendments tagged “Attack Audit 2026-03-22”.
30 Multi-persona complexity — Each new persona requires template creation and UX validation Medium Each new persona (dealer, sales rep, manufacturer) requires prompt templates, tour content, checklist items, dashboard layouts, and UX validation. Mitigated by the template system — all persona-specific behavior is configuration, not code. Each persona is ~1–2 weeks of template creation + testing. Only the contractor persona ships in Waves 1–4; other personas are post-launch additions. Cross-tenant B2B network layer adds complexity (data leakage risk, event bus namespacing) — mitigated by dedicated CrossTenantAccessService that validates tenant_relationships before any cross-tenant data access, plus penetration testing before B2B features go live. See Section 6a.
Top 3 Risks Requiring Immediate Attention

1. Adam dependency (single-person revenue engine) — Adam is the sole sales channel. If he becomes unavailable or warm leads deplete, acquisition stops entirely. This is existential — build inbound channels by Month 6 and hire SDR earlier if needed.
2. Slate PoC validation — Validates the entire product thesis. If Slate doesn't show measurable results by Week 4, we need to pivot agent priorities before scaling sales.
3. AI insurance coverage gap (PRE-LAUNCH GATE) — Verisk 2026 GL exclusions may leave AI-generated output claims uninsured. Obtain written E&O confirmation + evaluate standalone AI liability insurance (Munich Re / Armilla / Testudo, $5–$15K/year) before first paying customer. Hard launch gate.

Overall Risk Posture

Stress testing shows profitability at 20 customers and 35% churn. However, compound scenario of market contraction + accelerated churn + AI liability claim could threaten solvency. Maintain 6 months of Year 1 operating reserves ($20–30K, scaling to $100K+ as monthly costs grow). Setup fees front-load revenue and provide a substantial buffer, but the business is not immune to simultaneous tail risks.

Legal & Compliance Requirements (KB-Derived, 2026-03-21)

Research into AI design liability, intellectual property, and data privacy surfaced the following pre-launch legal requirements:

  • AI Design Liability (CRITICAL): AI-generated kitchen/bath layouts that violate building code create liability risk. No legal precedent yet but the legal community is actively warning. Required: ToS with explicit liability limitation, prominent “FOR PLANNING PURPOSES ONLY — NOT FOR CONSTRUCTION” disclaimers on ALL design outputs, Tech E&O insurance ($2,000–5,000/year), never use “certified” or “guaranteed” for AI layouts, log all designs with constraint validation results for defensibility.
  • NKBA Intellectual Property: NKBA guidelines are copyrighted; dimensional facts (e.g., “36 inches minimum clearance”) are NOT copyrightable (Feist v. Rural Telephone, 1991) but creative expression IS. “NKBA” is a registered trademark. Required: Encode rules as functional code using dimensional values only — never copy guideline text. UI must say “Code-Compliant” or “Clearance Verified” NOT “NKBA Compliant.” Add non-affiliation disclaimer. The mockup’s “NKBA Compliant” badge must be reworded.
  • Manufacturer Catalog Pricing: Dealer net pricing (multipliers, volume tiers) is often treated as trade secret. Required: Written permission from each manufacturer before storing pricing data. Dealer net costs visible ONLY to the authenticated contractor who owns the dealer account. Data licensing agreement template for manufacturer onboarding. Price data deletable per manufacturer request.
  • CCPA / State Privacy: Floor plans with customer addresses, project details, and pricing constitute personal information under CCPA and 15+ state privacy laws. Required: Privacy policy covering design data, DSAR handling process, data retention policy with deletion rights, design data encrypted at rest. Multi-tenant RLS isolation already implemented.
  • OFDA Standard Correction: OFDA (Office Furniture Dealers Alliance) XML is for commercial office furniture ordering, NOT kitchen cabinets. Kitchen cabinet manufacturers use proprietary ordering formats (manufacturer-specific portals, CSV/Excel uploads). References to “OFDA XML” in this plan should be read as “manufacturer-specific electronic order format” — the actual format varies by manufacturer (KraftMaid, Merillat, Yorktowne each have their own).
  • Construction Estimates: All estimates must be labeled “APPROXIMATE — For planning purposes only.” Never use “bid,” “quote,” or “guaranteed price” in estimate UI.

Pre-Launch Legal Budget: Estimated $18,000–$51,000 for: ToS + Privacy Policy drafting ($5K–$15K), IP review covering NKBA/manufacturer data/competitor analysis ($5K–$15K), Tech E&O insurance ($2K–$5K/year), data licensing agreement template ($3K–$8K), state-by-state estimate disclaimer review ($3K–$8K).

Terms of Service — Implementation Requirements (2026-03-21)

The full Terms of Service (/kiptra-legal/terms-of-service.md) contains legally critical provisions that must be enforced in application code. The following are mandatory implementation items:

  • ToS acceptance gate on first login: Checkbox + “I Accept” button before any feature access. Record acceptance timestamp, ToS version, user ID, and IP address in a tos_acceptances database table. Re-prompt when ToS version changes.
  • AI disclaimer injection on every design output: On-screen: “FOR PLANNING PURPOSES ONLY — Not for construction without professional verification.” In exported PDFs: full disclaimer on cover sheet AND footer of every page. In exported DXF/SVG/IFC: disclaimer in metadata/comment fields. In shared links: disclaimer banner above the shared design view. In estimate outputs: “APPROXIMATE COST RANGE — Not a bid, quote, or guaranteed price.”
  • “Clearance Verified” badge must include “Advisory only” tooltip: Text: “Based on industry dimensional guidelines. Advisory only — verify with a licensed professional before construction.”
  • Every estimate labeled “APPROXIMATE — Not a bid or quote”: Header: “APPROXIMATE ESTIMATE — For planning purposes only.” Footer: pricing source date, variance warning, “not a bid, quote, or contract.” Display “Prices last updated: [date]” with visual warning if >90 days old.
  • Export watermarks/disclaimers on all file formats: PDF (jsPDF cover sheet injection), PNG/JPEG (overlay text), CSV/XML (disclaimer row/comment). All exports include “Generated by Kiptra AI v[X.X] on [date]. For planning purposes only.”
  • Data deletion endpoint (CCPA compliance, 30-day grace period): DELETE /api/v1/users/:id/data — deletes all designs, BOMs, estimates, project data, uploaded documents. Retains anonymized aggregate analytics and audit logs (legal hold). 30-day grace period after account cancellation before permanent deletion. Email confirmation of deletion completion.
  • ToS version tracking with 30-day advance notice on changes: Track ToS version in system config. Notify users of material changes via email AND in-app banner with 30-day lead time. Re-acceptance required for material changes. Previous versions archived and accessible.
  • Database table: tos_acceptances — columns: id (UUID), user_id (FK), tos_version (text), accepted_at (timestamptz), ip_address (inet), user_agent (text), acceptance_method (text). RLS enabled — users can only see their own acceptances.
  • Pre-launch checklist: Attorney review of ToS, Privacy Policy drafted and linked, all disclaimer injection points implemented and tested, data deletion endpoint tested, ToS versioning system implemented, Tech E&O insurance active, manufacturer data licensing agreements signed.

Reference: Full ToS at /kiptra-legal/terms-of-service.md. Engineering implementation details in Section 16.13.8 of the Engineering Spec.

Patent Landscape — Clear Path Confirmed
  • USPTO search across Cyncly, 20-20 Technologies, Compusoft, Planit Software: zero blocking patents found.
  • Cyncly’s only expired patent (US7072729B2, design-to-manufacturing workflow) lapsed in 2018 for non-payment of maintenance fees. Final cessation 2023.
  • Chief Architect’s 7 patents cover architecture/AR navigation — none cover kitchen/cabinet/BOM design features.
  • Cyncly’s moat is copyright/trade-secret, not patents — their SHD AG lawsuit (August 2024) targets unauthorized use of catalog data and Catalogue Editor software, not software functionality or design tool concepts.
  • Safeguards: independent development provenance documented via git history, manufacturer CSV data only (no .CAT files), zero Cyncly-derived code.
  • Recommendation: FTO (Freedom to Operate) analysis with patent counsel ($5K–$15K) before production launch — standard practice for legal defensibility.
Engineering Spec Attack Audit (2026-03-22)

6 KB-informed attack agents audited the full 16,000-line Engineering Spec, finding 105 gaps (8 CRITICAL, 38 HIGH, 37 MEDIUM, 15 LOW). 90 fixes applied. Key changes:

8 CRITICAL Fixes:

  • Webhook signature verification must use raw body bytes (parsed JSON always fails)
  • Supavisor tenant context leak: fail-closed + integration test proving the attack vector
  • Next.js 15+ async APIs: params, searchParams, cookies, headers all return Promise
  • Never add 'use client' to layout.tsx (cascades to entire app)
  • Server Actions are public POST endpoints (must validate with Zod + auth inside every action)
  • BullMQ removeOnComplete/removeOnFail mandatory on every queue (Redis OOM without it)
  • Outbox pattern: DDL added (outbox_events table), webhook processing uses outbox
  • Wall/Room data model: WallSegment, WallOpening, RoomModel interfaces defined

Top Architecture Additions:

  • Global app.onError() handler with RFC 7807 error format
  • Mandatory middleware registration order (9-step sequence)
  • Cursor-based pagination on all list endpoints
  • Idempotency keys on all mutating endpoints
  • Branded types (TenantId, UserId) preventing cross-assignment
  • Per-execution token budget enforcement via onStepFinish
  • Separate Valkey instances for BullMQ vs cache
  • SSRF protection blocking private IP ranges
  • PII redaction in all logs (SHA-256 hashing)
  • KEK stored as Docker secret, not env var
  • Backup encryption mandatory
  • Deploy gates with GitHub environment protection
  • Incident response plan moved to Wave 1 (was Wave 4)
  • CodeQL + dependency review + secret scanning in CI
  • SLOs defined per endpoint category

K&B Design Tool Additions:

  • KBEditorStore Zustand interface with all slices
  • CabinetParameters with KCMA codes, door styles, overlay types
  • Catalog search API contract (POST /api/v1/kb/catalog/search)
  • Render queue API contract (POST /api/v1/kb/render)
  • AI loop error handling matrix (5 failure modes with recovery)
  • Dimension input UX (feet-inches parsing)
  • Snap/grid system specification
  • Keyboard shortcut map
  • Two-tier project versioning (client Zundo + server snapshots)
  • 7 starter kitchen templates
  • Appliance data model with electrical/plumbing requirements
  • PBR material library schema
  • 7 new NATS event subjects for K&B pipeline

Cross-Cutting:

  • Outbox events DDL + polling worker
  • Unified Result<T,E> error handling
  • TraceId propagation through Socket.io
  • AI testing architecture (MockLanguageModelV1, Promptfoo)
  • Capacity planning table (connections, memory, disk per scale)
  • Database rollback scripts mandatory
  • Feature flag gating for K&B module
  • Global + per-tier AI concurrency limits

Reference: Full amendments in Engineering Spec, tagged “Spec Amendment (Attack Audit 2026-03-22)”.
